Document 128 of 500

Trump survived a bullet. U.S. democracy will survive, too; Pendulum of opinion swings both ways

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-survived-bullet-u-s-democracy-will-survive/docview/3081472055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Trump+survived+a+bullet.+U.S.+democracy+will+survive%2C+too%3B+Pendulum+of+opinion+swings+both+ways&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is an emerging consensus that the nearly deadly bullet that grazed Donald Trump's upper right ear in Butler, Penn., last weekend has fired the Republican candidate back into the White House.
That view appears to extend to senior members of the Democratic party. "We've all resigned ourselves to a second Trump presidency," one source told online publication, Axios on Sunday.
There will be many twists and turns before the November election, but what is clear is that Trump's status as a champion of the under-represented has been enhanced by the assassination attempt. He has been masterful at creating an image of himself as a persecuted fighter against a "deep state" that is out to get him. The shooting enhances that victimhood.
He has been equally adroit at building a coalition of conservatives: people who resent government overreach, and those who believe they have become strangers in their own land because of cultural changes they don't agree with. Trump has united them in an us-versusthem identity.
The voters who elected him in 2016 are still with him, testimony to how hard it is to change the minds of people who have wrapped up their political beliefs with their identity.
He is benefiting from the anti-incumbency mood that is sweeping democracies around the globe, as high inflation has raised the cost of living everywhere.
And, of course, he is faced with a confused Democratic candidate who can't differentiate between the president of Ukraine and the president of Russia.
Americans rarely vote for incremental shifts and have a greater appetite for sweeping change than do other democracies. It seems likely that after a period of progressive government, the pendulum of opinion is likely to swing past the centre of gravity and go a similar distance on the other side.
The consolation for supporters of western liberal values like individual freedom, open markets and the rule of law is that after a period of time, the pendulum will likely come back to a point of rest.
The recent experience in Great Britain was that, after nearly a decade of turmoil caused by the referendum to leave the European Union, voters elected a Labour government that promised an end to chaos and, in the words of new Prime Minister Keir Starmer, "a politics that treads more lightly on all of our lives."
Brexit has resulted in a GDP that is estimated to be up to five per cent lower than it might have been and a recent YouGov poll suggested a majority of Britons now believe the negatives of leaving the EU outweigh the positives. Only 15 per cent think the positives outweighed the negatives, the poll said.
The broken promises have produced an anxious, battered country that desires some stability.
"We need a rediscovery of who we are," said Starmer. But British voters had to go through the Brexit ordeal to reach the conclusion that maybe the elites and experts were onto something when they said it was a bad idea.
America has not yet reached that state of exhaustion. Tribal emotions are still raging and the U.S.'s democratic institutions are in peril.
The framers of the Constitution knew they were likely to be subject to, in the words of Founding Father James Madison, "the turbulence and weakness of unruly passions." Mechanisms were built in to insulate the U.S. Constitution from the "mania of the moment." Those mechanisms will be severely tested if Trump is elected and lives up to his campaign utterances.
He holds a maximalist interpretation of unitary executive authority, a fancy way of saying he thinks he should be able to do whatever he wants as president.
This is not intended as an anti-Trump rant. He is proposing some policies that are reasonable and overdue, particularly those that course-correct the excesses of the illiberal left and its obsessive belief about obtaining justice for identity groups by deplatforming their enemies and imposing equity.
Trump is proposing reforms such as education programs that teach students to love their country, "not hate the country, like they're being taught right now."
But other proposals are misguided (a universal baseline tariff of 10 per cent on all imports), worrying (the suggestion that the U.S. might abandon NATO; the construction of internment camps for immigrants and homeless people) or simply anti-democratic (directing the Department of Justice to go after political enemies).
The theme of revenge for previous grievances runs throughout his unofficial platform. He has told his supporters: "I am your warrior. I am your justice. And for those who have been wronged and betrayed, I am your retribution."
Some, even much of this may be campaign rhetoric, but this will be a president who is much better prepared to push through his agenda than he was in 2016. Media reports suggest plans to strip civil service job protections are at an advanced stage, so that "deep state" defenders can be replaced with Trump loyalists.
Western liberalism has been under threat before from isolationism, protectionism, Bolshevism and fascism. It has adapted and survived, and it will again.
Trump's fiscal and trade policies, if implemented as advertised, will invite retaliation and drive-up inflation, as will his proposal to devalue the dollar to reduce the trade deficit and make exports cheaper.
We have already seen what happens to the popularity of governments that usher in higher prices.
Trump will test the guardrails imposed by the Founding Fathers. But they are robust.
Will the silent majority of American voters really sit idly by while 237 years of diversity of opinion is unravelled for the greater glorification of one man? The Supreme Court has granted immunity for all official presidential actions, nominally in order to keep politics from interfering with the president's duties.
But even the conservative majority would surely balk at overturning the 22nd Amendment, which prohibits a third term in office (and one which Trump has mused about challenging).
Partisanship and pre-existing beliefs are hard to shake. But they are not immutable.
Brexit has shown that disillusionment follows when it becomes clear to voters that their politicians have failed them.
A poll for NPR/PBS suggests that three-quarters of Americans want members of Congress to compromise. The exhausted majority are already tired of the tribalism, and that will only become more apparent.
As Abraham Lincoln once said: "Why should there not be a patient confidence in the ultimate justice of the people? Is there any better or equal hope in the world?" National Post jivison@criffel.ca

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade EU membership; Industry: 92111 :‎ Executive Offices 81394 :‎ Political Organizations

Subject: International trade; Political campaigns; Presidents; Democracy; EU membership; Voters; Homeless people; Political parties; Shootings; Candidates; Assassinations & assas sination attempts

Location: United States--US

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Trump survived a bullet. U.S. democracy will survive, too; Pendulum of opinion swings both ways

Publication title: National Post; Don Mills, Ont.

Document 129 of 500

The left blames 'rhetoric'; Somehow rude tweets are worse than assassination attempt

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/left-blames-rhetoric-somehow-rude-tweets-are/docview/3081471988/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=The+left+blames+%27rhetoric%27%3B+Somehow+rude+tweets+are+worse+than+assassination+attempt&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's more deranged critics are so focused on tone and language that they are either dismissing the assassination attempt against him or they are outright blaming him for the attack. As regrettable as the Saturday shooting at the Trump rally was, his critics seem to think, what's truly disturbing is the "rhetoric" that characterizes the U.S. presidential campaign.
And by rhetoric, of course, they don't mean the Democrats, and their water carriers in the media, constantly painting the former, and possible future, president as a fascist threat to the American republic. You know the kind of argument that might persuade an unstable 20 year-old owner of an AR-15 rifle that to save democracy, Trump can't simply be defeated at the polls, he must be eliminated.
No, Trump's critics mean his tone, his choice of words, are the real problem.
After being shot in the ear, Trump could be heard saying "fight, fight, fight." To any normal, well adjusted, person, that was a call for his supporters to stick with him in the election campaign. Politicians use fight and war terminology all the time, but when Trump uses such language, and only when Trump uses such language, it is interpreted as a literal call to violence.
So, predictably, a pearl-clutching CNN host scolded the presumptive Republican presidential nominee for his choice of words, calling them "odd." A British journalist similarly called them "depressing."
On Sunday, writing in The Nation, author Sasha Abramsky argued that it would be a "terrible mistake" to "let the narrative take hold that Biden and the Democratic Party are to blame for this event." Biden asking his supporters to forget about his mental capacity, and to, instead, "put Trump in a bullseye" apparently gets a pass. (Imagine what the reaction from progressives would be if Trump had said those words weeks before an assassination attempt on the current president.)
Abramsky doesn't even bother to address Biden's comments, instead writing that "Trump's violent rhetoric ought to be as disqualifying tomorrow as it was yesterday."
So, there must be a long list of statements by Trump where he objectively incited violence, right? Well, not exactly.
Among the examples of such "rhetoric" that Abramsky takes issue with, is when during the Black Lives Matter riots, Trump posted on Twitter about possible plans to send in the military to quell the unrest. Abramsky doesn't offer this context, and just highlights that the tweet included the phrase "When the looting starts, the shooting starts."
So actual violence in the form of rioting and looting should be allowed to unfold unchecked, but Trump's admittedly careless tweet about sending in the military, "disqualifying."
Another example from Abramsky stretches what could be counted as "violent rhetoric" to the point of ridiculousness. "This is a man who said there were 'very fine people'among the neo-Nazi marchers in Charlottesville in 2017," Abramsky wrote, referring to a news conference Trump gave at the time.
Pretending "very fine people" is somehow violent is, at least, less egregious than implying that Trump was calling neo-Nazis "fine people." In fact, anyone who looked at the transcript would see that "very fine people" was not a reference to neo-Nazis. "I'm not talking about the neo-Nazis and the white nationalists, because they should be condemned totally," Trump said.
But, whatever. Expecting progressive media to accurately report on Trump is a foolish exercise best left to toddlers.
None of this, however, is to suggest that Trump has comported himself as anything resembling a statesman, but "violent rhetoric" has little to do with it.
His attempt to overturn the 2020 election by falsely claiming he lost due to widespread fraud does indeed make him unsuitable for the presidency. The Jan. 6 riot, where Trump supporters stormed the U.S. Capitol, was a direct result of the former president's argument that the election had been stolen.
It is important to distinguish that it wasn't Trump's "rhetoric" that the protest "will be wild" that led to violence, it was his irresponsible claims about fraud. The tone, and the word choice are secondary if they matter at all, and entirely irrelevant without an underlying argument.
Trump's behaviour shouldn't be excused, but the Democrats and progressive journalists rarely bother to wonder why so many Americans were willing to believe the fraud story. If they bothered to ask, they might discover that the false claims that Russian "collusion" with Trump led to his election in 2016, bred skepticism and distrust in American institutions. The same could be said about the false claim that the story about Hunter Biden's laptop in the 2020 election was Russian disinformation.
As norm breaking and disruptive as Trump was and is, U.S. institutions - Congress, the press, intelligence agencies, the justice department - have all responded to him by debasing what they stand for, by overtly politicizing and twisting their mission. After years of that, it seems pretty obvious why Trump voters might be open to believing the election was stolen, even though it wasn't.
But for progressives, the blame is almost always on the "rhetoric." They aim to put words on equal footing with actual violence.
In Canada, a similar derangement happened after Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre tweeted in the aftermath of Saturday's shooting that "I am also happy that the suspected shooter is dead."
That would be a totally normal sentiment for anyone to think and even express, but because Poilievre has a good chance of becoming the next prime minister, his critics on social media spent the better part of the weekend rending garments and lighting themselves on fire, because he didn't offer a carbon copy of the Pablum delivered by pretty much every other world leader. Thoughts and prayers for anyone exposed to Poilievre's Twitter account I suppose.
The only one to blame for the Trump assassination attempt is the shooter, Thomas Matthew Crooks. Conservatives should avoid putting too much stock into blaming Biden's rhetoric. It's nonsense when the left does it, and it would be nonsense to do it here.
National Post

Business indexing term: Industry: 92111 :‎ Executive Offices

Document 131 of 500

John Ivison: Trump survived a bullet. American democracy will survive this, too

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/john-ivison-trump-survived-bullet-american/docview/3081394023/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=John+Ivison%3A+Trump+survived+a+bullet.+American+democracy+will+survive+this%2C+too&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

He is proposing some policies that are reasonable and overdue, particularly those that course-correct the excesses of the illiberal left and its obsessive belief about obtaining justice for identity groups by deplatforming their enemies and imposing equity.
Trump is proposing reforms such as education programs that teach students to love their country, "not hate the country, like they're being taught right now."
But other proposals are misguided (a universal baseline tariff of 10 per cent on all imports), worrying (the suggestion that the U.S. might abandon NATO; the construction of internment camps for immigrants and homeless people) or simply anti-democratic (directing the Department of Justice to go after political enemies).
The theme of revenge for previous grievances runs throughout his unofficial platform. He has told his supporters: "I am your warrior. I am your justice. And for those who have been wronged and betrayed, I am your retribution."
Some, even much of this may be campaign rhetoric, but this will be a president who is much better prepared to push through his agenda than he was in 2016. Media reports suggest plans to strip civil service job protections are at an advanced stage, so that "deep state" defenders can be replaced with Trump loyalists.
Western liberalism has been under threat before from isolationism, protectionism, Bolshevism and fascism. It has adapted and survived, and it will again.
Carson Jerema: The left pretends Trump's 'rhetoric' is worse than assassination attempt
Terry Glavin: Maybe Joe Biden should stop saying Trump will destroy democracy
Trump's fiscal and trade policies, if implemented as advertised, will invite retaliation and drive-up inflation, as will his proposal to devalue the dollar to reduce the trade deficit and make exports cheaper.
We have already seen what happens to the popularity of governments that usher in higher prices.
Trump will test the guardrails imposed by the Founding Fathers. But they are robust.
Will the silent majority of American voters really sit idly by while 237 years of diversity of opinion is unravelled for the greater glorification of one man?
The Supreme Court has granted immunity for all official presidential actions, nominally in order to keep politics from interfering with the president's duties.
But even the conservative majority would surely balk at overturning the 22nd Amendment, which prohibits a third term in office (and one which Trump has mused about challenging).
Partisanship and pre-existing beliefs are hard to shake. But they are not immutable. Brexit has shown that disillusionment follows when it becomes clear to voters that their politicians have failed them.
A poll for NPR/PBS suggests that three-quarters of Americans want members of Congress to compromise The exhausted majority are already tired of the tribalism, and that will only become more apparent.
As Abraham Lincoln once said: "Why should there not be a patient confidence in the ultimate justice of the people? Is there any better or equal hope in the world?"
jivison@criffel.ca( mailto:jivison@criffel.ca )

Document 132 of 500

Michael Taube: Ontario's plan to support local news without direct subsidies is groundbreaking

Author: Kline, Jesse

Publication info: Kline, Jesse. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-taube-ontarios-plan-support-local-news/docview/3080962788/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Michael+Taube%3A+Ontario%27s+plan+to+support+local+news+without+direct+subsidies+is+groundbreaking&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kline%2C+Jesse&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced a plan to help provide financial support for newspaper publishers in the province. The best part about it: it doesn't come at any cost to the taxpayers.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy sent letters to several provincial Crown corporations, including the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation, Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario. These agencies were directed to allocate one-quarter of their annual advertising budget, which is reportedly over $100 million, to Ontario's news publishers by Sept. 3.
This means there will be roughly $25 million available for news organizations in Ontario, including the National Post's parent company, Postmedia Network Inc., to fund local journalism.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," reads Bethlenfalvy's letter. It also highlights the Ontario government's belief there is a "compelling opportunity to leverage the advertising spends of Ontario's major government agencies to support Ontario workers and content."
This is a creative and forward-thinking economic plan by Premier Doug Ford and the Ontario PCs. It will help ensure that a strong focus on local news content will be maintained, and significant job opportunities will be available for young journalists and established veterans alike. Most importantly, it will play a vital role in the survival of Ontario newspapers.
The reality we all have to face is that there's going to be some level of financial involvement by federal and provincial governments in Canada's news industry, both now and in the future.
The vast majority of our newspapers, magazines and news websites currently receive some type of public funding. That being said, the use of direct subsidies can't be the only funding lifeline for Canadian publications, including newspapers. Ontario's directive to allocate part of the advertising budget of a number of provincial Crown corporations is, therefore, a step in the right direction.
This advertising budget is, unlike the introduction of direct subsidies, already earmarked and accounted for in annual government expenditures. It's an existing sum rather than an additional source of taxpayer money. Hence, the $25 million allocated for Ontario publications simply involves shifting one-quarter of the budget, while the remaining three-quarters can be used as the Crown corporations see fit.
The Ford government's policy will allow government agencies to continue to get their messages across to Ontarians, while investing in local journalism. The money is to be used for Ontario-based newspapers, news content and journalists working in the profession.
This funding directive isn't being handed out with no strings attached, either. A review will apparently be conducted on a quarterly basis. If the Ontario government feels that the main objectives of "promoting local content and culture and supporting Ontario jobs" aren't being met, it will consider withdrawing the funding altogether. Hence, it's up to Ontario newspapers to ensure that the money is being used effectively, wisely and, above all, properly.
Andrew MacLeod, Postmedia's president and chief executive officer, said, "I'm certainly extremely grateful to the leadership of Premier Ford and his team. My hope is this will serve as an example, a north star, not just for other provincial governments, but for the federal government and for major corporations."
I agree with MacLeod's sentiment wholeheartedly. There are ways for the public and private sectors to help and support Canadian journalism that don't involve direct subsidies. Ford and the Ontario PCs have identified one financial path to success. There are many others still to be considered and utilized.
National Post
Peter Sharpe: Ontario's plan to keep ad spending local is worthy of emulation
Paul Deegan: Ontario leads the way on sustaining quality journalism

Document 133 of 500

Carson Jerema: The left pretends Trump's 'rhetoric' is worse than assassination attempt

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carson-jerema-left-pretends-trumps-rhetoric-is/docview/3080961153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Carson+Jerema%3A+The+left+pretends+Trump%27s+%27rhetoric%27+is+worse+than+assassination+attempt&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's more deranged critics are so focused on tone and language that they are either dismissing the assassination attempt against him or they are outright blaming him for the attack. As regrettable as the Saturday shooting at the Trump rally was, his critics seem to think, what's truly disturbing is the "rhetoric" that characterizes the U.S. presidential campaign.
And by rhetoric, of course, they don't mean the Democrats, and their water carriers in the media, constantly painting the former, and possible future, president as a fascist threat to the American republic. You know the kind of argument that might persuade an unstable 20 year-old owner of an AR-15 rifle that to save democracy, Trump can't simply be defeated at the polls, he must be eliminated.
No, Trump's critics mean his tone, his choice of words, are the real problem.
After being shot in the ear, Trump could be heard saying "fight, fight, fight." To any normal, well adjusted, person, that was a call for his supporters to stick with him in the election campaign. Politicians use fight and war terminology all the time, but when Trump uses such language, and only when Trump uses such language, it is interpreted as a literal call to violence.
So, predictably, a pearl-clutching CNN host scolded the presumptive Republican presidential nominee for his choice of words, calling them "odd." A British journalist similarly called them "depressing."
On Sunday, writing in The Nation, author Sasha Abramsky argued that it would be a "terrible mistake" to "let the narrative take hold that Biden and the Democratic Party are to blame for this event." Biden asking his supporters to forget about his mental capacity, and to, instead, "put Trump in a bullseye" apparently gets a pass. (Imagine what the reaction from progressives would be if Trump had said those words weeks before an assassination attempt on the current president.)
Abramsky doesn't even bother to address Biden's comments, instead writing that "Trump's violent rhetoric ought to be as disqualifying tomorrow as it was yesterday."
So, there must be a long list of statements by Trump where he objectively incited violence, right? Well, not exactly.
Among the examples of such "rhetoric" that Abramsky takes issue with, is when during the Black Lives Matter riots, Trump posted on Twitter about possible plans to send in the military to quell the unrest. Abramsky doesn't offer this context, and just highlights that the tweet included the phrase "When the looting starts, the shooting starts."
So actual violence in the form of rioting and looting should be allowed to unfold unchecked, but Trump's admittedly careless tweet about sending in the military, "disqualifying."
Another example from Abramsky stretches what could be counted as "violent rhetoric" to the point of ridiculousness. "This is a man who said there were 'very fine people' among the neo-Nazi marchers in Charlottesville in 2017," Abramsky wrote, referring to a news conference Trump gave at the time.
Pretending "very fine people" is somehow violent is, at least, less egregious than implying that Trump was calling neo-Nazis "fine people." In fact, anyone who looked at the transcript would see that "very fine people" was not a reference to neo-Nazis. "I'm not talking about the neo-Nazis and the white nationalists, because they should be condemned totally," Trump said.
But, whatever. Expecting progressive media to accurately report on Trump is a foolish exercise best left to toddlers.
None of this, however, is to suggest that Trump has comported himself as anything resembling a statesman, but "violent rhetoric" has little to do with it.
His attempt to overturn the 2020 election by falsely claiming he lost due to widespread fraud does indeed make him unsuitable for the presidency. The Jan. 6 riot, where Trump supporters stormed the U.S. capitol, was a direct result of the former president's argument that the election had been stolen.
It is important to distinguish that it wasn't Trump's "rhetoric" that the protest "will be wild" that led to violence, it was his irresponsible claims about fraud. The tone, and the word choice are secondary if they matter at all, and entirely irrelevant without an underlying argument.
Trump's behaviour shouldn't be excused, but the Democrats and progressive journalists rarely bother to wonder why so many Americans were willing to believe the fraud story. If they bothered to ask, they might discover that the false claims that Russian "collusion" with Trump led to his election in 2016, bred skepticism and distrust in American institutions. The same could be said about the false claim that the story about Hunter Biden's lap top in the 2020 election was Russian disinformation.
As norm breaking and disruptive as Trump was and is, U.S. institutions - Congress, the press, intelligence agencies, the justice department - have all responded to him by debasing what they stand for, by overtly politicizing and twisting their mission. After years of that, it seems pretty obvious why Trump voters might be open to believing the election was stolen, even though it wasn't.
But for progressives, the blame is almost always on the "rhetoric." They aim to put words on equal footing with actual violence.
In Canada, a similar derangement happened after Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre tweeted in the aftermath of Saturday's shooting that "I am also happy that the suspected shooter is dead."
That would be a totally normal sentiment for anyone to think and even express, but because Poilievre has a good chance of becoming the next prime minister, his critics on social media spent the better part of the weekend rending garments and lighting themselves on fire, because he didn't offer a carbon copy of the pablum delivered by pretty much every other world leader. Thoughts and prayers for anyone exposed to Poilievre's Twitter account I suppose.
The only one to blame for the Trump assassination attempt is the shooter, Thomas Matthew Crooks. Conservatives should avoid putting too much stock into blaming Biden's rhetoric. It's nonsense when the left does it, and it would be nonsense to do it here.
National Post
Rahim Mohamed: The attempt on Trump's life has made his re-election all but inevitable
Terry Glavin: Maybe Joe Biden should stop saying Trump will destroy democracy

Document 134 of 500

Parental rights for all

Author: Zivo, Adam

Publication info: Zivo, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parental-rights-all/docview/3080872990/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Parental+rights+for+all&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Zivo%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The resurgent "parental rights" movement has spurred vigorous debate about how much control families should exert over their children's upbringing and education. While this is not a bad thing, supporters have often defended parental rights selectively, insofar as they entrench conservative values, and ignored the fact that progressive families have the right to raise their children how they see fit, too.
This should change. Though parental rights activism has existed for decades, support for the movement erupted in 2021, when American lawmakers began to pass legislation banning "critical race theory" from elementary and secondary schools. These reforms, which have since been expanded to restrict discussions of gender and sexuality, are now active in almost half of American states - and that's reasonable.
While basic anti-racism and LGBTQ+ education should undoubtedly be included in school curriculums, deeper analysis of these topics inevitably leads to political debates that many teachers and administrators are ill-equipped to handle.
Should racial justice be understood through a progressive lens (as epitomized by the work of "anti-racist" thinkers like Ibram X. Kendi and Robin DiAngelo) or a conservative one (in the tradition of Thomas Sowell, Shelby Steele and John Mc-Whorter)? Are radical approaches to gender and sexuality correct, or are "gender critical" perspectives more convincing? What middle ground can be found by recognizing the strengths and weaknesses of different frameworks?
These questions must be handled cautiously, but school boards have forgone nuance and instead pushed for the most radically progressive positions possible. There is nothing wrong with holding progressive views (political diversity is sacrosanct, after all), but privileging them so aggressively within the public education system amounts to indoctrination. Conservative families, in this case, have been fully justified in pushing back.
The parental rights movement then grew to encapsulate gender transitions in schools, which is where Canada entered the fray.
Over the past year, New Brunswick, Saskatchewan and Alberta have passed laws barring schools from formally recognizing changes to students'pronouns or names without parental consent. This makes sense, as socially transitioning gender-questioning children is a major psychological intervention with potentially lifelong consequences. For schools to secretly facilitate these transitions without parental involvement is, in almost all cases, a gross intrusion.
While these reforms illustrate the virtues of the parental rights movement, there are other areas where advocates have overreached and infringed upon the rights of progressive families - most notably with recent campaigns to ban LGBTQ+-related books and youth-oriented drag events.
Yes, conservative families should have the right to shield younger children from some books that they believe are harmful (controlling the intellectual diet of older teenagers is another matter). However, progressive parents also have the right to expose their children to books that align with their own family values, including LGBTQ+-related material.
Similarly, while conservative families should be informed of any drag events that their children might be exposed to, and should be given ample opportunity to opt out of them, progressive parents also have the right to take their children to these events should they choose to.
On both sides, we see valid expressions of parental rights.
Finding a balanced solution shouldn't be too complicated. Libraries and schools should simply provide a diverse selection of books that speak to both progressive and conservative values, and then allow parents to restrict which titles their own children can access. Youth-oriented drag events should be permitted with generous optout opportunities for parents, so long as dress codes are respected and no presented material is sexually explicit.
Yet these types of solutions have often been ignored by parental rights activists, who have instead prioritized blanket bans that unfairly impose conservative values on progressive families. While a parent should have the right to deny their own child access to certain books or events, they have no right to deny all other children access, as well. If you truly believe in parental rights, then you must accept that other parents may raise their children in ways you disagree with.
That being said, some conservatives claim that drag events, along with many queer books, are so harmful that no child should be able to access them, regardless of their family's politics. This approach is misguided.
Yes, a small number of books are undeniably child-inappropriate (pornography should never be given to kids) and, yes, a small fraction of drag performers dress inappropriately in libraries (there should be more regulation in these spaces to prevent this). However, the vast majority of cases are not that extreme. The harms identified by many parental rights activists are, generally speaking, contentious and boil down to ideological or cultural differences.
For example, most people can agree that books that contain gender theory, or which simply showcase gay and trans people, are not objectively harmful in the way that pornography is. Though some conservatives may ardently disagree, I fail to see how they differ from the ultra-progressives who, in recent years, have purged libraries of "problematic" materials based on specious "equity" grounds. The extremists on both ends lack intellectual humility and debase the pluralism inherent to democratic life.
But while ultra-progressives are generally transparent about their paternalism, and openly admit that they want to dictate the beliefs of other people's kids, parental rights activists hide this impulse. They proclaim that every parent should raise their child as they wish - unless that parent is a progressive. This is dishonest and hypocritical. Rights mean nothing unless they apply equally to everyone.

Document 135 of 500

Letters: Keep your hands off our homes, Justin Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-keep-your-hands-off-our-homes-justin/docview/3080184620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Keep+your+hands+off+our+homes%2C+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How would 'equity tax' solve housing crisis?
Re: Does Trudeau plan to put the squeeze on older homeowners? - Michael Higgins; and Tax on home equity is latest proposal in Liberals' bogeyman approach to housing - Kim Moody, both July 9
While Justin Trudeau claims to be listening to Canadians, he must have forgotten that line when appearing on a podcast for an organization that advocates taxing senior citizens' homes. To the best of my knowledge there are no seniors out there advocating for more taxes.
Survey after survey has indicated that seniors aren't selling; they prefer to stay where they are. So in essence this would be a penalty tax for those who, through no fault of their own, saw their homed rise in value to more than $1 million. How this would drive down housing prices or increase supply is woefully unclear.
However the whole idea is predicated on the Liberals staying in power for another four years. There is a better chance of aliens landing on the front lawn of Parliament Hill than that happening.
Jeff Spooner, Kinburn, Ont.
Two articles in the July 10 National Post seemed ironically juxtaposed. Jamie Portman wrote about Dean Jobb's new book about the famous "aristocratic burglar," Arthur Barry, of the 1920s. Barry stole "only from people who (could) afford the loss." Juxtaposed, on the front page, was the other article, asking if Trudeau intends to tax senior homeowners whose homes have grown in value over the years. It seems that Trudeau is today's Arthur Barry, stealing from those whom he thinks can best afford the loss.
Don D. Wallar, Newmarket, Ont.
To paraphrase Janis Joplin: "take another little piece of my home, now baby." A "home equity tax" would be a move towards socializing private property. That's what the intention is. Take it in little pieces until it's all gone to the government. This is communism by the instalment plan.
Government at all levels have "gummed up the works." Too many regulations and taxes have created a state of paralysis in the housing industry. Planning departments have become "housing prevention departments."
Here in West Vancouver, while the federal government has opened the immigration floodgates, the municipal government has gone in the opposite direction. It is just a bit more extreme than most big city municipalities. While the feds bring in more and more people, it allows less and less housing.
Private property is first and always an economic issue. Historically, it has been the foundation of prosperity. Attack it and you will see the result.
Denis Walsh, West Vancouver
'Great artists can be deeply flawed'
Re: Literary world shocked as Alice Munro's daughter reveals her mother stayed with her abuser - Tyler Dawson, July 8
Tyler Dawson's framing of Alice Munro's art in relation to her life is a debate worth having.
Actually, most know that art is not life. Yet, the revelation that Alice Munro, a master of literary art, failed profoundly in her personal life was both shocking and compelling. It's a story worthy of her own brilliant fiction.
Great artists can be deeply flawed individuals. The sacrifice of a child's well-being, particularly in the face of criminal abuse, makes this case especially tragic. Two truths can coexist: Munro was a brilliant writer, and she failed to protect her daughter from a sexual predator in their home.
Consequently we should continue to value Munro's contributions to literature. However, society must also hold her accountable for her complicity in her daughter's prolonged suffering. Praise the art, but condemn the artist's despicable behaviour. Andrea Robin Skinner's experience serves as a stark reminder that the impact of an author's personal choices are as significant as her creative work. Full disclosure of her hidden complicity in the sexual abuse of her daughter should be added as a footnote to her reputation.
The life of children is more important than art.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Americans were deluding themselves about Biden
Re: The American media's complicity in covering up Biden's mental feebleness - Father Raymond J. de Souza, July 7
While it's clear that President Joe Biden's mental acuity is in decline, what wasn't discussed in Raymond de Souza's exposé but which should be of greater concern is the oblivious delusion that infected so many of the "anyone-but-Trump" contingency. American credibility must be questioned when the president is displayed in such a light, as in a televised debate, or was he purposely and so obviously staged because change was needed and the president was refusing to leave gracefully?
Delusion is described as a false judgment about external reality, despite incontrovertible evidence to the contrary. Joe Biden is obviously too frail to be president, but America's delusion had reached such a pandemic state that it needed an immediate and public vaccination.
Paul Baumberg, Dead Man's Flats, Alberta
Fr. de Souza characterizes The New York Times' decision to publish an editorial demanding Joe Biden's withdrawal from the U.S. presidential race as "demonstrating the independent thinking for which the Gray Lady is known."
Where was this celebrated "independent thinking" in 2003 when, responding to public pressure for reviews of his journalism, the esteemed Gray Lady declined to return the 1932 Pulitzer Prize awarded to Walter Duranty, its Moscow bureau chief and Stalin apologist who, while aware of the mass starvation, declined to report on the Ukrainian famine as to do so would perhaps affront his ideology and certainly stymie his career prospects.
Conformity of thought and complicity in coverups have not only supplanted independent thinking and integrity at The New York Times, they are in fact its hallmarks and, as history confirms, its long-established stance.
Carol-Faye Petricko, Toronto
'Cowardice' at the University of Toronto
Re: U of T president should resign over his contemptible handling of the encampment - Conrad Black, July 6
While I do not always agree with his opinions, Conrad Black was one 100 per cent accurate in his description of, and analysis of, the encampment that blighted my alma mater. Similar cowardice has infected leadership throughout our community. The recent Toronto District School Board motion to effectively "teach" anti-Zionism/antisemitism through the invented construct of "anti-Palestinian racism" is but one serious further example.
Ronald Birken, Toronto
Now, when a bunch of protesters occupy my front lawn and demand to examine my investment portfolio, it appears I must first supply them with portable toilets. Then, if I want them to leave, I must go to court to ask for an injunction and a judicial assessment as to whether their antisemitic behaviour is so foul that I might be allowed to resist their demands that I sell my oil and gas stocks.
What King's College Circle needed was a couple burly farmers with a manure spreader and an ample supply of pig dung with which to fertilize the grass beneath all those festive tents.
Patrick Cowan, North York, Ont.
MAID has gone too far
Re: This doctor has helped more than 400 patients die. How many assisted deaths are too many? - Sharon Kirkey, July 6
Justin Trudeau promised he would put Canada back on the world stage. He certainly has accomplished that with MAID. Canada now has one of the highest assisted death rates in the world. The doctor featured in Sharon Kirkey's article was proud that she has helped more than 430 Canadians to die! What has happened to the doctor's Hippocratic oath: Do no harm?
When MAID started the majority of Canadians assumed it was for those with terminal diseases and endless severe suffering. The Trudeau government is now poised to extend MAID to the mentally ill and it has even been offered it to military veterans.
It is time this entire program underwent severe scrutiny. Life is too precious to allow the Trudeau government to continue playing God.
Larry Comeau, Ottawa
Having careened down that "slippery slope" to rock bottom, Canada's health-care system now rests in the hands of death providers, one of whom - Dr. Ellen Wiebe - waxed eloquent to Sharon Kirkey about her goal of "empowering" patients to die. Ultimately however, Kirkey leaves the reader with the question: who really gets to enjoy all that "empowerment"?
Kirkey is right to shed light on complaints about this whole issue of euthanasia, from the skyrocketing number of "assisted" dead in Canada, to the enthusiastic providers who describe their role as "heartwarming", "liberating," and providing "almost an adrenaline rush." Chilling? Definitely the darkest side of "woke."
Susan K. Riggs, Guelph, Ont.
'Not the best basis for choosing a human rights chief'
Re: Three Canadian Jewish groups call for removal of incoming human rights commissioner - Tyler Dawson, June 28
The federal government appears to be making the point that someone who is pro-Palestinian should be able to head the Canadian Human Rights Commission.
However, what appears to be missing are examples of him taking positions that would actually help the Palestinian population. Are there statements criticizing Palestinian leaderships for not successfully negotiating a Palestinian state despite numerous opportunities from 1947 on? What about statements complaining that Hamas had the opportunity to create a mini-Singapore and instead used its resources for war, aggression and crimes against humanity, leading to the death and destruction that is today's Gaza? Or how about some reference to the fact that Hamas did not built bomb shelters for Palestinian civilians or allow them into the miles of underground tunnels but instead used them as human shields? Has Birju Dattani been campaigning for freedom of speech, press, association and independent courts for the Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank areas under Palestinian Authority control?
Unfortunately, his main statements concerning the Palestinians appear to have been simply anti-Israel. Perhaps not the best basis for choosing a human rights chief in Canada.
Jason Reiskind, Ottawa
'Colour is not just a pigment of our imagination'
Re: The inherent irony in adapting card and board games for colour blind people like me - Colby Cosh, July 5
I have been blind for most of my 77 years, spent a 42-year career with the Canadian National Institute For The Blind, and chaired Ontario's Accessibility Standards Advisory Council following my 2009 retirement. After reading the commentary by Colby Cosh, it occurred to me that colour blindness has not been part of the discussion of barriers facing persons with disabilities. Some of the issues involved - such as distinguishing colour-coded playing cards - may be an inconvenience. But traffic lights must be considered specific barriers that present a physical threat. Although I am no longer formally involved in disability advocacy, Mr. Cosh makes a compelling case for colour blindness to be included in such discussions and legislation.
Colour is not just a pigment of our imagination.
Jim Sanders, Guelph, Ont.
An interesting account of how Colby Cosh perceives light and colour. I have some empathy with him as my left eye sees the world with a blue tint while my right eye sees the world with a pink tint, which, my ophthalmologist tells me, is not unusual and does not qualify as colour blindness. But it does demonstrate that seeing colours is not just a standard mechanical response that is the same for everybody everywhere. An oppressive presumption under Marxism.
It is also interesting that girls and women are less likely to suffer colour blindness, and they do seem to call the shots, when it comes to appearances. Ask Jill Biden.
Gordon Watson, Rocky Mountain House, Alta.
'Politicians and police must do more than decry antisemitism'
Re: It is up to all Canadians to stand against the hate directed towards Jews - Victoria Mancinelli, June 19
Regardless of what is happening in the Middle East, Jews in Canada have a right to feel safe. My son, who lives north of the town of Orillia, Ont., in a village with less than a handful of Jews, received a written death threat targeting both him and his family. He was identified as Jewish because he had a Mezuzah on his doorpost, as do numerous Jews worldwide.
As Canada Day was approaching, I was thinking about our national anthem. It claims that Canada is "glorious and free." Jews, however, are not free to display their Jewishness for fear of attack. This is evidenced by the shootings, arsons and vandalism of synagogues and Jewish schools and businesses and assaults against Jewish people who are attacked merely because they were Jewish.
Our anthem also claims to "stand on guard for thee." It seems that guarding our nation and its people excludes Jewish Canadians.
Our politicians and police services must do more than decry antisemitism; they must show they are serious about protecting Jewish people by prosecuting those who violate hate laws and other laws in the persecution of Jewish students and the general Jewish population.
Chuck Merovitz, Ottawa

Document 141 of 500

Ghosts of LCBO's past; The liquor control board should have been privatized 30 years ago

Author: Corcoran, Terence

Publication info: Corcoran, Terence. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ghosts-lcbos-past-liquor-control-board-should/docview/3079897705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ghosts+of+LCBO%27s+past%3B+The+liquor+control+board+should+have+been+privatized+30+years+ago&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=Corcoran%2C+Terence&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost 30 years ago, in 1995, the Ontario Progressive Conservative government led by Mike Harris promised to privatize the Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO). "We will sell off some assets, such as the LCBO," said the party's famed election document, the Common Sense Revolution (CSR). The LCBO could have been a true privatization - a full-fledged divestiture of a government monopoly into a new open and competitive market, but it never happened.
The failure to privatize the LCBO, lamentable from a consumer and economic perspective, remains a significant lost opportunity to demonstrate the benefits of privatization. If Harris had successfully de-monopolized the alcohol market, the whole concept of privatization would have been given a major boost. Instead, the government backed away from privatization of the alcohol market, preferring instead to allow the corporation to substitute modern marketing and retail razzle-dazzle to give the false impression it was offering the public the best of all worlds.
The LCBO failure is also a demonstration of the degree to which the Common Sense Revolution's starting principles fell short in grasping the essential benefits of private versus public ownership and control. Neo-liberalism isn't exactly a fine science. The Wikipedia entry on "Neo-liberalism" is a 30-page effort (including 400 footnote links to hundreds of warring academic papers), reflecting an economic and ideological scramble that dates back more than a century. But when the Harris government came to power, major elements of the free-market model were often overshadowed by fiscal policy objectives. With the LCBO, the Harris government veered off the neo-liberal course in pursuit of standard political objectives.
In 1995, the LCBO was a government owned and operated province-wide corporation that controlled liquor and wine wholesale and retail markets. Another private monopoly player, the Beer Store chain, while owned and operated by the brewing industry, was also essentially a government-sanctioned beer monopoly. The CSR neo-liberal objective should have been to privatize the alcohol market by selling the LCBO, deregulating the Beer Store monopoly and allowing beer sales through supermarkets and even corner stores. More importantly, dismantling the LCBO would allow other corporations to enter the alcohol retail business and provide consumers much more choice, which has been the Alberta experience. Notably, Alberta achieved a successful and deregulated approach without sacrificing provincial revenues.
The neo-liberal objective of privatization is to benefit consumers and enhance economic productivity through competition. Instead, the Harris government fell into the fiscal policy trap that routinely captures politicians, bureaucrats and corporate insiders. Instead of aiming to benefit consumers, the objective soon became how to maximize the fiscal return to government.
Never mind the consumer and the market. The objective became preserving - and enhancing - government revenues.
Over the decades, and long after the defeat of the Harris government, any impetus towards LCBO privatization inevitably faltered with the realization that the government would lose control of a "cash cow" - a risk highlighted by a succession of LCBO executives. In 2014, for example, LCBO president Bob Peter and chairman Edward Waitzer signed off on the corporation's annual report cleverly titled "20 years of growing dividends," a trend they portrayed as a great social good. "Two decades ago, LCBO's dividend transfer to the Ontario government was $630 million. In fiscal 2013-14, LCBO transferred a new, record dividend of $1.74 billion. This latest transfer marks the 20th consecutive dividend increase to the province. Adding up each dividend transfer over this 20-year period equals a total of $22.5 billion. These revenues help pay for important public services, such as health care and education, that benefit all Ontario residents."
The LCBO's self-portrayal as a major supplier of dogood cash to government developed during the Harris years, when it was in the executive hands of former interim Ontario PC leader and cabinet minister Andy Brandt, a shrewd political and business operator who was named CEO in 1991. Through to his retirement in 2006, Brandt transformed the LCBO into a modern retail monopoly empire by playing the role of cash dispenser to the government.
The University of Winnipeg's Malcolm Bird, in his masterful 250-page thesis on the Ontario and Alberta liquor markets, captures the Brandt/LCBO modus operandi: "Brandt's team was able to influence the LCBO's fate by ensuring that the board was actively meeting the needs of the government in power. Under this team, the LCBO was not a passive institution. It significantly increased its remittances to the provincial treasury and it managed to accomplish this by changing consumer preferences with respect to alcohol. No longer a sinful vice of its prohibition era ethos, alcohol is today viewed as a part of healthy, middle-class lifestyle in which moderation and individual consumer responsibility - as opposed to externally imposed controls - are key hallmarks."
To be fair to Brandt and his successors, the LCBO has indeed successfully turned itself into a flagship retail operation that generates growing revenues, including taxes and dividends to government. The money flow is all very welcome among the unions and statists who see government monopoly and profiteering as a social benefit. At the same time, as many have observed, by transforming itself into a slick retail giant, LCBO has won the support of consumers, even though they largely have no choice. LCBO is a market success in a market without competition. How difficult can that be? But what would an Ontario beer, wine and liquor market look like today if the Harris government had found a way to open the market to private and competitive forces? Malcolm Bird sees the Harris LCBO failure to privatize as a reinforcement of standard Canadian political ideas that are generally unreceptive to neoliberal principles. "When Canadian governments attempt to embrace neoliberal-type principles, they are engaged in an attempt to fundamentally change the governing structures in this country, because neoliberalism, in its purest form, calls for the state to withdraw from involvement in all areas where its intervention is not absolutely necessary. ... This tenet runs counter to the political, historical and institutional legacies of this country."
In Bird's view, the ability of decades of LCBO executives to maintain their control and ward off privatization provides a useful lesson to leaders in other government-controlled sectors. The ability of the LCBO to influence its fate and to successfully appeal to the needs of its political masters shows how executives and managers of agencies, hospitals, universities and especially remaining Crown corporations, can maneuver to ward off privatization. The LCBO lesson for executives is to focus on the needs of political superiors and determine, in raw practical, political terms, how the institution can help meet the political objectives.
For this reason alone, the Conservative failure to tackle the LCBO privatization deserves a capital F for failure. Not only did it allow a consumer-gouging monopoly to survive as a cash machine for government, preventing the benefits of competition and market freedom. It also helped establish a state corporate control model that to this day influences policy making. One can only imagine how the continuing debate over the provision of other government services such as health care would have evolved if only the Harris "revolutionaries" had been willing to force the conversion of the LCBO into another competitor in a free-market-driven wholesale and retail sector.
National Post Excerpted from The Harris Legacy: Reflections on a Transformational Premier, Alister Campbell editor, with permission from Sutherland House. Copyright 2023 by The Harris Legacy Project.

Business indexing term: Subject: Dividends Fiscal policy Privatization Consumers

People: Harris, Mike (politician)

Identifier / keyword: years; ontario; progressive; conservative; government; harris

Title: Ghosts of LCBO's past; The liquor control board should have been privatized 30 years ago

Place of publication: Don Mills, Ont.

Terence Corcoran: The LCBO should have been privatized 30 years ago

Author: Corcoran, Terence

Publication info: Corcoran, Terence. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/terence-corcoran-lcbo-should-have-been-privatized/docview/3079897371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Terence+Corcoran%3A+The+LCBO+should+have+been+privatized+30+years+ago&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Corcoran%2C+Terence&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost 30 years ago, in 1995, the Ontario Progressive Conservative government led by Mike Harris promised to privatize the Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO). "We will sell off some assets, such as the LCBO," said the party's famed election document, the Common Sense Revolution (CSR).?The LCBO could have been a true privatization - a full-fledged divestiture of a government monopoly into a new open and competitive market, but it never happened.
The failure to privatize the LCBO, lamentable from a consumer and economic perspective, remains a significant lost opportunity to demonstrate the benefits of privatization.?If Harris had successfully de-monopolized the alcohol market, the whole concept of privatization would have been given a major boost. Instead, the government backed away from privatization of the alcohol market, preferring instead to allow the corporation to substitute modern marketing and retail razzle-dazzle to give the false impression it was offering the public the best of all worlds.
The LCBO failure is also a demonstration of the degree to which the Common Sense Revolution's starting principles fell short in grasping the essential benefits of private versus public ownership and control. Neo-liberalism isn't exactly a fine science.?The Wikipedia entry on "Neo-liberalism" is a 30-page effort (including 400 footnote links to hundreds of warring academic papers), reflecting an economic and ideological scramble that dates back more than a century.?But when the Harris government came to power, major elements of the free-market model were often overshadowed by fiscal policy objectives.?With the LCBO, the Harris government veered off the neo-liberal course in pursuit of standard political objectives.
In 1995, the LCBO was a government owned and operated province-wide corporation that controlled liquor and wine wholesale and retail markets. Another private monopoly player, the Beer Store chain, while owned and operated by the brewing industry, was also essentially a government-sanctioned beer monopoly.?The CSR neo-liberal objective should have been to privatize the alcohol market by selling the LCBO, deregulating the Beer Store monopoly and allowing beer sales through supermarkets and even corner stores. More importantly, dismantling the LCBO would allow other corporations to enter the alcohol retail business and provide consumers much more choice, which has been the Alberta experience. Notably, Alberta achieved a successful and deregulated approach without sacrificing provincial revenues.
The neo-liberal objective of privatization is to benefit consumers and enhance economic productivity through competition. Instead, the Harris government fell into the fiscal policy trap that routinely captures politicians, bureaucrats and corporate insiders. Instead of aiming to benefit consumers, the objective soon became how to maximize the fiscal return to government. Never mind the consumer and the market. The objective became preserving - and enhancing - government revenues.
Over the decades, and long after the defeat of the Harris government, any impetus towards LCBO privatization inevitably faltered with the realization that the government would lose control of a "cash cow" - a risk highlighted by a succession of LCBO executives.?In 2014, for example, LCBO president Bob Peter and chairman Edward Waitzer signed off on the corporation's annual report cleverly titled "20 years of growing dividends," a trend they portrayed as a great social good. "Two decades ago, LCBO's dividend transfer to the Ontario government was $630 million. In fiscal 2013-14, LCBO transferred a new, record dividend of $1.74 billion. This latest transfer marks the 20th consecutive dividend increase to the province. Adding up each dividend transfer over this 20-year period equals a total of $22.5 billion. These revenues help pay for important public services, such as health care and education, that benefit all Ontario residents."
The LCBO's self-portrayal as a major supplier of do-good cash to government developed during the Harris years, when it was in the executive hands of former interim Ontario PC leader and cabinet minister Andy Brandt, a shrewd political and business operator who was named CEO in 1991. Through to his retirement in 2006, Brandt transformed the LCBO into a modern retail monopoly empire by playing the role of cash dispenser to the government.
The University of Winnipeg's Malcolm Bird, in his masterful 250-page thesis on the Ontario and Alberta liquor markets, captures the Brandt/LCBO modus operandi: "Brandt's team was able to influence the LCBO's fate by ensuring that the board was actively meeting the needs of the government in power. Under this team, the LCBO was not a passive institution. It significantly increased its remittances to the provincial treasury and it managed to accomplish this by changing consumer preferences with respect to alcohol. No longer a sinful vice of its prohibition era ethos, alcohol is today viewed as a part of healthy, middle-class lifestyle in which moderation and individual consumer responsibility - as opposed to externally imposed controls - are key hallmarks."
To be fair to Brandt and his successors, the LCBO has indeed successfully turned itself into a flagship retail operation that generates growing revenues, including taxes and dividends to government.?The money flow is all very welcome among the unions and statists who see government monopoly and profiteering as a social benefit. At the same time, as many have observed, by transforming itself into a slick retail giant, LCBO has won the support of consumers, even though they largely have no choice. LCBO is a market success in a market without competition. How difficult can that be?
But what would an Ontario beer, wine and liquor market look like today if the Harris government had found a way to open the market to private and competitive forces?
Malcolm Bird sees the Harris LCBO failure to privatize as a reinforcement of standard Canadian political ideas that are generally unreceptive to neoliberal principles. "When Canadian governments attempt to embrace neoliberal-type principles, they are engaged in an attempt to fundamentally change the governing structures in this country, because neoliberalism, in its purest form, calls for the state to withdraw from involvement in all areas where its intervention is not absolutely necessary.... This tenet runs counter to the political, historical and institutional legacies of this country."
In Bird's view, the ability of decades of LCBO executives to maintain their control and ward off privatization provides a useful lesson to leaders in other government-controlled sectors. The ability of the LCBO to influence its fate and to successfully appeal to the needs of its political masters shows how executives and managers of agencies, hospitals, universities and especially remaining Crown corporations, can maneuver to ward off privatization. The LCBO lesson for executives is to focus on the needs of political superiors and determine, in raw practical, political terms, how the institution can help meet the political objectives.
For this reason alone, the Conservative failure to tackle the LCBO privatization deserves a capital F for failure. Not only did it allow a consumer-gouging monopoly to survive as a cash machine for government, preventing the benefits of competition and market freedom. It also helped establish a state corporate control model that to this day influences policy making. One can only imagine how the continuing debate over the provision of other government services such as health care would have evolved if only the Harris "revolutionaries" had been willing to force the conversion of the LCBO into another competitor in a free-market-driven wholesale and retail sector.
Excerpted from "The Harris Legacy: Reflections on a Transformational Premier," Alister Campbell editor, with permission from Sutherland House. Copyright 2023 by The Harris Legacy Project.

Document 143 of 500

Keep ad spending local

Author: Sharpe, Peter

Publication info: Sharpe, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/keep-ad-spending-local/docview/3079897201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Keep+ad+spending+local&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=Sharpe%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Please forgive this recitation of a long-held and hopefully not controversial belief: a strong, free press is a pillar of any strong democracy. That's certainly been my experience through my career in business, and I've had a front-row seat to its impact in my role on the board of Postmedia, on which I've had the privilege to serve since the company's inception.
Our team, the largest group of journalists in Canada not employed by the CBC, does the same thing every day: it puts its head down and focuses on our mission to deliver ambitious, trusted and high-quality journalism.
To say the last 15 years has been transformational in our industry is to put it lightly. The business model that sustained journalism for centuries has been dramatically disrupted. In Canada, foreign tech giants like Alphabet (Google) and Meta (Facebook) currently control around 80 per cent of the online advertising market but contribute little in the way of employment or taxes. (While, in some cases, increasing access to disinformation that can actively harm our democracy.)
Disruption can happen in any industry, and businesses are responsible for sustaining their own futures. That includes Postmedia, and that's a responsibility that we take seriously. But the core of our business is journalism - and it's a business that sustains a strong democracy.
When a pillar of democracy is at stake, it's incumbent upon everyone in a position of power to ask what they can do to help and be open to new structures and paradigms. Great leaders step up.
That's why I was delighted when Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced its plan to support journalism in the province by devoting a quarter of its advertising budget, or roughly $25 million a year, to Ontario publishers. This will not just benefit Postmedia, but all publishers that provide important coverage of local issues throughout the province.
As part of this, Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy directed several provincial Crown corporations - including Metrolinx, the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation, Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario - to have plans in place to implement this directive by the beginning of September.
This is an example of true leadership by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, and governments in other jurisdictions across the country should follow suit. But please forgive me for reciting another long-held belief: a strong country with strong institutions is also the foundation for success in business, and the growth of great corporations. That's why corporate Canada also has a crucial role to play by putting its own oar in the water to support Canadian media.
Canada's business leaders and companies, which form the nucleus of our private sector, have the ability and the financial wherewithal to help strengthen our domestic news media industry, while growing their own businesses through advertising that reaches their target audiences.
Research from News Media Canada shows that more than 80 per cent of Canadians read newspaper content each week, whether in print or online, and upwards of 60 per cent read it on a daily basis.
By diverting a greater portion of their ad budgets away from foreign companies and towards local publishers that for decades have been a critical part of Canada's diverse communities, corporate Canada has an opportunity to reach its customers while supporting a vibrant news industry and helping to ensure the longterm viability of Canada's free press.
It's a win-win for everyone involved.
Postmedia Network Peter Sharpe is chair of the board of Postmedia Network Inc.
