Document 326 of 500

Randall Denley: If LCBO workers go on strike, Ontarians can live without them

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-if-lcbo-workers-go-on-strike/docview/3070744894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+If+LCBO+workers+go+on+strike%2C+Ontarians+can+live+without+them&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thank goodness that Ontario's courageous LCBO workers are prepared to stand up and defend the province's vital public services, even if they have to go on strike to do it.
"We can't stand by while Doug Ford gives away the LCBO's revenues to big box and convenience chain CEOs. It's on Premier Ford and the LCBO to make sure that public services and good jobs don't get left behind," union leader Colleen MacLeod said Tuesday
MacLeod is chair of the liquor board employees division of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU). As such, the protection of good jobs is definitely in her zone; public services, not so much.
Unless Premier Ford's plan to expand liquor offerings to more retail stores makes Ontarians thirstier, it's not difficult to imagine that more sales elsewhere will potentially reduce the number of LCBO jobs. That doesn't create any obligation for the LCBO and its owner, the provincial government, to protect every job at the LCBO.
The union wants more permanent employees and fewer casual workers, the opposite of what the LCBO needs when it's facing a shifting and uncertain retail environment. Then there is the issue of money. The union hasn't said how big a raise it's seeking. If recently arbitrated contracts with Ontario teachers are a guideline, OPSEU could expect somewhere between 2.5 and three per cent a year.
It would be difficult to justify more. Higher wages at the LCBO will mean either fewer dollars for those precious public services, higher prices for consumers, or both.
When one works for a golden goose like the LCBO, it's natural to expect more eggs, but LCBO workers are already well-paid compared to others doing the same kind of work. The LCBO says it employs 7,200 customer service representatives, who make up 81 per cent of its unionized workforce. The average full-time clerk makes $31.46 an hour; three-quarters of them earn the top hourly wage of $32.58. The average casual employee earns $22.59 an hour. By contrast, the job site indeed.com reports that average pay for a Loblaw cashier in Ontario is $17.39 an hour.
Speaking of Loblaw, OPSEU thinks it has a winning tactic in attacking Galen Weston, the former CEO and current board chair of the grocery giant.
OPSEU president JP Hornick said this week that "Ontarians have a choice. We don't have to let Doug Ford hand the alcohol market over to big box grocers and convenience chains like Loblaws and Circle K. We've had enough price gouging from them already on food and necessities. We don't need the price of booze to go up too. We don't have to let CEOs like Galen Weston win big while the rest of us in Ontario lose. We can and we will fight for the LCBO and strong public services."
Federal NDP leader Jagmeet Singh will no doubt be thrilled to have a supporter for his one-man campaign against Weston, but when it comes to price gouging, the grocer has nothing on the LCBO. The government liquor store had revenue of $7.4 billion in 2022-23 and paid the government a dividend of nearly $2.6 billion, almost one-third of all revenue. Loblaw had a net profit of 3.4 per cent in 2023 and pays a 1.3 per cent dividend.
The union's threat to strike as early as July 5 is a strategic error that will hurt its members more than the public. Grocery stores that already sell beer and wine will be allowed to sell cases of 24 beers starting Aug. 1. Convenience stores can start selling beer, cider and wine as soon as Sept. 5. The Beer Store will operate as usual.
Ontarians will still have plenty of choices other than the LCBO, unless they need hard liquor and haven't stocked up before the strike. Even then, the LCBO says it will keep its wholesale and retail operations running with other workers.
A strike doesn't seem like a smart play. If the LCBO stays open, the workers will have limited leverage. And a long-ish strike would only drive LCBO customers to other retailers, threatening the jobs and public services OPSEU holds so dear.
The LCBO's public sector workers are up against the reality that affects most people in the private sector: Their jobs depend on the success of their employer, and that is largely up to consumers.
Although the LCBO still controls all alcohol wholesaling, its retail monopoly is being weakened. That's the first step toward real competition and a normal marketplace. LCBO workers fear competition because public sector union power depends on government monopolies. That situation can't change fast enough.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist.
randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 327 of 500

Capital gains tax hike will hit farms, panel hears; Economy; Warnings to House finance committee

Author: Jordan Gowling

Publication info: Jordan Gowling. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/capital-gains-tax-hike-will-hit-farms-panel-hears/docview/3070112411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Capital+gains+tax+hike+will+hit+farms%2C+panel+hears%3B+Economy%3B+Warnings+to+House+finance+committee&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.1&amp;au=Jordan+Gowling&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Farming organizations are joining the chorus of concern over the federal government's capital gains tax changes, with one group warning a House of Commons committee Tuesday that family-owned farms will be negatively affected.
"This policy inadvertently targets farmers who produce food to meet domestic and global demand and, as small businesses that are family-run, they do not represent the wealthiest among us," Wheat Growers Association president Günter Jochum told the House finance committee. "By making farming financially less attractive, the number of farms will continue to dwindle, leading to greater consolidation and fewer familyowned farms."
The federal government's capital gains tax changes were introduced in April's budget and increase the inclusion rate from 50 per cent to 66.7 per cent for individuals with more than $250,000 in capital gains in a given year. Corporations will face the higher rate for all capital gains.
Jochum noted that most Canadian grain farms are structured as corporations and thus will be affected by the changes.
Last week, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland tabled a separate motion to approve the changes, which passed with support from the NDP and the Bloc Québécois. The changes are set to take effect on June 25 and the government has projected they will bring in $19 billion in revenue over the next five years.
These changes have faced heavy criticism by professionals, including doctors and certain independent business owners, who frequently incorporate and use their businesses for retirement planning purposes.
The Canadian Medical Association and the Canadian Federation of Independent Businesses have repeatedly expressed concern over the effects the changes will have on their members'future financial plans.
Similarly, Grain Farmers of Ontario, which represents 28,000 farming members, said the new rules will hit farmers when they try to pass down their properties to their children.
"Family farm succession faces enough challenges without forcing new tax measures on farmers during planting season," the group said in a statement released last Friday. "It is critical that the government fully assess the impacts of these changes, consult those who are impacted, and work to mitigate the impact on Ontario farms."
Farm property owners such as Jochum can take advantage of the Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption, which allows tax-free capital gains up to a new limit of $1.25 million on the sale of qualified farming and fishing properties. But he argues it's not enough in the face of rapidly appreciating farm property values.
Canadian farmland values increased by an average of 11.5 per cent last year, according to a report published by Farm Credit Canada.
While farmers do not have to pay capital gains on the sale of their primary residences, farmland sales are taxed.
"My farm is my retirement, and a family farm transfer is a very difficult job to do to satisfy everyone's needs within the family," Jochum said. "On the advice of my accountant, I did pay a little bit into RRSPs, but he said you are better off investing in your farm."
There are other tax benefits available to farmers, including a capital-gains tax deferral. Farmers are also entitled to what's called a capital gains reserve, which allows them to average capital gains income from a farm transfer over a number of years.
Nevertheless, Jochum said he expected a tax hit.
"When I consulted my accountant, he told me I will pay 30 per cent more in taxes," he said.
A study conducted by Grain Growers of Canada backs up Jochum's claim. In partnership with farm tax accountants, the study concludes that farms bought in 1996 and sold after the changes take effect, will see an increase of 31 per cent in taxes in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario.
The study also looked at a case study in Alberta, with the practice of a farmer and spouse both being shareholders in the farm, allowing for both to use their capital gains exemptions. While these help decrease the amount of taxes owed, it still showed a substantial increase in taxes owed.
The finance committee was meeting to hear from witnesses regarding the capital gains tax and other budget measures.
Financial Post

Document 330 of 500

Ivison: 20-point comeback election wins are as rare as hen's teeth, history shows

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ivison-20-point-comeback-election-wins-are-as/docview/3070025177/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ivison%3A+20-point+comeback+election+wins+are+as+rare+as+hen%27s+teeth%2C+history+shows&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In this week's video, John Ivison is joined by Abacus Data CEO David Coletto to discuss Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's recent comments that the polls should be disregarded because Canadians are not yet in "decision mode."
Trudeau told CBC's David Cochrane that what voters tell a pollster, "if they ever manage to reach you," is very different from the choice they will make come election time.
Abacus has just released a poll that has the Conservatives 20 points ahead and the Liberals with their lowest vote share since October 2015. Coletto said Trudeau may just be trying to bolster morale among Liberal supporters by telling them things aren't as bad as pollsters are saying.
"I think it is a little bit disingenuous to tell the public that, you know, the polls can't be trusted when we know that the Liberal Party does its own internal numbers and that the prime minister, when the polls favour him or a subject he likes, is himself regularly citing them....There is some science behind polling and it did sound like he was very much questioning what it is we do, and whether we can do it right anymore," he said.
Coletto looked for examples of political parties coming back to win an election after being 20 points behind a year ahead of the vote, and found they were few and far between.
"There are a few. I mean, I asked Twitter to share some. And, you know, (Brian) Mulroney, in between the '84 and '88 election, he came back. Mid-term, he was 20 points or so, 15 points behind Ed Broadbent, came back and won a convincing victory. Christy Clark in British Columbia is always used as an example... Some people challenged whether the polls were actually accurate or not in that campaign, but even within the campaign itself, she was trailing the NDP and pulled off a surprise victory.
"In Ontario, I always look at the 2011 election. Dalton McGuinty wasn't 20 points behind, but he was behind by 10 points and came back to win a minority government. But those are the few exceptions," he said.
Yet even those cases are not perfectly analogous to today, where the government has been in power for almost a decade with the same leader, who says he's going to run again.
"In Christy Clark's example, she replaced Gordon Campbell only two years earlier. Mulroney was only in his first term. So, that level of fatigue and ability to change people's minds probably was different than it is today," Coletto said.
He said there is still a chance for Trudeau.
"Fear of the unknown is still a powerful motivator. The one thing Justin Trudeau has going for him is that people know him. Right now, that's not a positive, but it means that they don't need to become comfortable with him again or they have a good sense of what he's going to do...I still think (Pierre) Poilievre could make mistakes and could fall into some of the traps that Conservative parties often fall into.
"There's evidence in our polling that there's still not a lot of clarity on what a Conservative government would do and whether it would do the things Canadians are comfortable with. The last point I'll make is what's new about this Conservative coalition, this 42 per cent of Canadians that they've now brought together, many of them have not voted Conservative, maybe ever. And the only thing they really agree on fundamentally is they want change," he said.
"When you get into the details of what it is a Conservative government might do, that coalition starts to fray. But 20 points is a big, big margin to close. So, even if the Liberals are able to take 10 back, I still think the Conservatives win a majority government with those kinds of numbers."
Coletto pointed out that, while voters were fatigued with the Harper government in 2015 and wanted change, Harper was not as unpopular or divisive as Trudeau is today. "And so for (Trudeau) to reflect on that and say: 'everything's going to be fine, I'm going to be able to pull this out, I've been underestimated before' - I think requires an amazing level of optimism."
Coletto said that in Mulroney's case, the Canada-U.S. free trade agreement allowed him to reframe the choice for people.
"But what is that for Mr. Trudeau? They think maybe Trump winning the U.S. presidency is that. I'm not convinced that's going to be enough because I think some Canadians will actually come to the conclusion that maybe the guy that Mr. Trump hates the most in the world, shouldn't be our prime minister. A Trump election could be that moment that causes Canadians to start to rethink, but it needs to be something that big. Because right now, (voters) are laser-focused on the cost of living and Mr. Poilieve is really owning that issue."
National Post
jivison@criffel.ca( mailto:jivison@criffel.ca )

Document 331 of 500

Origin of a new unity crisis; Immigration plus bad timing equals instability

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/origin-new-unity-crisis-immigration-plus-bad/docview/3069689664/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Origin+of+a+new+unity+crisis%3B+Immigration+plus+bad+timing+equals+instability&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If times are good, immigration does not drive politics. If times are bad, immigrants fast become a lightning rod - and a political football. This predictable pattern played out in recent European Parliament elections, where the economy, migration and war were the top issues. Nationalist right-wing parties promising to crack down on immigration, including that of Marine Le Pen in France and Georgia Meloni in Italy, won the day.
In Canada, a similar drama is unfolding. In the past two years Canada admitted 900,000 permanent residents and 1.5 million non-permanent residents. Over the same period, Canadians experienced record-high interest rates, inflation, and a housing shortage. Toronto and Vancouver are "impossibly unaffordable" housing markets, with Vancouver the third least affordable in the world after Hong Kong and Sydney, Australia.
Toronto now has 7.9 per cent unemployment, due to population growth outpacing job growth.
Are Canadians connecting the dots? Apparently so. Fortyfour per cent of us believe immigration is having a mostly negative effect on Canada, up six per cent from last year, compared with 42 per cent who view it positively. Fortysix per cent want immigration to decrease. Mario Canseco, whose firm The Research Company conducted the survey, said that housing is a major factor: "(Some question) if we can't really have homes for everybody here, how are we going to deal with another influx of immigrants?" These impacts were entirely predictable and mirror trends around the world.
And they could produce another toxic consequence: a fresh national unity crisis.
Last week, Quebec Premier François Legault gave Ottawa a bill for one billion dollars for immigration-related expenses. He trotted out charts of Quebec Labour Ministry data showing the province received 80,151 requests for social assistance between March 22 and Nov. 21, 2023, compared with 72,221 for the same period in 2022, the largest increase in 25 years. Over the same period, the monthly average of asylum seekers more than doubled, going from 19,455 adults to just over 40,000. Legault also said "100 per cent of the housing crisis problem" is due to temporary immigration.
To assist, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau gave Quebec $750 million, which Legault qualified as "disappointing," despite it being the second such payment to Quebec in less than a year. In January, Quebec received $100 million when both Legault and Ontario Premier Doug Ford complained about the cost of housing asylum seekers, and Ottawa tossed the two provinces a combined $362 million.
Cue the outrage in B.C., where this week Premier David Eby complained bitterly about the latest funding to Quebec coming "at the expense" of the West. "That announcement today with Quebec, frankly, is the straw that broke this camel's back," Eby said Monday at the Western Premiers'Conference in Whitehorse. "I'll say for British Columbia how frustrated we are to see the money being showered down on Quebec and Ontario, and us scrabbling around for what's left over. It's not acceptable."
But in Quebec, the issue isn't just money, it's also powers. This week, Legault told Radio-Canada that Quebec should have a referendum on its constitution, to give it full powers over immigration.
Why? Because immigration is also driving another potential referendum, this one on Quebec separation. Parti Québécois Leader Paul St-Pierre Plamondon declared that if elected, his party would hold another referendum on Quebec separation. The reason? Immigration is fuelling population growth in the rest of Canada and thereby reducing Quebec's share, presenting the province with a dilemma.
"Either we choose to raise our immigration thresholds to between 120,000 and 150,000 per year to try to maintain Quebec's proportion (of immigration to Canada), which means the decline of the French language and an acute housing crisis ... or we choose thresholds based on our capacity to receive, which means a significant drop in Quebec's political weight in Canada, possibly below 15 per cent," Plamondon said last month.
Federalists may shrug and say yes, but the polls show he'll never win. Separatism is polling at 35 per cent, while 54 per cent of Quebecers support remaining in Canada.
To which I say, yes. For now. But no one thought French voters would endorse Le Pen's party, either.
Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Population growth; Referendums; Immigration; Separatism; Political asylum

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 336 of 500

Farmers will take hit from capital gains tax changes, Commons committee told

Author: Jordan Go wling

Publication info: Jordan Gowling. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/farmers-will-take-hit-capital-gains-tax-changes/docview/3069607362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Farmers+will+take+hit+from+capital+gains+tax+changes%2C+Commons+committee+told&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Jordan+Gowling&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Farming organizations are joining the chorus of concern over the federal government's capital gains tax changes, with one group warning a House of Commons committee Tuesday that family-owned farms will be negatively affected.
"This policy inadvertently targets farmers who produce food to meet domestic and global demand and, as small businesses that are family-run, they do not represent the wealthiest among us," Wheat Growers Association president Günter Jochum told the house finance committee. "By making farming financially less attractive, the number of farms will continue to dwindle, leading to greater consolidation and fewer family-owned farms."
The federal government's capital gains tax changes were introduced in April's budget and increase the inclusion rate from 50 per cent to 66.7 per cent for individuals with more than $250,000 in capital gains in a given year. Corporations will face the higher rate for all capital gains.
Jochum noted that most Canadian grain farms are structured as corporations and thus will be affected by the changes.
Last week, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland tabled a separate motion to approve the changes, which passed with support from the NDP and the Bloc Québécois. The changes are set to take effect on June 25 and the government has projected they will bring in $19 billion in revenue over the next five years.
These changes have faced heavy criticism by professionals, including doctors and certain independent business owners, who frequently incorporate and use their businesses for retirement planning purposes.
The Canadian Medical Association and the Canadian Federation of Independent Businesses have repeatedly expressed concern over the impact the changes will have on their members' future financial plans.
Similarly, Grain Farmers of Ontario, which represents 28,000 farming members, said the new rules will hit farmers when they try to pass down their properties to their children.
"Family farm succession faces enough challenges without forcing new tax measures on farmers during planting season," the group said in a statement released June 14. "It is critical that the government fully assess the impacts of these changes, consult those who are impacted, and work to mitigate the impact on Ontario farms."
Farm property owners such as Jochum can take advantage of the Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption (LCGE), which allows tax-free capital gains up to a new limit of $1.25 million on the sale of qualified farming and fishing properties. But he argues it's not enough in the face of rapidly appreciating farm property values.
Canadian farmland values increased by an average of 11.5 per cent last year, according to a report published by Farm Credit Canada.
While farmers do not have to pay capital gains on the sale of their primary residences, farmland sales are taxed.
"My farm is my retirement, and a family farm transfer is a very difficult job to do to satisfy everyone's needs within the family," Jochum said. "On the advice of my accountant, I did pay a little bit into RRSPs, but he said you are better off investing in your farm."
There are other tax benefits that farmers can take advantage of, including a capital gains tax deferral. Farmers are also entitled to what's called a capital gains reserve, which allows them to average capital gains income from a farm transfer over a number of years.
Nevertheless, Jochum said he expected a tax hit.
"When I consulted my accountant, he told me I will pay 30 per cent more in taxes," he said.
A study conducted by Grain Growers of Canada backs up Jochum's claim. In partnership with farm tax accountants, the study concludes that farms bought in 1996 and sold after the changes take effect, will see an increase of 31 per cent in taxes in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario.
The study also looked at a case study in Alberta, with the practice of a farmer and spouse both being shareholders in the farm, allowing for both to use their capital gains exemptions. While these help decrease the amount of taxes owed, it still showed a substantial increase in taxes owed.
It's about time for a complete taxation review
Time to stop complaining about the capital gains hike
The finance committee was meet to hear from witnesses regarding the capital gains tax and other budget measures.
Bookmark our website and support our journalism: Don't miss the business news you need to know - add financialpost.com to your bookmarks and sign up for our newsletters here
. Email: jgowling@postmedia.com

Document 337 of 500

Ontario schools to remain open amid heat warning prompting safety concerns

Author: Hanick, Matw

Publication info: Hanick, Matw. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-schools-remain-open-amid-heat-warning/docview/3069589796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario+schools+to+remain+open+amid+heat+warning+prompting+safety+concerns&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hanick%2C+Matw&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Advocates have expressed concerns for students and educators in Ontario classrooms as the province enters a major heat wave this week.
Environment Canada said Monday a heat wave descending on parts of Ontario this week is expected to bring dangerously high temperatures, some as hot as 45 C with the humidity.
Despite the soaring temperatures, students can still expect to attend their regular classes.
Earlier today, the Toronto District School Board (TDSB) announced that schools will remain open during a heat warning despite "uncomfortable conditions" in some schools without air conditioning.
Advocates and parent groups have raised safety concerns on behalf of students and education workers attending school during the heat wave.
A dangerous heat wave is headed to Ontario. What is the plan for schools without A/C? How are students and education workers expected to endure such unsafe learning and working conditions? Please let us know what is happening at your family's school/s. #onted @ToddSmithPC
- Ontario School Safety (@ONSchoolSafety) June 16, 2024
Mary Jo Nabuurs, director of media relations and outreach at Ontario School Safety, a volunteer-led organization fighting for safe, in-person education, says that if the ministry truly prioritizes students and education workers being present at school, then having air conditioning during times like this is essential.
"Addressing air issues in schools should be paramount for the Ministry of Education," she told National Post "It's just we've learned long enough now it's time to do something"
"We have the tools and the infrastructure. We just need the will to fund it - because they fund it in their own offices all the time."
However, Nabuurs did acknowledge that there's "a big infrastructure backlog."
A spokesperson for the Ontario Minister of Education told CTV News Toronto that the government has made significant investments in the school boards, but it is up to them to allocate funding.
Toronto District School Board spokesperson Shari Schwartz-Maltz told CTV News Toronto on Monday that the board has 177 new schools with air conditioning, but it's not financially feasible to air condition all of its schools.
"I know someone in our group whose daughter has been diagnosed with asthma now, [which] is a challenge when it's extremely hot - and this child had to leave school early because of it," Nabuurs said.
Nabuurs added that academic performance and mental health can improve when air quality is improved and that poor air quality makes students groggier and causes them to fall behind.
Ontario Green Party Deputy Leader Aislinn Clancy issued a news release on Monday saying lack of access to cool classrooms affects students' and education workers' well-being and creates extreme risks for those with pre-existing health conditions.
Nabuurs says that until the government prioritizes schools the same way it prioritizes other institutions, children and education workers will continue to suffer.
"I think what we have to do is ask them, Why? Because, again, they know better, and they're doing it for themselves."
"It isn't the kids who are sick," Nabuurs said. "It's the structures they're learning in."
Record-breaking US heat wave scorches the Midwest as New York activates the National Guard
Heat wave toasts much of Ontario and Quebec, parts of Atlantic Canada hot, humid

Document 338 of 500

Tasha Kheiriddin: High immigration could see Quebec emigrate from Canada

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tasha-kheiriddin-high-immigration-could-see/docview/3069559674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Tasha+Kheiriddin%3A+High+immigration+could+see+Quebec+emigrate+from+Canada&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Forty-six per cent want immigration to decrease. It's not acceptable."

Why? Because immigration is also driving another potential referendum, this one on Quebec separation. Parti Québécois Leader Paul St Pierre Plamondon declared that if elected, his party would hold another referendum on Quebec separation The reason? Immigration is fuelling population growth in the rest of Canada and thereby reducing Quebec's share, presenting the province with a dilemma.
"Either we choose to raise our immigration thresholds to between 120,000 and 150,000 per year to try to maintain Quebec's proportion (of immigration to Canada), which means the decline of the French language and an acute housing crisis, ... or we choose thresholds based on our capacity to receive, which means a significant drop in Quebec's political weight in Canada, possibly below 15 per cent," Plamondon said last month.
Federalists may shrug and say yes, but the polls show he'll never win. Separatism is polling at 35 per cent, while 54 per cent of Quebecers support remaining in Canada.
To which I say, yes. For now. But no one thought French voters would endorse Le Pen's party, either.
Postmedia Network
Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.
Geoff Russ: Europe's nationalist right smashes the failing centre left consensus
Tasha Kheiriddin: Brace for a possible tsunami of illegal migrants if Trump is re-elected

Document 339 of 500

FIRST READING: New record-low poll shows Liberals are continuing to hemorrhage supporters

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-new-record-low-poll-shows-liberals/docview/3069504453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+New+record-low+poll+shows+Liberals+are+continuing+to+hemorrhage+supporters&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign up here
TOP STORY
Even with the Trudeau government already poised for a crushing defeat at the next federal election, each passing day is continuing to yield thousands of Canadians abandoning their plans to vote Liberal.
At least, that's the takeaway from a new Abacus Data poll showing the Liberals with their lowest-ever share of the popular vote since forming government in 2015.
According to the new survey, if an election were held tomorrow the Liberals would capture a mere 22 per cent of ballots cast.
When these kinds of numbers are projected across the 343 ridings that will be contested in the next election, the result is a House of Commons in which the Liberal caucus could very well plummet to fourth place.
A widely circulated projection of the Abacus poll by the election modeller Raymoud Liu shows the Liberals capturing just 37 seats. It would be a defeat on par with the 34 seats that Liberal Leader Michael Ignatieff captured in the 2011 election; the all-time worst showing for the federal Liberals in Canadian showing.
Latest Abacus poll modelled out
CON: 223 seats (+104)
BQ: 43 seats (+11)
NDP: 38 seats (+13)
LIB: 37 seats (-123)
GRN: 2 seats (-)
Conservative Majority Government
Feel free to ask for any ridings
- Charestiste???? (@RealAlbanianPat) June 16, 2024
In the modelled scenario, the Conservatives would capture a supermajority of 223 seats, the Bloc Québécois would form the official opposition with 43 seats and the NDP would constitute the third party at 38 seats. Trudeau, however, would be spared Ignatieff's fate of losing his own seat: Papineau, like much of Montreal, would probably continue to vote Liberal.
It's nothing new that the Trudeau government is running out of political capital; polls have been predicting a Liberal defeat for more than a year.
But the new Abacus poll shows that the Liberals are yet to hit rock bottom, and that an exodus of Liberal support is continuing to the tune of several thousand voters each day.
The last Abacus poll, published May 21, had the Liberals charting at 25 per cent. It's only three per cent higher than the most recent poll, but a drop of that size is equivalent to hundreds of thousands of lost voters.
If the next federal election has the same turnout as 2021 (17,209,811), each percentage point share of the popular vote is equivalent to about 172,000 ballots.
If the Liberals have indeed dropped three points in just three weeks, that represents about 516,000 lost voters as per the 2021 turnout - or, an average of about 25,000 lost voters per day.
Abacus polls work to a margin of error of 2.53 per cent (19 times out of 20), and would never purport to be accurate enough to chart the daily total of voters abandoning the Liberal Party.
But a daily exodus of several thousand Liberal voters is in line with what most pollsters are finding in recent months.
Leger has consistently tracked a 10-point reduction in Liberal support over the last calendar year; from 33 per cent in May, 2023 to 23 per cent last month.
If those figures hold in a federal election with a turnout of at least 17 million, that's the equivalent of the Liberals having alienated 4,600 votes per day for the last 365 days.
At a projected 223 seats, the Conservative share of the next House of Commons wouldn't be the largest parliamentary majority in Canadian federal history, but it wouldn't be far off.
A caucus of that size would represent 65 per cent of all seats in the House of Commons. Simply fitting them all into the House of Commons chamber would mean that many Conservative MPs would face opposition benches filled with more Conservatives.
Still, it's not quite the record. That was set in 1958, when the Progressive Conservatives under John Diefenbaker took 78.5 per cent of the House of Commons.
Trudeau says foreign interference affects all parties, contradicting NDP leader Singh
These two Canadian cities are deemed 'impossibly unaffordable' in new report
IN OTHER NEWS
Just after Russia sailed a pair of warships off the coast of Florida as an apparent show-of-force aimed at the U.S., news emerged that a Canadian warship was seen in Havana harbour tied up alongside one of the Russian ships. HMCS Margaret Brooke, one of Canada's new Arctic offshore patrol ships, spent two nights alongside Kazan, a Russian nuclear-powered attack submarine. Canada has only sent two warships to Cuba since the country's communist revolution, and all of them have occurred under the premiership of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. This is part of why Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre accused Trudeau of spending scarce military resources to send "a Canadian naval ship to Cuba alongside the Russian navy to honour Cuba's brutal communist government." But on Monday, Defence Minister Bill Blair said the ship was only there as part of a continental show of force. Or as Blair put it, to maintain "credible military presence in the sea and in the air around our continent."
Canada has better relations with Cuba than the U.S. does, so if the Americans wanted to send a warship into Havana harbour as a subtle signal to Moscow, it does make sense that they would ask the Royal Canadian Navy to do it. The Canadian Armed Forces, for its part, said the visit was in "recognition of the long-standing bilateral relationship between Canada and Cuba." So there's two going theories as to what happened:
Canada just happened to schedule a goodwill visit to a communist dictatorship at the exact moment that an international pariah country was doing the same - and an embarrassed minister of defence explained it away as a "credible military presence" operation.
Canada played wingman on a U.S. Navy-led exercise to shadow the Russian fleet, and shrewdly capitalized on its friendship with the Cubans in order to get within spitting distance of a Russian nuclear sub.
McGill University continues to be the home of a pro-Intifada tent encampment that has repeatedly endorsed the October 7 attacks against Israel and bars entry to anyone who doesn't publicly support the Jewish state's destruction. And now, according to the above literature, they're hosting a youth summer camp. Against images of armed Palestinian terrorists, the camp promises athletic drills, Arabic language instruction and "cultural crafts."

Document 340 of 500

Another poll shows Liberals yet to hit rock bottom; Party bleeding thousands of voters daily

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/another-poll-shows-liberals-yet-hit-rock-bottom/docview/3069344496/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Another+poll+shows+Liberals+yet+to+hit+rock+bottom%3B+Party+bleeding+thousands+of+voters+daily&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 341 of 500

The cost of our worrying failure of expertise; Competence crisis predates COVID

Author: Sarkonak, Jamie

Publication info: Sarkonak, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cost-our-worrying-failure-expertise-competence/docview/3069344487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=The+cost+of+our+worrying+failure+of+expertise%3B+Competence+crisis+predates+COVID&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Sarkonak%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some might say North America is in a competence crisis. Today's students can't read as well as their predecessors; workers are increasingly hired on non-meritocratic bases; medical errors and aviation "safety issues" are on the rise.
Meanwhile, decision-makers are often so risk-averse they struggle to decide anything. At small scales, everything still works. But at large scales, the effects can be disastrous.
It's a problem whose symptoms can be observed all over, but the key case study is probably COVID-19. The reasons for this are condensed into 118 minutes in the new, Canadian-produced documentary Covid Collateral.
It's a film about the suppression of scientists and the devastating effects of policy choices that only appeared to have universal support.
The documentary's focus is the public health system - the domain of scientists and bureaucrats - and the number of ways in which it failed.
It blames the suffocation of policy criticism and the memetic adoption of poorly supported pandemic interventions.
It hears primarily from those who saw holes in COVID policy, from the early extreme interventions in China, to the uncritical adoption of said extreme interventions in the West, to massively overestimated virus fatality rates, to dubious lockdowns, to the censorship of those who defied the "consensus" for these measures. In that way, it's one-sided, but then again, the viewer might not need the "pro-lockdown" side: anyone who wasn't living under a rock during the COVID years should be familiar with it.
What we didn't hear much of during the COVID years were dissenting voices. In Canada, there were a good number of skeptical healthcare workers who lost their jobs for publicly questioning restrictions; in Covid Collateral, they get to talk about it. Online, expert critics such as Stanford University's Jay Bhattacharya, a professor of medicine and economics, were victims of pre-Musk Twitter censorship. Now unmuzzled, Bhattacharya tells his side in the documentary.
Also interviewed is former White House Coronavirus Task Force member Scott Atlas, a familiar face for anyone who watched Trump-era COVID news conferences, who offers a view into the American executive office. A number of others chime in on policy and law, and even Roman Baber, former Ontario MPP ousted from the Progressive Conservative caucus, makes an appearance.
While politicians are mentioned here and there, Covid Collateral does its best to remain politically palatable to the average viewer by refraining from pinning blame on left or right. Ontario Premier Doug Ford, former New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern and former president Donald Trump appear in some frames, but they avoid being painted as the villains of the story. Nor does it get into the touchiest aspects of COVID science: vaccines are mentioned, but they aren't promoted, nor are they falsely decried as being part of some conspiracy; ivermectin gets a few minutes in, demonstrating the rigid refusal of authorities to entertain options (it did show promise in the early pandemic days).
The real culprit the documentary points to is the failure of expertise, which is a problem that predated and outlasted the pandemic. This big-picture problem is perhaps articulated best in a feature last year by Palladium Magazine, which argued that "Complex Systems Won't Survive the Competence Crisis." It wasn't about the COVID pandemic, but rather, the waning meritocratic nature of the bureaucracies holding up 21st century life in the West due to "systematic promotion of the unqualified and sidelining of the competent."
"The resulting norms have steadily eroded institutional competency, causing America's complex systems to fail with increasing regularity. In the language of a systems theorist, by decreasing the competency of the actors within the system, formerly stable systems have begun to experience normal accidents at a rate that is faster than the system can adapt."
In a way, that's what COVID felt like. The system received flawed inputs, and, rather than correct for them once these inputs were discovered to be flawed, the actors within couldn't, or wouldn't, deregulate. Instead, they sought more flawed inputs to justify more flawed measures, which harmed the general population in a number of ways. Covid Collateral tries to pinpoint those inputs, pondering why alternatives weren't taken.
For a final demonstration of her thesis, the documentary's producer, Vanessa Dylyn, is running into the same problems her subjects faced when it comes to getting her work out there.
"I was met with a wall of polite silence across Canada," she told journalist Paul Thacker earlier this spring, who was also interviewed in the documentary.
"But what was worse, I was met with hostility from my own colleagues. Outside of the key creatives, it was very hard to get production accountants, or business affairs people to work on the film. Even a closed-captioning technician turned it down. A publicist in Canada won't touch this."
Even after COVID-era health measures have been folded and put away, the stigma that comes with questioning them lives on. The crisis of competence will last a while longer yet.
National Post

Subject: Coronaviruses; Meritocracy; Pandemics; Censorship; COVID-19; Documentary films; Crisis intervention; Actors

Document 342 of 500

Geoff Russ: Europe's nationalist right smashes the failing centre left consensus

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/geoff-russ-europes-nationalist-right-smashes/docview/3069192668/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Geoff+Russ%3A+Europe%27s+nationalist+right+smashes+the+failing+centre+left+consensus&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: FRANKFURT - The European nationalist right is here to stay.
Despite the best efforts by the established mainstream parties to stave them off, nationalists now either lead governments or support them in Italy, the Netherlands, Sweden, and Finland.
From June 6 to 9, European parliament elections were held, and the results were a huge success for nationalists, which critics dismiss as far right regardless of the actual policies of each party, across the EU. Once isolated on the political fringes, the nationalist right of France, Italy, Austria and Poland won the most seats allotted to their respective countries, and in Germany the populist right AfD finished ahead of the ruling social democrats.
The business-first conservatives and social democrats who led the EU in the post-Cold War era have only themselves to blame for voters abandoning them in droves. This duopoly presided over two major economic downturns, first during the 2008 financial crisis, then after the COVID-19 pandemic.
Mass immigration is a major problem in Europe, with 5.1 million newcomers arriving in 2022 alone. Surveys show Europeans are more than uncomfortable with those numbers and the radical social and economic change that comes with it, but the political duopoly have been reluctant to take it seriously.
Still, European voters might have been prepared to look it over if their lives were getting better. Instead, they are getting worse and worse, and like the migration issue, the duopoly has had few or no answers.
Even prior to the pandemic in 2019, there were questions about whether Europe would ever recover, from the Great Recession a decade earlier, which drove up youth unemployment to levels currently above 20 per cent in France. Wages were made stagnant, and inflation in the aftermath of the pandemic is squeezing Europe's youth even tighter.
Both the business conservatives and social democrats have marketed themselves as EU's well-dressed technocrats since the end of the Cold War. That reputation deservedly lies in tatters.
In France, the old Gaullist conservatives have been gutted on both sides by the hard-right, populist National Rally headed by Marine Le Pen, and centrist President Emmanual Macron's Renaissance party. Now even Macron is suffering from the same reputational damage as the rest of the EU's technocrats, with France's fiscal situation deteriorating considerably in the past few years.
The irony of Macron's unpopularity is that he came to power in 2017 with a promise to bridge the divide between right and left in an effort to fix France's calcified economy, which grew less than one per cent in 2023. Having largely failed in that task, the nationalists are an appealing option for disaffected voters, especially the youth, during France's upcoming domestic parliamentary elections.
This was also true of last year's Dutch elections, where the right-wing populist PVV led by Geert Wilders won a strong plurality of seats. The PVV formed a government with the rest of the Dutch centre-right parties earlier this month and now dictates much of its legislative agenda, which includes cracking down on migration and gutting the Netherlands' tight green regulations.
Greece is an outlier, where the established centre-right New Democracy was rewarded with a second conservative majority last year because it governed with competence and has tangibly improved the state of the country. Granted, there is far more room to improve Greece compared to the rest of the EU, but it doesn't change the fact that voters reward good governments and punish poor ones.
Much like mass immigration, voters also do not appreciate their governments appearing to be more energized by idealistic, EU-wide campaigns to ban combustion engines than the cost of living. While the establishment conservative faction of the European parliament remains the largest, their leader has pledged to cut that ban in response to the nationalist gains in the election.
Europe may be excellent when it comes to letting cafes and bars operate with ease, but has a distinct fetish for regulating anything more advanced than pouring wine.
Last year, the European parliament passed a sweeping AI regulation bill that shackled the new and growing industry, with various MEPs who supported it were caught on camera clapping like seals and grinning like idiots.
Predictably, the European Court of Auditor released a report in May stating that the EU was falling far behind its peers when it came to building an AI ecosystem and drawing investment towards that sector.
If the EU wants to be anything greater than a glorified tourist destination populated by pensioners because the young people have fled to America for jobs, it requires a serious re-think on how it grows its economies. AI is going to be part of the modern economy, and failing to integrate it properly will be crippling.
It is not just the professional classes who are seeing their opportunities slashed due to the establishment's policies.
Young people and the working-classes typically compete for lower-paid, unskilled jobs, and adding millions of migrants to the mix has not been appreciated by Europe's youth and blue-collar communities. Both these demographics rejected Macron's pro-business centrists and voted in large numbers for France's National Rally in the European parliament election, especially outside Paris.
Adam Pankratz: The French rebuked Europe. Emmanuel Macron's humiliation is next
These two demographics would usually be expected to vote for the centre-left, but that half of the European establishment's duopoly went all-in on white-collar professionals and paid the price. The vote swing towards Europe's nationalist parties has largely been at the expense of the left.
As far back as 2016, the Financial Times reported that 45 per cent of France's blue collar workers and 38 per cent of the unemployed would vote for National Rally.
Furthermore, the political left in Europe has almost wholly rejected the ideals of patriotism or nationalism that the new right stands for. The centre-left's rhetoric sounds like canned metropolitan social liberalism, written by the human resources consultants who vote for them.
One of the few European centre-left leaders with any measurable popularity is Denmark's Mette Frederiksen, who stated in a biography that, "For me, it is becoming increasingly clear that the price of unregulated globalization, mass immigration, and the free movement of labour is paid for by the lower classes," and has governed accordingly.
With the nationalists poised to win more elections in the coming years, there will be less attention paid to how they win elections, and more towards how they actually govern. Thus far, Italy's right-wing Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni has presided over a reasonably competent government and earned the respect of her more moderate peers.
Not only a leading European nationalist, the recently-single Meloni has become one of Europe's most prominent leaders, and was greeted at last week's G7 meeting with two kisses on the cheek by the freshly-single Justin Trudeau.
Ten years ago, it seemed incomprehensible that a stalwart progressive like Trudeau would ever smooch a hard-right European nationalist. Now, Trudeau and his successors may just have to get used to it, unless it turns out the nationalists have no answers to Europe's woes either.
National Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Duopoly

Subject: Conservatism; Cold War; Pandemics; Duopoly; Voters; Parliaments; Populism; Immigration; Elections; Technocracy

Location: Italy; Greece; Netherlands; France; Europe

Identifier / keyword: frankfurt; european; nationalist; right; despite; efforts

Title: Geoff Russ: Europe's nationalist right smashes the failing centre left consensus

Publication title: National Post (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 17, 2024

Section: NP Comment

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 343 of 500

Jamie Sarkonak: 'Covid Collateral' puts spotlight on pandemic-era incompetence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jamie-sarkonak-covid-collateral-puts-spotlight-on/docview/3069040209/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Jamie+Sarkonak%3A+%27Covid+Collateral%27+puts+spotlight+on+pandemic-era+incompetence&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some might say North America is in a competence crisis. Today's students can't read as well as their predecessors; workers are increasingly hired on non-meritocratic bases; medical errors and aviation "safety issues" are on the rise. Meanwhile, decision-makers are often so risk-averse they struggle to decide anything. At small scales, everything still works. But at large scales, the effects can be disastrous.
It's a problem whose symptoms can be observed all over, but the key case study is probably COVID-19. The reasons for this are condensed into 118 minutes in the new, Canadian-produced documentary Covid Collateral
It's a film about the suppression of scientists and the devastating effects of policy choices that only appeared to have universal support.
The documentary's focus is the public health system - the domain of scientists and bureaucrats - and the number of ways in which it failed. It blames the suffocation of policy criticism and the memetic adoption of poorly supported pandemic interventions.
It hears primarily from those who saw holes in COVID policy, from the early extreme interventions in China, to the uncritical adoption of said extreme interventions in the West, to massively overestimated virus fatality rates, to dubious lockdowns, to the censorship of those who defied the "consensus" for these measures. In that way, it's one-sided, but then again, the viewer might not need the "pro-lockdown" side: anyone who wasn't living under a rock during the COVID years should be familiar with it.
What we didn't didn't hear much of during the COVID years were dissenting voices. In Canada, there were a good number of skeptical health-care workers who lost their jobs for publicly questioning restrictions; in Covid Collateral, they get to talk about it. Online, expert critics like Stanford University's Jay Bhattacharya, a professor of medicine and economics, were victims of pre-Musk Twitter censorship. Now unmuzzled, Bhattacharya tells his side in the documentary.
Also interviewed is former White House Coronavirus Task Force member Scott Atlas, a familiar face for anyone who watched Trump-era COVID news conferences, who offers a view into the American executive office. A number of others chime in on policy and law, and even Roman Baber, former Ontario MPP ousted from the Progressive Conservative caucus, makes an appearance.
While politicians are mentioned here and there, Covid Collateral does its best to remain politically palatable to the average viewer by refraining from pinning blame on left or right. Premier Doug Ford, former New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern and former president Donald Trump appear in some frames, but they avoid being painted as the villains of the story. Nor does it get into the touchiest aspects of COVID science: vaccines are mentioned, but they aren't promoted, nor are they falsely decried as being part of some conspiracy; ivermectin gets a few minutes in, demonstrating the rigid refusal of authorities to entertain options (it did show promise in the early pandemic days).
The real culprit the documentary points to is the failure of expertise, which is a problem that predated and outlasted the pandemic. This big-picture problem is perhaps articulated best in a feature last year by Palladium Magazine, which argued that "Complex Systems Won't Survive the Competence Crisis" It wasn't about the COVID pandemic, but rather, the waning meritocratic nature of the bureaucracies holding up 21st century life in the West due to "systematic promotion of the unqualified and sidelining of the competent."
"The resulting norms have steadily eroded institutional competency, causing America's complex systems to fail with increasing regularity. In the language of a systems theorist, by decreasing the competency of the actors within the system, formerly stable systems have begun to experience normal accidents at a rate that is faster than the system can adapt."
In a way, that's what COVID felt like. The system received flawed inputs, and, rather than correct for them once these inputs were discovered to be flawed, the actors within couldn't, or wouldn't, de-regulate. Instead, they sought more flawed inputs to justify more flawed measures, which harmed the general population in a number of ways. Covid Collateral tries to pinpoint those inputs, pondering why alternatives weren't taken.
For a final demonstration of her thesis, the documentary's producer, Vanessa Dylyn, is running into the same problems her subjects faced when it comes to getting her work out there.
"I was met with a wall of polite silence across Canada," she told journalist Paul Thacker earlier this spring, who was also interviewed in the documentary.
"But what was worse, I was met with hostility from my own colleagues. Outside of the key creatives, it was very hard to get production accountants, or business affairs people to work on the film. Even a closed-captioning technician turned it down. A publicist in Canada won't touch this."
Even after COVID-era health measures have been folded and put away, the stigma that comes with questioning them lives on. The crisis of competence will last a while longer yet.
National Post
Chris Selley: Dr. Fauci isn't a villain, but he has plenty to answer for
Colby Cosh: The bipartisan consensus against risky coronavirus research in China

Document 344 of 500

'Very narrow': Here's why 'hate crimes' are rarely charged and almost never prosecuted in Canada

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/very-narrow-heres-why-hate-crimes-are-rarely/docview/3069040185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27Very+narrow%27%3A+Here%27s+why+%27hate+crimes%27+are+rarely+charged+and+almost+never+prosecuted+in+Canada&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, June 10, Toronto police announced 32-year-old Lou-i Bou-Chahine with public incitement of hatred, under Section 319(1) of the Criminal Code. He allegedly stomped on an Israeli flag at Sunday's reportedly 50,000-strong Walk for Israel march.
It should be an interesting case to watch. With respect to alleged expressions of hate, Canadian politics has developed a rich lexicon of wishful thinking masquerading as fact since the October 7 terrorist attacks against Israel, and the ensuing and often antisemitic anti-Israel protests.
Nearly every prominent municipal, provincial and federal politician has by now said something like, "There is no place for antisemitism in Canada/British Columbia/Toronto" But clearly there is.
"Canadians live in a country where laws are supposed to be followed and police are supposed to enforce the law," Liberal MP Anthony Housefather complained in December when protesters loudly (and at times menacingly) occupied Toronto's Eaton Centre mall
"Police must begin consistently enforcing the law and arresting those shouting hate speech at ... rallies," Toronto lawyer Howard Levitt wrote in the Financial Post in February.
This is another common refrain. But police and prosecutors have made it very clear they'll enforce and not enforce whatever they deem appropriate in the circumstances.
To your average citizen, it's quite inscrutable and understandably confusing. As far as the law is concerned, "hate crimes" are a couple of separate things: standalone criminal offences for inciting hatred, like Bou-Chahine now faces; or an aggravating factor at sentencing for any other crime, should there be evidence of "hate" contributing to the motive. In both cases, the bar for conviction is very high.
But there could soon be a third, less rigorous category for prosecution, if the Liberals get their way and give the Canadian Human Rights Tribunal back its former power to penalize people on charges of non-criminal hate speech.
If Canadians are confused about the scope and nature of hate crime, you can hardly blame them. Activists and politicians are constantly calling for legal crackdowns that rarely materialize.
As a result, we're in danger of becoming a country where everyone yells "hate speech" at each other, growing ever more frustrated that nothing's being done about it - or worse, convinced that police are favouring "the other side."
Inconsistencies seem to be everywhere.
On June 6, Toronto police cleared out after just two days, while the encampment at the University of Toronto is now well into its second month. It's precisely the inverse of what you would expect, York traditionally being the natural home of untethered radicalism in the anglophone Canadian academy and U of T being something like the opposite. (The police at York used a trespassing law to enforce their eviction, not hate crime laws.)
Montreal Police didn't lay any charges in November last year when infamous extremist imam Adil Charkaoui hoisted a megaphone and implored Allah to "take care of these Zionist aggressors."
"Take care of the enemies of the people of Gaza," he beseeched, in what remains one of the ugliest, most transparently antisemitic diatribes heard publicly on Canadian soil since October 7. "Allah, identify them all, then exterminate them. And don't spare any of them."
The pro-Israel side was largely pleased in November when a Calgary man was charged for causing a disturbance with a "hate motivation attached" - to wit, by leading a chant of "from the river to the sea" at an anti-Israel demonstration. The "hate motivation" refers to Section 718.2(a)(i) of the Criminal Code, which says evidence of hateful motives can be considered an aggravating factor on sentencing for any criminal offence.
"This arrest will hopefully set a precedent not only in Calgary but in other cities," the Calgary Jewish Federation said in a statement
It did not. Or, at least it wasn't the precedent the federation seemed to hope for. The charge was stayed just a few days later, with the Crown declaring there was no possibility of a conviction.
Here in Toronto, meanwhile, it took six months for the Crown to drop "hate-motivated" vandalism charges against four (of 11) people charged with defacing a Toronto Indigo location with red paint over founder and CEO Heather Reisman's support for Israel.
In January, Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw boasted of charging a 41-year-old gentleman with "wilful promotion of hatred" against an identifiable group. The unnamed accused had allegedly publicly waved the flag of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine, a listed terrorist group since 2003 that is perhaps best known for organizing the slaughter of 28 people at the Tel Aviv airport in 1972
The wilful promotion of hatred charge falls under 319(2) of the Criminal Code, which is more serious than the aggravated-sentencing provisions, carrying with it its own potential two-year prison sentence. Among the few convicted under that section in Canadian history include James Keegstra, an Alberta high-school teacher who preached Holocaust denial to his students, and Don Andrews, leader of the proudly white-supremacist and Holocaust-denying Nationalist Party of Canada.
"We're not putting up with this kind of hateful conduct," Demkiw thundered. "This type of allegation points to an extremist, hateful perspective that we do not welcome in the city."
In April, the Crown dropped the charges.
I asked Sunil Gurmukh, a law professor at Western University who studies hate crimes, how he would summarize the rules to someone heading to a protest who might be worried about "crossing the line" into some manner of hate speech.
"It's a very narrow category," he said - that is, speech that "vilifies or dehumanizes people because of their membership in an identity group."
Gurmukh pointed to the so-called "hallmarks of hate" established by the Canadian Human Rights Commission and later adopted by the Supreme Court, as potentially helpful guidelines. Those "hallmarks" include speech that compares a group of people to "vermin," or other "dehumanizing" comparisons, or that "trivializes or celebrates past tragedy."
Not so easy to do if you're otherwise a normal, tolerant human being, in other words. You should have to work at it.
But Gurmukh said there has always been confusion about where exactly the line is. Quite apart from anything else, he noted, there is considerable discrepancy between the way Canadian police departments publicly define (and therefore presumably how they police) hate crimes - and they don't always seem to be on the same page as their local prosecutors, never mind with each other or with activists, who very often see obvious hate crimes where police and the judiciary do not.
"I think there's always going to be a tension about what conduct does or doesn't cross the line," Boris Bytensky, president of the Ontario Criminal Lawyers' Association, opined on a recent episode of University of Ottawa law professor Michael Geist's Law Bytes podcast.
Canadians might be especially confused about the aggravated-sentencing provisions.
This is what Section 718.2(a)(i), the aggravated-sentencing provision in the Criminal Code, says: "A court that imposes a sentence shall also take into consideration the following principles: evidence that the offence was motivated by bias, prejudice or hate based on race, national or ethnic origin, language, colour, religion, sex, age, mental or physical disability, sexual orientation, or gender identity or expression, or on any other similar factor."
To wade through the jurisprudence on Section 718.2(a)(i) in search of consistency and clarity is an exercise in confusion and frustration, certainly for the layperson.
Cases in which hate was considered but then rejected as an aggravating factor at sentencing include: a young offender who stabbed a fellow high-school student, in a dispute over a mutual acquaintance's stolen winter gloves, while calling the victim a "dirty ni-er"; an attack on a young bisexual man by an assailant yelling "fagg-t"; a drug dealer who killed a rival and called him "just another Paki"; and an Indigenous woman who walked out of a bar, declared "I hate white people," and ]any other similar factor[<ITAL]" (my italics).
In the trial of Richard Henry Bain, who failed in his goal to assassinate incoming Quebec premier Pauline Marois at her 2012 election-night party in Montreal, but did manage to murder stage technician Denis Blanchette, the judge decided "political thought, belief or opinion" ought to be considered one of those "similar factors." (Bain was fanatically hateful of Quebec separatists.)
In recent years, at least one judge has found hatred for homeless people a suitably "similar factor"; another concluded the same for hatred of police.
Reasonable people can disagree on those determinations. But the explanations for these judges' decisions were too cursory to be very useful as precedent for the layperson to follow. Having no clear or understandable line in the sand is troubling considering we're discussing limiting speech - arguably the most fundamental freedom in a democratic society.
This is not a healthy social dynamic, says University of Windsor law professor Richard Moon, especially if police are routinely making arrests that won't ever come to trial. The process is more than punishment enough, and the potential chilling effect on legal speech is severe.
This lack of clarity is why groups including the Alberta Hate Crimes Committee, the Canadian Race Relations Foundation and the Canadian Association of Chiefs of Police support the Liberal government's proposal to establish a new "standalone" hate-crime provision in the Criminal Code to replace Section 718.2(a)(i), the aggravated sentencing provision.
That standalone provision is being proposed in Bill C-63, the Liberal government's "online harms" legislation, despite it having no specific relation to the online world
Whereas today you might be charged with committing, say, vandalism in a hateful manner, and your hateful motivation might be considered at sentencing, under Bill C-63 you would be charged with vandalism and with a separate offence of having committed vandalism (or any other crime) with a hateful motivation.
(The existing, more serious standalone hate-speech offences - advocating genocide, public incitement of hatred, etc. - would be unchanged.)
Unlike most existing standalone hate-crime charges, prosecution under this new provision would not require sign-off from the provincial attorney general. The new law would provide for a life sentence in the most serious cases.
The history of Section 13, the controversial hate speech law the Liberals just revived
I chaired the Human Rights Tribunal. It has no business policing 'hate speech'
Supporters argue this would increase transparency, and it might well. As it stands judges don't generally quantify in their rulings how much more severe they're making a sentence because of the hateful motivations of the convicted. They just mull that over, along with the other aggravating and mitigating factors, then cite the closest sentencing precedent they can find, and come up with some numbers.
In a confounding case from 2019, the Ontario Court of Appeal found a trial judge had erred in reading gang membership into Section 718.2(a)(i) as a "similar factor," and applying the aggravated sentence to the accused, though it didn't convincingly explain why. (Are political parties and street gangs so conceptually different, really?) And then the appeal-court judges found the judge's error had somehow not affected the length or severity of the sentence, which it upheld.
So, was the hate motivation worth anything at sentencing, or wasn't it?
Supporters also argue having a standalone hate-crime provision instead of Section 718.2(a)(i) would aid in keeping hate-crime statistics, as convictions (rather than aggravated sentencing rulings) would automatically be recorded under existing record-keeping systems. Even finding cases where 718(a)(i) has been considered is quite a laborious, esoteric task. Thankfully the Department of Justice took it up in a 2020 study of such cases since 2007, as did Michelle Lawrence, now a law professor at the University of Victoria, in her 2009 master's thesis in criminology at Simon Fraser University
One could compellingly argue this lack of data is a dire shortcoming of Canada's court systems, however, rather than a good reason to alter the Criminal Code.
And the standalone provision has at least as many skeptics as supporters. Probably more.
"It is subject to be used and abused," Bytensky warned on Geist's podcast, noting the lack of need for an attorney general's sign-off. "It is a matter that a prosecutor could choose to hold over (the accused) in a case that may have very little to do with hate."
Geist, one of the "online harms" bill's most dedicated critics, said he worries that provision amounts to little more than "virtue signalling" by the government.
Moon, one of the country's foremost experts on hate crimes, agrees. "They're trying to say to various communities, 'look, we take this seriously'," he suggested.
As part of their online harms legislation, the Liberals are proposing to resuscitate Section 13 of the Human Rights Act This would bring back the one way to prosecute supposed hate speech through a quasi-judicial human right tribunal, without the palaver of having to prove criminal charges.
That section most famously embarrassed itself in the prosecution of Maclean's magazine for publishing a book excerpt.
In the wake of that debacle, the Canadian Human Rights Commission tagged Moon to weigh in on Section 13's probity. He determined, and it was.
"The use of censorship by the government should be confined to a narrow category of extreme expression - that which threatens, advocates or justifies violence against the members of an identifiable group," Moon wrote in his report.
"Less extreme forms of discriminatory expression, although harmful, cannot simply be censored out of public discourse. Any attempt to exclude from public discourse speech that stereotypes or defames the members of an identifiable group would require extraordinary intervention by the state and would dramatically compromise the public commitment to freedom of expression."
Moon is by no means some far-right lunatic, or else the Canadian Human Rights Commission wouldn't have asked him for his report. Inviting complainants to use the revitalized section 13 is a clear invitation to that law being weaponized by motivated partisans.
The whole idea behind the Online Harms Act, Moon compellingly argues, is that "hate," however you want to define it, is out of control in the digital world. So how on earth does a milquetoast piece of legislation propose to solve that, online or off?
Even if such prosecutions were properly the purview of a human rights body, Moon suggested to me, a tribunal's limited resources and limited case load would very likely lead to allegations of selective prosecutions. And if more resources were made available, they might quickly be consumed by opposing groups firing serial complaints at each other.
It's a solid general principle of democratic societies that citizens should know what's illegal and what isn't, and why. When it comes to Canada and hate speech, it seems that mission has barely even begun.
National Post
cselley@postmedia.com
