Document 365 of 500

Why Canadians are fleeing to Florida; Unhappy with cost of living, taxes or politics, more millennials are pulling up stakes to head south

Author: Kubes, Danielle

Publication info: Kubes, Danielle. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-canadians-are-fleeing-florida-unhappy-with/docview/3068274750/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Why+Canadians+are+fleeing+to+Florida%3B+Unhappy+with+cost+of+living%2C+taxes+or+politics%2C+more+millennials+are+pulling+up+stakes+to+head+south&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kubes%2C+Danielle&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The third time it happened, I started getting curious. I'd be in the grocery store or on a walk in Vaughan, my suburb just north of Toronto, and bump into an acquaintance. "Hey Danielle, we're moving to Florida next year. My husband just can't handle this country anymore." One by one, millennial families with young kids seemed to be emptying out of my neighbourhood.
And it doesn't seem to be a phenomenon confined to just my own circles. Daniel Mandelbaum, a Toronto-based immigration lawyer, has noticed it, too.
"There are more Canadians interested in moving to the United States in the last 12 months or so," he told me last week over the phone. "I'm seeing more inquiries saying, 'We are fed up with the cost of living, taxes, Canadian politics'and that they are moving to Florida or Texas.... Something is going on that is setting people off."
Mandelbaum says it makes sense that it's mostly millennial families that are moving because most Canadians who are able to go to the U.S. either move for a job opportunity or because they married an American. People in their late 20s and 30s fit that demographic.
The data also reflects my observations. According to a recent report by the CBC, Canadians are moving to the U.S. at levels not seen since the braindrain era in the early aughts. The report cited the American Community Survey, which found that 126,340 Canadians moved to the U.S. in 2022 - a 70 per cent increase from 2012. Of that number, 53,311 were born in Canada, about 50 per cent higher than the average number that moved to the U.S. before the COVID pandemic.
And it's likely a large portion of them are migrating to Florida, which is now the second-fastest growing state in the U.S.
Of my friends who have moved to the U.S., all have cited dissatisfaction with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's and Ontario Premier Doug Ford's COVID-era policies and the subsequent plunge in living standards.
Ben Feferman, a gaming consultant who managed to get an O-1 "extraordinary ability" visa and relocate his wife and four kids to the Sunshine State, told me over the phone in May that, "Our move was heavily motivated by COVID politics and policies. We felt there was no quality of life with endless lockdowns, restrictions and faux scientific public health policy. Especially with school closures."
Last week, I spoke with Rachel Azagury, CEO of marketing firm The Concept Agency, who is packing up her husband and two kids this summer to head south on an L-1 intracompany visa - and knows at least five other families doing the same. She supported Canada's lockdown measures at first, she says, and thought Florida's laissez-faire attitude toward social distancing was "nuts." But after a few months, she thought "there has got to be a different way."
"I realized we can't have an outdoor lifestyle here. We couldn't even go to the park," she says. For a time in 2020, Toronto cordoned off playgrounds and fined users. The pandemic "showed all of Canada's cracks."
The pact that citizens have always had with this country has delivered us a safe, tolerant, well-governed place; in return, we put up with -20 C winters, high taxes, limited job opportunities, bad shopping and expensive liquor. We looked down on the U.S. for gun crime, high health-care costs and fractured politics.
But post-COVID, that pact feels broken: good governance has dissolved, from health-care wait times, to passport lines, to infant Tylenol shortages. Crime is skyrocketing - police have even encouraged residents to leave their car fobs near the front door to avoid violent interactions during auto thefts.
Millennials, now all grown up, are making a new calculation: if we cannot ensure a solid economic future for our children, if we send well over half our paycheques (after income and consumption taxes) to the government but can't even secure a pediatrician in exchange, if our cars are stolen not once but twice in the same year from our driveways, then why are we still here? Instead, we figure, Florida has no state income taxes. Sales tax is only six per cent. Gas is less expensive. Under the state's school choice program, parents get school vouchers of around $8,000, which can be put toward private school or home school. All those savings, plus the likelihood of a higher salary, means that we could afford health care, a home in a gated community and private school tuition. And we wouldn't have to struggle to put our wiggy toddlers in a snowsuit every time we want to go outside.
In other words, middle-class millennials can simply enjoy a higher quality of life in the U.S.
Mandelbaum says that similar factors drove a Canadian exodus to the U.S. some two decades ago.
"That's a big problem," he says.
"I get the sense when I speak to clients that it's not a cycle, that there is an underlying problem, a fundamental problem with Canada's economy that people are expressing frustration (over), and are actually moving."
The biggest frustration is, of course, housing prices.
Home prices are astronomical, with property listings now averaging $1.25 million even in Vaughan. And remember, this is not in downtown Toronto - this is where people go when they want cheaper houses. In comparison, the average property price in Boca Raton, Fla. in April 2024, a prime destination for Canadian families, is roughly US$580,000 ($800,000) - about 35 per cent cheaper.
The Feferman family were longterm renters in Canada, but moving to Florida allowed them to become homebuyers for the first time. They paid just $750,000 for their house in a desirable neighbourhood close to their kid's school, and say a comparable one would cost about $1.5 million in Canada.
Another major source of frustration with Canada is navigating the health-care system. It was never great, but post-COVID, Ontario health care feels apocalyptic. My own family doctor reduced the days she works per week to open a Botox clinic because she says the government doesn't pay enough - and I don't blame her one bit.
Azagury says that during COVID, she had to wait nine months just to see a dermatologist.
"Is (American health care) going to be more expensive?" she says. "Maybe, but it's definitely better and faster."
Plus, it's possible that Canadians have been victims of fearmongering about the American health-care system. In truth, if you are employed in the U.S., you get health insurance with your job, and if you are a low-or middle-income earner, you can access government-run health coverage or a plan under Obamacare.
"When I actually looked into it and applied, it's an unbelievable system for the middle class," Feferman says.
"You get very generous tax credits that really subsidize health care, and in many ways, my feeling is that private health care delivers a better quality of care. There is also a lot more they do for preventative care."
The superiority complex that Canadian millennials had about the U.S. when we were growing up is officially over. Things are looking bleak here, and bright over there. Unless something changes, expect more and more of us to flee south.
National Post

Document 366 of 500

Chrystia freeland talks nonsense

Author: Mcparland, Kelly

Publication info: Mcparland, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chrystia-freeland-talks-nonsense/docview/3068273786/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chrystia+freeland+talks+nonsense&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Mcparland%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario had a cabinet shuffle last week during which an intriguing but little-remarked change took place.
Someone named Stan Cho will replace someone named Neil Lumsden for seating rights at Premier Doug Ford's cabinet table. The intriguing bit is that while Neil was minister of tourism, culture and sport, Stan will be minister of tourism, culture and gaming.
Gambling has replaced sport because it's all about money, and money is more important than sports. Sports knows this, which is why sporting broadcasts are now just pretexts to ceaselessly batter viewers with gambling ads. Wayne Gretzky, formerly a hockey hero, is now best known as a shill for BetMGM.
Canada's politics, like sports, has become all about money, thanks to governments increasingly inept at anything beyond writing cheques. They're desperate for it, open to any means of squeezing cash that can be used to fuel more promises. And no administration needs it more than the federal Liberals, who spend money with the desperate helplessness of alcoholics reaching for a bottle. After eight years of professing an intense dedication to responsible budget management, the best they could do for 2023 was a deficit of $50.9 billion.
Fifty billion. That's more than a billion dollars a week in borrowing just to keep the country running. Not coincidentally, the annual bill for interest payments has reached $46.5 billion. In effect, almost the entire deficit is going to pay charges for past deficits, contributing to more deficits in future. We're maxing our Visa card to pay interest to MasterCard, without ever reducing the balance on either.
Since coming to power in 2015 the Liberals have so willingly poured out gushers of money they'd succeeded in conditioning Canadians to treat the bounty as normal.
Everyone turns to Ottawa when they feel the pinch. The chief executive of WestJet Airlines professed recently he sees no reason a large airline like his should have to pay rent to use the country's airports. Ottawa, he says, should quit collecting rents and freeze other fees in the bargain. Because apparently it's the taxpayers'job to prop up big airlines.
Canada's media, meanwhile, has set up a "federally incorporated non-profit" conglomeration comprised of "12 independent media outlets" to divvy up the $100 million the feds have squeezed from Google on the basis that Canada's media also needs a government support system.
Three giant global auto manufacturers, Volkswagen, Stellantis and Honda, will collect somewhere between $23 billion and $28 billion in public funding to locate new EV plants in Ontario. The estimates vary and financing details are limited, but while those plants say they'll "create" about 6,500 jobs, Loblaw, Canada's biggest grocer, expects 7,500 new hires for 40 new stores it plans to build at a cost of $2 billion.
That $270,000 per job for Loblaw, $3.5-$4.3 million per job for Ottawa. When Ottawa says "invest" it means handing over your money and mine; Loblaw will actually risk its own. Yet while politicos trampled all over one another to pose for photo ops with the happy foreign recipients of federal subsidies, Liberals have repeatedly summoned Loblaw boss Galen Weston Jr. to Ottawa to be labelled a greedy capitalist and dressed down for building one of the country's few internationally successful firms, one that actually makes money and pays dividends.
Neither the prime minister nor his finance minister, Chrystia Freeland, is known to have ever run so much as a hotdog stand at their own risk. The prime minister gave speeches and was a teacher, briefly, before entering politics. His finance minister was a journalist, editor and wrote a book. Canada's newsrooms are filled with reporters and editors who have written books, but I wouldn't trust them with the country's finances.
The result is in the numbers: economic growth is an anemic one per cent, the lowest since Justin Trudeau took office and expected to stay that way for years. The OECD predicts Canada will be the worst-performing of 40 "advanced" economies until at least 2030. Unemployment is 6.2 per cent and rising as part-time jobs increase while full-time ones decrease. People with money just don't want to invest here; they'll invest in the U.S. no matter which of the deeply unappealing candidates wins the presidency in November, but not in Canada (at least not without government subsidies). The dollar is weak and expected to grow weaker. The main U.S. stock index, once virtually tied with Canada's, is now almost twice as high. For all America's weirdness, U.S. business is seen as dynamic and creative. Canada depends on how much people will pay us for natural resources, which we're embarrassed about producing.
Oops, now they're not low and the vigorish on her bets has hit $47.5 billion. Up $12 billion in a single year. Closing in on a billion a week, in interest payments alone.
Precisely how much has your life improved as a result of all that borrowing? There were lots of warnings that rates were bound to go up, but the finance minister preferred her own advice, and Freeland works for a prime minister who got all sniffy when questioned about inflation. "You'll forgive me if I don't think about monetary policy," he retorted. Bank of Canada? Like, yuck.
Think it's going to get better? The Trudeau government faces an election next year, and who thinks the biggest-spending government in Canada's history will do anything but open the floodgates even wider with its own survival on the line? Freeland's latest budget promised a tax raid on cottagers and doctors via a targeted hike in capital gains taxes. She has formalized the plan with specific details this week, and she's already smearing anyone who objects as a gluttonous plutocrat.
"Do you want to live in a country where those at the very top live lives of luxury, but must do so in gated communities behind ever higher fences, using private health care and airplanes because the public sphere is so degraded and the wrath of the vast majority of their less privileged compatriots burns so hot?" she said on the weekend. What nonsense. We're talking cottage owners, doctors, small businesses. Does your doctor live in a gated community and fly a private plane? Truth is, Ottawa needs the extra cash to pay for the promises Freeland made in the budget, which raised spending further even as she repeated her absolute determination to keep outlays under control. She made the promises against all common sense because the Liberals need NDP support to stay in office and care more about that than any extra damage they might do to the economy.
Incumbent governments are getting hammered in elections across the globe. France, Germany, Britain, India, the EU. Trudeau's troops must be terrified. Frightened governments do crazy things. This time next year we could be looking back on 2024 as the good old days, when borrowing was a mere pittance at 50 billion big ones.
National Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Borrowing Federal budget Government spending Government subsidies Budgets Subsidies

Subject: Borrowing; Politics; Prime ministers; Federal budget; Federal elections-Canada; Government spending; Government subsidies; Budgets; Tourism; Subsidies; Elections

Document 367 of 500

Ontario schools are finally doing their jobs; It's all thanks to Lecce's efforts as minister

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-schools-are-finally-doing-their-jobs-all/docview/3068273785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario+schools+are+finally+doing+their+jobs%3B+It%27s+all+thanks+to+Lecce%27s+efforts+as+minister&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stephen Lecce, Ontario's just-replaced education minister, is one of those relatively rare politicians who wants to accomplish something in the job and has the skills to do it.
In his five years as minister, Lecce changed the balance of power in education and the content of what's taught in schools. Both were long overdue and both were big achievements in a sector that offers an education minister plenty of antagonists and few friends.
For years, Ontario public education has lacked rigour, accountability and a clear sense of what was to be achieved in the 14 years children spend in government schools. The primary and secondary education system was something students just had to endure so they could go on to enduring the post-secondary system.
Under Lecce, Ontario's schools have become much more connected to real life and the world of work. Math, science and literacy are the foundational skills for success in life. They have been given primacy in an overhaul which has changed 80 per cent of the elementary curriculum.
Phonics, a tried and true method of teaching language, has been brought back. Ontario has also introduced Canada's first reading-screening program for all children from senior kindergarten to Grade 2. The goal is to identify children with reading problems at the earliest possible stage. The new math curriculum stresses financial literacy and coding.
In secondary schools, technological education is now a mandatory course requirement and the province has introduced a new pre-apprenticeship program that lets students learn skills in co-op placements as early as Grade 11. Lecce recently made a financial literacy test a graduation requirement. He also announced the return of home economics, although it might better be termed life skills. The idea is that students should leave school with at least some practical, real-world knowledge.
In the past, the provincial government, school boards and teaching unions spent a lot of time wrestling for power. Under Lecce, the province is finally firmly in control. Last year, the provincial government passed Bill 98, legislation that lets it mandate binding academic achievement standards for school boards, and unions have forgone strikes and work to rule, at least for now.
In a speech to the Empire Club late last year, Lecce said, "As education minister, I had little power to drive systemic change, and that ended with Bill 98." Lecce said the bill was "about repatriating power back to parents." Really, the power is in the hands of the province, not the parents, but that's an improvement over letting school boards run wild. Too many of them are left-wing talking shops more interested in the pursuit of social justice than they are in the pursuit of education.
Lecce has controlled the unions to the extent possible. Management of unions is a challenge for any education minister from any party, since Ontario's education unions have attack as their default mode. Even when Lecce left the portfolio to become energy minister, union leaders couldn't find a good thing to say about him.
The Elementary Teachers'Federation of Ontario said it was looking forward to a new minister, "after six years of being misled, dismissed, and undervalued by Minister Lecce," Ontario Secondary School Teachers'Federation president Karen Littlewood said Lecce was is "a great talker, but I don't know that we've actually had the action that we need in schools."
Lecce deserves significant credit for getting new contracts with all the education unions without labour disruptions. The minister was clear that the priority was keeping schools open.
Pay was ultimately settled through arbitration. That was a big improvement over the provincial government's attempt to use the notwithstanding clause to keep schools open in 2022. That blew up into a big labour protest and the government backtracked.
Not everything has been rosy under Lecce's leadership. During the pandemic, Ontario closed schools for 135 days longer than any other province, favouring perceived safety over learning. Students are still trying to overcome that learning deficit. That's not all on Lecce, of course: The Doug Ford administration overreacted to the pandemic on every front.
Ontario is also facing a shortage of teachers, a problem that was both foreseeable and solvable.
There are a lot of people to please in the education sector. Previous governments have focused too much on school boards and unions, not enough on parents. The strength of Lecce's approach has been the priority he has given to parents and students, delivering a refreshed curriculum and setting standards for results.
Lecce is an ambitious politician who wasn't afraid to shake up a staid sector and make overdue change. Ontario could use more like him.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist. randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 371 of 500

Could an iPhone save your life?

Author: Taveroff, Leigh

Publication info: Taveroff, Leigh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/could-iphone-save-your-life/docview/3068161027/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Could+an+iPhone+save+your+life%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Taveroff%2C+Leigh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As someone living in a small B.C. mountain town known for adventure sports and outdoor activities, I hear about search and rescue calls on a weekly basis. Some of them revolve around highly skilled athletes who have ventured into dangerous avalanche terrain or elite climbers who got stuck on a ledge trying to outdo an extreme personal goal. Most of the calls are for everyday people who have unintentionally gone off grid while exploring this beautiful patch of Earth, and have simply gotten in over their heads.
I've long been an Apple user - I have the latest iPhone 15 Pro Max and an ancient MacBook Air circa 2014 that just won't die (unsurprisingly, the model is no longer available but here's the new one) The thought of switching to Android/PC terrifies me (I've done so, in the name of research, but always come back to my Apple products), yet I have never tried the Apple Watch When I heard about the SOS features on the newer iPhones and Apple watches, I was determined to test them firsthand. Could they really help to save my life in an emergency situation?
Equipped with the latest in Apple technology, I set out into the wilderness to understand how the iPhone 15 Pro Max and Apple Watch Series 9 work in tandem to help keep nature lovers of all ability levels safe in the backcountry.
Set up your Medical ID through the Health App while connected to WiFi or cellular service.
First things first, set up your Medical ID
Before going on a hike, camping trip or any location where cell service may drop, set up your Medical ID in the iPhone Health app. This includes pertinent information such as medical conditions, allergies, blood type and your emergency contact. It's available on your lock screen so first responders have all of the information they need upon arrival.
Before venturing out on any solo (or group) adventure, big or small, ensure someone knows where you're going and how long you plan to be gone. Overpack essentials - protein bars and lightweight, moisture-wicking layers like merino wool don't take up much space in a backpack and will keep you comfortable should you get lost.
Understanding iPhone and Apple Watch safety features
There are a ton of features that make Apple's new technology worth the investment (the 15 Pro Max starts at $1,750 and the Apple Watch Series 9 starts at $550). The most impressive, in my opinion, are explained below.
Emergency SOS via Satellite - using iPhone 14 or above, connect with emergency services via satellite if you find yourself without WiFi or cell service and need assistance. Users are able to send encrypted texts via satellite which are decrypted by Apple and passed along to an emergency services dispatcher, or Apple's emergency relay center. Your location is shared with first responders and your emergency contacts are notified.
The best way to test this is to use the demo feature - in Settings, scroll down and find Emergency SOS. Click Emergency SOS via satellite and Try Demo.
With less than 30 per cent of Canada's landmass covered by cellular reception - this feature could be the difference between life and death in a life-threatening situation.
Once connected via satellite (pretty quick for me, but don't give up if it's taking a few minutes) emergency responders will talk you through next steps via text and let you know your contacts have been notified and help is on the way.
Once connected to emergency services via satellite, you'll be prompted for basic information about your situation, which will be sent to your emergency contact along with your coordinates.
Fall Detection - Recent iterations of the Apple Watch and Apple Watch Ultra can detect hard falls and connect you to emergency services, even if you're completely immobile. It's another life-saving feature that will alert your emergency contacts and connect you with help. For users with iPhone 14 or later, your Apple Watch can work with your nearby phone to contact help outside of cellular-connected areas via satellite.
I tried testing this feature but wasn't able to set off fall detection as I clearly wasn't falling hard enough. As a mountain biker, this assured me that the feature wouldn't activate every time I fell off my bike, but only when I truly needed it. I considered throwing my watch against a wall but decided I'd rather keep my watch intact and trust other people's experiences, like this injured hiker in Victoria whose watch prompted a rescue.
Fall Detection feature on Apple Watch Series 9.
Crash Detection - Similarly, crash detection is available on iPhone 14 or later and will detect a severe car crash, automatically alerting emergency services after 20 seconds unless you cancel.
If unresponsive, your iPhone will play an audio message for emergency services, informing them of the situation, providing your latitudinal and longitudinal coordinates plus an approximate search radius - a feature that reportedly saved the lives of two people in Nanoose Bay last January.
Compass app - More than just a standard compass (which is a fabulous tool for any explorer), the updated compass app gives Apple Watch users the ability to view and add waypoints - important to mark your last cellular connectivity point for users without the latest satellite capabilities. This allows you to add digital breadcrumbs of your journey, in case you need to backtrack.
For users with Apple Watch SE or Series 6 and later, there is a new Backtrack feature that makes it even easier to retrace your steps. Apple Watch Ultra users can take advantage of the Siren feature, which emits a distinct, high-pitch noise heard from up to 600 feet away.
Checking my waypoints and using the Backtrack feature on my Apple Watch.
Who should use the iPhone and Apple Watch safety features
Everyone. Setting up your Medical ID and ensuring the features are switched on will take less than five minutes and provide peace of mind for a lifetime (or as long as you have your watch/phone.)
These safety features are not just built for outdoor enthusiasts; fall detection could be crucial for our elderly population - it's automatically enabled for those 55 plus.
What: iPhone 15 Pro Max
Price: $1750+
Where to buy: Apple Best Buy Walmart
What: Apple Watch Series 9
Price: $550+
Where to buy: Apple Best Buy Amazon

instagram.com/leightav

Document 372 of 500

Randall Denley: Ontario schools are finally doing their jobs, thanks to one determined minister

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-ontario-schools-are-finally-doing/docview/3068127086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+Ontario+schools+are+finally+doing+their+jobs%2C+thanks+to+one+determined+minister&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stephen Lecce, Ontario's just-replaced education minister, is one of those relatively rare politicians who wants to accomplish something in the job and has the skills to do it.
In his five years as minister, Lecce changed the balance of power in education and the content of what's taught in schools. Both were long overdue and both were big achievements in a sector that offers an education minister plenty of antagonists and few friends.
For years, Ontario public education has lacked rigour, accountability and a clear sense of what was to be achieved in the 14 years children spend in government schools. The primary and secondary education system was something students just had to endure so they could go on and endure the post-secondary system.
Under Lecce, Ontario's schools have become much more connected to real life and the world of work. Math, science and literacy are the foundational skills for success in life. They have been given primacy in an overhaul which has changed 80 per cent of the elementary curriculum.
Phonics, a tried and true method of teaching language, has been brought back. Ontario has also introduced Canada's first reading-screening program for all children from senior kindergarten to Grade 2. The goal is to identify children with reading problems at the earliest possible stage. The new math curriculum stresses financial literacy and coding.
In secondary schools, technological education is now a mandatory course requirement and the province has introduced a new pre-apprenticeship program that lets students learn skills in co-op placements as early as Grade 11. Lecce recently made a financial literacy test a graduation requirement. He also announced the return of home economics, although it might better be termed life skills. The idea is that students should leave school with at least some practical, real-world knowledge.
If teachers can't handle cellphone ban, maybe they shouldn't be teachers
Board's cull of older books 'offensive,' minister says
In the past, the provincial government, school boards and teaching unions spent a lot of time wrestling for power. Under Lecce, the province is finally firmly in control. Last year, the provincial government passed Bill 98, legislation that lets it mandate binding academic achievement standards for school boards, and unions have forgone strikes and work to rule, at least for now.
In a speech to the Empire Club late last year, Lecce said, "As education minister, I had little power to drive systemic change, and that ended with Bill 98." Lecce said the bill was "about repatriating power back to parents." Really, the power is in the hands of the province, not the parents, but that's an improvement over letting school boards run wild. Too many of them are left-wing talking shops more interested in the pursuit of social justice than they are in the pursuit of education.
Lecce has controlled the unions to the extent possible. Management of unions is a challenge for any education minister from any party, since Ontario's education unions have attack as their default mode. Even when Lecce left the portfolio to become energy minister, union leaders couldn't find a good thing to say about him.
The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario said it was looking forward to a new minister, "after six years of being misled, dismissed, and undervalued by Minister Lecce," Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation president Karen Littlewood said Lecce is "a great talker, but I don't know that we've actually had the action that we need in schools."
Lecce deserves significant credit for getting new contracts with all the education unions without labour disruptions. The minister was clear that the priority was keeping schools open. Pay was ultimately settled through arbitration. That was a big improvement over the provincial government's attempt to use the notwithstanding clause to keep schools open in 2022. That blew up into a big labour protest and the government backtracked.
Not everything has been rosy under Lecce's leadership. During the pandemic, Ontario closed schools for 135 days longer than any other province, favouring perceived safety over learning. Students are still trying to overcome that learning deficit. That's not all on Lecce, of course: The Doug Ford administration over-reacted to the pandemic on every front.
Ontario is also facing a shortage of teachers, a problem that was both foreseeable and solvable. There's no solution to it yet, although expanding teachers' college places would seem the obvious fix.
There are a lot of people to please in the education sector. Previous governments have focused too much on school boards and unions, not enough on parents. The strength of Lecce's approach has been the priority he has given to parents and students, delivering a refreshed curriculum and setting standards for results.
Lecce is an ambitious politician who wasn't afraid to shake up a staid sector and make overdue change. Ontario could use more like him.
National Post
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist.
randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 373 of 500

Emma Teitel: Jewish kids are being victimized in Canada and nobody cares

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/emma-teitel-jewish-kids-are-being-victimized/docview/3067812624/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Emma+Teitel%3A+Jewish+kids+are+being+victimized+in+Canada+and+nobody+cares&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We are pleased to welcome Emma Teitel to National Post with her debut as a contributing columnist.
I?used to say I wouldn't send my kids to Jewish day school because it was too expensive. Now I'd rather not send them because there is an emerging trend in my country that involves antisemites shooting guns at Jewish schools under the cover of night. It's a trend that more casual Jew haters shrug their shoulders at. After all, kids aren't even at school after dark. And besides, have you seen Gaza?
I can't unsee Gaza. But forgive me if I'm also preoccupied with the once rising, now risen "tide" of antisemitism in Canada.? Forgive me as well if I am capable of simultaneously lamenting the killing of Gazan children and the grotesque fact that Jewish kids in Canada are being held responsible for their deaths.
Two Jewish schools shot at in the span of a week. A synagogue firebombed with congregants inside. A rock thrown through the window of another.? And from political leaders of every stripe: tweets.
It is not an exaggeration to suggest that among Canada's progressive left, there was exponentially more outrage at Ontario Premier Doug Ford's recent offensive comments in response to the shooting of a Jewish school in Toronto than there was outrage at the shooting itself.
Words are violence, and what not.
Speaking of words, this past winter, while war raged in Gaza, Anna Osver's 11-year-old daughter, who is Jewish, came home from her publicly funded Ottawa Catholic school in tears.
"All Jews have to die," a boy had told her. He then suggested she kill herself.
Osver immigrated to Canada from Israel in 2007. Before Israel, she lived in Ukraine where she grew up. "It was not easy for Jews in the USSR," she told me. "Now I am feeling what I felt there."
Go back to Europe,?they say.
At some Ottawa public schools, Jewish children are "being held collectively responsible for the war." This is according to Tamara Taub, the co-founder of Jewish Parents of Ottawa Students Association, a group formed specifically to deal with antisemitic bullying in the aftermath of October 7.
Taub recently conducted a survey of 66 Jewish kids from grades 6-12 in the Ottawa area, most of them students in the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board.
Seventy per cent said they have felt unsafe at school because they're Jewish.
Students report being labeled "murderers" and "baby killers" by their peers, having non-Jewish students scream "free Palestine" in their faces, chant Heil Hitler at them, demand they "pick a side" in the conflict, and even engage in physical violence.
Rabbi Andrew Rosenblatt: In Vancouver, we saw firsthand how hateful words turn into violence
Kenneth Green: I tried to enter the U of T encampment to speak with protesters, but I was blocked and cursed at
Sometimes the vitriol comes in an all-inclusive package.? In one incident, according to Taub, a Jewish student in grade 7 was asked by her peers if she supported Israel or Palestine. She declined to answer. When they pressed her, she conceded: "Israel." As she walked away from the group, someone threw a rock at her head.
Adults might be capable of admonishing the Israeli government without scapegoating Jews. Children are not so sophisticated. Indeed, if Canada's media class can't seem to denounce Benjamin Netanyahu without evoking the Third Reich, maybe it's foolish to expect gentile elementary-school children not to paint their Jewish peers with the same poisonous brush.
The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board says it is "taking a?number of measures to build a culture of belonging and?address issues of hate." According to its spokesperson, these include but are not limited to, an "anti-hate toolkit" as well as the expansion of a "safe schools reporting tool" that enables students to report bullying incidents anonymously.
Many Jewish parents, however, feel the board is not doing enough. Their frustration was palpable at a school board meeting late last month, during which school trustees sat mostly silent in the face of parent deputations about antisemitic bullying. When a Jewish high school student spoke about being branded a "baby killer" at school in the aftermath of October 7, the only school trustee to ask her a follow up question, and to offer a kind word, was herself Jewish. The others said nothing.
"We feel there is this concern that where we fall in the diversity and inclusion world seems to complicate things," said Taub. "We're not looked at as a minority."
In fact, as far as minorities go, Jews are minor. Muslims comprise roughly five per cent of the national population, Hindus 2.3 per cent and Sikhs 2.1. Jews make up 0.9 per cent of people in Canada.
None of whom, as far as I know, serve in Israel's war cabinet. Certainly, the children among them are not calling the shots in Rafah.
It is a perverse fact that Jewish kids in Canada are being scapegoated during the day by their peers and at night by gunmen. It is even more perverse that this is old news.
National Post
Emma Teitel is a national magazine award winning columnist and a Senior Consultant at Navigator Limited. She was previously a member of the Toronto Star's editorial board and a Star opinion columnist with a focus on city issues. Her work has also appeared in Maclean's Magazine and the Boston Globe. She lives in Toronto with her family.

Business indexing term: Industry: 61111 :‎ Elementary and Secondary Schools

Subject: Jewish schools; Students; Journalists; School boards; Antisemitism; Violence; Jewish people; Shootings; Bullying

Location: Canada; Israel; Gaza Strip

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110

Title: Emma Teitel: Jewish kids are being victimized in Canada and nobody cares

Publication title: National Post (Online); Toronto

Document 374 of 500

Danielle Kubes: It's no wonder that young Canadian families are fleeing to Florida

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/danielle-kubes-no-wonder-that-young-canadian/docview/3067812501/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Danielle+Kubes%3A+It%27s+no+wonder+that+young+Canadian+families+are+fleeing+to+Florida&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The third time it happened, I started getting curious. I'd be in the grocery store or on a walk in Vaughan, my suburb just north of Toronto, and bump into an acquaintance. "Hey Danielle, we're moving to Florida next year. My husband just can't handle this country anymore." One by one, millennial families with young kids seemed to be emptying out of my neighbourhood.
And it doesn't seem to be a phenomenon confined to just my own circles. Daniel Mandelbaum, a Toronto-based immigration lawyer, has noticed it too.
"There are more Canadians interested in moving to the United States in the last 12 months or so," he told me last week over the phone. "I'm seeing more inquiries saying, 'We are fed up with the cost of living, taxes, Canadian politics' and that they are moving to Florida or Texas.... Something is going on that is setting people off."
Mandelbaum says it makes sense that it's mostly millennial families that are moving because most Canadians who are able to go to the U.S. either move for a job opportunity or because they married an American. People in their late 20s and 30s fit that demographic.
The data also reflects my observations. According to a recent report by the CBC, Canadians are moving to the U.S. at levels not seen since the brain-drain era in the early aughts. The report cited the American Community Survey, which found that 126,340 Canadians moved to the U.S. in 2022 - a 70 per cent increase from 2012. Of that number, 53,311 were born in Canada, about 50 per cent higher than the average number that moved to the U.S. before the COVID pandemic.
And it's likely a large portion of them are migrating to Florida, which is now the second-fastest growing state in the U.S.
The spike doesn't surprise me. Of my friends who have moved to the U.S., all have cited dissatisfaction with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's and Ontario Premier Doug Ford's COVID-era policies and the subsequent plunge in living standards.
Ben Feferman, a gaming consultant who managed to get an O-1 "extraordinary ability" visa and relocate his wife and four kids to the Sunshine State, told me over the phone in May that, "Our move was heavily motivated by COVID politics and policies. We felt there was no quality of life with endless lockdowns, restrictions and faux scientific public health policy. Especially with school closures."
Last week, I spoke with Rachel Azagury, CEO of marketing firm The Concept Agency, who is packing up her husband and two kids this summer to head south on an L-1 intracompany visa - and knows at least five other families doing the same. She supported Canada's lockdown measures at first, she says, and thought Florida's laissez-faire attitude toward social distancing was "nuts." But after a few months, she thought "there has got to be a different way."
"I realized we can't have an outdoor lifestyle here. We couldn't even go to the park," she says. For a time in 2020, Toronto cordoned off playgrounds and fined users. The pandemic "showed all of Canada's cracks."
The pact that citizens have always had with this country has delivered us a safe, tolerant, well-governed place; in return, we put up with -20 C winters, high taxes, limited job opportunities, bad shopping and expensive liquor. We looked down on the U.S. for gun crime, high health-care costs and fractured politics.
But post-COVID, that pact feels broken: good governance has dissolved, from health-care wait times, to passport lines, to infant Tylenol shortages Crime is skyrocketing - police have even encouraged residents to leave their car fobs near the front door to avoid violent interactions during auto thefts.
Millennials, now all grown up, are making a new calculation: if we cannot ensure a solid economic future for our children, if we send well over half our paycheques (after income and consumption taxes) to the government but can't even secure a pediatrician in exchange, if our cars are stolen not once but twice in the same year from our driveways, then why are we still here?
Instead, we figure, Florida has no state income taxes. Sales tax is only six per cent. Gas is less expensive Under the state's school choice program, parents get school vouchers of around $8,000, which can be put towards private school or home school. All those savings, plus the likelihood of a higher salary, means that we could afford health care, a home in a gated community and private school tuition. And we wouldn't have to struggle to put our wiggy toddlers in a snowsuit every time we want to go outside.
In other words, middle-class millennials can simply enjoy a higher quality of life in the U.S.
Mandelbaum says that similar factors drove a Canadian exodus to the U.S. some two decades ago.
"That's a big problem," he says. "I get the sense when I speak to clients that it's not a cycle, that there is an underlying problem, a fundamental problem with Canada's economy that people are expressing frustration (over), and are actually moving."
The biggest frustration is, of course, housing prices.
Home prices are astronomical, with property listings now averaging $1.25 million even in Vaughan And remember, this is not in downtown Toronto - this is where people go when they want cheaper houses. In comparison, the average property price in Boca Raton, Fla. in April 2024, a prime destination for Canadian families, is roughly US$580,000 (C$800,000) - about 35 per cent cheaper.
The Feferman family were long-term renters in Canada, but moving to Florida allowed them to become homebuyers for the first time. They paid just C$750,000 for their house in a desirable neighbourhood close to their kid's school, and say a comparable one would cost about $1.5 million in Canada.
Another major source of frustration with Canada is navigating the health care system. It was never great, but post-COVID, Ontario health care feels apocalyptic. My own family doctor reduced the days she works per week to open a Botox clinic because she says the government doesn't pay enough - and I don't blame her one bit.
Azagury says that during COVID, she had to wait nine months just to see a dermatologist.
"Is (American healthcare) going to be more expensive?" she says. "Maybe, but it's definitely better and faster."
Plus, it's possible that Canadians have been victims of fear-mongering about the American healthcare system. In truth, if you are employed in the U.S., you get health insurance with your job, and if you are a low- or middle-income earner, you can access government-run health coverage or a plan under Obamacare.
"When I actually looked into it and applied, it's an unbelievable system for the middle class," Feferman says. "You get very generous tax credits that really subsidize health care, and in many ways, my feeling is that private health care delivers a better quality of care. There is also a lot more they do for preventative care."
The superiority complex that Canadian millennials had about the U.S. when we were growing up is officially over. Things are looking bleak here, and bright over there. Unless something changes, expect more and more of us to flee south.
National Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing prices Taxes Cost control; Industry: 92812 :‎ International Affairs

Document 375 of 500

Why are jewish children being victimized?; At some canadian schools, they're being held responsible for war's toll in Gaza, and no one seems bothered by this

Author: Teitel, Emma

Publication info: Teitel, Emma. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-are-jewish-children-being-victimized-at-some/docview/3067576236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Why+are+jewish+children+being+victimized%3F%3B+At+some+canadian+schools%2C+they%27re+being+held+responsible+for+war%27s+toll+in+Gaza%2C+and+no+one+seems+bothered+by+this&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Teitel%2C+Emma&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Today, we are pleased to welcome Emma Teitel to National Post with her debut as a contributing columnist. Iused to say I wouldn't send my kids to Jewish day school because it was too expensive.
Now I'd rather not send them because there is an emerging trend in my country that involves antisemites shooting guns at Jewish schools under the cover of night. It's a trend that more casual Jew haters shrug their shoulders at. After all, kids aren't even at school after dark. And besides, have you seen Gaza? I can't unsee Gaza. But forgive me if I'm also preoccupied with the once rising, now risen "tide" of antisemitism in Canada. Forgive me as well if I am capable of simultaneously lamenting the killing of Gazan children and the grotesque fact that Jewish kids in Canada are being held responsible for their deaths.
Two Jewish schools shot at in the span of a week. A synagogue firebombed with congregants inside. A rock thrown through the window of another. And from political leaders of every stripe: tweets.
It is not an exaggeration to suggest that among Canada's progressive left, there was exponentially more outrage at Ontario Premier Doug Ford's recent offensive comments in response to the shooting of a Jewish school in Toronto than there was outrage at the shooting itself.
Words are violence, and what not.
Speaking of words, this past winter, while war raged in Gaza, Anna Osver's 11-year-old daughter, who is Jewish, came home from her publicly funded Ottawa Catholic school in tears.
"All Jews have to die," a boy had told her. He then suggested she kill herself.
Osver immigrated to Canada from Israel in 2007. Before Israel, she lived in Ukraine where she grew up. "It was not easy for Jews in the USSR," she told me. "Now I am feeling what I felt there."
Go back to Europe, they say. At some Ottawa public schools, Jewish children are "being held collectively responsible for the war." This is according to Tamara Taub, the co-founder of Jewish Parents of Ottawa Students Association, a group formed specifically to deal with antisemitic bullying in the aftermath of October 7.
Taub recently conducted a survey of 66 Jewish kids from grades 6-12 in the Ottawa area, most of them students in the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board.
Seventy per cent said they have felt unsafe at school because they're Jewish.
Students report being labelled "murderers" and "baby killers" by their peers, having non-Jewish students scream "free Palestine" in their faces, chant "heil Hitler" at them, demand they "pick a side" in the conflict, and even engage in physical violence.
Sometimes the vitriol comes in an all-inclusive package. In one incident, according to Taub, a Jewish student in Grade 7 was asked by her peers if she supported Israel or Palestine. She declined to answer. When they pressed her, she conceded: "Israel." As she walked away from the group, someone threw a rock at her head.
Adults might be capable of admonishing the Israeli government without scapegoating Jews. Children are not so sophisticated. Indeed, if Canada's media class can't seem to denounce Benjamin Netanyahu without evoking the Third Reich, maybe it's foolish to expect gentile elementary-school children not to paint their Jewish peers with the same poisonous brush.
The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board says it is "taking a number of measures to build a culture of belonging and address issues of hate." According to its spokesperson, these include, but are not limited to, an "anti-hate tool kit" as well as the expansion of a "safe schools reporting tool" that enables students to report bullying incidents anonymously.
Many Jewish parents, however, feel the board is not doing enough. Their frustration was palpable at a school board meeting late last month, during which school trustees sat mostly silent in the face of parent deputations about antisemitic bullying.
When a Jewish high school student spoke about being branded a "baby killer" at school in the aftermath of October 7, the only school trustee to ask her a followup question - and to offer a kind word - was herself Jewish. The others said nothing.
"We feel there is this concern that where we fall in the diversity and inclusion world seems to complicate things," said Taub. "We're not looked at as a minority."
In fact, as far as minorities go, Jews are minor.
Muslims comprise roughly five per cent of the national population, Hindus 2.3 per cent and Sikhs 2.1. Jews make up 0.9 per cent of people in Canada.
None of whom, as far as I know, serve in Israel's war cabinet. Certainly, the children among them are not calling the shots in Rafah.
It is a perverse fact that Jewish kids in Canada are being scapegoated during the day by their peers and at night by gunmen. It is even more perverse that this is old news.
National Post Emma Teitel is a national magazine award-winning columnist and a senior consultant at Navigator Limited. She was previously a member of the Toronto Star's editorial board and a Star opinion columnist with a focus on city issues. Her work has also appeared in Maclean's magazine and the Boston Globe. She lives in Toronto with her family.

Letters: Name Canada's parliamentary traitors so they aren't re-elected

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-name-canadas-parliamentary-traitors-so/docview/3067253243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Name+Canada%27s+parliamentary+traitors+so+they+aren%27t+re-elected&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'Only "traitors" would protect the identity of traitors'
Re: LeBlanc says it would be 'irresponsible' to release names of MPs who aided foreign meddling - Ryan Tumilty, June 6
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc argues that releasing the names of MPs believed to be aiding foreign powers would be "irresponsible." In fact, the truly irresponsible action would be to not release the names of those believed to be working against the interests of our country. Making this information public now is especially important with an election looming that could otherwise see some of these people returned to office by unwitting constituents.
Barry Francis, Toronto
Only "traitors" would protect the identity of traitors. The Trudeau's government's protection of members of Parliament who have betrayed our country by helping foreign governments that hurt Canada is unconscionable. Considering that we once hanged traitors in Canada, these MPs should consider themselves lucky that likely the worst that will happen to them - if they are ever identified - is that they'll be voted out of office with a golden pension for life. Shame on them.
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
Actions should have consequences
Re: Government shuts down scandal-plagued cleantech fund after scathing auditor report - Christopher Nardi, June 4
Auditor general Karen Hogan found that one in six projects funded by Sustainable Development Technology Canada (SDTC) - to the tune of millions of dollars - were ineligible. She also found that the organization had serious governance issues.
In response, Innovation Minister François-Philippe Champagne announced that he was shutting down SDTC and transferring funding responsibilities to the National Research Council.
That is it? Those are the consequences?
Will attempts be made to recover ineligible expenditures? Will a "lessons learned" report be prepared by a central agency to avoid future cases? Will disciplinary measures be applied to the public servants involved? Will we have to await future AG reports to identify such incompetence? Were these procurement and funding problems isolated to the SDTC or are they systemic throughout federal agencies?
Champagne got off with a mea culpa and a few Hail Mary's for penance. Surely we can expect better. Actions should have consequences.
Garry Vollans, Ottawa
Desperately seeking effective leadership
Re: Is this what our D-Day heroes fought for? - Bruce Howitt, Letter to the editor, June 9
The anniversary of D-Day brought to mind two thoughts - one positive and one negative. It served as a reminder of how Canada punched above its weight in the life-and-death struggle to liberate Europe from the evil forces of fascism. The other thought was about leadership.
Eighty years later, Canada is facing mortal threats - both externally and internally. Externally, expansionist authoritarian regimes, environmental disasters and cyber misinformation are challenging western democracies. Internally, the collapse of our health-care system and housing supply and threats to our democratic system are alarming. Where is the leadership?
So-called leaders at all levels have been engaging in power contests, identity politics and woke policies while instilling shame in our country and its history. Without effective leadership this ends in disaster.
Ordinary Canadians - those ingenious, strong and good people who build this country every day - don't want sophomoric debate and posturing. They want leadership. While it won't solve all our problems, if the Liberal caucus forced a leadership change (which excluded the current cabinet) it would represent a major first step towards turning these challenges into opportunities.
Gerard Shkuda, Burlington, Ont.
Spending on beer sales hard to swallow
Re: Beer in corner stores, accomplished in the worst way possible - Chris Selley, May 27
Ontario Premier Doug Ford defeated Katherine Wynne in 2018 by noting her fiscal irresponsibility and the $1-billion scandal involving the cancellation of two gas-fired power plants to fulfill a campaign promise. Now Ford wants to give the Beer Store $225 million instead of waiting for a year for a contract to expire, so corner stores can sell beer a year earlier.
Ontarians must wait hours in emergency rooms or on gurneys in hospital hallways before they can see a doctor, or wait months for an MRI. Instead of using the above money to shore up our health-care system, Ford wants to spend it on corner-store beer sales. Talk about fiscal irresponsibility!
What happens to politicians after they're in power for a while?
Peter J. Middlemore Sr., Windsor, Ont.
Fun National Post party game: Who's the Parliamentary traitor?
Letters: Is this what our D-Day heroes fought for?
Health-care scare tactics hurting Canadians
Re: The private health-care scare doesn't work anymore. Someone tell Ontario's opposition parties - Randall Denley, June 6
As expected, the ill-informed opposition parties drag out the scare tactic of American-style health care to protect the canard of "universal health care."
The only thing universal in this world is that ultimately we all die, and a health-care system that cannot respond effectively enough to delay that inevitability is universally failing the taxpayers who fund it.
Where I currently live in Arkansas, emergency room wait times are measured in minutes. I have received an MRI on the day it was prescribed, and the longest I have had to wait for an MRI was one day. I get my blood test results on the day the blood is taken. I have full up-to-the minute access to my medical records. And I have built up a nice nest egg in my tax-deductible Health Savings Account to cover any out of pocket medical expenses.
These are positive outcomes from "for profit" health care that are denied to Canadians by blinkered, philistine politicians who are ideologically tied to a failing, out-of-date health-care solution.
Gordon S. Clarry, Rogers, Ark.

Document 388 of 500

On the map: New condo tower rides wave of development in Pickering

Author: Welch, Paul

Publication info: Welch, Paul. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/on-map-new-condo-tower-rides-wave-development/docview/3066836530/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=On+the+map%3A+New+condo+tower+rides+wave+of+development+in+Pickering&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Welch%2C+Paul&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The winds of change are blowing across Pickering, and Sevoy Developments is setting its sails accordingly with its new 1515 Pickering Parkway condo tower.
Nearby, CentreCourt Developments' new Pickering City Centre project proposes to surround a revitalized Pickering Town Centre Mall with 10 mixed-use towers and various green spaces and urban plazas; municipal plans for the 55-acre site include a library, community centre and performing arts hub.
As for 1515, the project's most important connection to the burgeoning Pickering City Centre lies in its namesake address, explains Sevoy's executive vice-president Jane Renwick. "Unlike other Durham communities such as Whitby, Newcastle and Uxbridge, Pickering lacks a traditional downtown. Now that's changing, and with 1515 Pickering Parkway being a five-minute walk from this new downtown, residents will have easy access to what is becoming the eastern core of the GTA."
The double-sided fireplace is a central feature of the lobby lounge.
That proximity has guided several aspects of the 571-unit tower's design. Rather than spreading retail businesses across its ground floor, the five-storey podium will include 12,000 square feet of indoor amenities: a lobby lounge with a double-sided fireplace; a fitness centre and yoga studio; a library with co-working spaces; a family entertaining room with a kitchenette, TV, games area, pool table and foosball; a pet playroom and spa; a workshop with a bike repair area, crafting tables, utility sink and work bench; bike lockers and a wash station; and on the fifth floor, two connecting party rooms with open-concept kitchens, dining areas, lounge seating and fireplaces. 1Valet's Smart Building Operating System, meanwhile, includes a smart entry system with video intercom and facial recognition, smart package delivery and tracking, and app-based amenity booking.
"We often see 'for lease' or 'for sale' signs on the ground floors of condo buildings, and that takes away from the vitality of the development," Renwick says. "With the mall and all the development around it being so close by, we chose to animate the street front of 1515 Pickering Parkway with amenities, and not with flashing lottery or takeout signs. It's a much better look, and the building will be more attractive for it."
The five-storey podium will include 12,000 square feet of indoor amenities that include a fitness centre and yoga studio.
More than 9,000 square feet of outdoor amenities begins atop the podium, where a landscaped terrace offers outdoor dining areas and a children's play structure. Set between the tower and Highway 401, a landscaped off-leash dog park exclusively for residents is "like a playground that can be sprayed down, and will likely be one of the most used amenities we have," Renwick says. "One of the great things that happened through COVID is that amenities went from being locked-off, bookable-only spaces to being spaces that residents can use anytime."
An in-house multimedia studio, meanwhile, aligns with the building's music-themed exterior design. The work of the architects at Toronto-based Arcadis features a black-and-white facade reminiscent of piano keys, with striking vertical louvres cladding the podium.
"For us, architecture is akin to composing music. It's about creating harmony between form, function and the environment," says Arcadis lead architect Stephen Hood.
While it may seem surprising that every unit in a tower overlooking North America's busiest highway includes a balcony, terrace or Juliet balcony, this again is by design, Renwick explains. "The 401 corridor actually delivers unobstructed views of downtown Toronto and Lake Ontario to the southwest, and of leafy Durham to the northeast. The podium wraps around the rooftop terrace, so it's protected from noise and wind, and the off-leash dog park provides a good-sized setback from the highway."
Ranging from studios to three-bedrooms, all units also feature 8.5-foot ceilings, energy efficient double-glazed windows, custom kitchen cabinetry and bathroom vanities, quartz countertops, and frameless glass showers, with larger units offering kitchen islands and soaker tubs. With some studios starting in the $400,000 range, Renwick says she expects first-time homebuyers and single professionals will be drawn to the project. "The worst thing to happen to a first-time house-hunter is that crushing feeling that they can't afford anything. So for those who are paying attention, we're getting the message out that this project is relatively affordable."
The 1515 Pickering Parkway sales centre is opening at 1360 Kingston Rd., Unit 12, in mid-June. Occupancy is slated for 2028. For more information, visit 1515PickeringPkwy.com.
On the fifth floor, residents have access to two connecting party rooms with open-concept kitchens, dining areas, lounge seating and fireplaces.
Three things
Spanning 14 lanes of Highway 401, the Pickering Pedestrian Bridge connects the Pickering GO Station and Pickering Parkway. In 2021 it was recognized by Guinness World Records as the longest enclosed pedestrian bridge on Earth.
Opened in 2023, the $500-million Pickering Casino Resort is home to a 96,000-square-foot casino and sportsbook, five restaurants (and a Starbucks), a 2,500-seat theatre and a 275-room hotel. 888 Durham Live Ave.
A few blocks south of the 401, the Pickering Nautical Village is dotted with boutiques, cafés and ice cream parlours, with two marinas set on sheltered Frenchman's Bay.

Business indexing term: Subject: Architecture; Corporation: Arcadis

Subject: Architecture

Document 389 of 500

Kelly McParland: Chrystia Freeland talks nonsense

Author: McParland, Kelly

Publication info: McParland, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kelly-mcparland-chrystia-freeland-talks-nonsense/docview/3066739863/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Kelly+McParland%3A+Chrystia+Freeland+talks+nonsense&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McParland%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario had a cabinet shuffle last week during which an intriguing but little-remarked change took place.
Someone named Stan Cho will replace someone named Neil Lumsden for seating rights at Premier Doug Ford's cabinet table. The intriguing bit is that while Neil was minister of tourism, culture and sport, Stan will be minister of tourism, culture and gaming.
Gambling has replaced sport because it's all about money, and money is more important than sports. Sports knows this, which is why sporting broadcasts are now just pretexts to ceaselessly batter viewers with gambling ads. Wayne Gretzky, formerly a hockey hero, is now best known as a shill for BetMGM.
Canada's politics, like sports, has become all about money, thanks to governments increasingly inept at anything beyond writing cheques. They're desperate for it, open to any means of squeezing cash that can be used to fuel more promises. And no administration needs it more than the federal Liberals, who spend money with the desperate helplessness of alcoholics reaching for a bottle. After eight years of professing an intense dedication to responsible budget management the best they could do for 2023 was a deficit of $50.9 billion.
Fifty billion. That's more than a billion dollars a week in borrowing just to keep the country running. Not coincidentally, the annual bill for interest payments has reached $46.5 billion. In effect, almost the entire deficit is going to pay charges for past deficits, contributing to more deficits in future. We're maxing our Visa card to pay interest to Mastercard, without ever reducing the balance on either.
Since coming to power in 2015 the Liberals have so willingly poured out gushers of money they'd succeeded in conditioning Canadians to treat the bounty as normal.
Everyone turns to Ottawa when they feel the pinch. The chief executive of Westjet Airlines professed recently he sees no reason a large airline like his should have to pay rent to use the country's airports. Ottawa, he says, should quit collecting rents and freeze other fees in the bargain. Because apparently it's the taxpayers' job to prop up big airlines.
Canada's media, meanwhile, has set up a "federally incorporated non-profit" conglomeration comprised of "12 independent media outlets" to divvy up the $100 million the feds have squeezed from Google on the basis that Canada's media also needs a government support system.
Three giant global auto manufacturers, Volkswagen, Stellantis and Honda, will collect somewhere between $23 billion and $28 billion in public funding to locate new EV plants in Ontario. The estimates vary and financing details are limited, but while those plants say they'll "create" about 6,500 jobs, Loblaw, Canada's biggest grocer, expects 7,500 new hires for 40 new stores it plans to build at a cost of $2 billion.
That $270,000 per job for Loblaw, $3.5-$4.3 million per job for Ottawa. When Ottawa says "invest" it means handing over your money and mine; Loblaw will actually risk its own. Yet while politicos trampled all over one another to pose for photo ops with the happy foreign recipients of federal subsidies, Liberals have repeatedly summoned Loblaw boss Galen Weston Jr. to Ottawa to be labelled a greedy capitalist and dressed down for building one of the country's few internationally-successful firms, one that actually makes money and pays dividends.
Neither the prime minister nor his finance minister, Chrystia Freeland, is known to have ever run so much as a hot dog stand at their own risk. The prime minister gave speeches and was a teacher, briefly, before entering politics. His finance minister was a journalist, editor and wrote a book. Canada's newsrooms are filled with reporters and editors who have written books, but I wouldn't trust them with the country's finances.
The result is in the numbers: economic growth is an anaemic one per cent, the lowest since Justin Trudeau took office and expected to stay that way for years. The OECD predicts Canada will be the worst-performing of 40 "advanced" economies until at least 2030. Unemployment is 6.2 per cent and rising as part-time jobs increase while full-time ones decrease. People with money just don't want to invest here; they'll invest in the U.S. no matter which of the deeply unappealing candidates wins the presidency in November, but not in Canada (at least not without government subsidies). The dollar is weak and expected to grow weaker. The main U.S. stock index, once virtually tied with Canada's, is now almost twice as high. For all America's weirdness, U.S. business is seen as dynamic and creative. Canada depends on how much people will pay us for natural resources, which we're embarrassed about producing.
Michael Higgins: To Freeland, the capital gains tax is all that is keeping Canada from anarchy
Matthew Lau: Liberal hike to job-killing capital gains tax is inexcusable
Oops, now they're not low and the vigorish on her bets has hit $47.5 billion. Up $12 billion in a single year. Closing in on a billion a week, in interest payments alone.
Precisely how much has your life improved as a result of all that borrowing? There were lots of warnings that rates were bound to go up, but the finance minister preferred her own advice, and Freeland works for a prime minister who got all sniffy when questioned about inflation. "You'll forgive me if I don't think about monetary policy," he retorted. Bank of Canada? Like, yuck.
Think it's going to get better? The Trudeau government faces an election next year, and who thinks the biggest-spending government in Canada's history will do anything but open the floodgates even wider with its own survival on the line? Freeland's latest budget promised a tax raid on cottagers and doctors via a targeted hike in capital gains taxes. Reports suggest she plans to formalize the plan with specific details this week, and she's already smearing anyone who objects as a gluttonous plutocrat.
"Do you want to live in a country where those at the very top live lives of luxury, but must do so in gated communities behind ever higher fences, using private health care and airplanes because the public sphere is so degraded and the wrath of the vast majority of their less privileged compatriots burns so hot?" she said on the weekend. What nonsense. We're talking cottage owners, doctors, small businesses. Does your doctor live in a gated community and fly a private plane?
Truth is, Ottawa needs the extra cash to pay for the promises Freeland made in the budget, which raised spending further even as she repeated her absolute determination to keep outlays under control. She made the promises against all common sense because the Liberals need NDP support to stay in office and care more about that than any extra damage they might do to the economy.
Incumbent governments are getting hammered in elections across the globe. France, Germany, Britain, India, the EU. Trudeau's troops must be terrified. Frightened governments do crazy things. This time next year we could be looking back on 2024 as the good old days, when borrowing was a mere pittance at 50 billion big ones.
National Post
