Document 406 of 500

Randall Denley: Doug Ford's newly bloated Ontario cabinet exhibits his government's worst instincts

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-doug-fords-newly-bloated-ontario/docview/3065610512/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+newly+bloated+Ontario+cabinet+exhibits+his+government%27s+worst+instincts&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford's Thursday cabinet shuffle illustrates two points. First, the premier has very few aces in his cabinet deck. Second, the new cabinet reflects the bloat that afflicts his government.
Back in 2018, the Ford cabinet had 20 members and vowed to trim spending. Now, the cabinet has 36 members, up from 30, and is the largest in the province's history. Nearly half the caucus is in cabinet. It's ridiculous.
The big news from the unexpected cabinet shuffle was the job switch involving (now former) education minister Stephen Lecce and (now former) energy minister Todd Smith. The two ministers with similar skills have swapped places, with Lecce to energy and Smith to education Both are strong communicators and both have managed complex policy issues in their portfolios.
The change will provide welcome relief for Lecce after five years on the hot seat. PC sources say Lecce wanted out. The minister has done an admirable job for the most part, refocusing the school system on useful skills and settling union contracts without labour disruptions.
Randall Denley: The private health-care scare doesn't work anymore. Someone tell Ontario's opposition parties
Randall Denley: Running against Justin Trudeau is Doug Ford's best reason for an early-ish election
Smith spent three years as energy minister. His big accomplishment was to put in place a plan for a huge expansion of the province's generating system. Implementing it will take skill: it's easy to approve things like small nuclear reactors and battery storage plants in theory; it's another thing to convince local communities that they want them nearby.
Both ministries are in the upper tier of responsibility, but switching ministers will do nothing to make the Ford cabinet stronger. It's a bit like the Toronto Blue Jays' experiment in moving first baseman Vladimir Guerrero to third base. It changes things up without making them better.
This cabinet shuffle tries to make up in numbers what the Ford cabinet lacks in strength. It would be difficult to argue that the premier's two top ministers, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy and Health Minister Sylvia Jones, are doing anything more than a mediocre job. Bethlenfalvy has the right background for his position, but his commitment to balancing the books has been weak. Jones is doing some things right in health, but she has failed to create confidence that government can handle its health-care challenge.
Despite that, there is no one in this group of 36 who jumps out as being worthy of either of those jobs. It's not unusual for a government's talent pool to get worse as time goes on and key players leave. Ford's cabinet hasn't really recovered from the retirements of Christine Elliott, Rod Phillips and Monte McNaughton, all top-level players. Steve Clark's resignation over the Greenbelt development fiasco also hurt Ford's team. In this shuffle, Clark is back as government house leader, which has been made a non-cabinet position. Think of it as a halfway house for Clark.
That said, this shuffle does bring in a couple of interesting new ministers. Natalia Kusendova-Bashta becomes minister of long-term care. She's a nurse and first-time cabinet minister in a challenging portfolio. Rob Flack is the new minister of farming, agriculture and agribusiness. He has a strong background in the field. He had been associate minister of housing.
Viewed charitably, the job of associate minister is a training ground for people with potential, a way to test their abilities and to open up more opportunities for caucus members. OK, but does Ontario really need an associate minister of mines, emergency preparedness or energy-intensive industries? In all, there are eight junior ministers in this cabinet. Has the work of cabinet magically expanded to make all these positions necessary?
This cabinet exercise is clearly intended to create as many jobs as possible. That's why Lisa Thompson is kept on as rural affairs minister, despite losing the agriculture portion of her portfolio. Neil Lumsden was minister of tourism, culture and sport. Now, he's just the minister of sport. These two have reached a political dead end. It would have been better to ease them out of cabinet than to keep them on to do next to nothing.
This shuffle will be seen as a prelude to a likely early election next spring. If so, what's the message? Ford and his team have presided over record spending and big deficits, failing to show the discipline to make tough decisions, even ones as small as demoting cabinet ministers. Adding unnecessary cabinet jobs financially benefits the lucky MPPs, further enhancing the perception that Ford takes care of insiders first.
It's time for Ford to remember that people elected him to control government, not inflate it.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist.
randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 407 of 500

FIRST READING: Have Canada's parliamentary 'traitors' committed literal treason?

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-have-canadas-parliamentary-traitors/docview/3065601293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Have+Canada%27s+parliamentary+%27traitors%27+committed+literal+treason%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign up here
TOP STORY
Amid revelations that the House of Commons contains MPs who have "wittingly" collaborated with foreign governments, Parliamentarians have begun openly referring to these unnamed colleagues as "traitors."
"Name The Traitors" wrote Independent MP Kevin Vuong in a Wednesday social media post.
It's a word with legal implications: Treason remains on the books as one of Canada's most serious criminal offences. Treason carries a maximum sentence of 14 years, with "high treason" being treated the same as first-degree murder (parole eligibility after 25 years).
But it's also one of the most rarely enforced sections of the Criminal Code, and has generally been reserved for acts of betrayal so severe that they ended up getting people killed.
Canada's most recent treason conviction was in 1947 - back when the crime still warranted the death penalty.
The traitor was Kamloops-born Kanao Inouye, and his crimes against Canada were far more bone-chilling than political corruption.
A student in Japan at the outbreak of the Second World War, Inouye became a prison guard tasked with overseeing prisoners of war captured after the fall of Hong Kong. There, he became notorious for his brutality against captive Canadians, shattering bones or inflicting elaborate tortures for even the most minor of perceived infractions. He was tried for high treason after the war ended, and executed in Hong Kong.
Contemporary Canadian criminal law divides treason into two categories. "High treason" applies exclusively to betraying Canada at a time of war, or attempting to assassinate the monarch. But even regular treason generally requires that the accused have used "force or violence" in an attempt to overthrow a Canadian government.
The treason sections of the Criminal Code of Canada. As indicated, it's pretty hard for a treason conviction to stick for anything short of armed rebellion against the Canadian federal government.
Canada's most well-known convicted traitor is Louis Riel, who is also Canada's only traitor to have been an MP
But Riel's treason conviction came only after he actively led an armed rebellion against the Dominion of Canada - and even then, he was the only rebellion leader to be hit the charge of high treason.
Treason charges were publicly discussed in the wake of the 1970 October Crisis, in which the Front de libération du Quebec (FLQ) murdered Quebec politician Pierre Laporte. Quebec justice minister Jérôme Choquette - who had himself been on the FLQ's hit list - said at the time that treason charges were in the offing for FLQ members.
Ultimately, however, prosecutors pursued charges of seditious conspiracy - which carries the same punishment as treason, and is arguably easier to prove.
Strangely, you don't need to be a Canadian citizen to be charged with treason. If a foreign agent were to be caught plotting the violent overthrow of the Canadian government, there's nothing in the law to prevent them being charged with high treason - even if they didn't technically betray anybody.
The only difference for Canadian citizens is that they don't have to be "in Canada" for treason to have occurred: As with Inouye, it applies to anybody with a Canadian passport, even if their crimes are committed abroad.
But even if the "wittingly" collaborationist parliamentarians were actively collaborating with the likes of Iran or China, the only likely way they could be hit with a treason charge is if they had handed over "military or scientific information" that compromised Canadian security.
Canadian treason has no clause for merely working with a foreign government in peacetime, even if that government is hostile to Canada.
This isn't to say that there aren't potential sanctions for parliamentarians who have knowingly collaborated with a foreign government.
A 24-page brief drafted by the department of justice notes that acts of foreign interference could be in violation of the Canada Elections Act, the Security of Information Act and a number of lesser charges in the Criminal Code, such as intimidation or bribery.
"For example, it is an offence for a foreign individual or entity to unduly influence an elector's vote," reads the brief.
In 2010, conservative political commentator Michael Taube mused in a legal essay that "treason as a common occurrence and a crime actively prosecuted may have run its course in Canadian society."
For one, he surmised that Canada's increasingly "activist" Supreme Court would approach treason the same way it had approached plenty of other serious crimes, in that it was "unwilling to enact proper amounts of punishment."
But Taube also noted that the Canada of 2010 just didn't seem to have all that much treason. An era of treason-like behaviour might come, he wrote, adding "for now, this is not the case."
IN OTHER NEWS
"Boo hoo, get over it," said Liberal MP Jennifer O'Connell during a discussion of foreign interference at a Thursday meeting of the Committee on Public Safety and National Security. The comment came after O'Connell repeatedly heckled Conservative MPs as they demanded the names of Liberals accused of foreign interference - prompting the Tories to raise O'Connell's "shouting" as a point of order. So she wasn't telling them to "get over" foreign interference, only to "get over" her repeated heckling of a committee meeting. But her thoughts on the former point aren't all that far off from "get over it." As she told the committee, the Conservatives are "throwing around fake sanctimonious accusations about numbers of compromised" that will "create mistrust and distrust in our democratic institutions."
(That same committee hearing also featured Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc saying it would be "irresponsible" to release the names of MPs identified in a recent high-level report as willingly collaborating with foreign governments. "These names are contained in intelligence reports, in some cases it's uncorroborated or unverified intelligence information," said LeBlanc, who was before the committee as a witness. O'Connell is LeBlanc's parliamentary secretary, which might explain why she was so vocally going to bat for him against the Tories)
A redevelopment of the Richmond Hospital in B.C. has risen from a projected $860 million to just under $2 billion. Although government costs are routinely tallied in the billions, this means that Richmond Hospital will now rank as one of the most expensive buildings in the world. According to a running tally maintained on Wikipedia, a C$2-billion hospital would rank as the planet's 54th most expensive building, coming in only a few spaces behind the inflation-adjusted cost of Dubai's Burj Khalifa, the world's tallest skyscraper.
Amid ever-rising levels of open antipathy towards Canadian Jews, one of the very few actions by any Canadian government thus far has been mandates in both B.C. and Ontario to introduce Holocaust education into the school curriculum. But even this basic gesture is falling apart in B.C. after the B.C. Teachers' Federation denied recognition and funding to the Holocaust and Antisemitism Educators Association. The BCTF didn't provide a reason, but like many public-sector unions they've been pretty open in their anti-Israel sentiment. While they've been lukewarm on Holocaust education, in March the union adopted a motion for Israel's 1948 creation to be taught in B.C. schools as "the Nakba" - an Arabic term meaning "catastrophe."
The treason sections of the Criminal Code of Canada. As indicated, it's pretty hard for a treason conviction to stick for anything short of armed rebellion against the Canadian federal government.

Document 408 of 500

Raymond J. de Souza: What the Trump verdict says about the sorry state of American justice

Author: Far Raymond J. de Souza

Publication info: Far Raymond J. de Souza. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/raymond-j-de-souza-what-trump-verdict-says-about/docview/3065587229/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Raymond+J.+de+Souza%3A+What+the+Trump+verdict+says+about+the+sorry+state+of+American+justice&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Far+Raymond+J.+de+Souza&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The tawdriness of it all - a presidential candidate paying off a porn star - was off-putting. But the Trump verdict is instructive.
Not that the guilty verdict means Trump is guilty of a crime. Not all appalling behaviour is criminal. The guilty verdict is not surprising because so fearsome is the power of America's prosecutorial state that police and prosecutors can usually convict anyone of anything if they are so determined. I wrote about how a Republican justice department did that to the longest-serving Republican senator, Ted Stevens, when Trump was indicted last year.
Canadians ought not be smug. In Ontario, a corrupt system falsely convicted parents of killing their own children. So terrifying was the power of the prosecutorial apparatus that some even confessed to doing what they had not done.
Yet my claim - that no one should be thought guilty solely on the basis of a conviction in an American court - strikes many as too broad. Yes, there are many guilty people who are convicted, but a mere conviction should not be sufficient to convince the impartial observer that justice was done.
The recent Trump trial is a teachable tawdry moment. The headlines blared that he was convicted on 34 felony counts. Thirty-four! It must have been a vast criminal conspiracy. The possible sentence - four years per count - could be 136 years.
Four lessons emerge from this trial about how American prosecutors abuse their power: inflation, imagination, multiplication, and documentation.
Inflation: Donald Trump was accused of paying hush money, which is not a crime at all, and "falsifying business records" to conceal it, which is a misdemeanour under New York State law. The misdemeanour can be charged as a felony if it is intended to facilitate another crime. So Trump was charged with felonies. Inflating charges is so common that people are often charged with racketeering, money laundering, fraudulent conspiracies, simply for intimidation purposes - and those charges are then dropped along the way. In Trump's case, the inflation was necessary for the political impact of a trial; a misdemeanour would be unlikely to produce five weeks in court.
Imagination: American prosecutors encourage juries to imagine more than the evidence shows, or even what the indictment charges. The "other crime," which raised Trump's misdemeanour charges to felonies, was never specified, even at trial. There were hints at an election finance violation, but that it is a federal matter, so it would not be obvious why the matter was being tried by the Manhattan prosecutor.
In another high-profile New York case, the appellate court recently overturned Harvey Weinstein's convictions on the grounds that the prosecutors invited the jury to imagine that he was guilty of a pattern of crimes for which he was not charged, let alone convicted.
Multiplication: Much was made of the finding of guilt on all 34 counts. In point of fact, either it would have been acquittal on all or guilty on all, because the 34 counts were all for the same thing. The charge was this: Trump paid $160,000 in hush money through his lawyer, and then covered it up by claiming it as legal expenses rather than a reimbursement.
Trump paid his lawyer in 11 monthly instalments, hence 11 crimes! Actually, each payment had three elements - the lawyer's invoice, the actual cheque, and the recording of the cheque in Trump's accounts. So 33 crimes! And then the 34th count was tacked on because one payment was recorded twice.
Consider the absurdity of the multiplication approach favoured by prosecutors. Had Trump paid his lawyer all at once, the 34 felony counts would have shrunk to three, and the corresponding possible sentence from 136 years to 12. If he paid without logging the invoice or recording the payment - one count. If he had paid in cash - no cheque to record - no counts.
Is it the prosecutor's position that it would have been better for Trump to have given the cash in the sleazy manner of a patron in a strip club? It's not difficult to envision a cash payment of that sum from a man of Trump's wealth. Recall that Brian Mulroney testified that for years he kept $225,000 in cash from Karlheinz Schreiber in his home safe and a New York hotel safety deposit box. If Mulroney kept that kind of cash lying around, Trump could have easily gone that route.
J.D. Tuccille: Democrats' weaponization of courts against Trump invites retaliation
Michael Higgins: Even Trump doesn't deserve this level of injustice
Documentation: Ever since the feds got Al Capone on tax evasion, American prosecutors are weirdly prone to charging people for faulty filings. They take it as point of pride that while they can't get the murder conviction, erroneously filling out Schedule B will have you in the slammer.
The aforementioned Senator Stevens was not charged with taking bribes, but for failing to include a "gift" on his Senate annual disclosure form.
Hunter Biden is currently on trial for a gun that he possessed for 11 days in 2018. What mischief did he get up to in less than a fortnight? Not much, but he was "false and fictitious" about his drug use in a "written statement on Form 4473." So states the indictment against him. He is not charged with misusing the gun, nor using drugs, but with multiple counts of lying on the form.
Trump was convicted of recording payments incorrectly. He paid his lawyer and called it a legal expense, but in fact it was a reimbursement. If Trump's lawyer had bought Yankee tickets for Trump, and then was reimbursed, and it was recorded as a legal expense rather than a reimbursement, the same charge would have applied. The crime was not the porn but the paperwork.
The reaction to the verdict has fallen along partisan lines. But partisanship can only explain the motivation for why this particular set of charges was brought. It doesn't explain why American justice normally works this way, for Republicans and Democrats, for the former president and the current president's son, for the rich and the poor, for the innocent and the guilty.
National Post

Business indexing term: Industry: 92111 :‎ Executive Offices

Location: New York; United States--US

People: Trump, Donald J

Document 409 of 500

The private health-care scare doesn't work anymore

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/private-health-care-scare-doesnt-work-anymore/docview/3065532954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=The+private+health-care+scare+doesn%27t+work+anymore&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Have you heard about the latest Ontario health-care scandal? According to the province's opposition parties, it's not that Ontarians are waiting more than three times as long as they should for key diagnostic tests. Instead, it's the government's plan to fix the problem.
On Monday, the Doug Ford government an-nounced the addition of 100,000 MRI and CT scans a year. How can that be bad? These are necessary diagnostic tests and the shortage of them contributes to long wait times for treatment.
Right on cue, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles responded to the announcement like a couple of welltrained parrots. They say the problem is that the government is using the private sector to add capacity.
"We are seeing the dismantling of our health-care system," Stiles said, before wandering off topic to talk about emergency room closures.
Not to be outdone, Crombie said the diagnostic-testing expansion "was just one more step in the privatization of our health-care system." She predicted that "people will be going into these clinics with their credit cards."
Crombie even called in backup in the form of MPP Adjil Shamji, the Liberal party's health critic and an actual doctor. He predicted that the private clinics would try to upsell their services, maybe by talking patients into a full-body MRI when they didn't need one.
What a pathetic display.
Instead of saying something intelligent like "What took the government so long?" the opposition party leaders fell back on their old privatization scare tactics. Their arguments don't make sense. Ontario already has seven such diagnostic clinics doing 150,000 tests, and they are paid for the service by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan just like the new ones will be. The tests have to be ordered by a doctor and OHIP will only pay for the test prescribed. Why would anyone want more? The provincial government didn't stress the very best point about asking the private sector to do more diagnostic testing. The substantial capital cost of expanding diagnostic imaging capacity will all be borne by the private sector.
Contrast that to what Ontario has traditionally done, which is about as dumb and outdated an approach as one could imagine. Say a hospital wants to add an MRI. The first step is for the government to approve it. Then it's up to the hospital to fundraise the money to buy the machine, a cost of more than $3 million. For a small hospital, that can take years. Once the hospital has held enough bake sales and sold enough raffle tickets to cover the cost, then the government provides the operating funds.
If you were looking for the slowest way to meet a pressing need, that would be it. Even in a country that ranks 25th among 29 comparator countries when it comes to the number of MRI machines per million people, the province of Ontario has been a laggard.
In 2019-20, the latest data available, Ontario had the lowest rate of CT machines per million of any province and only P.E.I. had a lower rate of MRIs.
In Ontario, the average wait time for MRIs is 90 days and for CT scans it's 81 days. The government says the private sector expansion will enable it to meet its target of 28 days for MRIs in every region in the province. If so, that will be a rare and significant win in a health system that struggles to keep up with almost every kind of demand.
Ontario already conducts two million tests a year. The mathematically inclined might wonder how adding 100,000 tests, a mere five per cent increase, can have such a dramatic effect. The short answer is that it doesn't.
The provincial government has also approved 49 new MRI machines for hospitals, most of which are in use now, and has increased operating hours for the machines already in place. The privately owned clinic expansion is the final piece of the picture.
Relying on privately owned clinics to help combat Ontario's health-care shortages is the smart play. Private clinics in Toronto and Ottawa are already doing hip and knee procedures covered by OHIP. That boosts the province's surgical capacity and takes some pressure off beleaguered hospitals.
The whole public-private health-care debate in Ontario, and the rest of Canada, is a bit of a farce. Most Ontario hospitals are notfor-profit entities, not owned by the government. Doctors'clinics are mostly owned by doctors. Much X-ray and blood test work is performed by the private sector.
We have a system of public payment and private provision.
The latest announcement from the Ontario government is more of the same.
That's only controversial to people whose ideology tells them that anything done by the public sector is good and anything by the private sector is bad. It's not a line of thinking that will fix Ontario's health-care supply shortages.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist. randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 410 of 500

Scott moe versus CRA; U you need to know about Saskatchewan's carbon tax fight with Ottawa; ' Uncharted territory'

Author: Joel-Hansen, Michael

Publication info: Joel-Hansen, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scott-moe-versus-cra-u-you-need-know-about/docview/3065532846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Scott+moe+versus+CRA%3B+U+you+need+to+know+about+Saskatchewan%27s+carbon+tax+fight+with+Ottawa%3B+%27+Uncharted+territory%27&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.1&amp;au=Joel-Hansen%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Government of Saskatchewan continues to fight Ottawa and the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) over the remittance of federal carbon taxes on natural gas in what is one of the few times a provincial government has actively refused to abide by federal legislation. Here's what you need to know about the dispute.
HOW DID IT ARISE?
The dispute began when the federal Liberal government in October 2023 announced an exemption on carbon taxes for home heating oils, which primarily applied to people in the Maritime provinces. In the immediate aftermath, Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe called for the exemption to be expanded to include natural gas, a move the federal Liberal government declined to make. Natural gas is the primary way people in Saskatchewan use to heat their homes.
The Saskatchewan Party in December 2023 announced it would no longer collect the federal carbon taxes on natural gas and, in turn, would not be remitting the money to the CRA.
During a visit to Saskatchewan to promote the most recent federal budget this April, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said the CRA would work to collect the withheld funds from the provincial government. The CRA in April also announced it would be auditing Saskatchewan over the government's refusal to remit the carbon tax on natural gas.
HOW MUCH IS AT STAKE?
Crown-owned SaskEnergy Inc. paid $172 million in carbon tax levies to the federal government in 2022, but that dropped in 2023-2024 to $125 million, which covered the period until January when the legislation taking the tax off bills went into force. Since full carbon pricing came into effect in 2019, $577.8 million in total has been paid to Ottawa.
The decision to remove the carbon tax from home natural gas bills saved the average home $400 per year, the Saskatchewan government said.
Failure to collect the tax does not contravene federal legislation, according to Dustin Duncan, Saskatchewan's minister of Crown investment corporations, including SaskEnergy. However, while speaking to the media in December 2023, he said that failure to remit the money at the end of every month does break the law. At the time, he said he was not expecting any legal action to come as a result.
WHY IS THE DISPUTE UNUSUAL?
Andrew Leach, a professor of economics and law at the University of Alberta, said the dispute between Saskatchewan and the CRA is rather unique, given that it involves a provincial government taking direct action to not comply with federal legislation.
"This is relatively uncharted territory, at least in our recent history," he said.
Leach said the legislation passed by the Saskatchewan Party giving it the power to not remit carbon taxes on natural gas made some unique changes, including changing which organization is, on paper, considered to be the official distributor of natural gas in Saskatchewan.
He said the legislation indicates the office of the minister responsible for SaskEnergy is the distributor of natural gas in Saskatchewan, but people's natural gas bills are still coming from SaskEnergy, the province's sole provider of natural gas, which makes it very clear what provincial entity is the official provider.
The legislation also protected the leadership at SaskEnergy from any potential legal consequences of the province's decision. But Leach said the legislation overall is of little consequence since it oversteps provincial jurisdiction.
"It's telling the federal government how to interpret federal legislation, which is beyond what provincial legislation can do," he said.
The CRA declined to provide comment on the dispute, citing the need to protect private information, and the premier's office did not respond to requests for comment.
Tom McIntosh, a professor in the department of politics and international studies at the University of Regina, said that appearing to stand up to the federal government has historically been a popular stance for Western provincial governments, regardless of their political stripe.
However, he said Moe's impulse to pick fights with the federal Liberal government has been especially strong, though this has sometimes been done to take the focus off concerns about its own governing record.
"Moe, no matter what, seems capable of turning this into, 'Well, yes, but the real enemy is Ottawa,'and it is quite remarkable how he's been able to do that," he said.
See MOE on FP2 The Saskatchewan Party under Moe has drawn a firm line in the sand against the federal government, including passing the Saskatchewan First Act in March 2023.
The province claims the legislation was passed to allow it to reassert its jurisdiction over its natural resources. The act also set up a tribunal that was tasked with assessing the potential impact of federal regulations on the provincial economy.
In April 2024, the tribunal was assigned to look at federal regulations regarding methane emissions along with the new oil emissions cap, and what those regulations could mean for the province's economy.
While much of the tension between Saskatchewan and the federal government has centred on environmental and natural resource policy, there have been other areas of contention.
In April 2023, the Saskatchewan Party passed the Saskatchewan Firearms Act in response to the federal Liberal government's gun buyback program. The new law required that gun owners be given fair compensation for weapons seized and also created a provincial firearms regulatory system.
In September 2021, the provincial government opted to take over appointing the chief firearms officer for Saskatchewan from the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.
McIntosh said the Saskatchewan Party government under Moe has gone in a different direction ideologically than when it was led by Brad Wall, who retired in 2018.
"Certainly, it's much more of that Reform-style populism than it is the kind of business conservatism that was the face of the Wall government," he said.
How might the dispute end? Raymond Blake, a professor of history at the University of Regina who has done extensive research on Canadian federalism, said the dispute is similar to one relating to oil during the 1980s that involved the federal government and the Maritime provinces.
"The big issue was the price of oil and who actually controlled it, and who should decide what is in the national interest," he said.
The matter eventually ended up in court, with the federal government emerging victorious. But not long after the federal Liberal government of the day was defeated by the Progressive Conservatives led by Brian Mulroney, the federal government made the decision to pursue a different track.
"We're going to work out a political deal between Ottawa and the provinces," Blake said of the move back then.
With the current federal Liberal government facing some very low polling numbers, he said it is quite possible Saskatchewan's dispute with the CRA will be resolved by political means rather than legal ones.
"Probably Moe and any other premiers might be wise to wait it out, and in the next election there may be new players and there may be new positions on these contentious issues," he said.
Financial Post mhansen@postmedia.com

Document 415 of 500

Randall Denley: The private health-care scare doesn't work anymore. Someone tell Ontario's opposition parties

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-private-health-care-scare-doesnt/docview/3065423499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+The+private+health-care+scare+doesn%27t+work+anymore.+Someone+tell+Ontario%27s+opposition+parties&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Have you heard about the latest Ontario health-care scandal? According to the province's opposition parties, it's not that Ontarians are waiting more than three times as long as they should for key diagnostic tests. Instead, it's the government's plan to fix the problem.
On Monday, the Doug Ford government announced the addition of 100,000 MRI and CT scans a year. How can that be bad? These are necessary diagnostic tests and the shortage of them contributes to long wait times for treatment.
Right on cue, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles responded to the announcement like a couple of well-trained parrots. They say the problem is that the government is using the private sector to add capacity.
"We are seeing the dismantling of our health-care system," Stiles said, before wandering off topic to talk about emergency room closures. Not to be outdone, Crombie said the diagnostic-testing expansion "was just one more step in the privatization of our health-care system." She predicted that that "people will be going into these clinics with their credit cards."
Crombie even called in backup in the form of MPP Adjil Shamji, the Liberal party's health critic and an actual doctor. He predicted that the private clinics would try to upsell their services, maybe by talking patients into a full-body MRI when they didn't need one.
What a pathetic display. Instead of saying something intelligent like "What took the government so long?" the opposition party leaders fell back on their old privatization scare tactics. Their arguments don't make sense. Ontario already has seven such diagnostic clinics doing 150,000 tests and they are paid for the service by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan just like the new ones will be. The tests have to be ordered by a doctor and OHIP will only pay for the test prescribed. Why would anyone want more?
The provincial government didn't stress the very best point about asking the private sector to do more diagnostic testing. The substantial capital cost of expanding diagnostic imaging capacity will all be borne by the private sector.
Contrast to that to what Ontario has traditionally done, which is about as dumb and out-dated an approach as one could imagine. Say a hospital wants to add an MRI. The first step is for the government to approve it. Then it's up to the hospital to fundraise the money to buy the machine, a cost of more than $3,000,000. For a small hospital, that can take years. Once the hospital has held enough bake sales and sold enough raffle tickets to cover the cost, then the government provides the operating funds.
If you were looking for the slowest way to meet a pressing need, that would be it. Even in a country that ranks 25(th) among 29 comparator countries when it comes to the number of MRI machines per million people, the province of Ontario has been a laggard. In 2019-20, the latest data available, Ontario had the lowest rate of CT machines per million of any province and only P.E.I. had a lower rate of MRIs.
In Ontario, the average wait time for MRIs is 90 days and for CT scans it's 81 days. The government says the private sector expansion will enable it to meet its target of 28 days for MRIs in every region in the province. If so, that will be a rare and significant win in a health system that struggles to keep up with almost every kind of demand.
Ontario already conducts two million tests a year. The mathematically inclined might wonder how adding 100,000 tests, a mere five-per-cent increase, can have such a dramatic effect. The short answer is that it doesn't.
John Ivison: Behold the shattered wasteland of Liberal government credibility
Randall Denley: Running against Justin Trudeau is Doug Ford's best reason for an early-ish election
The provincial government has also approved 49 new MRI machines for hospitals, most of which are in use now, and has increased operating hours for the machines already in place. The privately owned clinic expansion is the final piece of the picture.
Relying on privately owned clinics to help combat Ontario's health-care shortages is the smart play. Private clinics in Toronto and Ottawa are already doing hip and knee procedures covered by OHIP. That boosts the province's surgical capacity and takes some pressure off beleaguered hospitals.
The whole public-private health-care debate in Ontario, and the rest of Canada, is a bit of a farce. Most Ontario hospitals are not-for-profit entities, not owned by the government. Doctors' clinics are mostly owned by doctors. Much X-ray and blood test work is performed by the private sector.
We have a system of public payment and private provision. The latest announcement from the Ontario government is more of the same. That's only controversial to people whose ideology tells them that anything done by the public sector is good and anything by the private sector is bad. It's not a line of thinking that will fix Ontario's health-care supply shortages.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist.
randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 416 of 500

Liana Gordan: It's common sense for Conservatives to defend the unborn

Author: to, Special

Publication info: to, Special. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liana-gordan-common-sense-conservatives-defend/docview/3065229721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Liana+Gordan%3A+It%27s+common+sense+for+Conservatives+to+defend+the+unborn&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=to%2C+Special&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Supreme Court of the United States sent seismic cultural, political, and legal reverberations throughout the world upon overturning its precedent on abortion "rights," Roe v. Wade, in 2022. Shockwaves have been felt as far north as Canada - and no one seems to love talking about it more than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
In mid-May, Trudeau criticized the refusal of New Brunswick's premier, Progressive Conservative Blaine Higgs, to "respect a woman's right to choose," referring to a recent regulation that denied public funding for abortion procedures outside of hospitals. The prime minister added that Canadians were "seeing what happened in the United States." In essence, he maintained that abortion access in Canada is in serious jeopardy because some provinces don't fund all abortions - even though abortions in Canada aren't limited by law, and even though the procedure remains largely state-funded.
This political atmosphere in Canada is quite obviously not analogous to that of the U.S., where many near-total abortion bans have been enacted since the reversal of Roe v. Wade - but that doesn't matter to the Liberal politician who is desperately looking for an issue to "win" on. Unfortunately for Canada, abortion has become one such issue.
Trudeau argues that restrictions on abortion are more likely to enter force under a Conservative government. Recent events in politics seem to add credibility to his claims. In April, Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre suggested he might use a constitutional clause to enact criminal justice reforms that courts have previously struck down - a tactic that Liberals warn could be used to restrict abortion. Add to that a recent petition from a Conservative member of Parliament which calls upon the federal government to regulate abortion and you get a brewing political storm, especially in a country that has cemented abortion access into the heart of its cultural and political identity.
Poilievre's office has responded to Trudeau's assertions by stating that a "common sense Conservative government will not legislate on abortion and therefore will never use the section of the Constitution pertaining to this matter."
The Conservatives have been driving home the importance of enacting policy predicated upon "common sense" - self-evident truths, accessed using simple reason, applicable to the public conscience and written on the heart of the common man. This is a classic case of conservative doublethink; how is using the government to protect innocent human beings against lethal force not common sense?
Life, liberty and property are the conservative ideals of ages past, the foundational subjects of the government's protection and supervision. These principles are not only taken together as a group of rights which logically imply one another, but they are also ordered by importance.
There is no "common-sense" conservative action - be it tax reform, criminal justice or lowering the cost of living - without the recognition of the inalienable right to life. The pursuit of liberty and protection of one's property presupposes the existence of a subject; you can't advocate for policies that promote quality of life while refusing to recognize the right to life. It is a case of cognitive dissonance of gargantuan proportions.
More than that, Canadian policy on abortion flies in the face of "common sense." The Criminal Code defines a human being as anyone who has "completely proceeded, in a living state, from the body of its mother." This statement not only introduces a host of ethical concerns for pregnant women (who, when killed, are counted as only one homicide - even at 39 weeks pregnant), but flouts the clear scientific consensus: from the moment of fertilization, a new organism comes into existence, a living human being.
Those who object to protecting the unborn cannot do so on the basis that the fetus is not a human. Perhaps they may claim that the fetus, while human, is not yet a person: someone with moral status and worthy of protection under the law. While there are compelling arguments in support of fetal personhood, the Criminal Code takes no position on the matter. Instead, it defines humanity at an arbitrary and unscientific marker, effectively leaving a legal void in which the unborn are given no legal recognition whatsoever.
When the law reflects falsehoods of this magnitude, it ceases to become the mirror and sword of justice and instead becomes a means of facilitating harm. This contradiction has affected real victims, some of whom, to this day, have been left unrecognized.
In 2017, Arianna Goberdhan of Ajax, Ont. was murdered at 37 weeks pregnant, 20 days away from her due date. Her baby's name was Assara. They were buried alongside each other; they were both mourned. According to the law, however, Assara was not yet a "human" - therefore, she could not be a victim and her killing could not count as a crime. For the murder of two innocent human beings, the perpetrator is serving 17 years in jail, charged with only one count of homicide.
The progressive political class upholds "values" that are fundamentally at odds with the common (and common-sense) practices of Canadian society. It is normal to uplift mothers who mourn miscarriages - but don't you dare tell Assara's grandparents that they are right to grieve their lost granddaughter and demand justice for her murder. It seems that, in the minds of elites, mourning and hurt individuals who ask the government to fulfill its purpose are only out to "sneak" fetal personhood into Canadian law. In fact, progressive politicians called out Bill C-311, which sought to codify protections for pregnant women in the law before it was defeated last June, as a deceptive attempt to grant rights to fetuses.
For the most part, many Conservatives shied away from that accusation, claiming that the bill had nothing to do with abortion. But the bill had everything to do with abortion. That should be the point. If it is wrong to murder a woman who is pregnant, and it is intuitively the case that there are two victims, how does the status of the unborn magically change when the perpetrator of the crime has been solicited by the mother?
At the very least, Liberals are logically consistent: if a fetus isn't a person, abortion and fetal homicide cannot be legally punished. Conservatives, in refusing to act on abortion - while trying to introduce some legal recognition for fetuses - are the ones caught up in contradiction.
Instead of caving into the deceitful tactics of progressive politics, true conservatives would proudly defend life. A truly common-sense conservative movement would call out contradictions, scientifically erroneous definitions and injustices wherever they occur. Until the Conservatives sort out a hierarchy of rights in their proper order, they will remain part of a movement that stagnates on cultural and political change.
People want politicians with principles - and consistent ones at that.
National Post
Liana Gordan is a columnist at The Conservateur and contributor to UpwardNews. She is a philosophy student at Tyndale University.
Michael Taube: Liberals fearmonger over abortion rights that Poilievre won't touch
Sabrina Maddeaux: I'm pro-choice and Justin Trudeau's abortion politics infuriate me

Subject: Fetuses; Conservatism; Personhood; Law; Progressivism; Abortion; Politics; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Liana Gordan: It's common sense for Conservatives to defend the unborn

Document 417 of 500

William Watson: How to get 'working-class' voters to support Reagan-Thatcherism

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/william-watson-how-get-working-class-voters/docview/3065229386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=William+Watson%3A+How+to+get+%27working-class%27+voters+to+support+Reagan-Thatcherism&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At a recent Liberty and Leadership seminar sponsored by the Montreal Economic Institute, I heard former Harper policy adviser and current conservative ideas merchant Sean Speer grapple with the political puzzle of the age: how to enable conservative political parties that increasingly draw their support from "working-class" voters to implement the Reagan-Thatcher-style tax cuts and deregulation that are so obviously needed in an era that has turned into a rerun of the stagflationary spend-and-tax 1970s. (Personally, I would ditch the term "working-class": who doesn't work these days?)
In a way, this is the reverse of the "What's the matter with Kansas?" problem that has bedevilled the American left since even before Thomas Frank made it the title of his 2007 book. Kansas is a relatively poor state, Frank argued. Its economic interest should be to vote Democratic since the Democrats offer all sorts of programs and tax redistribution to benefit lower-income voters. And yet Kansans keep voting Republican.
"What could be the matter with Ontario's working class?" is the puzzle currently confronting Canadian conservative and Conservative strategists. How do you get Ontario workers to vote against their supposed economic interest? In theory, many should prefer Liberal redistributionist policies that are explicitly tilted toward "the middle class and those working hard to join it." How does a national Conservative and maybe even conservative party persuade such voters they need to elect a government that will go back to balanced budgets, less spending and lower taxes?
The Kansas paradox has two obvious explanations. One, the premise is wrong: Democratic redistribution isn't actually so good for Kansans and they understand that. And, two, (though it pains this economist to say so) there's more to life than economics: so-called cultural policies, even "culture wars," matter a lot. People from media-dominating elite culture just don't understand how ordinary folk - lumpen-folk, you might say, borrowing from the Marxian "lumpenproletariat" - hold fast to antiquated notions of patriotism, gender, tradition, religion, community, personal safety and so on. But they do (God bless 'em!).
That suggests two main strategies for getting so-called working-class people to support the sensible smaller-government, balanced-budget policies we so clearly need.
One is to continue to fight that battle head-on. Working-class Canadians, like just about all Canadians (short-sellers and McKinsey partners maybe excepted), do better in a good economy. And a good economy is more likely if people can get on with their ambitions with a minimum of taxation, interference and delay from gatekeepers. That's usually derided as "trickle-down" economics. But if conservatives insist on lower taxes for all, not just for people at the top, I suspect even "working-class" people are capable of understanding how that will produce a stronger economy. It's not trickle-down economics, it's works-better, common-sense, from-the-bottom-up-and-all-sides-out economics. For evidence, there's the fact that the first thing the Justin Trudeau Liberals did on coming into office was to raise the top marginal rate of income tax. It would be hard to argue the economic doldrums we've been adrift in since have nothing to do with that.
Incidentally, the upcoming vote on the Liberal plan to raise the capital gains tax, which clever Liberal operatives have hived out of the omnibus budget bill so as to dare the Conservatives to vote against it and thus reveal themselves as friends of "the rich," gives Pierre Poilievre and company a chance to pitch their lower-taxes-for-all line. They could offer amendments that actually do lower taxes for all, including people at the top. And when the Liberals and NDP defeat these amendments, they could vote against the capital gains hike but make clear that under a Conservative government taxes will be 1. lowered and 2. simplified.
On some parts of the traditional economic-conservative program ordinary folk are already fully on board. Former finance and prime minister Paul Martin used to say that on controlling deficits and debts the Canadian public was way out front of Ottawa. In economics, concern for budget balance is regarded as unsophisticated bunkum. But it's bunkum common people clearly subscribe to: even if you can, you shouldn't live continuously beyond your means. Most people already get that.
The second main strategy for winning over people who may not be crazy for Reagan-Thatcher economics is to do a Kansas: offer them attractive platform planks on cultural and other issues that, perfectly reasonably, they feel strongly about. The "Reagan Democrats" who gave Ronald Reagan two smashing election victories - by 8.8 million votes in 1980 and 16.9 million in 1984 - were not all low-tax, low-spending, green-eyeshade fiscal conservatives. They liked Reagan for his strong stands on communism, defence, social issues and elite idiocy - as well as for the strong economy his economic policies helped produce. So they voted for Reagan even if it cost them the short-term benefits of Democratic redistribution.
The future of productivity in Canada looks grim
Seven arguments against industrial policy
I suspect Ontario and other places in Canada are full of potential Poilievre Liberals, maybe even Poilievre NDPers, who are: tired of the Liberals, thoroughly annoyed by all the wokeness, and who don't like gatekeepers any more than Poilievre does and therefore will be willing to vote Conservative even if it means spending restraint in the interest of balanced budgets, lower interest rates and taxes, and faster economic growth.
Financial Post
Bookmark our website and support our journalism: Don't miss the business news you need to know - add financialpost.com to your bookmarks and sign up for our newsletters here

Title: William Watson: How to get 'working-class' voters to support Reagan-Thatcherism

Document 418 of 500

Winning over the working class

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/winning-over-working-class/docview/3065114664/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Winning+over+the+working+class&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.10&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At a recent Liberty and Leadership seminar sponsored by the Montreal Economic Institute, I heard former Harper policy adviser and current conservative ideas merchant Sean Speer grapple with the political puzzle of the age: how to enable conservative political parties that increasingly draw their support from "working-class" voters to implement the Reagan-Thatcher-style tax cuts and deregulation that are so obviously needed in an era that has turned into a rerun of the stagflationary spend-and-tax 1970s.

How does a national Conservative and maybe even conservative party persuade such voters they need to elect a government that will go back to balanced budgets, less spending and lower taxes? The Kansas paradox has two obvious explanations.
One, the premise is wrong: Democratic redistribution isn't actually so good for Kansans and they understand that. And, two, (though it pains this economist to say so) there's more to life than economics: so-called cultural policies, even "culture wars," matter a lot.
People from media-dominating elite culture just don't understand how ordinary folk - lumpen-folk, you might say, borrowing from the Marxian "lumpenproletariat" - hold fast to antiquated notions of patriotism, gender, tradition, religion, community, personal safety and so on. But they do (God bless 'em!).
That suggests two main strategies for getting so-called working-class people to support the sensible smaller-government, balanced-budget policies we so clearly need.
One is to continue to fight that battle head-on.
Working-class Canadians, like just about all Canadians (short-sellers and McKinsey partners maybe excepted), do better in a good economy. And a good economy is more likely if people can get on with their ambitions with a minimum of taxation, interference and delay from gatekeepers.
That's usually derided as "trickle-down" economics. But if conservatives insist on lower taxes for all, not just for people at the top, I suspect even "working-class" people are capable of understanding how that will produce a stronger economy. It's not trickle-down economics, it's works-better, common-sense, from-the-bottom-up-and-all-sides-out economics.
For evidence, there's the fact that the first thing the Justin Trudeau Liberals did on coming into office was to raise the top marginal rate of income tax. It would be hard to argue the economic doldrums we've been adrift in since have nothing to do with that.
Incidentally, the upcoming vote on the Liberal plan to raise the capital gains tax, which clever Liberal operatives have hived out of the omnibus budget bill so as to dare the Conservatives to vote against it and thus reveal themselves as friends of "the rich," gives Pierre Poilievre and company a chance to pitch their lower-taxes-for-all line.
They could offer amendments that actually do lower taxes for all, including people at the top. And when the Liberals and NDP defeat these amendments, they could vote against the capital gains hike but make clear that under a Conservative government taxes will be 1. lowered and 2. simplified.
On some parts of the traditional economic-conservative program ordinary folk are already fully on board. Former finance and prime minister Paul Martin used to say that on controlling deficits and debts the Canadian public was way out front of Ottawa. In economics, concern for budget balance is regarded as unsophisticated bunkum. But it's bunkum common people clearly subscribe to: even if you can, you shouldn't live continuously beyond your means. Most people already get that.
The second main strategy for winning over people who may not be crazy for Reagan-Thatcher economics is to do a Kansas: offer them attractive platform planks on cultural and other issues that, perfectly reasonably, they feel strongly about. The "Reagan Democrats" who gave Ronald Reagan two smashing election victories - by 8.8 million votes in 1980 and 16.9 million in 1984 - were not all low-tax, low-spending, green-eyeshade fiscal conservatives.
They liked Reagan for his strong stands on communism, defence, social issues and elite idiocy - as well as for the strong economy his economic policies helped produce. So they voted for Reagan even if it cost them the short-term benefits of Democratic redistribution.
I suspect Ontario and other places in Canada are full of potential Poilievre Liberals, maybe even Poilievre NDPers, who are: tired of the Liberals, thoroughly annoyed by all the wokeness, and who don't like gatekeepers any more than Poilievre does and therefore will be willing to vote Conservative even if it means spending restraint in the interest of balanced budgets, lower interest rates and taxes, and faster economic growth.
Financial Post

Document 425 of 500

Adam Zivo: Tales of a safe-supply child soldier

Author: Kline, Jesse

Publication info: Kline, Jesse. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/adam-zivo-tales-safe-supply-child-soldier/docview/3064875710/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Adam+Zivo%3A+Tales+of+a+safe-supply+child+soldier&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kline%2C+Jesse&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Beneath the fluorescent lights of a Vancouver-area basement, 17-year-old Logan (a pseudonym given to protect his safety) showed me his old stab wounds last month. He received these scars while working as a drug dealer for the infamous Brothers Keepers gang - a life he recently left after getting sober in jail. Logan wanted to tell me about how his gang was trafficking diverted "safer supply" drugs.
For an hour, I interviewed him under the supervision of his rehab centre. His story was illuminating, as it demolishes the B.C. NDP's claims that widespread trafficking of safer supply is not taking place.
Logan started doing drugs in the ninth grade - nothing crazy at first, just dab pens (portable vaporizers) that allowed him to discreetly get high on cannabis in school washrooms. But he quickly moved to other substances, such as mushrooms and LSD.
The following summer, he joined the Brothers Keepers, as some of his friends were already members and he wanted money to buy weed. Though he lived in Port Coquitlam, B.C., the gang installed him as a drug dealer in Vancouver's Downtown Eastside (DTES), the epicentre of Canada's addiction crisis.
The first time Logan went to sell drugs on the street, he says he was attacked by a homeless addict. Though he managed to defend himself, his hand was slashed open. Rather than seek medical attention, he returned to the traphouse (drug den) his gang operated from, where higher-ranking members, known as OGs (original gangsters), wrapped his wound.
The more senior gang members escorted him back to the DTES and kept watch over him as he continued to sell drugs. According to Logan, once the "junkies" saw that he was with the Brothers Keepers, they knew not to "f-k with" him and thus his career as a dealer, which lasted roughly 18 months, began. By that point, he had not yet entered Grade 10.
Logan primarily sold crack, fentanyl and "dillies," slang for Dilaudid, or hydromorphone, an opioid as potent as heroin, on the street. The latter two drugs were easy to procure. The gang cooked fentanyl in their traphouse, and a near-unlimited supply of hydromorphone could be purchased from homeless addicts who were enrolled in government-funded "safer supply" programs, according to Logan.
These programs distribute free addictive drugs, primarily hydromorphone, under the assumption that this saves lives by reducing the use of the riskier street supply. In reality, though, investigations by the National Post and others have found that many safer supply clients simply divert (sell or trade) their taxpayer-funded hydromorphone to purchase stronger substances, such as fentanyl, which then floods surrounding communities with the drugs, resulting in new addictions and relapses.
Logan said that every single homeless person he knew who was on safer supply would sell or trade their free drugs. "The homeless people would get the dillies for free off the prescription, and so they needed some sort of money to get fentanyl or crack or, you know, something like that - so they would come to us, as drug dealers, and we would buy them often like for $1 a pill," said Logan. "In my view, every homeless person who I knew would come to me right after they got their prescription."
He didn't even need to seek out safer supply clients, as they approached him incessantly, Logan says. He and his fellow dealers would usually acquire 14 dillies at a time, he said, as that is the maximum amount of safer supply a drug user can receive per day in B.C., but sometimes clients would save up their pills and sell them to him in bulk. He estimated that he acquired between 200-500 dillies every day he worked on the street, adding up to thousands of pills over the course of his career.
Logan believes that the safer supply clinics and pharmacies in downtown Vancouver "definitely" know that diversion is a massive issue. He says he bought diverted hydromorphone from clients right in front of these places all the time. "So many corner stores, pharmacies, would see us. You know, they can't do nothing," he said, noting that safer supply clients were constantly "f-ked out of their mind off fentanyl," which suggested that they were not actually being weaned off the street supply.
Logan says the cops who regularly patrolled the DTES rarely bothered him or his fellow gang members.
He said that sometimes different kinds of cops, who would pay more attention to dealers, would come into the DTES, but they were easy to spot. He believes that these officers were trying to investigate the local drug market.
In response to an inquiry, the Vancouver Police Department (VPD) strongly disagreed with Logan's allegation of lax policing. "Front-line officers in the Downtown Eastside and specialized drug investigators in our Organized Crime Section make it a priority to target and disrupt organized criminals who are fuelling the toxic drug crisis in the neighbourhood. We take this work very seriously. Anyone who traffics drugs - including diverted safe supply - could face arrest and criminal charges," wrote VPD media relations officer Steve Addison. I called 15 pharmacies in the DTES, and almost all of them either declined to respond or said that they were too busy to answer questions (many of their claims of being busy seemed genuine, but some did not). However, an employee of Omnicare Pharmacy said that, although they had never seen patients selling safer supply in front of their pharmacy, it was within the realm of possibility for nearby competitors, who they alleged were less careful about following provincial guidelines. An employee of Owl Drugs similarly said that they operated according to the rules and had never seen their clientele sell in front of their business, but that walk-in customers would tell them about safer supply diversion frequently occurring in the neighbourhood.
Logan and other gang members would work different streets in downtown Vancouver - mostly in Chinatown and the DTES - and then take the majority of their dillies back to the Brothers Keepers' traphouse after each shift, where they recorded their hauls in a notebook. "We would all bring it back to the house and put it in one big bag. And we counted the big bag - around 20,000 pills - and it was getting sent to Ontario," he said.
Logan claims that the gang shipped these pills interprovincially through a truck driver, and that, based on what he was told by other gang members, corruption and bribes made this process "so easy." Given the voracious market, the gang pressured him and his fellow dealers to procure as much hydromorphone as possible.
Sometimes the Brothers Keepers would crush the drugs, "add a little bit of fentanyl" into the resulting powder and then repress the mixture to create new, adulterated pills. This counterfeit hydromorphone was more potent, attractive and dangerous. The gang would also take the powdered drug and "take it into some room and put it in a different type of drug," said Logan.
According to Logan, when he was a dealer, other organized crime groups were also involved in the safer supply market to a lesser degree. For example, he said that the Bloods "didn't really like the dillies" because the Brothers Keepers dominated the market, but that they would still traffic safer supply in other parts of downtown.
To make extra money, Logan regularly took diverted dillies (always the "real" ones) back to Port Coquitlam, where he would sell them, he says, to other minors at $5 a pill. This part-time hustle was sustained by his stable customer base of around 15-20 youth - including Kamilah Sword, a 14-year-old girl who developed an opioid addiction due to these pills, and later died of drug-related causes.
Logan stressed that hydromorphone is "definitely a really f-cked drug," and that snorting just two to four of these pills was enough to induce an overdose in an inexperienced user. His younger customers quickly became addicted to the drug, he says, and developed ever-higher opioid tolerances - many would take 10 pills at a time and still wouldn't feel anything, he said. After a few months, some of them started asking him to sell them fentanyl.
"It definitely leads to harder, harder drugs, you know?" Logan said.
Yet both he and his friends would downplay the dangers of hydromorphone to new customers and tell them that the drug was not that dangerous. Even when kids were aware of how addictive it is, many would still want to experiment with the drug just to know what it feels like.
According to Logan, dealers generally sell hydromorphone pills to youth on social media - predominantly Snapchat and Instagram - but, as this can attract police attention, he personally focused on in-person sales in Lions Park, a popular greenspace in Port Coquitlam.
Logan didn't use opioids when he joined the Brothers Keepers, but it wasn't long before he, too, was gripped by them. All of his fellow dealers who sold pills also used them, so he joined in. The first time he tried hydromorphone, he said he felt "no pain, like you wouldn't feel any pain whatsoever. Like you can get hit by a train and you wouldn't feel pain, you know?" The feeling made it easier for him to sell drugs on the street.
As his opioid tolerance rapidly increased, he started using 20-25 pills a day. He tried to quit "many, many times," because "it's not much money if you're using your own supply," but all of his attempts failed. Within nine months of starting hydromorphone, he escalated to fentanyl.
This cycle of addiction and crime was eventually broken when Logan was jailed for assault. He quit drugs and, despite relapsing, is now in recovery.
I was unable to interview Logan's former gang associates, as he is no longer in contact with them. Due to confidentiality rules, his rehab centre was also unable to confirm whether his testimony was consistent with the information he had shared with staff during treatment.
However, I corroborated significant portions of Logan's story by interviewing a former associate and customer of his, Blake (also not their real name), who I had previously connected with while investigating safer supply diversion in the Vancouver area.
Blake confirmed that Logan was indeed part of the gang and that he had cooked fentanyl and sold diverted hydromorphone.
Although time and drugs have fogged Blake's memory, their description of Logan's operations matched Logan's testimony near-perfectly.
National Post
Adam Zivo is Executive Director of the Centre For Responsible Drug Policy.
Drug fail: The Liberal government's 'safer supply' is fuelling a new opioid crisis
Adam Zivo: Astonishing amounts of government-supplied opioids found for sale on Reddit
