Document 426 of 500

Poppy Coburn: Rishi Sunak is destined to become the U.K.'s Kim Campbell

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poppy-coburn-rishi-sunak-is-destined-become-u-k-s/docview/3064875604/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Poppy+Coburn%3A+Rishi+Sunak+is+destined+to+become+the+U.K.%27s+Kim+Campbell&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The British Tories knew their time was up. A platform of high taxation, unchecked public sector growth, and bizarre attachment to progressive social positions bored voters - and hardly differed from the yet-untainted centre-left opposition. Promising change while sitting in an incumbent government is tricky for even the most skillful political leader, a leader the party manifestly did not have.
A charismatic challenger played pied-piper to the disaffected right, luring voters towards his upstart Reform Party. Having hovered on the margins of the mainstream for years, Reform's leader threatened a total realignment of national politics, leaving himself at the centre. Worried talk of "zero seats" began to circulate, but a rumoured last-minute alliance was reportedly dismissed out of hand. Even the most miserable polls proved optimistic: the Tories were denied even the dignified title of official opposition party. The party was dead. Conservatism, however, was not.
Each day the election campaign goes on, this outcome, the destruction of of the Conservative Party, becomes all the more likely.
Comparisons to the obliteration of the Canadian Tories in 1993 are almost too easy. Reform, now led by intransigent Brexiteer Nigel Farage, is even polling at 12 per cent: the same voter share Preston Manning's Reform fielded before the election that returned them 52 candidates. Britain's natural party of government, no stranger to punishing defeats, stares down a truly existential threat. How, after 2019's unequivocal triumph, could this happen? Is Rishi Sunak really about to relive Kim Campbell's humiliation?
Labour's Keir Starmer may be leading the polls, but Monday all eyes turned to the (new) leader of Reform. Farage's speech promised, first and foremost, relief from the most boring general election in living memory. While depicted by friends and enemies as an agent of chaos - an image the man himself is fond of promoting - the Reform leader is in truth ploddingly predictable. His recent pantomime performance ("Oh yes I'm running... Oh no I'm not!") before formally confirming he is running, was less a reflection of a mischievous spirit and more of fundamental insecurity. A seven-time-unlucky MP candidate, Farage is well aware that success in politics rests predominantly on good fortune. Persistence, too: he is likely to win his contested seat in the southeastern coastal riding of Clacton.
Vast, fragile coalition governments of the kind seen in central Europe are as alien to Britons as they are to Canadians. We share a single-member plurality system, commonly known as "first-past-the-post." Smaller parties wishing to exert influence over national politics have to make do with compromise and, in the most fortuitous circumstances, minority partnerships propping up thin majorities. Farage achieved a great deal as leader of the United Kingdom Independence Party, pressuring a weak Conservative party under David Cameron towards a referendum on Europe. But an unelected outsider can't negotiate a deal. The agent of chaos found himself pushed into trying, and failing, to strike a humiliating electoral deal with Boris Johnson in 2019, well aware that his strength lay only in his ability to spoil things.
As it turned out, the Conservatives need no help. The "election-winning machine" showed signs of malfunction long before Liz Truss crashed out of office with little more than a wilted lettuce and a book deal to show for it. Sunak, a wooden politician with extraordinarily bad judgment, nonetheless inherited an essentially hopeless situation: a country that couldn't bounce back from the financial crisis, let alone the pandemic.
Those on the liberal end of the party are correct to point out his stupidity on campaigning on culture war issues; issues that only remind their voting base just how little they have managed to do in 14 years. The "red meat" has long gone rotten. The right wing of the party would be correct to remind to remind the liberal wing that "pragmatic, popular conservatism" is anything but - right-wing demagoguery gave the Conservatives their greatest electoral victory in living memory. Brexit secured the Tories a once-in-a-generation majority in 2019; it also burnt up enough parliamentary talent to guarantee they would lose it.
A campaigning slogan that might as well be "life is terrible, but Labour will make it worse" hardly inspires confidence. Nostalgic pundits keen to compare the God-like charisma of Tony Blair to the dead-eyed hunger of Sir Keir Starmer would do well to remember the warnings of canvassers in '97 of a prevailing attitude of disengagement from politics, and a lack of on-the-ground enthusiasm for New Labour. Massive majorities aren't always accompanied by the cheers of adoring crowds, as any Jeremy Corbyn campaigner could tell you.
Still, a little enthusiasm can't hurt, particularly from the voters you hope one day to bleed dry. If only the 18-to-24 age group showed up to vote, the Conservative party would return zero seats (the party's popularity with under-50s is shy one point the rating of disgraced royal Prince Andrew). Brexit is now associated in the minds of young voters with a higher cost of goods and record-breaking non-EU immigration - migration unpopular even with the supposedly "progressive" youth demographic. Conservative government, building on the foundations of Blair, has succeeded in turning the Island once known as "Great Britain" into a carehome with a health service attached.
Young Britons do not like mass immigration, or high taxation, or unaffordable housing. They do not like their alternating roles of cash cow and whipping boy, simultaneously scorned for their supposedly intractable Left-wing politics and bled dry by Britain's regressive welfare system.
Politicians have not yet found a way to vocalise the damage done to the terminally luckless millennial generation by Covid-19 lockdowns, but its consequences for our moribund social model will dictate the lives of the young far beyond this electoral cycle, exposing as farce the already shaky relationship between honest work and material prosperity. Sacrifice, without concession: this is the "social contract" facing taxpayers throughout the Western world.
Britain is poorer, woker, lonelier, and more exhausted than ever. I'd suggest you have committed anybody who tells you that Starmer's government of procuralism and devolution will reverse these trends to an insane asylum - and make sure to save space in the cell for the poor fellow insisting the Tories will be back in power come 2028. History echoes, but doesn't repeat. Whatever name it takes, the party to re-chart Britain's course will look towards the future: a radicalism and clarity of purpose that Farage and Manning could only have dreamed of.
National Post
Poppy Coburn is a British journalist. Follow her on X, @kafkaswife.

Document 427 of 500

Tales of a safe-supply child soldier; Teen describes how B.C. gang diverted government drugs

Author: Zivo, Adam

Publication info: Zivo, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tales-safe-supply-child-soldier-teen-describes/docview/3064754851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Tales+of+a+safe-supply+child+soldier%3B+Teen+describes+how+B.C.+gang+diverted+government+drugs&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Zivo%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Beneath the fluorescent lights of a Vancouver-area basement, 17-year-old Logan (a pseudonym given to protect his privacy and safety) showed me his old stab wounds last month. He received these scars while working as a drug dealer for the infamous Brothers Keepers gang - a life he recently left after getting sober in jail. Logan wanted to tell me about how his gang was trafficking diverted "safer supply" drugs.
For an hour, I interviewed him under the supervision of his rehab centre. His story was illuminating, as it demolishes the B.C. NDP's claims that widespread trafficking of safer supply is not taking place.
Logan started doing drugs in the ninth grade - nothing crazy at first, just dab pens (portable vaporizers) that allowed him to discreetly get high on cannabis in school washrooms. But he quickly moved to other substances, such as mushrooms and LSD.
The following summer, he joined the Brothers Keepers, as some of his friends were already members and he wanted money to buy weed. Though he lived in Port Coquitlam, B.C., the gang installed him as a drug dealer in Vancouver's Downtown Eastside (DTES), the epicentre of Canada's addiction crisis.
The first time Logan went to sell drugs on the street, he says he was attacked by a homeless addict. Though he managed to defend himself, his hand was slashed open. Rather than seek medical attention, he returned to the traphouse (drug den) his gang operated from, where higher-ranking members, known as OGs (original gangsters), wrapped his wound.
The more senior gang members escorted him back to the DTES and kept watch over him as he continued to sell drugs. According to Logan, once the "junkies" saw that he was with the Brothers Keepers, they knew not to "f--k with" him and thus his career as a dealer, which lasted roughly 18 months, began. By that point, he had not yet entered Grade 10.
Logan primarily sold crack, fentanyl and "dillies," slang for Dilaudid, or hydromorphone, an opioid as potent as heroin, on the street. The latter two drugs were easy to procure. The gang cooked fentanyl in their traphouse, and a near-unlimited supply of hydromorphone could be purchased from homeless addicts who were enrolled in government-funded "safer supply" programs, according to Logan.
These programs distribute free addictive drugs, primarily hydromorphone, under the assumption that this saves lives by reducing the use of the riskier street supply. In reality, though, investigations by the National Post and others have found that many safer supply clients simply divert (sell or trade) their taxpayer-funded hydromorphone to purchase stronger substances, such as fentanyl, which then floods surrounding communities with the drugs, resulting in new addictions and relapses.
Logan said that every single homeless person he knew who was on safer supply would sell or trade their free drugs. "The homeless people would get the dillies for free off the prescription, and so they needed some sort of money to get fentanyl or crack or, you know, something like that - so they would come to us, as drug dealers, and we would buy them often like for $1 a pill," said Logan. "In my view, every homeless person who I knew would come to me right after they got their prescription."
He didn't even need to seek out safer supply clients, as they approached him incessantly, Logan says. He and his fellow dealers would usually acquire 14 dillies at a time, he said, as that is the maximum amount of safer supply a drug user can receive per day in B.C., but sometimes clients would save up their pills and sell them to him in bulk. He estimated that he acquired between 200-500 dillies every day he worked on the street, adding up to thousands of pills over the course of his career.
Logan believes that the safer supply clinics and pharmacies in downtown Vancouver "definitely" know that diversion is a massive issue. He says he bought diverted hydromorphone from clients right in front of these places all the time. "So many corner stores, pharmacies, would see us. You know, they can't do nothing," he said, noting that safer supply clients were constantly "f--ked out of their mind off fentanyl," which suggested that they were not actually being weaned off the street supply.
The cops who regularly patrolled the DTES rarely bothered Logan or his fellow gang members.
He said that sometimes different kinds of cops, who would pay more attention to dealers, would come into the DTES, but they were easy to spot. He believes that these officers were trying to traffics drugs - including diverted safe supply - could face arrest and criminal charges," wrote VPD media relations officer Steve Addison.
I called 15 pharmacies in the DTES, and almost all of them either declined to respond or said that they were too busy to answer questions (many of their claims of being busy seemed genuine, but some did not).
However, an employee of Omnicare Pharmacy said that, although they had never seen patients selling safer supply in front of their pharmacy, it was within the realm of possibility for investigate the local drug market. In response to an inquiry, the Vancouver Police Department (VPD) strongly disagreed with Logan's allegation of lax policing.
"Front-line officers in the Downtown Eastside and specialized drug investigators in our Organized Crime Section make it a priority to target and disrupt organized criminals who are fuelling the toxic drug crisis in the neighbourhood. We take this work very seriously. Anyone who nearby competitors, who they alleged were less careful about following provincial guidelines. An employee of Owl Drugs similarly said that they operated according to the rules and had never seen their clientele sell in front of their business, but that walk-in customers would tell them about safer supply diversion frequently occurring in the neighbourhood.
Logan and other gang members would work different streets in downtown Vancouver - mostly in Chinatown and the DTES - and then take the majority of their dillies back to the Brothers Keepers'traphouse after each shift, where they recorded their hauls in a notebook.
"We would all bring it back to the house and put it in one big bag. And we counted the big bag - around 20,000 pills - and it was getting sent to Ontario," he said.
Logan claims that the gang shipped these pills interprovincially through a truck driver, and that, based on what he was told by other gang members, corruption and bribes made this process "so easy." Given the voracious market, the gang pressured him and his fellow dealers to procure as much hydromorphone as possible.
Sometimes the Brothers Keepers would crush the drugs, "add a little bit of fentanyl" into the resulting powder and then repress the mixture to create new, adulterated pills. This counterfeit hydromorphone was more potent, attractive and dangerous. The gang would also take the powdered drug and "take it into some room and put it in a different type of drug," said Logan.
According to Logan, when he was a dealer, other organized crime groups were also involved in the safer supply market to a lesser degree. For example, he said that the Bloods "didn't really like the dillies" because the Brothers Keepers dominated the market, but that they would still traffic safer supply in other parts of downtown.
To make extra money, Logan regularly took diverted dillies (always the "real" ones) back to Port Coquitlam, where he would sell them to other minors at $5 a pill. This part-time hustle was sustained by his stable customer base of around 15-20 youth - including Kamilah Sword, a 14-year-old girl who developed an opioid addiction due to these pills, and later died of drug-related causes.
Logan stressed that hydromorphone is "definitely a really f--cked drug," and that snorting just two to four of these pills was enough to induce an overdose in an inexperienced user. His younger customers quickly became addicted to the drug, he says, and developed ever-higher opioid tolerances - many would take 10 pills at a time and still wouldn't feel anything, he said. After a few months, some of them started asking him to sell them fentanyl.
"It definitely leads to harder, harder drugs, you know?" Logan said.
Yet both he and his friends would downplay the dangers of hydromorphone to new customers and tell them that the drug was not that dangerous. Even when kids were aware of how addictive it is, many would still want to experiment with the drug just to know what it feels like.
According to Logan, dealers generally sell hydromorphone pills to youth on social media - predominantly Snapchat and Instagram - but, as this can attract police attention, he personally focused on in-person sales in Lions Park, a popular green space in Port Coquitlam.
Logan didn't use opioids when he joined the Brothers Keepers, but it wasn't long before he, too, was gripped by them. All of his fellow dealers who sold pills also used them, so he joined in. The first time he tried hydromorphone, he said he felt "no pain, like you wouldn't feel any pain whatsoever. Like you can get hit by a train and you wouldn't feel pain, you know?" The feeling made it easier for him to sell drugs on the street.
As his opioid tolerance rapidly increased, he started using 20-25 pills a day. He tried to quit "many, many times," because "it's not much money if you're using your own supply," but all of his attempts failed. Within nine months of starting hydromorphone, he escalated to fentanyl.
This cycle of addiction and crime was eventually broken when Logan was jailed for assault. He quit drugs and, despite relapsing, is now in recovery.
I was unable to interview Logan's former gang associates, as he is no longer in contact with them. Due to confidentiality rules, his rehab centre was also unable to confirm whether his testimony was consistent with the information he had shared with staff during treatment.
However, I corroborated significant portions of Logan's story by interviewing a former associate and customer of his, Blake (also not their real name), who I had previously connected with while investigating safer supply diversion in the Vancouver area.
Blake confirmed that Logan was indeed part of the gang and that he had cooked fentanyl and sold diverted hydromorphone.
Although time and drugs have fogged Blake's memory, their description of Logan's operations matched Logan's testimony near-perfectly.
National Post

Document 428 of 500

Daycare audit would expose ineptitude

Author: Lau, Matw

Publication info: Lau, Matw. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/daycare-audit-would-expose-ineptitude/docview/3064753004/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Daycare+audit+would+expose+ineptitude&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.10&amp;au=Lau%2C+Matw&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last month the Association of Day Care Operators of Ontario sent a letter to the auditor general of Canada asking for an audit of the Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care program (CWELCC). It says the Liberal government has so far failed to deliver on the promise of universal childcare and there are "numerous reports which appear to indicate that the challenges facing the CWELCC run much deeper than simple growing pains."
The letter, co-signed by the executive director of the Canadian Council of Montessori Administrators, chair of the Alberta Association of Childcare Entrepreneurs and executive director of the Ontario Federation of Independent Schools, further says the federal program "may in fact be having such a detrimental impact on Canada's licensed child care sector that many families who rely on the program are at risk of losing access not just to their $10-per-day spaces, but to any licensed spaces at all."
Auditing the government takeover of child care is certainly reasonable. Since the implementation of CWELCC through agreements between Ottawa and the provinces, government intrusion into the child-care sector has increased significantly. The government tells child-care providers how much they can charge in fees, tells them what kinds of fees they can charge, and has imposed all sorts of administrative burdens. The Liberal government is clearly interested in regulating the child-care sector. But who is keeping an eye on the regulator? If the auditor general or anyone else did audit the Liberals'national child-care program, here is what they would find. In Wellington County, Ont., the provincial target is to add 1,721 child-care spaces between 2022 and 2026, but as local media reported last week, the county "has confirmed 7,800 unique children are on the waiting list to be placed in child care programs." That is 7,800 children on waiting lists today, versus a target of adding 1,721 spaces over a four-year period of which two years is already gone. A separate news story in May said the "city and county of Peterborough are still on track to add 485 new child-care spaces by 2026, according to a new city staff report." But the city has 3,600 children on waitlists - more than seven times the number of additional spaces planned.
More from Ontario in the past month: A May 21 headline in Now Toronto advises parents to put their unborn children on waitlists now since "many Toronto parents are very frustrated over lengthy waitlists for daycare programs." That's not encouraging news, either. Yet another report from May says Simcoe County, Ont., faces a "critical need" for more affordable child care spots but a local councillor says "we are not going to achieve" the provincial goal for the creation of spots.
When it comes to child-care accessibility, Ontario is clearly in a mess. What about other provinces? Again, just looking at evidence from the past month, CBC News reported, based on an access-to-information request, that 68 per cent of 2,621 respondents to the Newfoundland and Labrador government's child-care survey "have attempted to find child care and, at the time of answering the survey, had not secured a spot. More than 800 of those respondents are on more than five waitlists, while 181 respondents are on at least 21 lists." That is an awfully high proportion of people who cannot access child care, including many who seem very desperate to get it.
In Manitoba, a recent Global News headline reads, "'Scary': Parents reeling as child-care in Manitoba remains inaccessible." The story quotes a mom and early childhood educator who cannot find care for her own children and says waitlists at her centre doubled when the Manitoba government announced it would roll out the $10 per day program. Again, from Global News last month: "B.C. mother says lack of child care could leave her homeless." A separate Global News story in May quotes the director of a child-care centre in Saskatchewan with a waitlist of 2,100 children: "We certainly have quality childcare in Saskatchewan, but accessibility to it is next to impossible." And another report: "Call being made in N.S. to expand federal support to private daycares amid 'childcare crisis.'" Noticing a pattern? Here's a one-line summary of the audit report: the Liberals spent tens of billions of taxpayers'dollars on child care and made it less accessible than ever.
Financial Post Matthew Lau is a Toronto writer.

June 4, 2024 (Page FP9)

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apdnnationalpost&amp;atitle=June+4%2C+2024+%28Page+FP9%29&amp;title=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=26&amp;issue=183&amp;spage=FP9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%282011-%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Title: June 4, 2024 (Page FP9)

First page: FP9

Country of publication: Canada

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

Document 434 of 500

Toronto riding sets record for number of candidates; Federal byelection; Protest effort urges reform to electoral system

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/toronto-riding-sets-record-number-candidates/docview/3064412795/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Toronto+riding+sets+record+for+number+of+candidates%3B+Federal+byelection%3B+Protest+effort+urges+reform+to+electoral+system&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA As nominations closed Monday on the Toronto-St.
Paul's federal byelection, an enduring election activism campaign has ensured it'll be one for the record books.
As of early Monday afternoon, 77 candidates were registered to contest the riding that has been held by the Liberals for more than three decades, which will easily break Winnipeg South Centre's record-breaking 48-name ballot during last year's federal byelection.
That election's 50-by-30 centimetre ballot was so large - nearly the size of a single page of the National Post - it required an amendment to the Canada Elections Act to legally allow it.
These swollen ballots come courtesy of the Longest Ballot Committee, an ongoing protest urging reforms to Canada's election laws, which Prime Minister Justin Trudeau vowed to fix during the 2015 election campaign.
"The longest ballot is a fun way to start important conversations about our democracy," read a written statement to the National Post from Kieran & Tomas Szuchewycz, the organizers behind the committee.
"Most voters recognize that politicians have too much skin in the game when it comes to deciding election rules. It's a straightforward conflict of interest, but MPs would prefer no one pointed this out."
Voters in the downtown Toronto riding head to the polls June 24.
All but five of the candidates running in Toronto-St.
Paul's list Kieran Szuchewycz as their official agent.
Parties contesting the election include the Liberals, Greens, NDP, Marijuana, Centrist and Rhinoceros.
Among the committee's gripes are Trudeau's failed promise to do away with first-past-the-post (FTTP) elections, in which winning candidates are chosen based on whoever gets the most votes.
Proponents say other methods, including ranked ballots, produce results far more consistent with the will of the voters.
The first candidate officially registered for the byelection was Sébastien CoRhino, from Rimouski, Que., who despite being leader of the Rhinoceros Party chose instead to run as an independent, and let caucus mate Sean Carson run under the party banner.
"Maybe it's the first time it's happened in history, a political leader who's not running for his party," CoRhino told the National Post.
"It gives you a lot of freedom to do and say whatever you want without (toeing) the party line."
Also appearing on the ballot is Donovan Eckstrom, from Grande Prairie, Alta., whose platform largely centres around promoting western Canadian ideals. He's also tweeted his ire about Toronto Maple Leafs forward Mitch Marner, who he says is "a bum."
"I believe in Alberta, I believe in what Alberta can bring, I believe Alberta is the best province," he told the National Post.
"To bring that Albertan spirit to Toronto - as some might say the centre of Canada - I think it's a powerful manoeuvre, and thanks to the Longest Ballot Committee, Torontonians now have a choice, which is simply sensational."
Current election rules allow eligible candidates to contest elections from anywhere in Canada, even if they don't reside within the riding.
Also on the ballot is Agnieszka Marszalek, a school teacher from Pickle Lake, a remote northern Ontario community about 500 km north of Thunder Bay. She said she is running to raise awareness of the problems in Canada's electoral system.
"We have loopholes that nobody fixes, and yet people complain about how our system is ineffective - then do something," she said.
"If more people become active participants in our political system, in our political life, then more things would actually be changed, and politicians would start looking at the interests of the citizens, not the lobbyists lining their pockets."
Liberal MP Carolyn Bennett, who was re-elected nine times in the riding, resigned her seat in January and, the same day, was named Canada's ambassador to Denmark.
Meanwhile, the organizers of the protest say election reform in Canada isn't an impossible dream.
"Gerrymandering once existed in Canada, but then vanished the moment the job of drawing riding boundaries was taken out of the hands of politicians and passed to a non-partisan commission," the Szuchewyczs said in their statement.
"This lesson can and should be applied to the rest of our democracy. We believe an independent body made up of everyday Canadians would do a far better job delivering the fair, ethical, and robust democracy we voters deserve."
National Post bpassifiume@postmedia.com x.com/bryanpassifiume

Document 435 of 500

Coach questions trans tennis policy; Wants 'complex issues' to be fair to teen girls

Author: Kirkey, Sharon

Publication info: Kirkey, Sharon. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/coach-questions-trans-tennis-policy-wants-complex/docview/3064412294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Coach+questions+trans+tennis+policy%3B+Wants+%27complex+issues%27+to+be+fair+to+teen+girls&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kirkey%2C+Sharon&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Junior competitive tennis coach Michael Paduch is aware that he's wading into an emotionally charged debate by questioning a new policy that allows transgender players to compete against other girls, "without restriction."
"It's a complex issue; it's an issue a lot of people care about," Paduch said. "But I have a keen interest in ensuring that female tennis in Canada is not affected negatively by policies that are put in place."
The certified tennis instructor is referring to an email that popped into his inbox informing Ontario Tennis Association (OTA) member clubs of a newly implemented Transgender Athlete Participation Policy for all OTA-sanctioned events.
Under the policy, players may participate in the gender category in which they self-identify, "without restriction," and with no requirement for gender-reassignment interventions, such as hormone therapy, nor any requirement to disclose transgender identity or history to the OTA or any of its coaches, staff or officials.
The eligibility rules stand for league and recreational levels, as well as provincial competitive levels.
Paduch is a certified instructor who has been coaching girls'and boys'competitive tennis for 15 years, taking some to the national level.
He wonders why he and other coaches hadn't been more broadly consulted on a transgender policy that's less restrictive than the rules adopted by the sport's global governing body.
Under the International Tennis Federation's (ITF) policy, a male-to-female transgender player who wishes to compete against biological females must provide a written and signed declaration that her gender is female; must demonstrate to the ITF's satisfaction that her serum testosterone has been below a maximum limit continuously for at least 12 months; and must be "ready, willing and able to continue to keep it below that level for so long as she continues to compete in the female category of competition."
Paduch, who also is a certified wheelchair tennis coach for those with disabilities, is aware of the risks of voicing concerns about a hot-button issue most people are reluctant to speak about publicly.
"I don't think there is anybody in the coaching community who would not agree with the statement that tennis should be as inclusive as possible," he said.
However, "We accepted a much more watered down, much weaker policy than the ITF's, potentially at the expense of girls," he said. He said he's not anti-trans, or opposed to trans athletes competing in female categories, but believes there should be some hormonal restrictions on their participation. "I believe in policies that are built based on sports science," he said.
"In theory, right now anybody who is completely fully male in their teens in Ontario could compete against girls and could be winning titles based on this policy."
It is the latest example of how sports bodies in Canada, for the most part, have adopted more liberal rules around trans inclusion than a growing number of international federations governing sports.
The OTA guidelines "are intended to ensure, insofar as possible, that transgender athletes are not excluded from any opportunity to participate in sporting competitions and are provided a sporting environment free of discrimination," the policy reads.
But Paduch said they offer an unfair competitive advantage to players born male who register in girls'and women's categories based upon self-identified gender status alone.
The Ontario Tennis Association's policy is virtually identical to one implemented last year by Tennis Canada, which also recommends allowing athletes, including high-performance athletes, to compete without restrictions in the gender category consistent with their gender identity.
Tennis Québec, by contrast, requires trans athletes wishing to compete in Tennis Québec-sanctioned tournaments in the category of their new gender to provide a change of sex designation certificate, issued by a Quebec civil registry office.
"In the case of Ontario, we have not done that," Paduch said. "It is essentially an honour-based system, and we all know honour-based systems tend to be ripe for abuse or misrepresentations."
The policy adopted by the world governing body of tennis is also a more "science-based policy that seems to have checks and balances in place to ensure that girls in particular are not affected negatively by participation of fully hormonal males in the teenage years," said Paduch, who is also an ITF tournament director.
"At that stage of development, a fully hormonal male who declares themselves a woman has a significant physiological advantage in terms of heart size, density of muscles and ability to perform," he said.
"Once you get into the teenage years, girls are just not able to keep up."
Athletes who want to participate in competitions and games sanctioned by the International Tennis Federation must comply with ITF rules. Yet in order to gain that international level of play, "you have to be exposed to high-level competition," said Paduch, who played varsity tennis competitively in Poland, where he grew up.
"There is a theoretical possibility that (biological girls) will be prevented from reaching those high levels of girls'competition if there is an increasing number of hormonally stronger former males taking those spots from girls," Paduch said.
The Ontario Tennis Association and Tennis Canada did not respond to several requests for an interview.
But the debate has become a flashpoint in Canada. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who has pledged to create biological-female-only sports leagues for women and girls, has criticized the "unfair disadvantage" young female athletes face when competing against those born biologically male. Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre has said female sport categories, and change rooms, should be off-limits to "biological males."
The Canadian Centre for Ethics in Sport maintains that athletes should be permitted to participate in the gender category of their choosing. A scientific review published by the Ottawa-based non-profit in 2021 concluded that trans sport policies "use arbitrary bounds that are not evidence based" and that "available evidence indicates trans women who have undergone testosterone suppression have no clear biological advantages over cis women in elite sport."
But other research suggests that testosterone suppression has only a modest effect on diminishing muscle mass and strength.
Depending on the sport, "the performance gap between males and females becomes significant at puberty," often amounting to 10 to 50 per cent, added the authors of the review, published in the journal Sports Medicine.
It's not known how many trans athletes are entering tennis competitions. The sport was the first pro sport to open to transgender athletes, when Renée Richards successfully sued to play at the 1977 U.S. Open after undergoing sex-reassignment surgery.
In a 2022 interview with the New York Post, Chris Evert, who faced Richards six times, winning every match, described struggling to beat Richards when Richards was 43 and Evert was ranked No. 1 in the world. "The wingspan. The size of the heart. The size of the lungs. The speed. The fast twitch muscles. The testosterone," Evert said. "There's just everything pointing to the fact that men are quicker, stronger, etc. than women - especially after puberty."
Richards would go on to coach tennis legend Martina Navratilova to several Grand Slam wins, but Navratilova herself has faced fierce criticism for posting comments on social media that say allowing trans females who haven't undergo sex reassignment to compete in women's categories is "not fair" and "not right." After World Athletics, the international governing body for track and field, last year voted to exclude maleto-female trans athletes who have experienced puberty from female World Rankings competition, Navratilova wrote in Britain's The Times that the "best idea" would be an "open category" for trans athletes.
Paduch is concerned that, without "a medical aspect of certification," the new tennis policy could have a profound impact on girls' tennis. "We can't just be inclusive at the expense of girls," he said.
National Post

Document 436 of 500

Pay, working conditions scaring off judges: chief justice

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pay-working-conditions-scaring-off-judges-chief/docview/3064412221/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Pay%2C+working+conditions+scaring+off+judges%3A+chief+justice&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Supreme Court Chief Justice Richard Wagner says it's "very difficult" to be a judge in Canada, that the job doesn't pay enough in some provinces and that conditions are "deteriorating" for magistrates across the country.
During an annual press conference with reporters Monday, Wagner called on provincial governments to increase the resources available to judges to help make the job more attractive to potential candidates and easier for current justices.
That could include a raise, particularly in provinces where cost-of-living concerns are hitting hardest, he suggested. Wagner is also head of the Canadian Judicial Council (CJC), which represents federally appointed judges.
"I'm told by some chief justices in the country, specifically in provinces where the cost of living is higher like B.C. and Ontario, that they have a hard time to attract candidates for the bench," Wagner said.
Federally appointed judges'salaries are set by the Judges Act. Currently, Superior Court, provincial Court of Appeal, Federal Court and Federal Court of Appeal judges make $396,700 annually, while their chief justices earn $435,000.
Judges who live in northern territory receive an additional $12,000 allowance.
Supreme Court justices earn $472,400 yearly, while Chief Justice Wagner makes $510,000.
Wagner said that judges'salary is one issue, but that working conditions are "very difficult" these days because they lack support in most provinces.
He cited a lack of administrative assistance for judges and increasingly obsolete technology used by courts. Judges and legal experts across the country have also denounced a lack of courtrooms, insufficient numbers of clerks and registry employees and a concerning number of judicial vacancies in recent years.
These issues are pushing some lawyers away from the judiciary, Wagner said.
"It has become very difficult for judges to work and it is known ... in the legal community. You will see some lawyers who will refuse to apply if only because of that," he told reporters.
"I think that judges have good conditions, I must say. I think we are privileged in a way, but (judges) are doing a very difficult task. For the last couple of years, we have seen the conditions deteriorate."
Wagner also said the CJC will soon launch a study into judges'health and well-being to better understand the challenges they face.
Judicial vacancies are a key concern to Wagner. Last year, he wrote to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau highlighting his deep concern with a growing number of vacancies (roughly 80 at the time) and the pace at which the Liberals were appointing judges.
On Monday, Wagner said he was encouraged by a wave of appointments that has reduced vacancies to 57 but insisted more work still needs to be done.
"I would expect that the government would appoint judges in a timely fashion, given that they know, very often months before, that the position will become vacant in six months," he said.
"I saw some improvements and I am confident now that the situation can be corrected," he added.
Wagner also took an unusually harsh swipe at certain politicians, pundits and members of the media, whom he accused of reacting to Supreme Court of Canada rulings before reading them and then spreading "disinformation" about the decisions.
He was particularly irked about controversy around a criminal case decision about a sexual assault written by Supreme Court of Canada Justice Sheila Martin this year in which she used the term "person with a vagina."
Some politicians and columnists, including in the National Post, accused the Supreme Court of trying to erase the word "woman" (which appears frequently in the ruling). The controversy snowballed when Quebec's National Assembly passed a unanimous motion tabled by Coalition Avenir Quebec Minister Martine Biron denouncing the court's use of the phrase.
"It's important that these debates happen respectfully and, above all, in an informed manner. People should at least read the rulings before criticizing them. We can see the damages that are caused when a judicial decision is reported erroneously, or out of context, for sensationalist reasons," Wagner said.
He said the saga around the ruling was an example of disinformation.
"An elected official read an article ... Their reading was wrong, and if the person had read properly, they would have noted that under no circumstances did the Supreme Court want to devalue the notion of a woman in Canada," he said without identifying the politician.
National Post cnardi@postmedia.com

Document 437 of 500

Working conditions for Canadian judges are 'deteriorating,' says chief justice of the Supreme Court

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/working-conditions-canadian-judges-are/docview/3064262818/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Working+conditions+for+Canadian+judges+are+%27deteriorating%2C%27+says+chief+justice+of+the+Supreme+Court&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Supreme Court Chief Justice Richard Wagner says it's "very difficult" to be a judge in Canada, that the job doesn't pay enough in some provinces and that conditions are "deteriorating" for magistrates across the country.
During an annual press conference with reporters Monday, Wagner called on provincial governments to increase the resources available to judges to help make the job more attractive to potential candidates and easier for current justices.
That could include a raise, particularly in provinces where cost-of-living concerns are hitting hardest, he suggested. Wagner is also head of the Canadian Judicial Council (CJC), which represents federally appointed judges.
"I'm told by some chief justices in the country, specifically in provinces where the cost of living is higher like B.C. and Ontario, that they have a hard time to attract candidates for the bench," Wagner said.
Federally appointed judges' salaries are set by the Judges Act and receive a cost of living boost yearly.
Currently, Superior Court, provincial Court of Appeal, Federal Court and Federal Court of Appeal judges make $396,700 annually, while their chief justices earn $435,000. Judges who live in northern territory receive an additional $12,000 allowance.
Supreme Court justices earn $472,400 yearly, while Chief Justice Wagner makes $510,000.
Wagner said that judges' wages and salary are one issue, but that working conditions for sitting justices are "very difficult" these days because they lack support in most provinces.
He cited a lack of administrative assistance for judges and increasingly obsolete technology used by courts. Judges and legal experts across the country have also denounced a lack of courtrooms, insufficient numbers of clerks and registry employees and a concerning number of judicial vacancies in recent years.
These issues are pushing some lawyers away from the judiciary, Wagner said.
"It has become very difficult for judges to work and it is known ... in the legal community. You will see some lawyers who will refuse to apply if only because of that," he told reporters.
"I think that judges have good conditions, I must say. I think we are privileged in a way, but (judges) are doing a very difficult task. For the last couple of years, we have seen the conditions deteriorate."
Wagner also said the CJC will soon launch a study into judges' health and well-being to better understand the challenges they face.
'Divorced from reality': Why Canadians are losing patience with public servants
Why Mitch Marner and Alberta have become big issues in a record-breaking Toronto byelection
Judicial vacancies are a key concern to Wagner. Last year, he penned a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau highlighting his deep concern with a growing number of vacancies (roughly 80 at the time) and the speed (or lack thereof) at which the Liberals were appointing judges.
On Monday, Wagner said he was encouraged by a wave of appointments by the government in recent months that has reduced vacancies to 57 but insisted more work still needs to be done.
"I would expect that the government would appoint judges in a timely fashion, given that they know, very often months before, that the position will become vacant in six months," he said.
"I saw some improvements and I am confident now that the situation can be corrected," he added.
Wagner also took an unusually harsh swipe at certain politicians, pundits and members of the media, whom he accused of reacting to Supreme Court of Canada rulings before reading them and then spreading "disinformation" about the decisions.
He was particularly irked about controversy around a criminal case decision about a sexual assault penned by Supreme Court of Canada Justice Sheila Martin earlier this year in which she used the term "person with a vagina."
Some politicians and columnists, including in National Post, accused the Supreme Court of trying to erase the word "woman" (which appears frequently in the ruling). The controversy snowballed when Quebec's National Assembly passed a unanimous motion tabled by CAQ Minister Martine Biron denouncing the Supreme Court's use of the phrase "person with a vagina."
"It's important that these debates happen respectfully and, above all, in an informed manner. People should at least read the rulings before criticizing them. We can see the damages that are caused when a judicial decision is reported erroneously, or out of context, for sensationalist reasons," Wagner said.
He said the saga around the decision penned by Martin was an example of disinformation.
"An elected official read an article ... that commented one of our decisions and interpreted it wrongly," he said without identifying the politician.
"Their reading was wrong, and if the person had read properly, they would have noted that under no circumstances did the Supreme Court want to devalue the notion of a woman in Canada."
Biron responded on social media Monday afternoon that she read the Supreme Court of Canada's ruling at the time but did not intend to pick a fight with the court.
"My role as Minister responsible for the Status of Women is to protect and defend the rights of women. I intend to take every opportunity to fulfil that duty," she wrote.
National Post

Document 438 of 500

Mitch Marner, electoral reform and Alberta are issues in a record-breaking Toronto byelection. Here's why

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mitch-marner-electoral-reform-alberta-are-issues/docview/3064220040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Mitch+Marner%2C+electoral+reform+and+Alberta+are+issues+in+a+record-breaking+Toronto+byelection.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - As nominations close Monday on the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection, an enduring election activism campaign has ensured it'll be one for the record books.
As of early Monday afternoon, 77 candidates are registered to contest the riding that has been held consistently by the Liberals for over three decades, which will easily break Winnipeg South Centre's record-breaking 48-name ballot during last year's federal byelection.
That election's 50-by-30 centimetre ballot was so large - nearly the size of a single page of the National Post - it required an amendment to the Canada Elections Act to legally allow it.
These swollen ballots come courtesy of the Longest Ballot Committee, an ongoing protest urging reforms to Canada's election laws, which Prime Minister Justin Trudeau vowed to fix during the 2015 election campaign.
"The longest ballot is a fun way to start important conversations about our democracy," read a written statement to the National Post from Kieran & Tomas Szuchewycz, the organizers behind the committee.
"Most voters recognize that politicians have too much skin in the game when it comes to deciding election rules. It's a straightforward conflict of interest, but MPs would prefer no one pointed this out."
Voters in the downtown Toronto riding head to the polls June 24.
All but five of the candidates running in Toronto-St. Paul's list Kieran Szuchewycz as their official agent.
Parties contesting the election include the Liberals, Greens, NDP, Marijuana, Centrist and Rhinoceros.
Among the committee's gripes are Trudeau's failed promise to do away with first-past-the-post (FTTP) elections, in which winning candidates are chosen based on whoever gets the most votes.
Proponents say other methods, including ranked ballots, produce results far more consistent with the will of the voters.
The first candidate officially registered for the byelection was Sébastien CoRhino, from Rimouski, QC, who despite being leader of the Rhinoceros Party chose instead to run as an independent, and graciously let caucus mate Sean Carson run under the party banner.
Activists load up ballot in Toronto-area byelection with 40 candidates to protest Trudeau
Long ballot activists planning to make short work of Toronto byelection
"Maybe it's the first time it's happened in history, a political leader who's not running for his party," CoRhino told the National Post.
"It gives you a lot of freedom to do and say whatever you want without (toeing) the party line."
Also appearing on the ballot is Donovan Eckstrom, from Grande Prairie, Alta., whose platform largely centres around promoting western Canadian ideals. He's also tweeted his ire about Toronto Maple Leafs forward Mitch Marner, who he says is "a bum."
"I believe in Alberta, I believe in what Alberta can bring, I believe Alberta is the best province," he told the National Post.
"To bring that Albertan spirit to Toronto - as some might say the centre of Canada - I think it's a powerful maneuver, and thanks to the Longest Ballot Committee, Torontonians now have a choice, which is simply sensational."
I think Marner is a bum! Get rid of him!
Vote Donovan Eckstrom in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection.#cdnpoli #TOStPauls
- Donovan Eckstrom (@DonovanEckstrom) May 29, 2024
Current election rules allow eligible candidates to contest elections from anywhere in Canada, even from those who don't reside within the riding.
Also on the ballot is Agnieszka Marszalek, a schoolteacher from Pickle Lake - a remote northern Ontario community about 500 km. north of Thunder Bay - who said her involvement in the campaign is to raise awareness of the problems in Canada's electoral system.
"We have loopholes that nobody fixes, and yet people complain about how our system is ineffective - then do something," she said.
"If more people become active participants in our political system, in our political life, then more things would actually be changed, and politicians would start looking at the interests of the citizens, not the lobbyists lining their pockets."
Liberal MP Carolyn Bennett, who was re-elected nine times during her time in office, resigned her seat in January and, the same day, was named Canada's ambassador to Denmark.
Meanwhile, the organizers of the protest say election reform in Canada isn't an impossible dream.
"Gerrymandering once existed in Canada, but then vanished the moment the job of drawing riding boundaries was taken out of the hands of politicians and passed to a non-partisan commission," the Szuchewyczs said in their statement.
"This lesson can and should be applied to the rest of our democracy. We believe an independent body made up of everyday Canadians would do a far better job delivering the fair, ethical, and robust democracy we voters deserve."

Document 439 of 500

Eric Kaufmann: Creating a post-woke society

Author: to, Special

Publication info: to, Special. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/eric-kaufmann-creating-post-woke-society/docview/3064077935/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Eric+Kaufmann%3A+Creating+a+post-woke+society&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=to%2C+Special&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a new book, Eric Kaufmann examines the rise of left-liberal extremism and discusses how to check this dominant hybrid ideology.
"The Third Awokening" revolves around the left-liberal anti-racism taboo of the mid-1960s. Like the big bang, this was a cosmic event; its logic has been progressively expanding, defining our social universe. It has introduced a zone of unbounded identitarian sacredness around race - a form of social kryptonite which irradiated anyone standing in its way. This powerful magic was borrowed by the feminist and later LGBTQ+ movements, weaponized by the revolutionary left and stretched to new frontiers of microscopic and confected emotional grievance. Along the way, it has eroded freedom, truth and excellence while vandalizing cherished national identities and undermining social cohesion. Until the taboo is reformed into a proportionate norm like any other, cultural socialism will remain a dominant force in polite society.
While there is no question that the energy behind cancel culture has peaked, my view is that, far from disappearing, the underlying ideology is likely to maintain or increase its power in the years ahead. Its wings have been clipped, but the core myths and symbols from which it springs remain intact. This seedbed stands ready to nourish another moral panic the next time a white policeman kills an unarmed Black man or a whistleblower exposes a high-profile sexual predator.
Young people, especially young women, are much less tolerant of speech which offends historically disadvantaged race, gender and sexual identity groups than older generations. This is especially true of gen Z (zoomers) and millennials educated at the best universities. As they become the median employee in elite institutions and attain positions of power, they are likely to upend the country's classical liberal and patriotic creed. The senior liberals who are behind the modest anti-woke correction in the mainstream media will have left the scene as part of the inevitable generational turnover of institutional leadership.
While radical ideas like critical race theory or gender ideology have gained ground, they only succeeded because they resonated with an established left-liberal hypersensitivity around identity issues. This attention to the demand side, or consumption, of ideas, is missing from many books which focus only on the radicals and the ideas they produce.
Left-liberals, not revolutionary radicals, were also responsible for a number of woke innovations. Like water gradually heating to boiling point, their sensibility evolved incrementally from the '70s through a process of therapeutic "concept creep" in which ever-finer microaggressions came to be declared traumatic. This is how we evolved from "crippled" to "handicapped" to "disabled" to "differently abled." It also accounts for the ratcheting extremism of a series of U.S. Supreme Court decisions between the '60s and '80s.
Throughout the book, I show how the left-liberal majority in cultural institutions like universities is ambivalent about cancel culture but attracted to diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) policies such as diversity statements or broad definitions of harassment, which drive cancel culture. While fear is an important aspect of conformity - as it was during the McCarthy era - left-liberals today, like radicals, worship the totems of equal outcomes and harm protection for minorities. They accept that this is the north star toward which morality must orient.
This renders modern liberals powerless in the face of radicals to their left. Like pious Muslims trying to argue against Islamic fundamentalists who point to passages in the Qur'an to authorize their violent global jihad, left-liberals are tied into a common moral framework with the fundamentalists, making it nearly impossible to resist their claims. While Patrick Deneen, Yoram Hazony and other post-liberals believe that the entire philosophy of liberalism is to blame, I believe liberal ideas can be salvaged if today's left-liberals come to understand, question and control their "minorities good, majorities threatening" emotional reflex.
In the final part of the book, I outline a "12-point plan" for rolling back progressive extremism in our institutions to re-balance cultural equality with freedom and national community. Most of my proposed reforms are directed toward conservatives because it is only when they succeed that moderate liberals can win the internal battles against radicals - such as the Democratic politicians known as the "Squad" - who influence the cultural tone in their coalition.
I urge conservatives to use legislation and executive action at federal and state levels to intervene in public bodies and schools. The goal is to enforce political neutrality and introduce new conditions on public funding that require recipients to uphold political nondiscrimination and free speech. Legislation and executive orders are needed to proactively dismantle the DEI apparatus and ethos of the public sector and school system. While the battle of ideas is the only way to ultimately prevail, it will take decades to change public attitudes among younger generations. And while lawfare can protect dissenters' speech rights in some contexts, this is expensive, stressful and can be gamed by organizations. Ultimately, most will want to avoid the hassle, choosing instead to self-censor.
Conservatives must upgrade the back end of their operation, relying not just on election victories, but also on mobilizing and organizing between elections. Regardless of what you think of the National Rifle Association, pro-life movement, Straussians or the Federalist Society, they show that conservatives can be focused and effective. Nurturing a pipeline of elite talent, even where the right is vastly outnumbered - as in Ivy League law schools - is a vital task. For at present, Republican administrations (or conservative ones in other western countries) lack the cadres of qualified appointees necessary to repopulate the bureaucracy and public bodies that have drifted left over time. Politicians lack the grounding in conservative and classical liberal ideas to help them resist the inevitable allure of acceding to progressives in institutions.
The goal is nothing short of a revolution in ethos, from a leftist focus on equity and diversity to a neutral and depoliticized public service concentrating on excellence and serving the country. The cultural left has spent several decades attacking meritocracy because outcomes are not equal across identity groups. They have undermined national narratives and symbols in the name of multiculturalism because the past, like the present, is not equal. People must understand that the future of our civilization is at stake. Changing the flag flying over public buildings from the Stars and Stripes to the Chinese star is, at one level, a trivial act, but none of us question its importance. Why, then, is it so difficult for many to grasp why flying the Progress Pride or Black Lives Matter flag is so subversive?
Reform of public schools must be the highest priority. Studies show that school indoctrination really works and is casting tomorrow's leaders and voters to be champions of DEI. Conservative governments need to purge woke politicization from the classroom, making this an overriding goal. School choice can do little more than nip at the edges of the problem. As the example of Twitter/X in relation to would-be alternatives like Gab and Parler shows, reforming the mainstream is more effective than starting separate institutions. This is true for all but the most competitive sectors (such as online podcast media), with most spheres of society involving varying degrees of market power which raise stiff barriers to new entrants while entrenching the power of established players.
Government regulation, not market competition, is therefore vital to taming the power of woke. No politician understands this better than Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis, who is the policy leader in this regard. However, his activism is an awkward fit with many in the conservative political world. It puzzles fiscal conservatives such as George W. Bush, Nikki Haley or Britain's Boris Johnson, whose political instincts were forged in the '80s during the Cold War and stagflation - or by writers formed in this crucible. They are primarily oriented against government power and have been only too willing to submit to cultural left speech policing and affirmative action in order to placate liberals in the media and well-heeled donors.
As a result, conservative politicians have heretofore provided little resistance to equity-diversity (read: discrimination to achieve equal outcomes) or inclusion (read: control over freedom of speech). Both the public and an important tranche of conservative intellectuals have been ignored by conservative career politicians. This will have to change if we want our institutions to better reflect the mores of the wider society. This new paradigm is fully in accord with liberalism, but is about defending the liberty of citizens from institutions and private threats more than from executive government. It harks back to an older liberal tradition rooted in the works of Thomas Hobbes and John Locke. It recognizes that government is accountable and transparent in a way that institutions are not.
While culture is partly downstream of politics, lasting change can only come from the battle of ideas. The lineaments of the culture complex that nourishes both left-liberals and radicals must, to paraphrase postmodernists, be decentered. So long as our value system is based around the "minorities good, majority bad" reflex, a catastrophizing "fascist scare" approach to cultural conservatism, and race, sex and LGBTQ+ taboos, nothing will change. We must return to where it all began, planing our totalizing taboos down to proportional norms like any other.
This will allow a new, resilient, post-woke society to arise that will lift majority and minority alike. The push for more equal results and better harm protection for minorities has brought considerable benefit to our world. But it has overreached, damaging human flourishing. Just as we defeated communism but absorbed some of its insights to forge a mixed welfare-state form of capitalism, our task today is to defeat cultural socialism and restore cultural wealth while accepting that some attention to equal outcomes and psychological harm protection for minorities is part of the good society.
Excerpted with permission from "The Third Awokening: A 12-Point Plan for Rolling Back Progressive Extremism" by Eric Kaufmann.

Document 440 of 500

Ontario tennis coach worries new transgender athlete policy unfair to girls

Author: Kirkey, Sharon

Publication info: Kirkey, Sharon. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-tennis-coach-worries-new-transgender/docview/3064077410/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario+tennis+coach+worries+new+transgender+athlete+policy+unfair+to+girls&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kirkey%2C+Sharon&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Junior competitive tennis coach Michael Paduch is aware that he's wading into an emotionally charged debate by questioning a new policy that allows transgender players to compete against other girls, "without restriction."
"It's a complex issue; it's an issue a lot of people care about," Paduch said. "But I have a keen interest in ensuring that female tennis in Canada is not affected negatively by policies that are put in place."
The certified tennis instructor is referring to an email that recently popped into his inbox informing Ontario Tennis Association (OTA) member clubs of a newly implemented Transgender Athlete Participation Policy for all OTA-sanctioned events.
Under the policy, players may participate in the gender category in which they self-identify, "without restriction," and with no requirement for gender-reassignment interventions, such as hormone therapy, nor any requirement to disclose transgender identity or history to the OTA or any of its coaches, staff or officials.
The eligibility rules stand for league and recreational levels, as well as provincial competitive levels.
Paduch, a certified tennis instructor who has been coaching girls' and boys' competitive tennis for 15 years, taking some players, a number of them girls, to the national level, wondered why he and other coaches hadn't been more broadly consulted on a transgender policy that's less restrictive than the rules adopted by the sport's global governing body.
Under the International Tennis Federation's (ITF) policy, a male-to-female transgender player who wishes to compete against biological females must provide a written and signed declaration that her gender is female; must demonstrate to the ITF's satisfaction that her serum testosterone has been below a maximum limit continuously for at least 12 months; and must be "ready, willing and able to continue to keep it below that level for so long as she continues to compete in the female category of competition."
Paduch, who also is a certified wheelchair tennis coach for those with disabilities, is aware of the risks of voicing concerns about a hot-button issue most people are reluctant to speak about publicly.
"I don't think there is anybody in the coaching community who would not agree with the statement that tennis should be as inclusive as possible," he said.
However, "We accepted a much more watered down, much weaker policy than the ITF's, potentially at the expense of girls," he said. He said he's not anti-trans, or opposed to trans athletes competing in female categories, but believes there should be some hormonal restrictions on their participation. "I believe in policies that are built based on sports science," he said.
"In theory, right now anybody who is completely fully male in their teens in Ontario could compete against girls and could be winning titles based on this policy."
It is the latest example of how sports bodies in Canada, for the most part, have adopted more liberal rules around trans inclusion than a growing number of international federations governing sports.
The OTA guidelines "are intended to ensure, insofar as possible, that transgender athletes are not excluded from any opportunity to participate in sporting competitions and are provided a sporting environment free of discrimination," the policy reads.
But Paduch said they offer an unfair competitive advantage to players born male who register in girls' and women's categories based upon self-identified gender status alone.
The Ontario Tennis Association's policy is virtually identical to one implemented last year by Tennis Canada, which also recommends allowing athletes, including high-performance athletes, to compete without restrictions in the gender category consistent with their gender identity.
Tennis Québec, by contrast, requires trans athletes wishing to compete in Tennis Québec-sanctioned tournaments in the category of their new gender to provide a change of sex designation certificate, issued by a Quebec civil registry office.
"In the case of Ontario, we have not done that," Paduch said. "It is essentially an honour-based system, and we all know honour-based systems tend to be ripe for abuse or misrepresentations."
The policy adopted by the world governing body of tennis is also a more "science-based policy that seems to have checks and balances in place to ensure that girls in particular are not affected negatively by participation of fully hormonal males in the teenage years," said Paduch, who is also an ITF tournament director.
"At that stage of development, a fully hormonal male who declares themselves a woman has a significant physiological advantage in terms of heart size, density of muscles and ability to perform," he said.
"Once you get into the teenage years, girls are just not able to keep up."
"I don't think there is anybody in the coaching community who would not agree with the statement that tennis should be as inclusive as possible."
Athletes who want to participate in competitions and games sanctioned by the International Tennis Federation must comply with ITF rules. Yet in order to gain that international level of play, "you have to be exposed to high-level competition," said Paduch, who played varsity tennis competitively in Poland, where he grew up.
"There is a theoretical possibility that (biological girls) will be prevented from reaching those high levels of girls' competition if there is an increasing number of hormonally stronger former males taking those spots from girls," Paduch said.
The Ontario Tennis Association and Tennis Canada did not respond to several requests for an interview before deadline.
But the debate has become a flashpoint in Canada. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who has pledged to create biological-female-only sports leagues for women and girls, has criticized the "unfair disadvantage" young female athletes face when competing against those born biologically male. Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre has said female sport categories, and change rooms, should be off-limits to "biological males."
The Canadian Centre for Ethics in Sport maintains that athletes should be permitted to participate in the gender category of their choosing. A scientific review published by the Ottawa-based non-profit in 2021 concluded that trans sport policies "use arbitrary bounds that are not evidence based" and that "available evidence indicates trans women who have undergone testosterone suppression have no clear biological advantages over cis women in elite sport."
But other research suggests that testosterone suppression has only a modest effect on diminishing muscle mass and strength.
Depending on the sport, "the performance gap between males and females becomes significant at puberty," often amounting to 10 to 50 per cent, added the authors of the review, published in the journal Sports Medicine.
It's not known how many trans athletes are entering tennis competitions. The sport was the first pro sport to open to transgender athletes, when Renée Richards successfully sued to play at the 1977 U.S. Open after undergoing sex-reassignment surgery.
Renee Richards, left, with Martina Navratilova, after Navratilova was inducted into the International Tennis Hall of Fame on July 15, 2000.
In a 2022 interview with the New York Post, Chris Evert, who faced Richards six times, winning every match, described struggling to beat Richards when Richards was 43 and Evert was ranked number one in the world. "The wingspan. The size of the heart. The size of the lungs. The speed. The fast twitch muscles. The testosterone," Evert said. "There's just everything pointing to the fact that men are quicker, stronger, etc. than women - especially after puberty."
Richards would go on to coach tennis legend Martina Navratilova to several Grand Slam wins, but Navratilova herself has faced fierce criticism for posting comments on social media that say allowing trans females who haven't undergo sex reassignment to compete in women's categories is "not fair" and "not right."' After World Athletics, the international governing body for track and field, last year voted to exclude male-to-female trans athletes who have experienced puberty from female World Rankings competition, Navratilova wrote in Britain's The Times that the "best idea" would be an "open category" for trans athletes.
Paduch is concerned that, without "a medical aspect of certification," the new tennis policy could have a profound impact on girls' tennis. "We can't just be inclusive at the expense of girls," he said.
National Post
