Document 441 of 500

Kelly McParland: Doug Ford remains true to his hoserhood, rushes beer into corner stores

Author: McParland, Kelly

Publication info: McParland, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kelly-mcparland-doug-ford-remains-true-his/docview/3063327732/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Kelly+McParland%3A+Doug+Ford+remains+true+to+his+hoserhood%2C+rushes+beer+into+corner+stores&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McParland%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some 40-plus years ago a seminal event took place in Toronto when Rick Moranis and Dave Thomas, in their guise as SCTV's Bob and Doug McKenzie, staged a Hoser Parade through the streets of the city.
It was a funny idea that celebrated the notion of hoserhood and its fixation on toques, hockey sticks and an excessive fondness for beer. What essentially began as some guys in a car with a bunch of beer quickly swelled to unexpected proportions as thousands of Torontonians jammed the sidewalks for an impromptu party.
I can't say for sure whether Doug Ford, then a teenager from the blue collar suburb of Rexdale on the cusp of legal drinking age, was there that day warbling the strange McKenzie-inspired anthem of the Great White North, an off-the-wall ditty like the mating call of some weird snow bird - "a roo-koo koo-rooooo!" - but he unquestionably absorbed the vibe. Half a lifetime later as Ontario's premier he still holds dear the idea of a province devoted to nothing so much as ready access to cheap beer.
It's entirely possible he'd accept that characterization. The Average Joe routine was always the shtick of the Ford brothers - Doug and his ill-fated brother, former Toronto mayor Rob. The Fords know where they come from. Yes they have money, but they don't go in for pretensiousness or puffery. No fancy-pants Liberal academics here. Every summer they hold Fordfest, a giant get-together with free burgers, hot dogs, music and rides, open to anyone who feels like turning up.
The premier's sense of himself would explain the revelation that his Progressive Conservatives intend to pay $225 million to a group of international brewers to break their monopoly on beer sales and open the market to corner stores and supermarkets. According to the official announcement, Ford's government "is delivering on its commitment to expand the province's alcohol beverage marketplace to increase choice and convenience for shoppers."
Beer has long been a part of the Ford oeuvre. In his first campaign as leader in 2018 he ran on a slogan of "buck-a-beer" and a pledge to repeal the province's Depression-era drinking laws and free it from the clutches of the billion-dollar multinationals. He'd probably have done so sooner if someone hadn't worked out the painful cost of cancelling the generous contract approved by the previous Liberal government. Blowing that much money so soon after entering office would be a bad look for a party that vowed to halt wasteful spending and get the budget under control.
Six years later, Ford is suddenly willing to pay up anyway, a debatable move given that the contract ends in 19 months anyway, meaning everything Ford is doing now could be done for free a little later. If he expected hoser Ontario to rise up and shower him with praise and beer suds he's been disappointed: it took about eight seconds to calculate $225 million would go a long way towards remedying a serious nursing shortage plaguing the province, or do other good works. It's more than two-thirds the amount Ontario is to get from Ottawa in a deal to alleviate a critical housing shortage, an issue many would view as of somewhat greater import than the need for beer in corner stores.
Several guesses have been offered up as to why the premier suddenly chose to move on beer. One is that he genuinely believes Ontarians love their brewskis and will thank him for making them easier to obtain. Another guess is that he's keen to call an early election before rookie Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie can gain any momentum, and thinks the beer vote will be of assistance. A third, proffered by unnamed sources cited by the Toronto Star, is that Ford fears federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre will win the next election and fulfill his pledge to control spending, possibly including a reduction of transfers to Ontario.
If that last one is true it would be decidedly ironic. The original Ford brand was one of tighfisted management. The 2018 Tory campaign promised a "line-by-line" audit of Liberal spending habits and a laser-like focus on returning to balanced budgets. Instead the PCs have delivered a series of deficits and growing debt, culminating in March's unveiling of the biggest-spending budget in Ontario history, complete with a $9.8 billion shortfall.
The Fordian reluctance to listen to know-it-alls is a big reason the Tories have stumbled into some doozies in the mistake department. There was the badly bungled plan to slice off hunks of Toronto's protective greenbelt and deliver them to wealthy developers, for which he later said he was "very, very sorry." Or his artless attempt to instal his police pal Ron Taverner as head of the Ontario Provincial Police over other more likely alternatives, which went awry because Taverner - great guy, Ron - lacked the stature, qualifications or history. Or his death-bed promise to former Mississauga mayor Hazel McCallion - a classic Fordian - to blow up a regional government she disliked, only to be forced to break his pledge when the other regional members went ballistic.
The premier has survived all that and more, helped by a weak set of opposition leaders and, perhaps, his willingness to take the rap for errors, apologize profusely and move on. Imagine where the federal Liberals might be if Justin Trudeau were ever capable of admitting a mistake.
It's still possible he'll have to walk back the beer plan, if the fancypants critics succeed in ganging up on him. He also might get away with it. His Tories remain well ahead of both opposition parties in the polls and easily won two recent byelections. Crombie, who succeeded McCallion as Mississauga mayor, has yet to gain much traction or a seat in the legislature, and is already being pummelled by Conservative attack ads. Asked point blank on a radio talk show whether he's planning an early election Ford pledged "one hundred per cent we are not having an election this fall, this summer," but avoided answering when pressed on chances of a vote before the scheduled date of June 2026.
If forced to retreat, my guess is Ford would roll his eyes, head home to the suburbs, have a few "pops" as Don Cherry, another vintage Fordian, would say, and wonder what happened to the good old days when you could grab a two-four, find a few buddies and just enjoy life. A roo-koo koo-rooooooo!
National Post

Subject: Beer; Negative campaigning

Identifier / keyword: years; seminal; event; place; toronto; moranis

Title: Kelly McParland: Doug Ford remains true to his hoserhood, rushes beer into corner stores

Publication title: National Post (Online); Toronto

Document 442 of 500

Letters: Political leaders are giving Canadians a license to hate

Author: Letters

Publication info: Letters. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-political-leaders-are-giving-canadians/docview/3063327694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Political+leaders+are+giving+Canadians+a+license+to+hate&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Letters&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Canada Accepts Hamas Accounts Without Evidence, Tristin Hopper; No Peace If Hamas Survives, Casey Babb; MPs Advised To Lock Office Doors, Avoid Meetings, all, May 30; PM, Ford Denounce 'Vile Acts' Of Antisemitism, May 31.
Canadian government officials, such as Foreign Minister Mélanie Joly, regularly accept Hamas's accusations that Israel is targeting civilians as accurate news that's worthy of repeating. And when evidence proves those accounts to be biased or outright lies - as in the case of the many civilian deaths in a camp near Rafah, which appear to have been caused by exploding munitions hidden in the area and not the Israeli airstrike itself - Joly does not retract her accusations.
One might also note that neither she nor our prime minister has ever declared unequivocal support for Israel's right to respond to the October 7 attack and defend itself against the ongoing existential threat of Hamas, a declared genocidal, and inhumane, terrorist organization operating next door to it.
With Canada's most important voices speaking only negatively about Israel, is it any wonder that many in Canada see it as a license to protest against Israel, while also threatening Canada's Jewish community?
Ron Hoffman, Toronto.
Yet another violent attack on a Jewish school has taken place in Montreal, one of a series of shootings in the past months aimed to intimidate and terrorize the Jewish community. But should we be surprised at this latest violent incident?
Antisemitism has been tolerated on our university campuses and on our streets under the guise of free speech. An effigy referencing concentration camp prisoners was hung at McGill's Roddick Gates. Yet the police are instructed to stand by during this and other hateful protests and not intervene to avoid escalating tensions. This would not be the strategy for any other community; action would be taken immediately and decisively.
When antisemitic words inevitably lead to violent actions, our politicians issue statements of condemnation and sympathy, but little is done other than to step up security around Jewish institutions. And so, the cycle of hate continues and grows larger. The history of genocide has proven that when hate is normalized in a society, it emboldens groups to commit acts of violence with no concern for the repercussions. Is this where we're headed?
It's time for Montreal Mayor Valérie Plante and the city's police force to hear the warning bells and act before it's too late. They must push for more education to prevent ignorance and hate, they must intervene during hateful incidents and they must end the normalization of antisemitism. The next shooting at a school could be deadly for both the victims and the city.
Marcy Bruck, Montreal.
Writer Casey Babb is correct: if Palestine is given statehood during this war, the "long-term consequences ... would be remarkably bad for international security." If Hamas is rewarded for its guerrilla warfare, it will empower terrorist groups and dictators everywhere.
This is not just a Middle East problem - it is a global problem. It can't be contained with border walls or immigration restrictions. Like climate change, it affects us all. It's not just happening in places where Jews live, work, learn and pray, it's impacting everyone. MPs have been advised to lock the doors of their constituency offices and avoid taking meetings without an appointment, after the notice, "Rafah is burning, Toronto will too," was painted in an MP's office and safety concerns due to death threats skyrocket.
It's time for everyone to pull their heads out of the sand!
S. R. Isenberg, Vaughan, Ont.
Our divisive PM
Re: Power Of Ugly Needs A Rethink, Kelly McParland, May 25.
Conflating what Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says with what Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says, as columnist Kelly McParland does, is wrong. Critiquing the government in power is central to the duties of the Opposition, which is made all the more easy by the Trudeau Liberals' endless stream of poor policies, conduct and results.
From Day 1 as prime minister, Trudeau has consistently disrespected and divided Canadians for partisan political gain. Poilievre criticizes Trudeau for the many Liberal government decisions that hurt Canadians. Trudeau responds with outright lies or smears based on flimsy or non-existent pretenses. Justin Trudeau is the most divisive and negative politician in Canada today. Second place isn't even close.
Gary Krieger, Toronto.
Hamilton's 'mistake'
Re: Courts Can't Save Jama, Colby Cosh, May 30.
Conspiracy theorist, spewer of anti-Israel sentiment, pro-Palestinian organizer of Israeli Apartheid Week at McMaster University ... do these sound like the qualifications for someone aspiring to serve as an Ontario MPP? To think that Sarah Jama was the best candidate to replace former NDP leader Andrea Horwath in Hamilton Centre is an insult to all worthy public servants.
During her short tenure, her only interest appears to be self-promotion. Let us hope that the voters of Hamilton Centre have learned a sad lesson and will not make the same mistake again.
Susan Silverman, Toronto.
No responsibility
Re: Speaker Insists His Decisions Unbiased, May 29.
Have we reached the ultimate absurdity in the concept of accountability? The Speaker of the House says that he is not responsible for the content of an invitation sent out with his name on it, to a gathering in his riding, put on by his riding association, for his benefit. No, he says, it was someone else. He had nothing to do with it. Why, he didn't even know what was in the invitation.
There was a time when a U.S. president put a sign on his desk that read, "The buck stops here." Every day, we hold CEOs and generals and managers responsible for what goes on under their watch. But not, it seems, the Speaker. It seems that people do all sorts of things for him, and he pays no attention to them, so he can't be held responsible. Shame.
Tom Curran, Prince Edward County, Ont.
Climate questions
Re: What The IPCC Actually Says About Climate Change And Droughts, Roger Pielke Jr., May 30.
Given what Roger Pielke Jr. told a U.S. Senate committee about what the IPCC actually says, I have numerous questions.
Why do the remedies proposed by politicians include arbitrarily cancelling many of our rights and freedoms? Why are the most vociferous proponents of drastic climate laws, like U.S. President Joe Biden and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, all seemingly exempt from these restrictions? Why is our out-of-control global population not considered a factor? And lastly, why is any person or group who dares to question these political edicts viciously attacked?
This is obviously not about "science," it's about control.
Carol Joseph, Burlington, Ont.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish life & ethics; Politics; Prime ministers; Climate change; Genocide

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; United States--US; Canada; Israel

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: canada; accepts; hamas; accounts; without; https

Document 449 of 500

Chris Selley: I miss the old, useful Bloc Québécois

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chris-selley-i-miss-old-useful-bloc-québécois/docview/3063027943/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Chris+Selley%3A+I+miss+the+old%2C+useful+Bloc+Qu%26eacute%3Bb%26eacute%3Bcois&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it came to sentencing Alexandre Bissonnette, who slaughtered six worshippers at the Islamic Cultural Centre in Quebec City in 2017, some Quebec nationalists were among the very small crowd insisting that 40 years without parole was unconscionably long. The sentence was permitted - and was subsequently handed down - under a Stephen Harper-era law, that has since been struck down by the Supreme Court
"Canada is neither the United States, with the death penalty and 150-year prison sentences, nor one of the countries where many Muslim immigrants are born and where murderers and political opponents are killed and tortured," the late journalist and nationalist icon Denise Bombardier wrote (in French, in the Journal de Montréal) of some Quebec Muslims' public support for the maximum possible penalty. "The Muslim community in Quebec can express its anger, its worry, but if resentment overcomes (them) ... they risk marginalizing themselves and becoming allergic, in some way, to Quebec society."
"The reaction of this small community, two years later, leaves us little room to want to take these people in our arms and comfort them," Journal columnist Lise Ravary (who, to be fair, is not the same level of ultra-nationalist Bombardier was) concurred "We cannot force the courts to pass sentences based on the victims' culture of origin."
It was all quite astonishing. A Research Co. poll conducted in April suggests (not at all surprisingly) that 52 per cent of Quebecers at least somewhat support "reinstating the death penalty for murder in Canada." It's not often proudly Québecois commentators and the federally appointed judiciary find themselves together, marooned on an ice floe, arguing with completely unearned confidence against well-documented public opinion.
That soft-on-crime incident came to mind while considering the Bloc Québécois' latest stunt; namely, a proposed tough-on-crime law that would use the notwithstanding clause to exempt the most serious and violent crimes from the Jordan decision that the Supreme Court got dead rightin 2016: If the courts can't manage to try you for a serious crime within 30 months - subject to various exceptions for extenuating circumstances - then your fundamental right to an expedient trial has been violated, and the Crown must concede defeat.
(This isn't some newfangled faddish "right," just to be clear. It's in Magna Carta.)
One can imagine Bloc strategists sketching out their genius on a whiteboard.
"On Quebec's behalf, we wish to legitimize usage of the notwithstanding clause. So let's force the Liberals to defend letting accused murderers, rapists and child pornographers off Scot-free! Ha ha!"
"The Conservatives might be a threat to us in the next federal election. If we force Pierre Poilievre's hand on his support for using the notwithstanding clause, we can turn the federalists against each other to our benefit! Ha ha!"
The notion of lowering standards for the most serious trials isn't new; notably, it has been suggested as a way of convicting more people of sexual assault, which for various reasons can be very difficult to prove. But it's always a really terrible idea. The last place you want to start chipping away at time-tested standards of proof is where the maximum legal penalty applies to the accused.
In Canada, moreover, it's easy to imagine where such a law might lead: Longer and longer and longer delays.
As it stands, the federal and provincial governments have suffered remarkably little for failing to adequately resource the justice system. When Justin Trudeau finally wanders off into the sunset, I doubt many commentators will mention his borderline-psychotic refusal to fill judicial vacancies, now at a record high, as one of his signature failures. Similarly, I doubt Ontario Premier Doug Ford will be much remembered for failing to staff courthouses such that they can hold as many trials as need holding.
I have no idea why politicians don't suffer for these failures: Canadians seem plenty concerned about baddies getting out of jail too early, but hardly at all about baddies who never even come to trial because the justice system, like so much else in this country, is broken. Whatever the reason, give politicians a chance to break it more, and you can bet they'll oblige.
Once upon a time, this is the sort of thing I would have expected the Bloc to inveigh against. Under Lucien Bouchard's and Gilles Duceppe's leadership, the Bloc Québécois often served a very useful role in the House of Commons. When the Liberals, Conservatives and NDP inevitably lost themselves up their own bungholes, a Bloc MP was often ready with a pertinent question about something that actually mattered: forestry, or fisheries, or criminal justice, or military procurement.
Often the questions mattered mostly or exclusively to Quebec, but at least they mattered to someone. Representing one's constituents is after all the whole idea of Parliament. Knowing it could never alone form government, and having no set-in-stone ideology other than separatism itself, allowed the Bloc a freedom in Parliament that other parties don't have. Under the current leader Yves-Francois Blanchet, it's just another smirking stunt factory.
National Post
cselley@postmedia.com
Get more deep-dive National Post political coverage and analysis in your inbox with the Political Hack newsletter, where Ottawa bureau chief Stuart Thomson and political analyst Tasha Kheiriddin get at what's really going on behind the scenes on Parliament Hill every Wednesday and Friday, exclusively for subscribers. Sign up here
Chris Selley: A tale of two completely screwed-up Crown corporations

Title: Chris Selley: I miss the old, useful Bloc Québécois

Document 450 of 500

'They're wrong': Ontario finance minister defends new budget amid objections across the political divide

Author: Blaff, Ari David

Publication info: Blaff, Ari David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theyre-wrong-ontario-finance-minister-defends-new/docview/3063008587/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27They%27re+wrong%27%3A+Ontario+finance+minister+defends+new+budget+amid+objections+across+the+political+divide&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Blaff%2C+Ari+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy says critics have unfairly attacked the province's 2024 budget.
'Well, they're wrong," the minister told the National Post editorial board in late May, "and I'll tell you why."
"We are committed to a path to balance. I believe strongly in balancing the books," Bethlenfalvy said. The minister blamed the province's prolonged deficit on stagnating revenues "because the economy is slow."
Last year, the minister pledged the province was on a pathway to balancing its books by 2024 and even projected a $200-million surplus. However, in late March, Bethlenfalvy revealed that Ontario's forthcoming budget would more than triple its deficit from $3 billion to nearly $10 billion.
"We're not immune from global forces in the economic environment, so our revenues are down and the costs for many things are up, including for governments," Bethlenfalvy told reporters following his presentation of the budget at Queen's Park in late March. "You can either put on the brakes, or you can keep going forward, supporting the infrastructure spend, supporting the economy, supporting workers, and that's the way we've chosen."
The Ontario finance ministry's plan included increased funding packages earmarked for housing, health care, infrastructure and education. Many conservatives expressed dismay that Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government failed to live up to its earlier promises. Meanwhile, Ontario Liberals and New Democrats assailed the budget for failing to properly commit to revitalizing the province.
"What we got today just goes to show how deeply out of touch and out of ideas Doug Ford's Conservatives are," Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles said. "It's half-measures after half-measures and, you know, really at the end of the day, we're spending more and getting less at the end of the day from this government."
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie called it "a do-nothing budget," adding, "it's not worth the paper it's printed on."
Bethlenfalvy told the Post editorial board such criticism failed to account for the complexity of Ontario's current economic picture.
"We're the only major jurisdiction that has a path to balance," the minister said, pointing to Quebec, British Columbia and the federal government in Ottawa that he argued were on the wrong financial track.
"I can't control revenues, we can just put in the conditions," Bethlenfalvy explained. "Our expenditures, I think, are very reasonable given that we have a growing population. I think we're doing very well. We had over a million-people increase in the last two years; we have an aging population; and our big components of spending are health care, education and social services. So I think we're doing very well."
Bethlenfalvy reiterated his advocacy for better infrastructure, pointing to his meeting with federal Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland in December, alongside other provincial finance ministers from across the country.
"I'm a long-term thinker. We gotta think through (an economic) cycle. You know, interest rates will abate, and financiers will come back in and that doesn't mean we slow down investing in infrastructure; investing in the conditions to building more housing," Bethlenfalvy told the Post.
"As much as I want to balance the books today, I'm equally focused on building the infrastructure - not just the housing - but also, how are you gonna move people? You know, we've got four subway (construction projects) going on at the same time, we're $30 billion invested in building highways, $70 billion for subways and public transit. The schools, were $16 billion for building schools, building health care hospitals."
When asked whether he or the Ford government had any interest in expanding the province's private health-care options, the minister demurred.
"I don't think there's a national consensus for that," he said. "I don't see any major federal parties campaigning" for that. "If someone's got a better idea, sure, there could be a conversation on that. But right now, we're certainly in Ontario focused on the pooling of risk and getting better outcomes and respecting taxpayers' money as we do it."
"I got an appetite for a lot of things; I'm not sure that would be the one I would lean into. But you'd have to ask the premier and the health and care minister."
Randall Denley: Doug Ford abandons fiscal responsibility
Ontario's budget deficit ballooning to $9.8B next year, almost double its projection
Bethlenfalvy spoke about his start in politics by recalling his family's flight from Europe and the Second World War in the mid-1940s and his earlier years in Montreal.
"I was just very grateful for what Canada gave them. I remember being a young kid saying, 'Well, what a great country.' 'Cause I heard all the horror stories of war. I became a federalist very early on and was very upset when Parti Québécois won in 1976," he said.
"I think at that point, I became more active in politics. I was part of the 1980 referendum for the 'No' side and then when (Brian) Mulroney ran for the (federal conservative) leadership in '83, I volunteered for that and so became a conservative at a young age."
The memory of his mother's best friend, who similarly left Hungary but emigrated to Venezuela instead, left an imprint on Bethlenfalvy. "That always impacted me. That two countries who kind of had the resources and the opportunity; one went one way, and the other went the other way. Why is that?"

I miss the old, useful Bloc Québécois

Author: Selley, Chris

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-miss-old-useful-bloc-québécois/docview/3062956347/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=I+miss+the+old%2C+useful+Bloc+Qu%26eacute%3Bb%26eacute%3Bcois&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 452 of 500

Workers on edge as immigration window closes; Labour;raised bar for residency in canada could force thousands to leave country

Author: Naimul Karim

Publication info: Naimul Karim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/workers-on-edge-as-immigration-window-closes/docview/3062956228/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Workers+on+edge+as+immigration+window+closes%3B+Labour%3Braised+bar+for+residency+in+canada+could+force+thousands+to+leave+country&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.1&amp;au=Naimul+Karim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fatemah Ahmedi* feels like a loser.
The 32-year-old web designer quit her job in Iran and essentially restarted her career in 2018 when she decided to spend her life's savings to attend school in Canada. In the past six years, she studied, worked during the pandemic-induced labour shortage and paid her taxes with the hopes of becoming a permanent resident one day.
But with her work permit set to expire in December and the conditions to become a permanent resident a lot tougher than when she first entered Canada, or even a year ago, the Toronto resident faces the "unfair" prospect of returning to Iran by year-end.
"(This phase) actually feels like a big waste in my life," she said. "A waste of my money, a waste in my mental health. I invested a lot in this country. Going back without getting anything makes me feel like a loser."
About 500 kilometres away from Ahmedi, in a rural town in Ontario, Dinesh Chandra* faces an even tougher situation.
The 29-year-old, who came to Canada from India as a student about five years ago, had to quit working as a cybersecurity analyst in February since his work permit ended in March.
Today, he delivers pizza for a store that's owned by his friend and earns cash under the table.
"I think I earn as much as I used to pay in taxes every month while working as a cybersecurity analyst," he said. "This is a drastic change in my life."
Chandra, whose parents took out a number of loans to send him to Canada, said he has worked on projects that helped prevent hacking attempts against Canadian financial institutions and saved hundreds of thousands of dollars'worth of client investments from hackers.
While he contributed to the economy, he never utilized "a single government benefit," even when he got laid off, he said.
Chandra feels "embarrassed" at the possibility of heading back to India without gaining permanent residency, despite all the effort he has put in for the past five years.
"My wife is in India, and I don't know what to tell her," he said.
There are thousands of highly skilled, temporary foreign residents who face the possibility of exiting Canada by yearend despite spending several years contributing to the economy because the conditions to immigrate through some of the more popular programs, and the points system being used, have become harder to satisfy in the past year, experts say, and that could worsen the country's already struggling labour productivity.
Some immigration consultants say about 80,000 temporary residents - about a quarter of the total - have post-graduate work permits (PGWP) that will expire in 2024. Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) has yet to provide an updated number.
The federal government extended work permits for thousands of individuals by 18 months in March 2023 when Canada was facing a labour crunch, but immigration lawyers and consultants don't expect a similar decision in 2024. UNCONVENTIONAL PATHWAYS This has forced many foreign workers to take unconventional pathways to extend their stay in Canada. For example, one of the reasons why former cybersecurity analyst Chandra decided to work at his friend's pizza shop was because he knew he was going to get all the documents and support he needed from his employer to extend his work permit.
But while there are ways to extend a work permit, they aren't always easy.
"The company you work for will need to share a lot of information, including their financials, with the government for a work-permit extension and that feels like a hassle for many," Chandra said.
The government also evaluates the necessity of the position before providing an extension. In Chandra's case, he is confident of an extension since the rural town he lives in is dominated by retirees and needs delivery people.
But he wouldn't have had to depend on this "drastic move" had he scored the required number of points needed for a temporary resident to become a permanent Canadian resident, a figure that seems to have been out of reach for thousands in the past year.
There are many immigration programs in Canada, but most temporary residents and foreigners living outside the country either immigrate as skilled workers or get nominated by a province. These programs are managed by an online system called Express Entry, which started about a decade ago and provides prospective immigrants with points for their education levels, work experience, English and French language proficiency, age and other factors.
The higher applicants score - out of a total of 1,200 - the higher the chance of becoming permanent residents, which eventually leads to citizenship. The system is designed in a way to attract young, skilled people from around the world.
For example, applicants aged below 30 receive the highest possible number of points in the age category. Applicants also receive points for Canadian educational degrees and for working in Canada. In addition, people can receive 600 additional points if they get nominated by their respective province under the Provincial Nomination Program (PNP).
As such, many temporary residents, such as students, come to Canada and invest three to six years of their lives - while they study and earn a post-graduate work permit - with the hopes of receiving those additional points to make the cut and becoming permanent residents.
Whiletemporary residents aren't promised a permanent stay, they are influenced by the system's stakeholders, such as foreign brokers or even comments made by political ministers, and often assume they are most likely going to make the cut. That's one reason why many spend their life's savings or take out additional loans to study in Canada, immigration lawyers say.
"Nobody was told, 'If you come to Canada, you can successfully apply for a permanent residency,'" Zeynab Ziaie Moayyed, an immigration lawyer and a partner at Visa Law Group PC, said. "But for a couple of years, we had minister after minister saying we want the world's best and brightest, and we want you to come and stay here."
RISING CUTOFF SCORES
The problem, immigration lawyers say, is that the Express Entry's Comprehensive Ranking System's (CRS) cutoff score - which generally changes biweekly, but is what newcomers have to beat to become a permanent resident - is a lot higher today than when it just started. The scores have never remained so high for such a long period of time.
An applicant in 2017 with a score of around 470 wouldn't even need to enter Canada for the extra points to become a permanent resident. If under 30, and armed with a foreign master's degree, three years of foreign work experience and a decent language test score, the applicant could beat the cutoff score that ranged from around 440 to 480.
However, things began to change during the COVID-19 pandemic. In February 2021, Canada reduced its score for newcomers working in Canada to 75 and admitted a record-breaking 27,300 people in just one draw. This was unheard of at the time.
In the same year, it admitted thousands of newcomers through a new program for new graduates from Canadian institutions and workers in essential occupations.
These two steps encouraged more people to apply to Canada as international students and temporary workers, hoping to convert their stay into a permanent residency, immigration lawyers say.
But from September 2021 to July 2022, the government didn't conduct any general draws to admit permanent residents because it continued processing the thousands it had already let in. And when the first general draw took place after nearly nine months, the cutoff score had ballooned to 557.
Since then, the cutoff score for general applicants has hovered between the high 490s and the mid-550s, so many former international students who spent five years or more of their lives studying and working in Canada now find themselves in a position where they don't qualify.
"Canada is setting the bar higher," Kareem El-Assal, founder of Section 95, an organization that provides insight on Canadian immigration, said. "Essentially, the Express Entry system is becoming more selective than Harvard."
CATEGORY-BASED SELECTION
The IRCC said the cutoff score for each round of invitations isn't set by the government, but is a result of the size and composition of the application pool and Canada's annual permanent residency targets.
"If the CRS cutoff score is high, it means a large number of candidates in the pool have obtained high scores and meet the criteria for a given round," the department said in a statement.
This means that if there are more candidates with high points in the pool, the overall cutoff score is going to be higher since Canada's permanent residency admission targets remain the same - about 500,000 people annually until 2026. But there are at least 2.5 million temporary residents, including students, workers and asylum seekers, already in the country.
Despite the government's assertion that it doesn't have much of a role in adjusting cutoff scores, some immigration lawyers and consultants partly attribute the high scores to a new policy introduced last year that allowed the government to bypass the points and ranking system in the Express Entry stream.
Since 2023, the federal government has been conducting category-based invitations. Under this policy, the IRCC can pick and choose applicants belonging to certain in-demand occupations or groups.
For example, the government on April 24 invited 1,400 people from the pool who had scores above 410 and were proficient in French. A month prior to that, 1,500 people who had scores above 388 were invited from the same group.
Similarly, 975 people with scores of 430 and above and who worked in the transport sector were invited on March 13, while 4,500 people who had scored above 491 were invited from the science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM) category in April.
This leaves fewer invitations for general applicants or for people who don't fall into any of these categories, which further drives up the competition and cutoff scores. In the last draw for people in the general category, the score was 529. Prior to that, it was 549. This change wasn't expected by many temporary residents who had already invested plenty of time and money in Canada.
Overall, the IRCC has invited about 47,000 people since June 2023 through these special draws in six categories: health care, agriculture, transport, STEM, trades such as plumbers and construction workers, and people with a strong proficiency in the French language. The majority of the invitations were made under the French language category.
These categories were selected based on labour market information and projections and inputs received from the provinces and territories, IRCC said on its website. The new policy could benefit Canada's economy by bringing in the kinds of professionals the country requires, but immigration consultants say it could also have drawbacks. A LOST OPPORTUNITY El-Assal said the whole point of starting the Express Entry system was to invite the highest-scoring individuals, since an analysis by Statistics Canada showed they would likely integrate well into the economy.
"Now the government is saying we are putting Statistics Canada's research aside and we just want to prioritize these six categories," he said. "Is that what Canada wants to do from an immigration point of view? That's an open debate. I think this is a question that we really need to explore. Me, personally, I have a lot of trust in Statistics Canada."
Bank of Nova Scotia economist Rebekah Young said she was a bit "cynical" about the government's ability to pick winners.
"I don't think they are going to solve many issues by trying to narrowly target specific skill sets as the track record is weak in this regard, while losing out on the high potential that we have got here already," she said. "Certainly, the higher scoring is better through an economic lens and generally better through a productivity lens ... these would be ideal new Canadians from an economic standpoint."
Young said Canada's housing problems cannot be solved by just importing construction workers, noting that despite record-high employment in the sector, it's producing fewer houses. There is a "pretty high unemployment" rate in some of the big sectors for people already in Canada and the government isn't doing a great job of training Canadians in some of these spaces, she said.
The upward pressure on the Express Entry pool means there currently could be at least 50,000 highly skilled applicants with points ranging between 470 and 600 who aren't sure about their Canadian futures.
Losing a large chunk of this group could be a lost opportunity for Canada, immigration consultants say.
"Someone under the age of 30, who has a master's degree from Canada and multiple years of work experience in Canada can barely scrape the 500 mark, and even they are facing a hard time," Moayyed said. "This is the premium talent that countries want and they will now choose to go elsewhere."
The government seems to be aware of this and has announced a number of work-permit extensions in the past that allowed newcomers to stay beyond the typical three-year work permit after completing their educational degrees.
Former immigration minister Sean Fraser announced an 18-month extension last year. Current minister Marc Miller announced an extension for 6,700 workers in Manitoba in early May.
These kinds of moves suggest the government is of two minds: it wants temporary residents to stay, but it doesn't have the political capital to let these highly skilled temporary residents stay permanently, according to immigration lawyer Steven Meurrens.
"It creates this weird scene where the government is like, yeah, we want you to stay permanently, but we are not going to do anything to make that happen," he said.
PROCESSING DELAYS The government has faced criticism in the past couple of years as the population grew at record rates - by at least a million each year - primarily due to an increase in temporary residents at a time when the country was going through a housing crunch.
This was followed by the government placing caps on international students, holding the number of permanent residents it admits annually and announcing its intention to reduce the overall number of temporary residents.
Muerren said there is merit in trying to slow the population's growth, but that shouldn't impact the people who are already working in Canada.
"It's a lost opportunity for them and their employer, and presumably having to replace them is going to be a drag for employers," he said. "I struggle with the idea that people who are already working in positions should have to leave just because of an arbitrary permanent residency cap."
Aside from the skillbased federal immigration programs, the provincial streams also seem to be facing issues. Provinces can nominate someone for entry based on their needs through the Provincial Nomination Program. Once nominated, an applicant can receive 600 points that are then added to the person's Express Entry score, making it easier to pass the cutoff.
However, the increasing pressure on the provinces in the past year or so has led to delays in processing applications, pauses in crucial immigration programs and sudden changes in requirements, which even led to a protest in Prince Edward Island in May.
The Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program, one of the more popular PNPs among immigrants, currently has an application processing delay of about eight months to nine months, according to immigration consultant Kanwar Sierah.
"This means that if someone applies to Ontario today and their work permit is expiring by the end of this year or early next year, they cannot expect the province to process their application before the expiry," he said. "That's a big problem."
AGAINST THE ODDS Despite all the obstacles, some people have managed to meet the permanent residency requirements before the expiration of their work permits, albeit through rather tough, unconventional pathways.
Jaspreet Singh*, 24, who came to Canada in 2018, earned a diploma in automotive business studies. He couldn't find a job in his field and, therefore, the required points to become a permanent resident.
With less than a year left on his work permit, he decided to take a gamble and change his career to a profession that was in demand in Canada and, particularly, Ontario. He became a certified pest controller and the federal government soon added the profession to a trade list that would give him extra points in the Express Entry pool, which helped him get over the cutoff.
"Never in my life did I think I would be killing bugs," Singh said. "It did not feel great in the beginning, but today, I am one of the top-selling specialists in my company."
Siyam Ahmed*, who came to Canada in 2015, made the Express Entry cut in April thanks to the category-based STEM draw as a software developer.
The catch, though, is that he works as a software tester. His company decided to support him with the appropriate letters since it didn't want to lose him and because the responsibilities of his roles overlapped to a certain extent.
For many others, however, concerns continue as their work-permit expiry date approaches. Some, such as Montreal-based system testing engineer Vineeth Thomas*, have left it to fate.
"If I have to go back, I have to go back," said the 28-year-old, who currently has 490 points. "It will be difficult to restart your life from zero after spending five years here. But it is what it is."
* Names have been changed to protect identities. nkarim@postmedia.com

Document 454 of 500

Canadian woman, 93, wanted to give her kids a gift.Instead she got hit with a $40K capital gains tax bill; Explainer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-woman-93-wanted-give-her-kids-gift/docview/3062956129/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canadian+woman%2C+93%2C+wanted+to+give+her+kids+a+gift.Instead+she+got+hit+with+a+%2440K+capital+gains+tax+bill%3B+Explainer&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A gift to her daughter and grandson has left a 93-yearold Ontario woman with sticker shock.
Liz Diachun told CTV News she was planning to give her family two lots on her farm property in Warkworth to help them establish homes. However, her lawyer informed her that despite the land being a gift, it still needed to be appraised at fair market value for tax purposes.
The two lots were appraised at $ 125,000 and $145,000, totalling $270,000, leaving Diachun with a tax bill of about $40,000, an amount she said she cannot pay.
"I'm on pension. How am I going to pay for that?" said Diachun. "I'm not one of the wealthy. I'm 93 years old. Who is going to give me a mortgage? Who is going to give me a loan?" Diachun is hopeful she can still find a way to make the gifts happen, but the clock is ticking. Beginning next month, the capital gains tax inclusion rate will increase from 50 per cent to 67 per cent for amounts over $250,000. Here's what to know.
WHAT IS CAPITAL GAINS TAX?
Capital gains tax applies to the profit made from selling a capital asset, such as stocks, real estate or other investments.
The current system includes an inclusion rate of 50 per cent, meaning that only half of the capital gain is considered taxable income. The taxable capital gain is taxed at personal income tax rates, which vary depending on an individual's total income and the tax bracket they fall into.
HOW IS IT CHANGING?
Under the new federal budget, the inclusion rate will increase from 50 per cent to 66.5 per cent for capital gains exceeding $250,000 per year for individuals.
The changes are scheduled to take effect on June 25, though the Liberals have yet to introduce the legislation to increase the capital-gains inclusion rate. The delay means Canadians like Diachun are in a tricky situation as they make decisions about selling, or gifting, major assets.
Earlier this week Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said the legislative process will begin in the "coming weeks and certainly before the House rises (on June 21)."
In the meantime, Canadians are mulling major decisions without firm details about how the new policy might affect their tax bill.
For corporations and trusts, meanwhile, the new 66.5 per cent inclusion rate will apply to all capital gains, without the $250,000 threshold.
There will also be a onetime increase in the lifetime capital-gains exemption for small-business shares and qualifying farm or fishing properties, increasing from $1 million to $1.25 million.
WHEN WAS CAPITAL GAINS TAX ESTABLISHED AND HOW HAS IT CHANGED? It was first introduced in 1972 as part of the tax reforms implemented by then finance minister Edgar Benson, a member of Liberal prime minister Pierre Trudeau's government.
The tax was intended to broaden the tax base and tax income from investments. The rate when first implemented was 50 per cent, though it has fluctuated over the years, including jumping to 66 per cent in 1988 and 75 per cent in 1990.
In 2000, under then finance minister Paul Martin, the rate dropped back to 50 per cent.
The upcoming changes are part of a strategy to make "the wealthiest Canadians pay their fair share," according to the Liberal government.
National Post

Document 459 of 500

May 31, 2024 (Page A9)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/may-31-2024-page-a9/docview/3066189497/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 460 of 500

FIRST READING: Quebec, one of Canada's last anti-Conservative voting blocks, is now leaning Tory

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-quebec-one-canadas-last-anti/docview/3062853268/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Quebec%2C+one+of+Canada%27s+last+anti-Conservative+voting+blocks%2C+is+now+leaning+Tory&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign up here.
TOP STORY
For one of the first times in nearly 40 years, a poll has emerged showing the Conservative party as a possible contender in Quebec.
While the province is still poised to be swept by the Bloc Québécois in the next federal election, it's a sign that one of Canada's last remaining anti-Conservative voting blocs may be softening.
A new Leger poll found that the Conservatives are tied for first place in Quebec with the Bloc Québécois, with both parties polling at 29 per cent support.
The Liberals, in turn, have dropped to third place with 26 per cent.
It's not only one of the best-ever Quebec showings for the Conservatives, but it's one of the only times that they've polled better than the Liberals.
To be sure, the Conservatives are still performing horribly in Quebec compared with the rest of the country. In every other major province, the Tories are either capturing a majority of the popular vote, or they have a 20-point lead.
But being tied for first place is a near-miraculous result given that the entire history of the modern Conservative Party of Canada has seen it brutally rejected by Quebecers.
First poll in many months to have the Conservatives tied or ahead in Quebec
- Polling Canada (@CanadianPolling) May 29, 2024
The Conservative party has contested seven federal elections since its founding in 2003. In each one, the party was lucky to score third place in Quebec, both in terms of popular vote or seat count.
All of those election campaigns inevitably featured media speculations about a "Conservative breakthrough" in Quebec. Since 2003, in fact, the phrase "Conservative breakthrough in Quebec" has appeared in a Canadian news article more than 40 times.
But every single time, when voting day came around, the Conservatives were once again humiliated in Quebec. When former Conservative leader Stephen Harper captured a majority government in 2011, he won just five of Quebec's 75 seats. When the Conservatives were endorsed by Quebec Premier François Legault in 2021, they won just 10 of 78 seats.
And this pattern has generally been repeating ever since the 1960s.
In federal elections that haven't involved Brian Mulroney, Quebec has traditionally been a place where federal conservative parties go to die.
Quebec never elected a single MP from the Reform party or Canadian Alliance, the right-wing populist parties that formed the core of the modern Conservatives.
And the province spent whole decades in which only one or two ridings were held by the Progressive Conservatives. In 1980 - the last federal election contested by Pierre Trudeau - the final Quebec seat count was 74 for the Liberals, and one for the PCs. And even then, Progressive Conservative candidate Roch La Salle only held onto his rural riding of Joliette by 384 votes.
The only exception, as mentioned, was Mulroney, who swept Quebec's seats in both 1984 and 1988, before much of his Quebec wing spun off to form the Bloc Québecois.
Although Conservative party leader Pierre Poilievre speaks the best French of a Conservative party leader since the formation of the modern party (albeit with an Alberta accent), the irony is that he's seeing massive gains in Quebec despite doing very little to woo Quebecers in particular.
Terry Glavin: Canada slowly acknowledging there never was a 'mass grave'
'Overwhelming majority of the Canadian poor are white,' report finds
One of his only real Quebec-specific policy promises, in fact, has been to spare Radio-Canada even as he defunds its English equivalent, the CBC.
It was in a January interview with Radio-Canada that Poilievre explained his Quebec strategy as treating Quebecers as if they "have the same preoccupations as other Canadians."
If the strategy is working, it helps that Quebec is only now being sidelined by many of the same economic and social woes that have characterized the rest of Canada's experience of the latter years of the Trudeau government. This is particularly true of housing affordability, an issue to which Quebec used to be relatively immune
Even with this week's new Leger results, Quebec remains one of the only Canadian voting blocs that hasn't dramatically turned towards the Conservatives in the last year.
In recent months, polls have progressively shown the party capturing solid pluralities in demographics that traditionally had very little to do with them, including young people, women and union members
IN OTHER NEWS
Death threats against MPs have risen about 800 per cent since 2019, prompting the House of Commons' sergeant-at-arms to counsel Parliamentarians to keep their office doors locked and avoid in-person meetings. "If an appointment with a constituent is necessary, Members or their employees should consider virtual meetings for the foreseeable future," reads a memo obtained by the National Post
The Calgary-based Aristotle Foundation is the think tank that has previously poured cold water on the notion - officially held by the Trudeau government - that Canada is a "systemically racist" country founded on white supremacy. In a new report, they added to this line of argument by noting that the vast majority of Canadian poor are white, which would not seem to fully align with the expected results from a bastion of entrenched white supremacy.
The federal government handed out $400 million in bonuses in the last fiscal year, according to a new tabulation completed by the Canadian Taxpayers Federation. That figure is divided almost equally between the bonuses paid to civil servants ($211 million) and the bonuses paid to employees of Crown corporations such as VIA Rail or CBC ($196 million). It's the $14.9 million in CBC executive bonuses that have gotten the most attention of any of these, mostly because they were paid out even as the broadcaster laid off hundreds of employees and petitioned the federal government for more money. The position of CBC president Catherine Tait is that the bonuses represent "performance pay" built into existing employment contracts, and are thus not technically bonuses. The Canadian Taxpayers Federation's position, meanwhile, is that the government is so terrible at almost everything right now that it's weird that one per cent of federal spending is going to bonuses. "Bonuses are for when you do a good job, they shouldn't be handed out like participation ribbons," said Franco Terrazzano, the group's federal director.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau endorsed a controversial decision this week by France to invite Russian officials to commemorations marking the 80th anniversary of D-Day. "As we commemorate the victory over fascism and Nazism in World War Two, it's important to recognize all the countries that were involved," Trudeau told reporters, adding that he still had "extreme disagreement" with Russia's attempt to destroy Ukraine. The obvious criticism of the position is that neither Russia nor the USSR participated in D-Day (and the invasion was partially justified as a means to check Russia's penchant for subjugating European countries).
Get all of these insights and more into your inbox by signing up for the First Reading newsletter here

Michael Taube: Ontario's domination of global EV manufacturing benefits us all

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-taube-ontarios-domination-global-ev/docview/3062846448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Michael+Taube%3A+Ontario%27s+domination+of+global+EV+manufacturing+benefits+us+all&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is a world leader in the production of several natural resources, including oil and gas, gold, copper and potash. Few would have ever predicted we would become a major player in, of all things, the electric vehicle (EV) industry.
Yet, that's exactly what has happened.
BloombergNEF's annual global lithium-ion battery supply chain ranking, which was released in February, saw Canada displace China in the top position for 2023. Factors that go into the ranking include raw materials, battery manufacturing, downstream demand, infrastructure, innovation and environmental and social governance (ESG).
Canada moved into first place with "consistent manufacturing and production advances, and strong ESG credentials," according to BNEF. Companies like Umicore, Volkswagen, Ford, Stellantis and LG Energy Solutions have also "made announcements to invest in the country."
This brings us to the province that's helping lead the way in EV battery production: Ontario.
Here's a recent example. Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau held a joint news conference on May 14 to announce the Japanese multinational Asahi Kasei Corporation's plan to spend about $1.6 billion on building an EV battery component plant in Port Colborne, Ont. The plant's construction will start in late 2024, and should be up and running in 2027. Asahi Kasei will receive investment support from Ontario and tax credits from the federal government in return.
"Today's announcement is more good news for Ontario's world-leading electric vehicle manufacturing sector and the thousands of workers who are benefiting from the billions of investments being made in our province," Ford said in the news release.
It's encouraging to see Ontario's increased role in this rapidly developing market. The province leads by enhancing and improving EV battery production. At the same time, it's is helping increase overall market share and influence in North America - and providing more consumer choice for the EV battery chain instead of leaving everyone to rely solely on communist-governed China.
Issues with electric vehicle production still remain. They're not cost-effective for the average consumer, although some hybrid versions are gradually getting there. While it's nice to know you can potentially receive a federal or provincial rebate after purchasing a Tesla, very few Canadians have the disposable income to make that purchase to begin with. Plus, the forthcoming construction of numerous EV charging stations across the country will cost plenty of taxpayer dollars and take up a fair amount of real estate.
Meanwhile, environmental concerns with EV batteries, including the disposal of older ones, evoke waves of frustration. One notable voice is Rowan Atkinson, the British comedian and creator of popular characters like Mr. Bean. Atkinson holds bachelor's and master's degrees in electrical engineering. He was an early adopter of electric vehicles, but has spoken out against them and highlighted concerns about lithium-ion batteries.
"They're absurdly heavy, huge amounts of energy are required to make them, and they are estimated to last only upwards of 10 years," he wrote in The Guardian last June. "It seems a perverse choice of hardware with which to lead the automobile's fight against the climate crisis."
Hence, Ontario has an important role to play in the present and future direction of EV production.
"Over the last four years, Ontario has secured $43 billion in new auto and EV investments, more than any U.S. state in the same time period," Vic Fedeli, the provincial minister of economic development, told me in a recent email. "Global auto companies are choosing Ontario because we check all the boxes for what they need to be successful. We have the best talent in the world, a robust EV ecosystem, access to clean and reliable power, critical minerals and so much more. For these reasons, Ontario was also able to land Honda's $15 billion investment, the largest auto investment in Canadian history."
Fedeli also noted that Ontario has rapidly grown into an EV manufacturing powerhouse.
"When we took office, Ontario's auto sector had been steadily declining for years," he said. "In 2019, Reuters reported that companies planned to spend $300 billion globally in the EV sector, but none of it was planned for Canada. Fast forward to today, where Ontario has now landed $43 billion in new EV investments in just four years."
The minister is working to ensure the province "can capitalize on the massive economic opportunity this presents, which is why we have worked to build out our end-to-end EV supply chain." Indeed, "as global demand for EVs continues to ramp up, we are making sure that the vehicles and batteries of the future will be made-in-Ontario, adding tens of thousands of good-paying jobs in the process."
In Fedeli's view, "Ontario is perfectly positioned to be a global leader in EV production for decades to come." That's great news for Ontario, Canada and the entire world.
National Post

Document 462 of 500

Randall Denley: Running against Justin Trudeau is Doug Ford's best reason for an early-ish election

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-running-against-justin-trudeau-is/docview/3062846411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+Running+against+Justin+Trudeau+is+Doug+Ford%27s+best+reason+for+an+early-ish+election&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was a brief outbreak of election fever in Ontario this week after Premier Doug Ford refused to rule out an early election, even though the official election date is still two years away. Most of the victims of this fever were journalists and unnamed political insiders, all craving some excitement.
The speculation started when Ford gave a cagey answer to a dumb question. Ford had just announced that a big expansion of beer and wine retailing would start this year, not in 2026 as previously announced.
The rush to broaden alcohol retailing, delivering on a promise made six years ago, made reporters wonder if the move might mean an early election was in the offing. It's hard to imagine why it would be, but the question put the premier in a bit of a box. If he ruled out an early election and then ultimately called one at some point, he would be accused of going back on his word. When he ducked the question, speculation started to mount.
On Wednesday, Ford narrowed the scope of that speculation when he told Ottawa talk-radio host Bill Carroll that an election would definitely not come this year. Of course, that still leaves spring of 2025 as a possibility.
The only thing that's missing in the early election narrative is a compelling reason why Ford would even want to go to the polls early. Sure, the province's NDP and Liberal leaders look scarcely more viable than their vanquished predecessors, but that's unlikely to change any time soon.
Bonnie Crombie, the newish Liberal leader, has an apparent gift for saying stupid things In response to the election rumours, she said, "They have a majority government. I can't envision the reason why they would want to call an early election. It's very disrespectful, is very anti-democratic."
After you figure out why an election is anti-democratic, ask yourself why a person who fancies herself the next premier of Ontario would not have cheered the prospect of an early vote. Surely Crombie should have taken the opportunity to tell people what a great alternative her party offered and encouraged Ford to take her on.
Crombie didn't do much better in her criticism of the earlier timeline on expanded alcohol retailing, which she called "a blatant attempt by Doug Ford to buy the next election."
Buy the election? How does that work? The retail expansion benefits the government, not drinkers. Ford says the larger retail network would bring in between $895 million to $1.6 billion for the province. People will be able to buy wine and beer in more locations, but the government still determines what can be sold and sets a minimum price.
The PC perspective is that delivering on the booze promise is not the harbinger of an election, but just one more step towards a future platform that will highlight promises kept. It is a step, but an exceptionally small one.
If the Ford government wanted to please voters by keeping an old election promise, it would have done better to follow through on the 2018 pledge to cut the second income tax bracket by 20 per cent, saving people $786 a year. Instead, the government is running radio ads touting savings from cancelling the Drive Clean emissions test, a change it made in 2018. And what about that six-year-old promise to balance the budget?
While the alcohol retail change won't trigger an early election, the PCs might nevertheless want to consider a vote next spring.
Whether the PCs call an election next spring or June 2026, there will likely be both a federal and a provincial election within a year of one another (the federal election is scheduled for October 2025). Elections drain volunteer and donor resources. Better to be the first to hold an election than the second.
There is also some value to calling the provincial election while Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is still in office. The prime minister's general incompetence and over-the-top progressivism causes constant damage to the Liberal brand. To make it even better, he's dragging the witless NDP leader Jagmeet Singh down with him. Ford couldn't find a better guy than Trudeau to make sure the words Liberal and NDP continue to make Ontarians wince.
As a bonus, Trudeau is so loathed right now that he makes Ford look good in comparison.
Some argue that the PCs will want to go early because a Pierre Poilievre government would quickly become unpopular and hurt the provincial party's chances. It's possible, but that thinking depends on Ontarians giving Poilievre a majority, then immediately turning around and electing the Liberals or NDP provincially. It seems unlikely.
It's easy to overthink election strategy. The simplest one is best. Do a competent job and keep your promises. In Canadian politics, that defines overachieving.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist.
randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 463 of 500

Canadians on the hook for citizenship handout

Author: Sarkonak, Jamie

Publication info: Sarkonak, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-on-hook-citizenship-handout/docview/3062762265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canadians+on+the+hook+for+citizenship+handout&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Sarkonak%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A CBC ad campaign last summer tried to define the increasingly amorphous sense of "Canadianness" on which its brand is built: "It's not how Canadian you are; it's who you are in Canada." But, if the Liberals have their way, even this tag line will be outdated soon.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller, you see, hopes to extend eligibility for Canadian citizenship to anyone whose grandparent was born on this country's soil. He has accordingly tabled Bill C-71, currently in first reading, which would make the necessary changes to the Citizenship Act. Miller hopes to pass the law before the end of June. (Miller is also seeking to legitimize an unknown number of illegal immigrants.)
The motive for such a law comes from a court - the Ontario Superior Court of Justice, a trial court whose decisions do not apply in other provinces and whose logic in this particular case was never reviewed by any appeal judges of higher rank. In December, it declared Canada's rules on the heritability of citizenship unconstitutional.
Since 2007, the rules have operated like this: generation zero is born on Canadian soil and obtains citizenship; generation one, if born outside Canada, is entitled to receive citizenship by blood; however, generation two, if again born outside Canada, has no right to receive citizenship by blood.
Bill C-71 would make that second generation born abroad automatically eligible for citizenship, as long as their generation one parent lived in Canada for at least three years before birth. The government has no estimate for the size of this population.
The Ontario trial judge, whose reasoning, I repeat, has not been reviewed by any other higher court, figured the old rules were unfair. They violated novel intersectional equality rights by reinforcing "the stereotype that ... first generation born abroad Canadians and their children are parasites or leeches, in the sense defined by the Merriam Webster dictionary as 'a person who seeks support from another without making an adequate return.'" The judge added that women in this class are particularly disadvantaged, as they must "make choices between their careers, financial stability and independence, and health care on the one hand, and the ability to ensure their child receives Canadian citizenship on the other."
Mobility rights were also violated by the law: "It is unrealistic and impractical to expect Canadian citizens abroad to return to Canada to give birth when the decision to do so will be accompanied by the financial difficulties, professional risks, and health risks I have described above."
Applying the court's logic to any other situation reveals the absurdity of it all. If withholding citizenship from Canadian spawn two generations removed from home is discrimination, why not three? Four? And if any rule somehow can be perceived by a judge to reinforce a negative stereotype, what else violates equality rights? Any reasonable government would have appealed, but not our feds. This decision granted legalistic cover to hand out more passports Oprah-style, and a higher court may not have been so generous.
The PR campaign to advance C-71 has taken care to focus on the saddest, most sympathetic stories that can be found: the cases of Type-A parents whose children have high "Canadian-ness" - speak our language, participate in our culture, share our values - but can't, for whatever administrative reasons, obtain citizenship. These individual cases could be resolved through ministerial intervention today by Miller, which he knows and admits, but his government wants a rule so broad to include all.
On the other hand, there are others who barely have a Canadian connection at generation zero. Some are passport babies, whose mothers travelled to Canada for the purpose of obtaining citizenship for their children. According to Canadian Institute for Health Information data, compiled by analyst Andrew Griffiths for Policy Options magazine, there have been more than 40,000 of such births from 2010 to 2022.
Others have obtained Canadian privileges but have returned home. This was especially apparent in 2006, when the Lebanon civil war broke out that July. Some 40,000 people in Lebanon were registered with the Canadian embassy at the time, and $94 million was spent to evacuate about 14,000 of them to Canada; by September, the government estimated that 7,000 of those evacuees had returned to Lebanon, providing the catalyst for the Harper government to tighten citizenship rules in the first place.
New conflicts shake out new numbers. After fighting erupted in Sudan last year, prompting Canada to evacuate 175 Canadian citizens and permanent residents, Post columnist John Ivison spoke with a government source who estimated that up to half of the evacuees were "refugees who were granted status in Canada and then returned to Sudan, with some continuing to claim welfare and child benefits."
"Most of these people have been living in Sudan for years," said the source. "Sometimes they never really lived in Canada and don't speak English or French."
And who knows what the tally in Gaza is; in November, the foreign affairs department estimated that 600 Canadians, permanent residents and family members were in the strip. Some of these no doubt include aid workers, but by news reports, they also include young families who are clearly being raised intentionally abroad.
Those children can grow up elsewhere, without learning any English or French, without becoming attuned to our ways of life, our common sense of right and wrong; without ever paying Canadian taxes. Without giving anything in return, they can turn to the Canadian state for help - rescue, health care, and so on. The same can be said for their children, who only need to spend a few years in Canada to be eligible to pass on the same to their children.
The Liberal bill would ensure that the rest of Canada - those of us who have received the Canadian tradition and intend to preserve it for our children, who have a direct interest in our state's success, who pay income taxes throughout our lives - could be obligated to support three whole generations of convenience-citizens as if they were our countrymen the whole time. It would do so under the guise of helping a narrow group of expats who can, at best, receive help from the minister, and, at worst, have their children apply for citizenship the normal way.
National Post
