Document 464 of 500

Poilievre hopes to break Conservative curse in Quebec; One of country's last anti-Tory voting blocs

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-hopes-break-conservative-curse-quebec/docview/3062762174/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Poilievre+hopes+to+break+Conservative+curse+in+Quebec%3B+One+of+country%27s+last+anti-Tory+voting+blocs&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A poll has emerged showing the Conservative party as a possible contender in Quebec.
While the province is still poised to be swept by the Bloc Québécois in the next federal election, it's a sign that one of Canada's last remaining anti-Conservative voting blocs may be softening.
A new Leger poll found that the Conservatives are tied for first place in Quebec with the Bloc Québécois, with both parties polling at 29 per cent support.
The Liberals, in turn, have dropped to third place with 26 per cent.
It's not only one of the best-ever Quebec showings for the Conservatives, but it's one of the only times that they've polled better than the Liberals.
To be sure, the Conservatives are still performing horribly in Quebec compared with the rest of the country. In every other major province, the Tories have at least a 20-point lead.
But being tied for first place is a near-miraculous result given that the history of the modern Conservative Party of Canada has seen it brutally rejected by Quebecers.
The Conservative party has contested seven federal elections since its founding in 2003. In each one, the party was lucky to score third place in Quebec, both in terms of popular vote and seat count.
All of those campaigns inevitably featured media speculations about a "Conservative breakthrough" in Quebec. Since 2003, in fact, the phrase "Conservative breakthrough in Quebec" has appeared in a Canadian news article more than 40 times.
But every single time, when voting day came around, the Conservatives were once again humiliated in Quebec. When then Conservative leader Stephen Harper captured a majority government in 2011, he won just five of Quebec's 75 seats. When the Conservatives were endorsed by Quebec Premier François Legault in 2021, they won just 10 of 78 seats.
And this pattern has generally been repeating ever since the 1960s.
In federal elections that haven't involved Brian Mulroney, Quebec has traditionally been a place where federal conservative parties go to die.
Quebec never elected a single MP from the Reform Party or Canadian Alliance, the right-wing populist parties that form the core of the modern Conservatives.
And the province spent whole decades in which only one or two ridings were held by the Progressive Conservatives. In 1980 - the last federal election contested by Pierre Trudeau - the final Quebec seat count was 74 for the Liberals, and one for the PCs. And even then, Progressive Conservative candidate Roch La Salle only held onto his rural riding of Joliette by 384 votes.
The only exception was Mulroney, who swept Quebec's seats in both 1984 and 1988, before much of his Quebec wing spun off to form the Bloc Québécois.
Although Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks the best French of a Conservative party leader since the formation of the modern party (albeit with an Alberta accent), the irony is that he's seeing massive gains in Quebec despite doing little to woo Quebecers.
One of his only real Quebec-specific policy promises, in fact, has been to spare Radio-Canada even as he defunds its English equivalent, the CBC.
It was in a January interview with Radio-Canada that Poilievre explained his Quebec strategy as treating Quebecers as if they "have the same preoccupations as other Canadians."
If the strategy is working, it helps that Quebec is only now being sidelined by many of the same economic and social woes that have characterized the rest of Canada's experience of the latter years of the Trudeau government. This is particularly true of housing affordability, an issue to which Quebec used to be relatively immune.
Even with this week's new Leger results, Quebec remains one of the only Canadian voting blocs that hasn't dramatically turned toward the Conservatives in the last year.
In recent months, polls have progressively shown the party capturing solid pluralities in demographics that traditionally had little to do with them, including young people, women and union members.
National Post

Business indexing term: Industry: 81394 :‎ Political Organizations

PM, ford denounce 'vile acts'of antisemitism; 'I've just had it up to here'

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-ford-denounce-vile-actsof-antisemitism-ive/docview/3062761933/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=PM%2C+ford+denounce+%27vile+acts%27of+antisemitism%3B+%27I%27ve+just+had+it+up+to+here%27&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford decried the shootings in the past week at Toronto and Montreal Jewish schools and vowed to do everything in their power to catch and punish the perpetrators.
Trudeau started off his remarks Thursday morning at the grand opening of Sanofi's $800-million vaccine manufacturing facility in Toronto by saying that, just a short drive away, parents were dropping off their daughters at a Jewish girls'elementary school that was fired on by gunmen over the weekend.
Another Jewish school in Montreal was hit by at least one bullet Wednesday night.
"I'm, of course, relieved that no one was hurt. I'm disgusted by these vile and despicable acts of antisemitism. It needs to stop now," said Trudeau. "To Jewish parents and students across the country, we stand with you. We will always work to safeguard your right to live proudly Jewish lives in Canada."
Trudeau said the federal government is working with local law enforcement and will use "all available federal resources to ensure that the perpetrators are held to account." He added, "We cannot and will not let this antisemitism stand in Canada."
Ford said he agreed with Trudeau and promised the province would "throw every single resource" to catch the perpetrators, and "throw them in jail, throw away the key."
See ANTISEMITISM on A6 "Folks, let's cut to the chase here. What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? That is just unacceptable. Imagine that. Imagine a little kid in a school because they're from the Jewish faith, someone goes by and start shooting through the school windows?" "These guys need to be caught. They need to be punished. They need to be thrown in jail, and I'll tell you, we have zero tolerance for this anywhere in Ontario," he said.
Federal Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Ya'ara Saks thanked Trudeau and Ford for their words of support and said that, as a Jewish MP, "allies matter."
"As a Jewish mother in this community, we have the right to be safe and feel safe each and every day," she said.
Trudeau and Ford weren't forthcoming with concrete details when asked by a reporter what resources their governments would use to jail the perpetrators, given that politicians are not supposed to direct the police's actions.
Trudeau pointed to his government's Security Infrastructure Program, which provides support for communities at risk of hate-related crimes and incidents at places of worship and schools, but he said there is "much more to do."
"The core thing that we have continued to call for is an end to hatred and intolerance in Canada, whether it be antisemitism or Islamophobia," he said.
"We are seeing that events overseas that are devastating and incredibly difficult to watch should not be spilling over into Canadians hating on other Canadians. We are all united in our strength and diversity and our desire for a better country in a better world."
Ford said that Ontario "will throw every single resource of increased funding for the police" and has "given thousands of dollars to places of worship."
And he had a clear message to potential newcomers to Canada.
"Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this. Simple as that. You want to come to Canada? You want to be a resident of Ontario? You get along with everyone. I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from."
Ford touted Ontario's diverse population as its "No. 1 selling tool around the world."
"We have 110 nationalities here in Ontario, 200 languages being spoken, and guess what? Ninety-nine per cent of the people get along. There are wars going on all around the world. But we still get along," he said. "Enough is enough. I've just had it up to here."
"People outside those communities, they've had it too. They aren't used to this happening here in Ontario. I'm done with this stuff."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow said that the city has been conducting "safety walks" in collaboration with the police, local councillors and different communities. Chow said some places of worship or schools may consider putting up shatterproof glass, fencing or new lighting.
Ford expressed shock at hearing that shatterproof glass might be necessary in Ontario schools. "We have to have this in Ontario schools, like, where have we gone? Again, we're all just fed up with this nonsense. I'll tell you that. It shouldn't have to be that way."
He said that he was not criticizing Chow, adding "I love the mayor by the way."
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 466 of 500

Ascendant Ontario leads EV manufacturing

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ascendant-ontario-leads-ev-manufacturing/docview/3062760617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ascendant+Ontario+leads+EV+manufacturing&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is a world leader in the production of several natural resources, including oil and gas, gold, copper and potash. Few would have ever predicted we would become a major player in, of all things, the electric vehicle (EV) industry.
Yet, that's exactly what has happened.
BloombergNEF's annual global lithium-ion battery supply chain ranking, which was released in February, saw Canada displace China in the top position for 2023. Factors that go into the ranking include raw materials, battery manufacturing, downstream demand, infrastructure, innovation and environmental and social governance (ESG).
Canada moved into first place with "consistent manufacturing and production advances, and strong ESG credentials," according to BNEF. Companies like Umicore, Volkswagen, Ford, Stellantis and LG Energy Solutions have also "made announcements to invest in the country."
This brings us to the province that's helping lead the way in EV battery production: Ontario.
Here's a recent example.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau held a joint news conference on May 14 to announce the Japanese multinational Asahi Kasei Corporation's plan to spend about $1.6 billion on building an EV battery component plant in Port Colborne, Ont.
The plant's construction will start in late 2024, and should be up and running in 2027. Asahi Kasei will receive investment support from Ontario and tax credits from the federal government in return.
"Today's announcement is more good news for Ontario's world-leading electric vehicle manufacturing sector and the thousands of workers who are benefiting from the billions of investments being made in our province," Ford said in the news release.
It's encouraging to see Ontario's increased role in this rapidly developing market. The province leads by enhancing and improving EV battery production. At the same time, it's is helping increase overall market share and influence in North America - and providing more consumer choice for the EV battery chain instead of leaving everyone to rely solely on communist-governed China.
Issues with electricvehicle production still remain. They're not cost-effective for the average consumer, although some hybrid versions are gradually getting there.
While it's nice to know you can potentially receive a federal or provincial rebate after purchasing a Tesla, very few Canadians have the disposable income to make that purchase to begin with.
Plus, the forthcoming construction of numerous EV charging stations across the country will cost plenty of taxpayer dollars and take up a fair amount of real estate.
Meanwhile, environmental concerns with EV batteries, including the disposal of older ones, evoke waves of frustration. One notable voice is Rowan Atkinson, the British comedian and creator of popular characters like Mr. Bean. Atkinson holds bachelor's and master's degrees in electrical engineering. He was an early adopter of electric vehicles, but has spoken out against them and highlighted concerns about lithium-ion batteries.
"They're absurdly heavy, huge amounts of energy are required to make them, and they are estimated to last only upwards of 10 years," he wrote in The Guardian last June. "It seems a perverse choice of hardware with which to lead the automobile's fight against the climate crisis."
Hence, Ontario has an important role to play in the present and future direction of EV production.
"Over the last four years, Ontario has secured $43 billion in new auto and EV investments, more than any U.S. state in the same time period," Vic Fedeli, the provincial minister of economic development, told me in a recent email.
"Global auto companies are choosing Ontario because we check all the boxes for what they need to be successful. We have the best talent in the world, a robust EV ecosystem, access to clean and reliable power, critical minerals and so much more. For these reasons, Ontario was also able to land Honda's $15 billion investment, the largest auto investment in Canadian history."
Fedeli also noted that Ontario has rapidly grown into an EV manufacturing powerhouse.
"When we took office, Ontario's auto sector had been steadily declining for years," he said. "In 2019, Reuters reported that companies planned to spend $300 billion globally in the EV sector, but none of it was planned for Canada. Fast forward to today, where Ontario has now landed $43 billion in new EV investments in just four years."
The minister is working to ensure the province "can capitalize on the massive economic opportunity this presents, which is why we have worked to build out our end-to-end EV supply chain." Indeed, "as global demand for EVs continues to ramp up, we are making sure that the vehicles and batteries of the future will be made in Ontario, adding tens of thousands of good-paying jobs in the process."
In Fedeli's view, "Ontario is perfectly positioned to be a global leader in EV production for decades to come." That's great news for Ontario, Canada and the entire world.
National Post

Document 467 of 500

BC United's desperate attack on Conservatives

Author: Pankratz, Adam

Publication info: Pankratz, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bc-uniteds-desperate-attack-on-conservatives/docview/3062760533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=BC+United%27s+desperate+attack+on+Conservatives&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Pankratz%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As BC United tanks in the polls, it has done what any party would do: searched within itself for solutions, focused its message and trained its sights on the incumbent government, the B.C. NDP.
Of course, I'm only kidding.
Taking on an incumbent government that crushed you in the last election requires discipline, rigour and a purpose the electorate can identify with. Currently, BC United has none of the above, so it has chosen a different path: casting aspersions on the BC Conservatives and their enormous lead in the polls. It is a curious path for the rebranded Liberals and their leader, Kevin Falcon, who previously declared his return to politics was to save the province from the NDP.
That is why the recent strategic turn to attack the other party of the right is so eyebrow-raising. Recently, BC United has been dumping what is clearly election-period opposition research on BC Conservative candidates months out from an election. The tactic reeks of desperation. No party would do this so early unless there were no options left.
In any given election, most parties end up having to ditch a candidate or two because of odd or distasteful past comments or actions. This is nothing new. Whether it's an NDP candidate tossing out wild accusations to hurt others'credibility, a Conservative candidate peeing in coffee cups in the course of his day job or Liberals candidates in Ontario and B.C. making off-colour comments, you can count on a few embarrassing moments from the past to surface and cause candidates to be ejected. (Interestingly, one of the few non-ejectable offences seems to be a party leader wearing blackface, but that is by the by).
As a rule, any embarrassing or ejection-worthy information is kept by opposing parties until the election is actually underway and regular people are paying attention. The embarrassing information is then dumped in an attempt to wrong-foot one's opposition, smear them as loonies with no standards and cause maximum electoral damage. Timed properly, this can work. At other times no one cares. Releasing information in late May when the election is in October is textbook no-onecares time, and is only done if you're extra desperate.
To be as fair as one can to the sinking BC United clan, the accusations levelled deserve some scrutiny. Two frankly wacky vaccine skeptics in ex-candidates Stephen Malthouse and Jan Webb are not the desideratum of any legislature. The pronouncements on gender and Black Lives Matter by Damon Scrase, now an ex-candidate too, are certainly controversial and not compatible with a serious party.
Yet even in their apparent concern for intelligence and diversity, BC United is disingenuous. Malthouse was dropped at the end of March for his vaccine views. Scrase, while not ejected by the party, has already left (presumably under pressure from within). Webb was also removed early in the year. All in all, this represents little more than the standard candidate culling that happens in most elections. The BC United attempt to make these three obscure figures the emblem of an unserious BC Conservative party is a Hail Mary with almost zero chance of success.
The real question for any non-NDPer in B.C. is, why the right is fighting itself? NDP strategists are presumably doing cartwheels down their halls.
In B.C., the political equation has always been as simple as it is bedevilling to those who try to solve it. Unite the right to beat the NDP. That's it. B.C. is a fundamentally free enterprise and centre-right-leaning province that manages to offer itself up to the NDP every now and again when the centre-rights decide a collective trepanning is preferable to the betterment of the whole province. It's an old story out west: a bickering right means an NDP premier.
Falcon and BC United assumed that an old message with old candidates was enough for British Columbians. It has proved not to be so. British Columbians, like many Canadians, are looking for legitimate change.
BC United wanted to be a big tent party where all were welcome. That laudable goal, however, lost itself in its own meaninglessness. No political party welcomes all. They welcome a group of people with a message and a purpose. BC United has failed to communicate what that is to B.C. The BC Conservatives - regardless of whether you like their message - have managed to stake out clear territory to the right of the centre. B.C. is responding in kind.
None of this reality seems to matter to BC United or Falcon at the moment. In a recent interview, Falcon chose not to demarcate BC United from the NDP. Instead, he called the BC Conservatives names, commented on "how not ready they are for prime time" and accused them of running "clown car" candidates. His message to British Columbians: "Don't be confused and don't be conned."
The current BC United strategy is walking extremely close to calling BC Conservative supporters a "basket of deplorables." That didn't work well the first time it was tried, and it's hard to see how it will perform any better this time around.
National Post Adam Pankratz is a lecturer at the University of British Columbia's Sauder School of Business.

Title: BC United's desperate attack on Conservatives

Document 468 of 500

Holding election while Trudeau in power would be smart move for Ford

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/holding-election-while-trudeau-power-would-be/docview/3062760329/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Holding+election+while+Trudeau+in+power+would+be+smart+move+for+Ford&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was a brief outbreak of election fever in Ontario this week after Premier Doug Ford refused to rule out an early election, even though the official election date is still two years away. Most of the victims of this fever were journalists and unnamed political insiders, all craving some excitement.
The speculation started when Ford gave a cagey answer to a dumb question. Ford had just announced that a big expansion of beer and wine retailing would start this year, not in 2026 as previously announced.
The rush to broaden alcohol retailing, delivering on a promise made six years ago, made reporters wonder if the move might mean an early election was in the offing. It's hard to imagine why it would be, but the question put the premier in a bit of a box. If he ruled out an early election and then ultimately called one at some point, he would be accused of going back on his word. When he ducked the question, speculation started to mount.
On Wednesday, Ford narrowed the scope of that speculation when he told Ottawa talk-radio host Bill Carroll that an election would definitely not come this year. Of course, that still leaves spring of 2025 as a possibility.
The only thing that's missing in the early election narrative is a compelling reason why Ford would even want to go to the polls early. Sure, the province's NDP and Liberal leaders look scarcely more viable than their vanquished predecessors, but that's unlikely to change any time soon.
Bonnie Crombie, the newish Liberal leader, has an apparent gift for saying stupid things. In response to the election rumours, she said, "They have a majority government. I can't envision the reason why they would want to call an early election. It's very disrespectful, is very anti-democratic."
After you figure out why an election is anti-democratic, ask yourself why a person who fancies herself the next premier of Ontario would not have cheered the prospect of an early vote. Surely Crombie should have taken the opportunity to tell people what a great alternative her party offered and encouraged Ford to take her on.
Crombie didn't do much better in her criticism of the earlier timeline on expanded alcohol retailing, which she called "a blatant attempt by Doug Ford to buy the next election."
Buy the election? How does that work? The retail expansion benefits the government, not drinkers. Ford says the larger retail network would bring in between $895 million to $1.6 billion for the province. People will be able to buy wine and beer in more locations, but the government still determines what can be sold and sets a minimum price.
The PC perspective is that delivering on the booze promise is not the harbinger of an election, but just one more step toward a future platform that will highlight promises kept. It is a step, but an exceptionally small one.
If the Ford government wanted to please voters by keeping an old election promise, it would have done better to follow through on the 2018 pledge to cut the second income tax bracket by 20 per cent, saving people $786 a year. Instead, the government is running radio ads touting savings from cancelling the Drive Clean emissions test, a change it made in 2018. And what about that six-yearold promise to balance the budget? While the alcohol retail change won't trigger an early election, the PCs might nevertheless want to consider a vote next spring.
Whether the PCs call an election next spring or June 2026, there will likely be both a federal and a provincial election within a year of one another (the federal election is scheduled for October 2025). Elections drain volunteer and donor resources. Better to be the first to hold an election than the second.
There is also some value to calling the provincial election while Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is still in office. The prime minister's general incompetence and over-the-top progressivism causes constant damage to the Liberal brand. To make it even better, he's dragging the witless NDP leader Jagmeet Singh down with him. Ford couldn't find a better guy than Trudeau to make sure the words Liberal and NDP continue to make Ontarians wince.
As a bonus, Trudeau is so loathed right now that he makes Ford look good in comparison.
Some argue that the PCs will want to go early because a Pierre Poilievre government would quickly become unpopular and hurt the provincial party's chances. It's possible, but that thinking depends on Ontarians giving Poilievre a majority, then immediately turning around and electing the Liberals or NDP provincially. It seems unlikely.
It's easy to overthink election strategy. The simplest one is best. Do a competent job and keep your promises. In Canadian politics, that defines overachieving.
National Post Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Document 474 of 500

Canadian, 93, wanted to give her kids a gift. Instead she got slapped with $40K in capital gains tax bill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-93-wanted-give-her-kids-gift-instead-she/docview/3062687224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canadian%2C+93%2C+wanted+to+give+her+kids+a+gift.+Instead+she+got+slapped+with+%2440K+in+capital+gains+tax+bill&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A gift to her daughter and grandson has left a 93-year-old Ontario woman with sticker shock.
Liz Diachun told CTV News she was planning to give her family two lots on her farm property in Warkworth to help them establish homes. However, her lawyer informed her that despite the land being a gift, it still needed to be appraised at fair market value for tax purposes.
The two lots were appraised at $125,000 and $145,000, totalling $270,000, leaving Diachun with a tax bill of about $40,000, an amount she said she cannot pay.
"I'm on pension. How am I going to pay for that?" said Diachun. "I'm not one of the wealthy. I'm 93 years old. Who is going to give me a mortgage? Who is going to give me a loan?"
Diachun is hopeful she can still find a way to make the gifts happen but the clock is ticking. Beginning next month, the capital gains tax inclusion rate will increase from 50 per cent to 67 per cent for amounts over $250,000?. Here's what to know.
Why Freeland's delay in tabling capital gains tax has Canadians facing costly conundrums
Channelling NDP, Chrystia Freeland defends higher taxes on those who 'complain ... bitterly'
What is capital gains tax?
Capital gains tax applies to the profit made from selling a capital asset, such as stocks, real estate, or other investments.
The current system includes an inclusion rate of 50 per cent, meaning that only half of the capital gain is considered taxable income.
The taxable capital gain is taxed at personal income tax rates, which vary depending on an individual's total income and the tax bracket they fall into.
How is it changing?
Under the new federal budget, the inclusion rate will increase from 50 per cent to 66.5 per cent for capital gains exceeding $250,000 per year for individuals.
The changes are scheduled to take effect on June 25, though the Liberals have yet to introduce the legislation to increase the capital gains inclusion rate. The delay means Canadians like Diachun are in a tricky situation as they make decisions about selling, or gifting, major assets.
Earlier this week Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said the legislative process will begin in the "coming weeks and certainly before the House rises (on June 21)."
In the meantime, Canadians are mulling major decisions without firm details about how the new policy might affect their tax bill.
For corporations and trusts, meanwhile, the new 66.5 per cent inclusion rate will apply to all capital gains, without the $250,000 threshold.
There will also be a one-time increase in the lifetime capital gains exemption for small business shares and qualifying farm or fishing properties, increasing from $1 million to $1.25 million.
When was capital gains tax established and how has it changed?
It was first introduced in 1972 as part of the tax reforms implemented by Finance Minister Edgar Benson, a member of Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau's government.
The tax was intended to broaden the tax base and tax income from investments similarly to income from labour. The rate when first implementation was 50 per cent, though it has fluctuated over the years, including jumping to 66 per cent in 1988 and 75 per cent in 1990.
In 2000, under Finance Minister Paul Martin, the rate dropped back to 50 per cent.
The upcoming changes are part of a strategy to make "the wealthiest Canadians pay their fair share," according to the Liberal government.

Document 475 of 500

'I've just had it up to here': Trudeau and Ford decry 'vile and despicable' antisemitic acts at Jewish schools

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ive-just-had-up-here-trudeau-ford-decry-vile/docview/3062684915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27I%27ve+just+had+it+up+to+here%27%3A+Trudeau+and+Ford+decry+%27vile+and+despicable%27+antisemitic+acts+at+Jewish+schools&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford decried the shootings in the past week at Toronto and Montreal Jewish schools and vowed to do everything in their power to catch and punish the perpetrators.
Trudeau started off his remarks Thursday morning at the grand opening of Sanofi's $800-million vaccine manufacturing facility in Toronto by saying that, just a short drive away, parents were dropping off their daughters at a Jewish girls' elementary school that was fired on by gunmen over the weekend.
Another Jewish school in Montreal was also hit by at least one bullet Wednesday night.
"I'm, of course, relieved that no one was hurt. I'm disgusted by these vile and despicable acts of antisemitism. It needs to stop now," said Trudeau.
"To Jewish parents and students across the country, we stand with you. We will always work to safeguard your right to live proudly Jewish lives in Canada."
Trudeau said the federal government is working with local law enforcement and will use "all available federal resources to ensure that the perpetrators are held to account."
"We cannot and will not let this antisemitism stand in Canada," he added.
Ford said he agreed with Trudeau and promised that the province would "throw every single resource" to catch the perpetrators, and "throw them in jail, throw away the key."
"Folks, let's cut to the chase here. What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? That is just unacceptable. Imagine that. Imagine a little kid in a school because they're from the Jewish faith, someone goes by and start shooting through the school windows?"
"These guys need to be caught. They need to be punished. They need to be thrown in jail, and I'll tell you, we have zero tolerance for this anywhere in Ontario," he said.
Terry Newman: Universities need to wake up to the reality of the anti-Israel campus movement
Bloc tables bill that would make it impossible for judges to throw out delayed violent crime cases
Federal Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Ya'ara Saks thanked Trudeau and Ford for their words of support and said that, as a Jewish MP, "allies matter."
"As a Jewish mother in this community, we have the right to be safe and feel safe each and every day," she said.
Trudeau and Ford weren't forthcoming with concrete details when asked by a reporter what resources their governments would use to jail the perpetrators, given that politicians are not supposed to direct the police's actions.
Trudeau pointed to his government's Security Infrastructure Program, which provides support for communities at risk of hate-related crimes and incidents at places of worship and schools, but he said there is "much more to do."
"The core thing that we have continued to call for is an end to hatred and intolerance in Canada, whether it be antisemitism or Islamophobia," he said.
"We are seeing that events overseas that are devastating and incredibly difficult to watch should not be spilling over into Canadians hating on other Canadians. We are all united in our strength and diversity and our desire for a better country in a better world."
Ford said that Ontario "will throw every single resource of increased funding for the police" and has "given thousands of dollars to places of worship."
And he had a clear message to potential newcomers to Canada.
"Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this. Simple as that. You want to come to Canada? You want to be a resident of Ontario? You get along with everyone. I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from."
Ford touted Ontario's diverse population as its "number one selling tool around the world."
"We have 110 nationalities here in Ontario, 200 languages being spoken, and guess what? Ninety-nine per cent of the people get along. There are wars going on all around the world. But we still get along," he said. "Enough is enough. I've just had it up to here."
"People outside those communities, they've had it too. They aren't used to this happening here in Ontario. I'm done with this stuff."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow said that the city has been conducting "safety walks" in collaboration with the police, local councillors and different communities. Chow said some places of worship or schools may consider putting up shatterproof glass, fencing or new lighting.
Ford expressed shock at hearing that shatterproof glass might be necessary in Ontario schools. "We have to have this in Ontario schools, like, where have we gone? Again, we're all just fed up with this nonsense. I'll tell you that. It shouldn't have to be that way."
He said that he was not criticizing Chow, adding " I love the mayor by the way."

Document 476 of 500

Adam Pankratz: Petty BC United won't save itself by attacking rising Conservatives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/adam-pankratz-petty-bc-united-wont-save-itself/docview/3062684755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Adam+Pankratz%3A+Petty+BC+United+won%27t+save+itself+by+attacking+rising+Conservatives&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As BC United tanks in the polls, it has done what any party would do: searched within itself for solutions, focused its message and trained its sights on the incumbent government, the B.C. NDP.
Of course, I'm only kidding.
Taking on an incumbent government that crushed you in the last election requires discipline, rigour and a purpose the electorate can identify with. Currently, BC United has none of the above, so it has chosen a different path: casting aspersions on the BC Conservatives and their enormous lead in the polls. It is a curious path for the rebranded Liberals and their leader, Kevin Falcon, who previously declared his return to politics was to save the province from the NDP
That is why the recent strategic turn to attack the other party of the right is so eyebrow-raising. Recently, BC United has been dumping what is clearly election-period opposition research on BC Conservative candidates months out from an election. The tactic reeks of desperation. No party would do this so early unless there were no options left.
In any given election, most parties end up having to ditch a candidate or two because of odd or distasteful past comments or actions. This is nothing new. Whether it's an NDP candidate tossing out wild accusations to hurt others' credibility, a Conservative candidate peeing in coffee cups in the course of his day job or Liberals candidates in Ontario and B.C. making off-colour comments, you can count on a few embarrassing moments from the past to surface and cause candidates to be ejected. (Interestingly, one of the few non-ejectable offences seems to be a party leader wearing blackface, but that is by the by).
As a rule, any embarrassing or ejection-worthy information is kept by opposing parties until the election is actually underway and regular people are paying attention. The embarrassing information is then dumped in an attempt to wrong-foot one's opposition, smear them as loonies with no standards and cause maximum electoral damage. Timed properly, this can work. At other times no one cares. Releasing information in late May when the election is in October is textbook no-one-cares time, and is only done if you're extra desperate.
To be as fair as one can to the sinking BC United clan, the accusations levelled deserve some scrutiny. Two frankly wacky vaccine skeptics in ex-candidate Stephen Malthouse and candidate Jan Webb are not the desideratum of any legislature. The pronouncements on gender and Black Lives Matter by Damon Scrase, now an ex-candidate too, are certainly controversial and not compatible with a serious party.
Yet even in their apparent concern for intelligence and diversity, BC United is disingenuous. Malthouse was dropped at the end of March for his vaccine views Scrase, while not ejected by the party, has already left (presumably under pressure from within). Webb remains a candidate. But, all in all, this represents little more than the standard candidate culling that happens in most elections. The BC United attempt to make these three obscure figures the emblem of an unserious BC Conservative party is a Hail Mary with almost zero chance of success.
The real question for any non-NDPer in B.C. is, why the right is fighting itself? NDP strategists are presumably doing cartwheels down their halls.
In B.C., the political equation has always been as simple as it is bedevilling to those who try to solve it. Unite the right to beat the NDP. That's it. B.C. is a fundamentally free enterprise and centre-right-leaning province that manages to offer itself up to the NDP every now and again when the centre-rights decide a collective trepanning is preferable to the betterment of the whole province. It's an old story out west: a bickering right means an NDP premier.
Falcon and BC United assumed that an old message with old candidates was enough for British Columbians. It has proved not to be so. British Columbians, like many Canadians, are looking for legitimate change.
BC United wanted to be a big tent party where all were welcome. That laudable goal, however, lost itself in its own meaninglessness. No political party welcomes all. They welcome a group of people with a message and a purpose. BC United has failed to communicate what that is to B.C. The BC Conservatives - regardless of whether you like their message - have managed to stake out clear territory to the right of the centre. B.C is responding in kind.
None of this reality seems to matter to BC United or Falcon at the moment. In a recent interview, Falcon chose not to demarcate BC United from the NDP. Instead, he called the BC Conservatives names, commented on "how not ready they are for prime time" and accused them of running "clown car" candidates. His message to British Columbians: "Don't be confused and don't be conned"
The current BC United strategy is walking extremely close to calling BC Conservative supporters a "basket of deplorables." That didn't work well the first time it was tried, and it's hard to see how it will perform any better this time around.
National Post
Adam Pankratz is a lecturer at the University of British Columbia's Sauder School of Business.
Adam Pankratz: BC United killed its electoral chances by rejecting conservatism
Adam Pankratz: Government via virtue signal can't last in B.C.

FIRST READING: Canada (again) endorses a Hamas report without waiting for evidence

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-canada-again-endorses-hamas-report/docview/3062413626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Canada+%28again%29+endorses+a+Hamas+report+without+waiting+for+evidence&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign up here.
TOP STORY
For the second time in the Gazan conflict, the Trudeau government immediately jumped on Hamas reports that Israel was intentionally targeting civilians, only for evidence to emerge that the terrorist group may not have been telling the full truth.
The incident in question occurred Sunday night when a large explosion and fire was recorded at an encampment housing Palestinians refugees near the city of Rafah.
The Hamas-affiliated health ministry in Gaza, Gaza civil defence agency and Gaza media office all reported the explosion as the result of an intentional Israeli strike on a marked refugee area. Hamas's military wing called it a "Zionist massacre of civilians," and used it as justification for launching a barrage of rockets into Israel.
Palestine Red Crescent - a Palestinian humanitarian group that routinely refers to Israel as "the occupation" - reported that a "large number of martyrs" had been created after "Israeli aircraft have shelled several tents in this area." At least 45 people were killed and more than 200 injured, according to various media reports.
The Palestine Red Crescent ambulance crews are transporting a large number of martyrs and injured individuals following the occupation's targeting of the displaced persons' tents near the United Nations headquarters northwest of #Rafah #Gaza
- PRCS (@PalestineRCS) May 26, 2024
It was these initial reports that drove media accounts of the explosion as being the aftermath of an "Israeli strike on Rafah camp," in the words of the Washington Post
They also drove official Canadian pleas for an immediate end to the Israeli operation.
"We are horrified by strikes that killed Palestinian civilians in Rafah," was the response of Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, who then demanded an "immediate ceasefire."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh even went so far as to call the Rafah explosion evidence of "genocide," and called for Canadian sanctions on Israel and seemed to imply that Canada, by saying Canada should back the International Court of Justice and International Criminal Court, should support the attempt of an International Criminal Court prosecutor to have Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu arrested.
The immediate response of Israeli authorities was to call the refugee camp fires an accident. Netanyahu called it a "tragic mishap" and an Israel Defense Forces (IDF) spokesman called it a "devastating incident, which we did not expect."
The IDF did strike a compound in the area on Sunday night, killing Hamas commanders Yassin Rabia and Khaled Najjar.
On Tuesday, the IDF released a satellite image asserting that the target they struck was "a closed structure away from the tent area." According to the image, it was roughly 180 meters from the Al-Mawasi Humanitarian Area, in which the fires occurred and civilians were killed.
In an English-language press conference, the IDF said the strike had been carried out with precision munitions that would not have been able to generate the level of damage seen in videos of the camp that night.
This was backed up by CNN analysis published on Wednesday, which identified the remnants of a GBU-39 small diameter bombs at the site. Made by Boeing, it's explicitly marketed as a "precision-strike" weapon that minimizes "collateral damage."
Rear-Adm. Daniel Hagari, chief military spokesman for the IDF, said Tuesday that the damage to the camp was the result of "secondary explosions," possibly from unknown weapons caches ignited by the strike.
Official Israeli sources say the secondary explosions theory is backed up by videos of the fire taken by Gazan civilians, which show multiple blasts following the initial strike.
In one widely circulated video, a narrator can be heard speculating in Arabic that the fire was due to a strike on "a vehicle filled with ammo and weapons."
NEW: Video captured just after the attack in Rafah last night provides revealing details.
The Gazan narrator explicitly mentions that the target of the Israeli attack was a Hamas jeep loaded with weapons.
At 21 seconds into the video, secondary explosions are visible,...
- Eitan Fischberger (@EFischberger) May 27, 2024
Canada's response to the whole saga was very similar to the Trudeau government's official reaction to an Oct. 17 explosion in the courtyard of the Al-Ahli Arab Hospital in Gaza City.
At that time, Joly jumped on initial media accounts - derived from Hamas-affiliated reports - that the explosion was the result of an intentional Israeli strike.
"Bombing a hospital is an unthinkable act, and there is no doubt that doing so is absolutely illegal," she wrote in a post to X.com.
Soon after, analysis by Israel, the U.S. and multiple conflict watchers concluded that the explosion had almost certainly been the result of a misfired Palestinian rocket that had been intended for Israel. What's more, Hamas accounts that the explosion had killed more than 500 were found to be wildly overblown.
The recent Canadian reaction to the Rafah explosion stood in sharp contrast to that of the United States. Although the administration of U.S. President Joe Biden has been extremely critical of Israel's Rafah operation - the refugee camps there shelter roughly 1.3 million Palestinians - U.S. authorities steered clear of blaming the explosions on the IDF.
"Israel has a right to go after Hamas, and we understand this strike killed two senior Hamas terrorists who are responsible for attacks against Israeli civilians," said a White House spokesperson earlier this week.
Trudeau might have saved his marriage by quitting, and other revelations from a new book
What is flagpoling in Canada, and why is Chuck Schumer angry about it?
IN OTHER NEWS
At a recent gathering of the House of Commons environmental committee, the NDP's Laurel Collins reacted badly to a witness's assertion that climate policy is sometimes motivated by "hysterics." "Hysteria refers to something that is wildly emotional," she said, adding that it was a sexist term. Collins then apologized for her visible emotion before declaring "climate emergencies are not gender neutral; the degradation of ecosystems disproportionately impacts women and girls." The meeting was about climate impacts on the Canadian financial system, and the "hysterics" line was uttered by Terrence Keeley, CEO of the Impact Evaluation Lab. Specifically, Keeley had said in his opening comments, "business and finance have crucial roles to play in forging national and transnational outcomes - including greater social inclusivity and environmental sustainability - but their most appropriate roles have become increasingly maligned and misunderstood as climate hysterics have become more commonplace."
This is the Toronto constituency office of Liberal MP Julie Dzerowicz, with the unknown vandals apparently misspelling "Rafah." Not for the first time, anti-Israel protesters have seemed to target an MP who is actually pretty vocal in her condemnation of Israel. At about the time this was happening, Dzerowicz was issuing a statement calling for an "immediate ceasefire" in Gaza that would have the ultimate effect of leaving Hamas in place.
A new Leger poll commissioned by Postmedia has found that nearly half of Canadians believe Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is refusing to resign simply because he likes being prime minister. Respondents were asked why Trudeau is remaining prime minister, and 47 per cent agreed with the explanation of "he likes being prime minister and doesn't want to leave." Just 23 per cent of respondents agreed with the reason that Trudeau usually cites for why he's not resigning; "he has more policies he wants to implement."
In a bid to combat "menstrual stigma," the Canadian government inaugurated an official commemoration of Menstrual Hygiene Day. It mostly involved Liberal MPs (such as cabinet minister Seamus O'Regan, above) wearing bracelets consisting of 23 white beads and five red beads - the red beads serving to approximating the length of a typical menstrual cycle. To this, Ontario MPP Goldie Ghamari declared on social media that if any men wore such a bracelet to her parliament she would "tear it off their wrists myself." The B.C. capital, meanwhile, marked Menstrual Hygiene Day by illuminating its city hall in red and hoisting a special flag.

Document 478 of 500

Kelly McParland: Let federal bureaucrats work from home - anywhere in the country

Author: McParland, Kelly

Publication info: McParland, Kelly. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kelly-mcparland-let-federal-bureaucrats-work-home/docview/3062395442/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Kelly+McParland%3A+Let+federal+bureaucrats+work+from+home+-+anywhere+in+the+country&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McParland%2C+Kelly&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Things are getting rough for Canada's federal civil servants. Not all their problems are of their own making. It's almost tempting to feel sorry for them.
They're an easy group to lampoon, if not always fairly. They're better paid than people in similar jobs outside the government. They get better benefits, better pensions and they're almost impossible to fire.
Thanks to the Trudeau government's hiring binge, there's more of them than ever before in Canada's history. There were 257,034 in 2015, and 357,247 as of last year. The surge is an offshoot of the Liberals' spending binge, which continues apace. Yet they have less and less to do, because the same government that hired them doesn't trust them enough to handle the programs it dreams up for them. Instead, tens of billions of dollars have gone to consultancy firms and outside contractors to do the work the public service used to handle. And when those programs go awry - as they've done with disturbing frequency under the Liberals - the politicians hide in their offices while bureaucrats are dragged before public inquiries to be humiliated by opposition members of Parliament who would much rather be questioning the political masters who should be the ones accepting responsibility but are too craven to do so.
It's an ugly situation, and it's not getting better. The Trudeau government has done more to concentrate power in the Prime Minister's Office than any previous regime. It likes to craft its own policies, favouring those designed more for show than effect, then dodges the blame when the ill-advised and poorly-designed programs blow up on it. The whole idea of a permanent civil service is to have experienced people with strong administrative and operational backgrounds able to offer objective advice to johnny-come-lately politicians who think that getting elected makes them experts on everything from the first day they take office, even if they totally lack a background in the complex issues for which they're suddenly responsible.
Yet it's hard to feel entirely sympathetic. It's not like the government had a hard time finding tens of thousands of new hires to add to the ranks of bureaucrats it inherited. No one had to twist arms to find people happy to take the jobs, yet the moaning from those same ranks never seems to cease. There's always something that isn't 100 per cent satisfactory, the latest woe being widespread discontent over the Treasury Board's demand that public employees start showing up at least three days a week in the offices that were designed to house them and that pepper downtown Ottawa. The city, considered one of the country's most pleasant, is filled with parks, galleries, museums and attractive, popular neighbourhoods and has proven immune to economic downturns.
The bureaucrats' unions are outraged at what they call "arbitrary and one-size fits all policy changes" that were dropped on them without warning and are demanding "a fair, well-defined approach to hybrid work that considers health, safety and operational efficiency while delivering the services Canadians rely on." They question why people who have been successfully working from home under temporary conditions dictated by the COVID crisis should be forced to once again start commuting to little cubicles in faceless federal buildings, many of which the government is keen to sell off in any case.
Sharon DeSousa, executive vice-president of the Public Service Alliance of Canada, recently aired her complaints about the hybrid model to an Ottawa radio station: "They're trying to find space in cafeterias and hallways and trying to hold meetings. It's not working, it's not consistent, and before they move to a three-day work week, they should have fixed the two-day work week." It's no secret that one motive for the decree is the city's eagerness to bring some life to downtown streets that have never been known for their vibrancy and have become even quieter and emptier since office workers started staying home.
Fair enough. Not everyone is keen on the time, expense and annoyances involved in slogging off to the office to set up the same laptop and perform the same duties they could as easily have done from home. Yet within the body of their beefing lives a potential alternative: if it's not necessary for Ottawa's army of government employees to work from the office, why is it necessary for them even to work from Ottawa? Canada is filled with people who could do as much from smaller centres where costs are lower, homes are more affordable, traffic is less onerous and the income would do much to support local economies.
Past governments have made efforts to decentralize services, moving offices to St. John's or Winnipeg, with limited success. A 1994 government study assessing 30 years of reorganization efforts concluded that "the reformed management practices and structures repeatedly advocated had not materialized in any meaningful way." Another three decades later, its ranks have been increased, decreased and increased again, yet Ottawa today remains overwhelmingly the centre of Bureaucrat World and its ever-disgruntled populace.
So change it. Anything that can be done from home in Kanata or Nepean can be done from Truro, Lethbridge, Moose Jaw or Chilliwack. No need for tiring commutes, unreliable transit, echoing streets or other such horrors. Simply decree that any new hire that can be done from home has to be done somewhere other than Ottawa.
Municipal authorities would undoubtedly protest, but so what? It's not like they're famed for their dynamism and skill. Who needed a national emergency to get rid of a bunch of horn-honking truckers? Get the bureaucrats out of Ottawa and the city might be forced to drum up other ways to support itself, possibly even involving private enterprise, entrepreneurial skill and something other than a steady supply of reliable government largesse.
All that's required is the will. It's not entirely clear what Pierre Poilievre means when he says he'll get rid of the gatekeepers, but redistributing public employees across the country and away from a comfy, complacent world that empties at 4 p.m. and needs a "Nightlife Economy Action Plan" and a $112,000-per-year "night mayor" to run it can't be a bad start.
Best of all, no one outside Ottawa would complain in the slightest.
National Post
Jesse Kline: Trudeau's bloated public service monster turns on him
Randall Denley: A $14B victory for pampered public sector workers over Ontario taxpayers

Document 479 of 500

Courts can't save Jama; It was an activist stunt by her lawyers to try

Author: Cosh, Colby

Publication info: Cosh, Colby. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/courts-cant-save-jama-was-activist-stunt-her/docview/3062295710/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Courts+can%27t+save+Jama%3B+It+was+an+activist+stunt+by+her+lawyers+to+try&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Cosh%2C+Colby&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Tuesday morning, a three-judge Ontario Superior Court panel published its widely foreseen ruling against Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama, the pro-Palestine chauvinist who was censured by the legislature last October. Three days after the Hamas murder and kidnapping rampage that sickened the world, Jama sent out a tweet denouncing Israel, calling for an end to "all occupation of Palestinian land" and to the Jewish state's "apartheid" policies.
It was nothing she hadn't been saying for decades, and if she said it now, with the memory of the October 7 pogrom fading fast on the political left, there might not be so much fuss. In the moment it looked like appalling wormbrained opportunism even to her NDP colleagues, who soon ushered her out of their caucus. The day after Jama's offending tweet, she apologized and found the strength to write "To be clear, I unequivocally condemn terrorism by Hamas on thousands of Israeli civilians."
Despite the new-found clarity (which was mixed with denials of the use of sexual violence by Hamas), the legislative assembly voted on Oct. 18 to censure Jama and to authorize the Speaker not to recognize her on the floor. She is still now condemned to invisibility in debates of the full assembly, although she is allowed to participate in committees and vote.
This led her to launch a lawsuit against the Speaker seeking judicial review of his decision to allow a vote on the censure motion. Despite the patent improbability of her cause, she found lawyers willing to go looking for unknown holes in the ancient constitutional axiom of parliamentary privilege.
It turns out that the rules protecting a legislature's right to regulate its proceedings are pretty thoroughly copper-bottomed. The Superior Court decision is mostly a repetitive recitation of quotes to this effect from earlier rulings and authorities; one feels it could have been a great deal shorter. It's established black-letter law that an assembly can penalize a member for outside acts or deeds: "The legislature ... has the power to impose rules and sanctions pertaining to conduct that occurs outside their chambers."
And while a court can potentially decide that some actions of a legislature (and a speaker acting on its behalf) are not shielded circumstantially by privilege, the regulation of debate is the innermost core of that privilege. It's the substantive central reason it has been recognized and upheld against all comers for 300 years.
Jama's expensive and famous human rights lawyers know all this, and they did not recoil from wasting a court's time and resources in a partisan cause. This is approved behaviour in their social circle and they will wear their cataclysmic defeat on the constitutional merits as a badge of heroism. As a matter of mere sincerity, one hopes they were honest with their client about the inane hopelessness of her suit, and that she was capable, as a legislator, of understanding it.
National Post

Title: May 29, 2024 (Page FP10)

Publication title: National Post (2011-); Toronto, Canada

Volume: 26

First page: FP10

Number of pages: 1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 29, 2024

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Document 487 of 500

FIRST READING: Conservative strategist says blackface scandal caused them to lose 2019 election

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-reading-conservative-strategist-says/docview/3061658787/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=FIRST+READING%3A+Conservative+strategist+says+blackface+scandal+caused+them+to+lose+2019+election&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First Reading is a daily newsletter keeping you posted on the travails of Canadian politicos, all curated by the National Post's own Tristin Hopper. To get an early version sent directly to your inbox, sign up here.
TOP STORY
In a recent podcast, senior Conservative strategist Fred DeLorey said that the 2019 blackface scandal actually caused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to win re-election.
"I think that issue actually got him re-elected because Conservatives just went on that like a dog on a bone. They dropped all their messaging on affordability and the economy and they focused on blackface," DeLorey said in a May 27 episode of his podcast On Background
The occasion was an interview with political journalist Stephen Maher, whose new book The Prince contains the most detailed account to date of how the Liberal campaign reacted to the scandal.
On Sept. 19, 2019 - just eight days after the writs had been dropped for the 43rd federal election - Time Magazine published photos showing Trudeau wearing brownface at an Arabian Nights-themed party at West Point Grey Academy, the Vancouver private high school where Trudeau worked as a teacher.
This spawned the release of multiple other photos showing Trudeau wearing full-body ethnic makeup at points throughout his 20s, including one time where he wore blackface to perform an impression of singer Harry Belafonte.
Maher's book includes the detail that Trudeau had told his advisors that all these photos existed, but they had simply carried on with the hope that they would never be published.
Maher's own take on the scandal, as written in The Prince, is that if they had come out in the 2015 election they would have scotched Trudeau's chances of ever becoming prime minister in the first place.
"They would have reinforced the Conservative message about Trudeau and raised doubts within his multicultural progressive coalition," wrote Maher
But when they came up in 2019, DeLorey's view was that they caused Conservatives to lean on the notion that Trudeau was a racist, when most voters were more inclined to the view that he was just an idiot.
"They tried to make the ballot question, 'Is Justin Trudeau racist or not?' He's not. No one believes that. And we lost our message track." DeLorey told Maher. "Yes, he was a dumb kid, but it doesn't mean he was racist."
According to the polls, however, the Conservatives were always the underdog in the 2019 election, and the blackface scandal did surprisingly little to alter Canadian voting intentions.
A Sept. 17 Leger poll issued on the eve of the blackface photos being made public showed the Liberals polling at 34 per cent to the Conservatives' 33 per cent.
Two weeks later - after images of Trudeau in blackface had exploded around the world - another Leger poll delivered the near-identical result of having the Liberals and Conservatives tied at 34 per cent.
And both Leger polls were right on target with the final voting results. In terms of popular vote, the Conservatives received 34.34 per cent of ballots to the Liberals' 33.12 per cent.
Despite this plurality in votes, the Conservatives won just 121 seats to the Liberals' 157.
The gap was explained by what Liberal strategists would later term "vote efficiency": The Liberals were able to win more close ridings where the vote was split by multiple parties, while the Conservatives mostly strengthened their vote share in places that were already sending Conservative MPs to Ottawa.
DeLorey, now an Ottawa-based PR consultant, was a Conservative organizer as far back as 2004, when he was involved with Stephen Harper's successful leadership bid. DeLorey was an unsuccessful Tory candidate in Peter MacKay's old riding of Central Nova, N.S., in 2015, and in the 2021 federal election would be the Conservatives' national campaign director.
At the height of the blackface scandal in 2019, DeLorey appeared in several media interviews providing the Conservative perspective on the issue.
In a panel discussion on Global News, DeLorey differed from the show's other progressive-leaning guests in not bringing up the racial undertones of the blackface photos. Rather, he focused on how Trudeau had been initially cagey about how many other photos there were.
"At the end of the day, I think this campaign is becoming an issue of trust," he said. "Mr. Trudeau is continually showing that he can't be trusted, that he's not being honest."
IN OTHER NEWS
This is Richard Lyall, president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario, pictured at the exact moment where a Conservative MP asked him if it was possible to fulfill the Trudeau government's housing plan of building two million extra homes by 2030. Tracy Gray, vice chair of the Standing Committee on Human Resources, noted that the Trudeau government's recently released housing pledges would require Canadian builders to start cranking out a new home every 57 seconds. When she then asked Lyall how realistic this was, he replied "not a chance" (sparking laughter from the committee).
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation just unearthed documents showing that Parks Canada spent $12 million to shoot 84 deer The deer in question were invasive European fallow deer that lived on Sidney Island, a Parks Canada-controlled piece of land off the B.C. Coast. Twelve million dollars only represents about 11 minutes' worth of federal spending this year, but it's a helpful illustration of how the current government is able to spend ever-increasing quantities of money while service delivery keeps getting noticeably worse. A few points to note:
The cull could conceivably have been done for free simply by unleashing local hunters on the island (there really are no shortage of armed locals who like shooting deer).
The actual costs of deer eradication (a complex affair involving helicopters and foreign hunters on special permits) still only came to $4 million. The rest was allocated to things like salaries ($2.3 million), analysis and studies ($1.4 million) and "miscellaneous costs" ($3.3 million).
About a quarter of the animals shot by helicopter weren't actually European fallow deer, and were simply collateral damage.
Sol Mamakwa, an MPP in the Ontario Legislature, delivered a 10-minute address on Tuesday in the language of Anishininiimowin, also known as Oji-Cree. Spoken by about 15,000 people in Northern Ontario, Anishininiimowin is the sixth most-spoken Indigenous language in Canada. The top spot is held by Cree, with about 100,000 speakers, with Inuktitut in second place. Tuesday was the first time an Indigenous language has been used to officially address the Ontario legislature, but it happens all the time in Nunavut, where Inuktitut is one of two official languages recognized by the territorial assembly.
Get all of these insights and more into your inbox by signing up for the First Reading newsletter here

Subject: Native languages; Conservatism; Deer; Racism; Inuktitut language; Books; Prime ministers; Elections; Scandals

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 488 of 500

Colby Cosh: Courts can't save pro-Palestinian MPP Sarah Jama from parliamentary exile

Author: Cosh, Colby

Publication info: Cosh, Colby. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/colby-cosh-courts-cant-save-pro-palestinian-mpp/docview/3061633826/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Colby+Cosh%3A+Courts+can%27t+save+pro-Palestinian+MPP+Sarah+Jama+from+parliamentary+exile&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Cosh%2C+Colby&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Tuesday morning, a three-judge Ontario Superior Court panel published its widely foreseen ruling against Hamilton Centre MPP Sarah Jama, the pro-Palestine chauvinist who was censured by the legislature last October. Three days after the Hamas murder and kidnapping rampage that sickened the world, Jama sent out a tweet denouncing Israel, calling for an end to "all occupation of Palestinian land" and to the Jewish state's "apartheid" policies.
It was nothing she hadn't been saying for decades, and if she said it now, with the memory of the October 7 pogrom fading fast on the political left, there might not be so much fuss. In the moment it looked like appalling wormbrained opportunism even to her NDP colleagues, who soon ushered her out of their caucus The day after Jama's offending tweet, she apologized and found the strength to write "To be clear, I unequivocally condemn terrorism by Hamas on thousands of Israeli civilians."
Despite the new-found clarity (which was mixed with denials of the use of sexual violence by Hamas), the legislative assembly voted on Oct. 18 to censure Jama and to authorize the Speaker not to recognize her on the floor. She is still now condemned to invisibility in debates of the full assembly, although she is allowed to participate in committees and vote.
This led her to launch a lawsuit against the Speaker seeking judicial review of his decision to allow a vote on the censure motion. Despite the patent improbability of her cause, she found lawyers willing to go looking for unknown holes in the ancient constitutional axiom of parliamentary privilege
It turns out that the rules protecting a legislature's right to regulate its proceedings are pretty thoroughly copper-bottomed The Superior Court decision is mostly a repetitive recitation of quotes to this effect from earlier rulings and authorities; one feels it could have been a great deal shorter. It's established black-letter law that an assembly can penalize a member for outside acts or deeds: "The legislature ... has the power to impose rules and sanctions pertaining to conduct that occurs outside their chambers."
And while a court can potentially decide that some actions of a legislature (and a speaker acting on its behalf) are not shielded circumstantially by privilege, the regulation of debate is the innermost core of that privilege. It's the substantive central reason it has been recognized and upheld against all comers for 300 years.
Jama's expensive and famous human rights lawyers know all this, and they did not recoil from wasting a court's time and resources in a partisan cause. This is approved behaviour in their social circle and they will wear their cataclysmic defeat on the constitutional merits as a badge of heroism. As a matter of mere sincerity, one hopes they were honest with their client about the inane hopelessness of her suit, and that she was capable, as a legislator, of understanding it.
National Post
Jonathan Kay: Just ignore Sarah Jama's keffiyeh and leave cancel culture to progressives
Jamie Sarkonak: It's up to voters to silence Ontario MPP Sarah Jama - not the government
