Document 489 of 500

Matthew Lau: Fixing health care requires markets, prices and profits

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/matthew-lau-fixing-health-care-requires-markets/docview/3061633622/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Matthew+Lau%3A+Fixing+health+care+requires+markets%2C+prices+and+profits&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Deputy Prime Minister and Finance MinisterChrystia Freeland spent much of last week insisting that enormous sums of federal spending will ensure a strong public health care system for years to come. Speaking in Ottawa on Tuesday, she said that this year "provinces and territories are receiving $52.1 billion from the federal government through the Canada Health Transfer. This is the largest amount ever." It will rise to nearly $55 billion next year and $57.4 billion the following year. Then in Toronto on Wednesday, Freeland gave a speech trumpeting the government's "historic $200 billion, 10-year health care plan."
Also last week, the federal government issued a news release that again cited the $52.1-billion and $200-billion figures, and added mention of $500 million for a youth mental health fund, $3.2 million to "strengthen supply management capacity for drugs and medical devices," and student loan forgiveness of up to $60,000 for doctors and $30,000 for nurses who work in rural and remote communities. In addition, it mentioned, without giving dollar amounts, the government's spending initiatives on national dental care, national pharmacare and taxpayer-funded contraceptives.
All of this increased spending, we are told, will "reduce backlogs, improve primary care, and cut wait times." The spending of many more billions of dollars will "mean shorter wait times for surgeries and in the emergency room. It's going to make it easier to see a doctor or a nurse practitioner so all Canadians can get the care they need." Why Canadians today are suffering from massive health care backlogs that need reducing, insufficient primary care that needs improving, lengthy wait times for surgery and in the emergency room that need shortening, and difficulties accessing doctors and nurse practitioners - all this after the Liberals have been in government for nearly nine years - Chrystia Freeland did not explain.
Should Canadians believe higher spending will really deliver the promised results? Politicians of all stripes and at all levels of government boast at every opportunity about how much public money they are spending on health care, yet access to health care is declining. In the Fraser Institute's annual survey in 2023, physicians reported a median wait time of 27.7 weeks between referral from a general practitioner and receipt of treatment - up from 18.3 weeks in 2015. More spending, but worse access. Someone once told me never to donate money to a hospital: the hospital staff certainly work hard to provide the best care they can for patients, but the money will be blown away by a bureaucrat or politician with the stroke of a pen or keyboard.
In her famous "the lady's not for turning" speech in 1980, less than two years after the United Kingdom's "winter of discontent," Margaret Thatcher observed, "If spending money like water was the answer to our country's problems, we would have no problems now. If ever a nation has spent, spent, spent and spent again, ours has. Today that dream is over. All of that money has got us nowhere, but it still has to come from somewhere."
Canadian health care well illustrates this point. Enormous government expenditures and expanded government involvement have not improved access to medical care; they have made taxpayers poorer and access worse - except, that is, to physician-assisted suicide, which is now so accessible and offered so readily that Canada has become infamous for it.
$160 billion says Trudeau doesn't buy his own carbon tax pitch
The Competition Bureau should leave airlines alone
Shawn Whatley, former Ontario Medical Association president, describes overbearing government control of health care in Canada: "The state dictates who can enter a clinic; whom doctors must see; who must be referred; what kinds of care must be offered; what notes must be taken; what notes must never be taken; data to report; and how to report the data." Canada's socialized health care, he continues, "forces doctors to be socialized with respect to bills, fees, services, reporting, and much more." No wonder higher spending fails to deliver better care. In a Post column earlier this year, Whatley argued that "In family practice, computers and paperwork have replaced patient care. Family doctors spend 19 hours on paperwork each week." The latest funding agreement between the federal and Ontario governments, he said, "represents more central planning designed to fix problems created by central planning."
Centrally planned health care does not work, and pouring more money into it will not make it work. Whether in health care or in other sectors, what drives the efficient provision of high-quality goods and services are prices, markets and profits. Without them Canadian health care is doomed to continue failing.

Subject: Primary care; Emergency medical care; Medical technology; Health care

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: deputy; prime; minister; finance; https; freeland

Document 490 of 500

Speaker insists his decisions unbiased; Commons

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/speaker-insists-his-decisions-unbiased-commons/docview/3061502559/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Speaker+insists+his+decisions+unbiased%3B+Commons&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA Speaker Greg Fergus insisted he is running an impartial House of Commons, as Liberal and NDP MPs appear ready to vote to keep him in the chair amid charges of partisanship.
Last week, Fergus was accused of partisanship after an invite went out to a Liberal party event that indicated he would appear and included an attack against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. The Liberal party has since apologized for the invite and said they included the language without Fergus's knowledge.
The Conservatives raised the Speaker's participation in the event as a point of privilege in the House of Commons and deputy Speaker Chris d'Entremont found on Monday there was a prima facie case Fergus had violated the rules of the House.
That set up Tuesday's debate, with the Conservatives calling for Fergus to resign, but the Liberals and the NDP moved to put a time limit on that debate, and a vote was expected later Tuesday.
The House of Commons is just weeks from rising for a summer recess and a lengthy debate on Fergus or a move to replace him as Speaker would make it difficult for the Liberals to pass several bills before the House rises.
"I hope all the decisions that I've pronounced in this place would be judged by any fair minded person as being decisions that can stand the test of time and that were impartial," Fergus said.
Conservative MP Jamil Jivani asked Fergus to explain more about the latest incident and why MPs should trust him to oversee the House of Commons.
"Before MPs vote tonight on if you should remain chair, why should we believe you?" Jivani asked.
Fergus has previously been criticized for appearing in a video that was played at the Ontario Liberal Party convention and has been accused of bias for his decision to remove Poilievre from the chamber earlier this month, when the Conservative leader described the prime minister as "wacko."
Conservative MP Michelle Rempel Garner went back further to 2016, long before Fergus became Speaker. Fergus was then parliamentary secretary to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and criticized the NDP after the "elbowgate" incident, when Trudeau elbowed former NDP MP Ruth Ellen Brosseau. Fergus said at the time the way the NDP was acting was "reminiscent of a dive in the 2014 World Cup."
Rempel Garner said that incident and his partisanship more generally prevents him from managing harassment issues in the House.
"Do you think opposition MPs would feel safe or empowered to report instances of any sort of harassment?" she asked Fergus. "You said I believe him, not I believe her and that is what is wrong with every workplace around the world."
Fergus said his comments in 2016 were not an indication that he didn't believe Brosseau, but how he felt the NDP were acting as a party.
National Post

Subject: Conservatism; Partisanship; Prime ministers; Members of Parliament

People: Fergus, Greg

Identifier / keyword: ottawa; speaker; fergus; insisted; running; impartial

Document 491 of 500

Fixing health care requires markets, prices and profits

Author: Lau, Matw

Publication info: Lau, Matw. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fixing-health-care-requires-markets-prices/docview/3061502485/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Fixing+health+care+requires+markets%2C+prices+and+profits&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.10&amp;au=Lau%2C+Matw&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chrystia Freeland spent much of last week insisting that enormous sums of federal spending will ensure a strong public health care system for years to come. Speaking in Ottawa on Tuesday, she said that this year "provinces and territories are receiving $52.1 billion from the federal government through the Canada Health Transfer. This is the largest amount ever." It will rise to nearly $55 billion next year and $57.4 billion the following year. Then in Toronto on Wednesday, Freeland gave a speech trumpeting the government's "historic $200 billion, 10-year health care plan."
Also last week, the federal government issued a news release that again cited the $52.1-billion and $200-billion figures, and added mention of $500 million for a youth mental health fund, $3.2 million to "strengthen supply management capacity for drugs and medical devices," and student loan forgiveness of up to $60,000 for doctors and $30,000 for nurses who work in rural and remote communities. In addition, it mentioned, without giving dollar amounts, the government's spending initiatives on national dental care, national pharmacare and taxpayer-funded contraceptives.
All of this increased spending, we are told, will "reduce backlogs, improve primary care, and cut wait times." The spending of many more billions of dollars will "mean shorter wait times for surgeries and in the emergency room. It's going to make it easier to see a doctor or a nurse practitioner so all Canadians can get the care they need." Why Canadians today are suffering from massive health care backlogs that need reducing, insufficient primary care that needs improving, lengthy wait times for surgery and in the emergency room that need shortening, and difficulties accessing doctors and nurse practitioners - all this after the Liberals have been in government for nearly nine years - Chrystia Freeland did not explain.
Should Canadians believe higher spending will really deliver the promised results? Politicians of all stripes and at all levels of government boast at every opportunity about how much public money they are spending on health care, yet access to health care is declining. In the Fraser Institute's annual survey in 2023, physicians reported a median wait time of 27.7 weeks between referral from a general practitioner and receipt of treatment - up from 18.3 weeks in 2015. More spending, but worse access. Someone once told me never to donate money to a hospital: the hospital staff certainly work hard to provide the best care they can for patients, but the money will be blown away by a bureaucrat or politician with the stroke of a pen or keyboard.
In her famous "the lady's not for turning" speech in 1980, less than two years after the United Kingdom's "winter of discontent," Margaret Thatcher observed, "If spending money like water was the answer to our country's problems, we would have no problems now. If ever a nation has spent, spent, spent and spent again, ours has. Today that dream is over. All of that money has got us nowhere but it still has to come from somewhere."
Canadian health care well illustrates this point.
Enormous government expenditures and expanded government involvement have not improved access to medical care; they have made taxpayers poorer and access worse - except, that is, to physician-assisted suicide, which is now so accessible and offered so readily that Canada has become infamous for it.
Shawn Whatley, former Ontario Medical Association president, describes overbearing government control of health care in Canada: "The state dictates who can enter a clinic; whom doctors must see; who must be referred; what kinds of care must be offered; what notes must be taken; what notes must never be taken; data to report; and how to report the data." Canada's socialized health care, he continues, "forces doctors to be socialized with respect to bills, fees, services, reporting, and much more." No wonder higher spending fails to deliver better care. In a Post column earlier this year, Whatley argued that "In family practice, computers and paperwork have replaced patient care. Family doctors spend 19 hours on paperwork each week." The latest funding agreement between the federal and Ontario governments, he said, "represents more central planning designed to fix problems created by central planning."
Centrally planned health care does not work, and pouring more money into it will not make it work. Whether in health care or in other sectors, what drives the efficient provision of high-quality goods and services are prices, markets and profits. Without them Canadian health care is doomed to continue failing.
Financial Post

Business indexing term: Subject: Health care industry Government spending

Document 492 of 500

Ontario's push for corner-store booze has dizzying tab; Beer Store's $225M payout hard to swallow

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-push-corner-store-booze-has-dizzying-tab/docview/3061502483/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+push+for+corner-store+booze+has+dizzying+tab%3B+Beer+Store%27s+%24225M+payout+hard+to+swallow&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Selley%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford promised Ontarians beer in corner stores, supermarkets and big-box stores, and by God he has delivered. As of Sept. 5, all Ontario convenience stores meeting eligibility criteria will be allowed to sell beer, wine, cider and premixed drinks. As of Oct. 31, the privilege will be extended to all grocery and big-box stores. The province says it expects as many as 8,500 new booze-procurement sites to come online under the new regime. By Ontario standards, it's absolutely revolutionary.
The new regime is also, of course, hilariously complicated. And absurdly, offensively expensive.
It is fair to describe the new regime as somewhat more competitive, and certainly more convenient. In addition to offering potentially thousands of new locations, supermarkets (including the roughly 450 already licensed) will be able to offer volume discounts on beer - that is, a 24-pack will cost less per bottle than a six-pack. This was a privilege hitherto reserved for The Beer Store, the American-, Belgian-and Japaneseowned conglomerate that dominated beer sales in Ontario from the end of Prohibition until fairly recently.
Private retailers will even be able to set their own prices, which until now has been considered blasphemy.
It is not fair to describe the new regime, as the government does, as an "open" market.
Near as I can tell, Ontario will by 2026 have the following retail environments in place:
The Beer Store. Smelly, surly and the best-available value. Only beer - no cider or mixed drinks. It's in the name.
LCBO locations. Government-run liquor stores retain their near-absolute monopoly on hard liquor sales, in addition to selling beer (especially craft beer, in which The Beer Store's owners aren't so interested), wine and everything else.
LCBO-and/or The Beer Store-branded "agency stores" in rural areas that sell everything the LCBO does, but operate inside of convenience stores, small supermarkets and other local businesses, and are staffed by non-government employees.
The existing supermarkets licensed to sell beer, cider and wine (and in rare cases all three!), plus scores of new outlets - the new 8,500 new locations.
The Beer Store maintains a monopoly (in urban areas) on wholesale for bars and restaurants and on refunding cans and bottles, although its new "master framework agreement" (MFA) doesn't even oblige it to maintain its current number of locations - which in urban areas have been dwindling rapidly. I'm a 17-minute walk from my nearest Beer Store. The house I grew up in, in the heart of midtown Toronto, is a 45-minute walk. I'm not shlepping a leaky garbage bag full of empty cans either distance.
(Thankfully, as of 2026, larger booze retailers will have to accept empties for return - but there's really no good reason to wait. Those L CBO -branded agency stores take empties back, no matter how small they are, as do depanneurs and supermarkets in Quebec.)
The most contentious part of this plan, and rightly so, is that the government is gifting The Beer Store $225 million - ostensibly to speed things up. The province's MFA with the foreign-owned beer-hemoth, which grants the company all manner of unearned privileges - and would otherwise rule out the reforms Ford's government desires - doesn't expire until Dec. 31, 2025.
So this is a sort of consolation prize to The Beer Store's owners, Anheuser-Busch InBev SA (market capitalization US$159 billion), Molson Coors (US$14.6 billion) and Sapporo Holdings Ltd. (US$3.6 billion).
Some have quite rightly asked: Is it really worth $225 million not to have to wait another 18 months? The answer is absolutely not. It's grotesque.
What the government should have done was simply cancel the existing MFA with The Beer Store via legislation, on the grounds it was a product of rank political corruption. (In fact it said it would do this, but legislation to that effect was never enacted.)
The beer lobby haunts the dreams of Queen's Park staffers the way the dairy lobby haunts the dreams of Parliament Hill staffers. In 2014, the Toronto Star's Martin Regg Cohn reported that "the Speaker of the Legislature (had been) unceremoniously summoned to the Premier's Office several years ago by a top staffer, where a Labatt operative was waiting to lecture him about the serving of rival craft beer on the premises."
Alternatively, the government could just have framed the $225-million bonus as a fair-and-square penalty for early termination of the MFA.
Instead, from a free-marketer's standpoint, it twisted the knife: The payout is ostensibly to help the mega-brewers "make the necessary investments over the next 19 months to support a stable transition to a more open and convenient marketplace, including funding to protect jobs across the province and to keep The Beer Store locations open for the continued availability of recycling and bottle return."
It's the world we live in, I suppose. We have infinity bazillion dollars for electric-vehicle battery plants, even when it works out to $500,000 or $1 million per job. Why shouldn't The Beer Store benefit as well? It's just that I seem to recall a guy campaigning for the Ontario Progressive Conservative party leadership on a promise of no more corporate welfare. Ford was his name.
Ontario's beer market needs to be set free, not constrained under a whole new Byzantine set of rules. But it is, undeniably, progress of a sort.
National Post cselley@postmedia.com

Document 493 of 500

Tory strategist says blackface scandal helped Trudeau

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-strategist-says-blackface-scandal-helped/docview/3061502099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Tory+strategist+says+blackface+scandal+helped+Trudeau&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recent podcast, senior Conservative strategist Fred DeLorey said that the 2019 blackface scandal actually caused Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to win re-election.
"I think that issue actually got him re-elected because Conservatives just went on that like a dog on a bone. They dropped all their messaging on affordability and the economy and they focused on blackface," DeLorey said in a May 27 episode of his podcast On Background.
The occasion was an interview with political journalist Stephen Maher, whose new book The Prince contains the most detailed account to date of how the Liberal campaign reacted to the scandal.
On Sept. 19, 2019 - just eight days after the writ had been dropped for the 43rd federal election - Time Magazine published photos showing Trudeau wearing brownface at an Arabian Nights-themed party at West Point Grey Academy, the Vancouver private high school where Trudeau worked as a teacher.
This spawned the release of multiple other photos showing Trudeau wearing full-body ethnic makeup at points throughout his 20s, including one time where he wore blackface to perform an impression of singer Harry Belafonte.
Maher's book includes the detail that Trudeau had told his advisers that all these photos existed, but they had simply carried on with the hope that they would never be published.
Maher's own take on the scandal, as written in The Prince, is that if they had come out in the 2015 election they would have scotched Trudeau's chances of ever becoming prime minister in the first place.
"They would have reinforced the Conservative message about Trudeau and raised doubts within his multicultural progressive coalition," wrote Maher.
But when they came up in 2019, DeLorey's view was that they caused Conservatives to lean on the notion that Trudeau was a racist, when most voters were more inclined to the view that he was just an idiot.
"They tried to make the ballot question, 'Is Justin Trudeau racist or not?' He's not. No one believes that. And we lost our message track." DeLorey told Maher. "Yes, he was a dumb kid, but it doesn't mean he was racist." According to the polls, however, the Conservatives were always the underdog in the 2019 election, and the blackface scandal did surprisingly little to alter Canadian voting intentions.
A Sept. 17 Leger poll issued on the eve of the blackface photos being made public showed the Liberals polling at 34 per cent to the Conservatives'33 per cent.
Two weeks later - after images of Trudeau in blackface had exploded around the world - another Leger poll delivered the near-identical result of having the Liberals and Conservatives tied at 34 per cent.
And both Leger polls were right on target with the final voting results. In terms of popular vote, the Conservatives received 34.34 per cent of ballots to the Liberals'33.12 per cent.
Despite this plurality in votes, the Conservatives won just 121 seats to the Liberals' 157.
The gap was explained by what Liberal strategists would later term "vote efficiency": The Liberals were able to win more close ridings where the vote was split by multiple parties, while the Conservatives mostly strengthened their vote share in places that were already sending Conservative MPs to Ottawa.
DeLorey, now an Ottawa-based PR consultant, was a Conservative organizer as far back as 2004, when he spearheaded Stephen Harper's successful leadership bid. DeLorey was an unsuccessful Tory candidate in Peter MacKay's old riding of Central Nova, N.S., in 2015, and in the 2021 federal election would be the Conservatives'national campaign director.
At the height of the blackface scandal in 2019, De-Lorey appeared in several media interviews providing the Conservative perspective on the issue.
In a panel discussion on Global News, DeLorey differed from the show's other progressive-leaning guests in not bringing up the racial undertones of the blackface photos. Rather, he focused on how Trudeau had been initially cagey about how many other photos there were.
"At the end of the day, I think this campaign is becoming an issue of trust," he said. "Mr. Trudeau is continually showing that he can't be trusted, that he's not being honest." National Post

Document 494 of 500

Speaker insists his decisions have been unbiased, as Commons considers voting him out

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/speaker-insists-his-decisions-have-been-unbiased/docview/3061290399/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Speaker+insists+his+decisions+have+been+unbiased%2C+as+Commons+considers+voting+him+out&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Speaker Greg Fergus insisted he is running an impartial House of Commons, as Liberal and NDP MPs appear ready to vote to keep him in the chair amid charges of partisanship.
Last week, Fergus was accused of partisanship after an invite went out to a Liberal party event that indicated he would appear and included an attack against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. The Liberal party has since apologized for the invite and said they included the language without Fergus's knowledge.
The Conservatives raised the Speaker's participation in the event as a point of privilege in the House of Commons and deputy Speaker Chris d'Entremont found on Monday there was a prima facie case that Fergus had violated the rules of the House.
That set up Tuesday's debate, with the Conservatives calling for Fergus to resign, but the Liberals and the NDP moved to put a time limit on that debate, and a vote on the issue was expected later Tuesday.
The House of Commons is just weeks from rising for a summer recess and a lengthy debate on Fergus or a move to replace him as Speaker would make it difficult for the Liberals to pass several bills before the House rises.
"I hope all the decisions that I've pronounced in this place would be judged by any fair minded person as being decisions that can stand the test of time and that were impartial," Fergus said.
Conservative MP Jamil Jivani asked Fergus to explain more about the latest incident and why MPs should trust him to oversee the House of Commons.
"Before MPs vote tonight on if you should remain chair, why should we believe you?" Jivani asked.
Fergus has previously been criticized for appearing in a video that was played at the Ontario Liberal Party convention and has been accused of bias for his decision to remove Poilievre from the chamber earlier this month, when the Conservative leader described the prime minister as "wacko."
House Speaker Greg Fergus under fire for link to event attacking Poilievre; Liberal party apologizes
Legislation banning replacement workers unanimously passes in House of Commons
Conservative MP Michelle Rempel Garner went back further to an incident in 2016, long before Fergus became Speaker. Fergus was then parliamentary secretary to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and criticized the NDP after the "elbowgate" incident.
During that incident, Trudeau elbowed former NDP MP Ruth Ellen Brosseau in the chamber. Fergus said at the time the way the NDP was acting was "reminiscent of a dive in the 2014 World Cup."
Rempel Garner said that incident and his partisanship more generally prevents him from managing harassment issues in the House of Commons.
"Do you think opposition MPs would feel safe or empowered to report instances of any sort of harassment?" she asked Fergus.
"You said I believe him, not I believe her and that is what is wrong with every workplace around the world."
Fergus said his comments in 2016 were not an indication that he didn't believe Brosseau, but how he felt the NDP were acting as a party. He said if an MP brought a harassment concern to him he would handle it appropriately.
"I believe that members should feel fully confident in my ability."
Fergus was asked by Liberal MPs on the committee how he thought they should respond to social media posts, sometimes done by other members of Parliament that led to harassment online.
Fergus said he sympathized, but said there is no role for the Speaker in dealing with that issue.
"There have been instances of members posting comments regarding fellow members on social media that some would qualify as inappropriate or even harassment. While obviously a serious matter, it is not one that the speaker has the jurisdiction over."
National Post
rtumilty@postmedia.com

Identifier / keyword: ottawa; speaker; fergus; insisted; running; impartial

Title: Speaker insists his decisions have been unbiased, as Commons considers voting him out

Document 495 of 500

Joe Oliver: The Trudeau Liberals have eroded all five pillars of prosperity

Author: Watson, William

Publication info: Watson, William. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/joe-oliver-trudeau-liberals-have-eroded-all-five/docview/3060990644/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Joe+Oliver%3A+The+Trudeau+Liberals+have+eroded+all+five+pillars+of+prosperity&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Watson%2C+William&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's standard of living is in decline, both in absolute terms and compared to our southern neighbour and other wealthy countries. A Fraser Institute analysis shows that real GDP per capita was lower during the pre-recession period 2016-19 than in any similar period since 1985. As of the last quarter of 2023 it was below its value for 2019:Q2. It's no surprise that 44 per cent of Canadians now say money is their leading source of stress.
What explains Canada's dreadful performance? As set out by Arthur Laffer, of Laffer Curve fame, prosperity has five pillars: restrained government spending, low taxes, minimal regulation, sound money and free trade. The Liberal government has rejected, undermined or neglected each of the five. Our weak record and disheartening prospects have not been caused by external forces but by dysfunctional government policies.
Canada is blessed by enviable geology and geography - immense natural resources and a friendly superpower next door - which Canadians too frequently take for granted. Because our border is safe and our population well off by world and historical standards, progressive politicians feel free to obsess about issues irrelevant or actually harmful to economic growth, jobs, affordability, a sound currency, security and national unity.
Let's review the litany of debilitating missteps, starting with the size and role of government. The federal public service reached over 274,000 employees in 2023, an increase of 40.4 per cent since 2015. A bloated bureaucracy drains resources from the private sector, reducing economic efficiency. In the last eight years, the depletion has been rapid. Federal spending swelled from 12.8 per cent of GDP in 2015 to 16.1 per cent in 2023. Federal debt more than doubled, from $612 billion to a staggering $1.4 trillion - over $143,000 for a family of four. Interest now costs Ottawa $47.2 billion a year, rising to $64.3 billion by 2028-29. This is fiscal profligacy writ large.
Tax increases discourage economic growth. The Laffer curve demonstrates that taxes set too high can actually reduce tax revenue. Out of 61 US jurisdictions and Canadian provinces, the top three personal marginal income tax rates are imposed by Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia and Ontario. Nine Canadian provinces rank in the top 10, all are in the top 15, and Canada ranks fifth out of 38 OECD countries. Corporate income tax rates are also higher here than in the U.S., the U.K. and the OECD on average. High taxes damage affordability, reduce competitiveness, discourage innovation and entrepreneurship, accelerate capital flight and weaken productivity. The proposed increase in the capital gains inclusion rate for both individuals and companies and the phase-out of accelerated capital depreciation will seriously exacerbate those negatives.
Since 2015, intrusive regulations have proliferated across the economy, imposing burdensome compliance costs that are particularly harmful to small and medium- sized enterprises. The resource industry, which accounts for 19.2 per cent of GDP and 58 per cent of merchandise exports, has been targeted by draconian regulation deliberately designed to block energy projects. The result is an opportunity loss in the hundreds of billions of dollars and mounting.
A stable money supply is critical for economic stability. To cope with out-of- control government spending, the Bank of Canada expanded the money supply dramatically, pushing it to $3.6 trillion, 83 per cent more than when the Liberals took office. As a result, in 2022 inflation hit a 40-year peak of 6.8 per cent. Consumer prices are now 27 per cent higher than in 2015. Rising prices disproportionately affect low- and middle-income Canadians, who are also vulnerable to hikes in interest rates, including mortgage rates up 50 per cent from 2015. In aggregate, total mortgage payments could rise by as much as $4 billion this year.
Free trade had been a cornerstone of Canada's economic policy for decades, promoting growth and prosperity. But last year Canada lost bragging rights as America's biggest trade partner to Mexico. Instead of pursuing our comparative advantage in natural resources, Liberal policies purposely stymie the development and export of oil and gas. In a memorably inane comment, the prime minister claimed there was never a strong business case for liquified natural gas. The government should leave the assessment of business cases to business.
Barriers to interprovincial trade, a related problem, have continued to elude meaningful progress despite repeated promises. The Montreal Economic Institute estimates that removing those barriers would yield an average increase in Canadians' incomes by 5.5 per cent, or $1,800. According to the IMF, it could boost GDP by $80 billion.
Hands off my pension! That includes you, Chrystia Freeland
Liberals need to ditch Steven Guilbeault's radical activism
The government's score for supporting the mainstays of prosperity is zero for five. Rather than correcting course, Justin Trudeau seems increasingly disconnected from reality and fixated on maintaining a perfect losing streak. Doubling down on big government, high taxes and hostility to resource development will do the trick.
Joe Oliver was minister of natural resources and finance in the Harper government.
Bookmark our website and support our journalism: Don't miss the business news you need to know - add financialpost.com to your bookmarks and sign up for our newsletters here

Location: United States--US; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Organization for Economic Cooperation & Development; NAICS: 541720

Identifier / keyword: standard; living; decline; absolute; terms; compared

Document 496 of 500

Common sense in language policy; William Watson

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/common-sense-language-policy-william-watson/docview/3060924158/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Common+sense+in+language+policy%3B+William+Watson&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was a lovely story from The Canadian Press in yesterday's Post about two Ontario politicians getting together over breakfast recently to invent another feel-good collective right - and, it would appear, at least a six-figure budget line to go along with it. It concerns Ontario government house leader Paul Calandra and Sol Mamakwa, NDP member of the provincial legislature for Kiiwetinoong, a giant riding in northwestern Ontario, which incidentally has only a third the population of the average Ontario riding.
It seems Calandra, best known to Canadians outside Ontario for his tearful apology for providing evasive answers in the House of Commons in 2014 while serving as parliamentary secretary to Prime Minister Stephen Harper, was annoyed by Mamakwa complaining at a Queen's Park reception that he wasn't allowed to speak his language in the legislature.
When his staff informed him that was true - only English and French were allowed in the legislature (a basic parliamentary fact a government house leader really should know) - Calandra became even more annoyed and now the rule is English, French "or an Indigenous language spoken in Canada." On being elected, new members will inform the clerk of the legislative assembly of Ontario which language they intend to use and the clerk will arrange for translation.
The existing rule was "just wrong," CP quotes Calandra as saying. "It just seemed like this is common sense and I can't believe that we don't actually allow it to happen."
The new rule may be many things - a lovely gesture, a continuing apology, a woke token of respect and welcome - but the one thing it definitely is not is common sense. Statistics Canada's latest numbers show that in 2021 just over one in 10 First Nations people living in Ontario could speak an Indigenous language. Only 6,165 could speak Mamakwa's language - Anishininiimowin or (its settler, i.e., English name) Oji-Cree. And that was down 15 per cent from just five years earlier.
In 2021, Ontario's population was 14,223,942, which means the legislature is now going to provide simultaneous translation for a language spoken by one in every 2,307 Ontarians.
This is a great new rule for translators and for the union workers who do renovations in the Ontario legislature - since it seems the translation space will have to be redesigned and expanded - but it is not a great rule for anyone else. And you know the effect will spread: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau isn't going to let Ontario Premier Doug Ford, of all people, out-do him in political gesturing.
One of the great achievements of Pierre Trudeau, Justin's father, carrying through on Lester Pearson's initiative, was the Official Languages Act, which confirmed that English and French were the official languages of Canada and that the federal government had to provide services in both. But that right, later largely enshrined in Pierre Trudeau's other great legacy, the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, came with a common-sense qualifier: "where numbers warrant." Services will be provided in the minority language where numbers warrant.
There have been many disputes over the years, including court cases, over just how big the numbers have to be to warrant this or that. But cost and inconvenience were clearly part of the common-sense equation. Not any more, it seems. For those of us who came of political age in Canada in the 1960s, bilingualism, biculturalism, Trudeaumania, reconciliation with Quebec and openness to French were all very exciting (especially, I concede, for English Montrealers). Across the country parents of good will enrolled their kids in French immersion. When Quebec responded with a separatist government in 1976 and referendums on independence in 1980 and 1995, enthusiasm waned, to say the least.
But now Marc Tanguay, interim leader of the Quebec Liberal party (currently running at six per cent in the polls), seems smitten with the B&B spirit of the '60s. He wants the Canada of 2050 to be a place where half of us can speak French - at the moment just under 30 per cent can - and to that end he wants Ottawa to source more immigrants from French-speaking places.
Great, you're probably thinking, another expensive, unrealistic 2050 target. Seats in Quebec are always a concern for any federal party hoping to form a government but my guess is that outside Quebec popular support for a new bilingualism push is below net-zero - especially with the current Quebec government and the separatist party leading it in the polls gradually tightening restrictions on the province's English speakers (who number, in terms of home-users, more than 850,000 people, almost four times the number of Indigenous-Canadians who can speak a First Nations language well enough to carry on a conversation).
At Confederation, Quebec's population was just less than a third of Canada's. It's now just over a fifth. Quebec currently seems less enthusiastic about immigrants - French or English - than the rest of the country does. If actual immigration reflects that, how long before the rest of the country decides Quebec's numbers as a share of Canada's population no longer warrant the country's bilingual/bicultural political superstructure?
Financial Post

Business indexing term: Industry: 92112 :‎ Legislative Bodies

Document 498 of 500

Postmedia sells three newspapers to Manitoba's Klein Group

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/postmedia-sells-three-newspapers-manitobas-klein/docview/3060924155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Postmedia+sells+three+newspapers+to+Manitoba%27s+Klein+Group&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Postmedia Network Inc., the company that owns the National Post and dozens of other newspapers across Canada, has announced that it is selling three newspapers based in Manitoba and Western Ontario.
The papers, the Winnipeg Sun, the Graphic Leader in Portage la Prairie and the Kenora Miner & News, will be sold to the Winnipeg-based Klein Group Ltd.
No job losses are anticipated, the company said in a news release announcing the sale on Monday. Financial details of the agreement have not been released.
The Klein Group is headed up by Kevin Klein, a former Manitoba PC cabinet minister, one-time Winnipeg city councillor and former publisher of the Sun.
"In an age where information is abundant and easily accessible, the role of local journalism is more crucial today than ever before," Klein said in a news release.
In recent months, Klein had been writing a column for the Winnipeg Sun, following his defeat in last year's provincial election.
The Klein Group, according to the press release, is owned entirely by Manitobans and aims for "factual and unbiased news."
The Sun began publishing in 1980 after Southam Newspapers closed the Winnipeg Tribune after 90 years.
It was later acquired by Quebecor in 1983. In 1999, Quebecor purchased the Sun Media newspaper chain, which included Sun papers in Edmonton, Calgary and Toronto. It became part of the Postmedia chain in 2014 when the Sun titles were purchased from Quebecor for $316 million.
While the Winnipeg Sun, Graphic Leader and Kenora Miner & News will have new ownership, it will retain a relationship with Postmedia, receiving editorial content from Postmedia journalists, Postmedia CEO Andrew MacLeod said.
The deal will close June 1.
National Post

Document 499 of 500

Poilievre at the centre of conservatism

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-at-centre-conservatism/docview/3060924154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Poilievre+at+the+centre+of+conservatism&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'One of the recurring the mesin Pierre Poilievre's political life is good timing," writes conservative commentator Andrew Lawton in his new biography of the Opposition leader and likely future prime minister of Canada.
And Lawton's Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life indeed chronicles a series of eerily serendipitous occurrences in the subject's life, starting, quite literally, from the day he was born.
While 16-year-old Calgary high school student Jacqueline Farrell was in labour with her first child on June 3, 1979, then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau was simultaneously clearing out his desk, serving out his last full day in office prior to ceding power to upstart Progressive Conservative Joe Clark.
Trudeau the elder would, of course, return to power less than a year later, but this is nevertheless a fortuitous omen for a man who, 45 years later, looks all but certain to vanquish son Justin Trudeau.
This is the first of many such coincidences documented in A Political Life: a thoroughly researched and lucid account of Poilievre's improbable journey from a "remarkably ordinary" upbringing in his adoptive parents'Calgary household to his current perch within striking distance of Canada's highest office.
Lawton draws heavily from interviews with a handful of Poilievre's contemporaries, including University of Calgary classmate Shuvaloy Majumdar (now MP for Calgary Heritage), Ian Brodie, ex-chief of staff to former prime minister Stephen Harper, and Poilievre's one-time political mentor John Baird, formerly foreign affairs minister under Harper. Perhaps most impressively, Lawton was able to get backroom powerhouse Jenni Byrne, who dated Poilievre for over a decade while the two were scaling the ranks of Ottawa, to speak on-record about her relationship with the would-be prime minister.
The book is broken into three parts. Part I, titled "early years," doubles as a serviceable history of the uniting of Canada's fractured right in the early 2000s. Through a mixture of gumption and happenstance, a college-aged Poilievre sporting frosted tips, finds himself at the centre of multiple key junctures of this chaotic and definitive time frame.
A defining moment for Poilievre came at the age of 20, when he was a finalist in an essay competition sponsored by Frank Stronach, the Austrian-born founder of Ontario-based auto parts manufacturer Magna International.
Writing in response to the prompt, "As prime minister, I would...", Poilievre submitted a classically liberal manifesto titled "Building Canada through freedom." While the essay was not awarded the competition's top prize, it did lay out an ideological North Star guiding Poilievre through his subsequent two decades in politics. Apropos of our current political moment, the essay argued for the abolition of the federal tax on capital gains, saying this reform would "free the (entrepreneurial) eagle from its cage."
Poilievre would soon return to his home turf of Calgary, where he had a front-row seat to the wrangling of the unite the right movement's three leading figures. Starting out on the board of Reform Party founder Preston Manning's riding association in southwest Calgary, Poilievre would soon switch allegiances to Manning's rival Stockwell Day. (One interesting tidbit unearthed by Lawton is that Poilievre himself launched the website "www.DraftStockDay. com" as a second-year university student).
He would later help Ezra Levant, now the head of Rebel News, in a failed effort to hold on to the Canadian Alliance's nomination in Calgary Southwest after the party's incoming leader Stephen Harper was parachuted into the riding. It is an episode that doesn't appear to have engendered any bad blood between Poilievre and the future prime minister.
Parts II and III turn to Poilievre's 18-year (and counting) tenure in Parliament and eventual leadership aspirations. While, as a well-known conservative pundit, Lawton is clearly sympathetic to his subject, he steers clear of a temptation to veer into hagiography. Lawton has his share of pointed words for Poilievre, criticizing him, for example, for his inconsistent support of the leaders of Ottawa's 2022 Freedom Convoy protests. (The convoy was the subject of Lawton's last book.) Lawton is also critical of Poilievre for immediately caving to left-wing outrage and denouncing "far-right" German politician Christine Anderson during a 2023 visit to Canada, where she was photographed having lunch with three sitting Conservative MPs.
Lawton also shares some juicy tidbits from the notoriously private Poilievre's personal life, including a few intimate details from his early courtship with now wife Anaida - Lawton reports that Poilievre dropped an "L-bomb" two weeks into the couple's relationship - a gesture that wasn't immediately reciprocated. The book also reveals that Poilievre's now five-year-old daughter Valentina is on the autism spectrum. Both Poilievres have referenced their daughter's "special needs" publicly but the couple has never gone into detail about her condition.
Lawton also touches, briefly, on Poilievre's reconciliation in his 20s with biological mother "Jackie," who now works as a nurse in North Carolina. Another notable passage reveals that Poilievre's adoptive father Donald came out as gay shortly after divorcing wife Marlene in the early 1990s. Lawton reports that Poilievre has long maintained a positive relationship with Donald and his long-term partner Ross McWhirther.
Andrew Lawton's A Political Life paints a compelling portrait of a man who was bitten by the political bug at a young age and has long seemed almost preordained to one day lead Canada. It's a book that, above all, conveys Poilievre's remarkable knack for timing, indicating that he is the right leader for our turbulent times.
National Post

A messy Conservative nomination race

Author: Sarkonak, Jamie

Publication info: Sarkonak, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/messy-conservative-nomination-race/docview/3060924099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=A+messy+Conservative+nomination+race&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Sarkonak%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Five candidates were campaigning to become the Conservative nominee for the Ontario riding of Aurora-Oak Ridges-Richmond Hill, but on Tuesday's vote, only two will be on the ballot: Costas Menegakis and Carmine Perrelli.
But those who didn't make the cut haven't accepted defeat quietly - making for an optics problem, and perhaps a public confidence problem.
The three who didn't make the list are former National Post columnist Sabrina Maddeaux, Aurora, Ont. town councillor Rachel Gilliland and small business owner Yun Liu. The first two women have alleged irregularities in the race with respect to one candidate in particular. The party was dismissive of their concerns, and in turn, made allegations of its own against them.
Maddeaux made her exit first. On May 9, one day after nominations closed, she suspended her campaign with an announcement that has now been viewed more than one million times on X. She complained that she had been the subject of smear emails in April - emails that had somehow reached new members whose contact information was not supposed to be available to candidates. She had also been alerted that one candidate was targeting the new addresses she'd signed up, which were also supposed to be off-limits until closure.
"(The email) was full of mischaracterizations and also, (these were) things that maybe I wouldn't write today, but were written 14 years ago when I was 22. I don't think anyone probably agrees with everything they did or said when they were 22, 14 years later," she told me over the phone.
The email, which I have seen, cited a few articles written by Maddeaux more than 10 years ago, with headlines such as "In Defense of Lingerie for Little Girls," and "Never Say Never: The Day I Got Vajazzled for Pride." It encouraged recipients to vote against Maddeaux because "Sabrina consistently shows a lack of judgment and professionalism needed to represent the Conservative Party."
Prior to the email's release, Maddeaux could also feel the party sentiment cool. In January, she received support from MPs Scott Aitchison and Shuv Majumdar, who canvassed with her in the first week of February, as well as Adam Chambers. Brad Vis, another Conservative MP, endorsed her as well, though his X, formerly Twitter, post doing so has since been deleted.
Menegakis entered the race in the second week of February, and around this time, support for Maddeaux ebbed. MPs didn't associate with her apart from a group panel discussion on Conservatism in late February.
Gilliland followed Maddeaux in raising concerns about the nomination race by going public on Wednesday. Upon the party's refusal to approve her candidacy (first at the electoral district level, then at the national level on appeal), she released a statement, expressing frustration with the process. She cited irregularities and suspected rule violations, such as "negative character emails against 3 candidates sent to new members prior to the release of any final lists, targeted emails and door knocks on new members as recent as April 2024, current EDA board president and other board members actively campaigning in favour of a candidate."
Maddeaux and Gilliland both pointed to the Menegakis campaign. Maddeaux says it was Menegakis'volunteers who were showing up at the doorsteps of new members she had recently signed. Another source with the party, speaking on background, noted that MailChimp was the service used to send out the emails that disparaged Maddeaux. Menegakis, this source said, was the only candidate whose campaign used MailChimp to reach members over email.
Gilliland had similar troubles with her new sign-ups. On March 17, she claims to have received six text messages from homeowners questioning how their addresses were circulated to another campaign that was making rounds. At that point, this information should not have been made available - and the high frequency of these reports suggested that whoever was getting to these houses was probably targeting certain homes, rather than going up and down the block.
"It's one thing to have an old list, old information," Gilliland said. But for those who were new members, who had never been affiliated with the party in the past, "there's absolutely no way there would be any old information on this person."
A couple of puzzled, newly signed members even sent her screenshots of doorbell camera footage, which I have seen, showing visits to their doors: one, from a man who appears to be Menegakis. The man's face is slightly obscured, but his build and hair are consistent with Menegakis's likeness.
Another photo sent to Gilliland by a supporter showed a doorstep visit by the electoral district association president, Raphael Chang, holding an inscrutable paper document. It wasn't necessarily evidence of rule-breaking: Conservative Party rules don't require electoral district association presidents to remain neutral in the campaign nomination process unless they are members of the candidate nomination committee, which is responsible for overseeing the election. Gilliland said Chang was not on this committee. However, presidents are involved in choosing who sits on the nomination committee, so if Chang was supporting a campaign, that allegiance could be perceived as an unfair advantage.
Chang and Menegakis did not return any requests for comment.
The party's response to all this? When asked by a National Post reporter, Conservative Party spokeswoman Sarah Fischer denied the allegations from Maddeaux and said she had been named in other complaints about using emails that should have been inaccessible. As to whether an investigation found the complaints well-founded, Fischer's statement didn't say, nor did she respond to my own questions on the matter.
Gilliland received a more subtle treatment. On Thursday, a senior party source on background told Post reporter Catherine Lévesque that the councillor was denied her candidacy because she'd inappropriately used her town's website to sell memberships. Gilliland denies any rule-breaking, and said that the town website linked to her personal website, which links to her candidate website. Her memberships, she says, were all signed up on paper. (Her candidate website does allow donations, however.)
And so, the Conservative members are left with Menegakis and Perrelli, which is barely a race.
Perrelli, a Richmond Hill, Ont. city councillor, doesn't appear to be an optimal candidate. He's a former president of foster care company Expanding Horizons which has had its share of bad press. In 2019, with Perrelli at the head, a 15-year-old in care was fatally stabbed by another boy. A CBC investigation into the matter in 2021 reported that a 24-yearold with no experience raising kids had been working as a foster parent at the home in question with little support, and that the company's consulting psychiatrist had given up his license in 2016.
More recently, Perrelli has faced reprimands for violating his city council's code of conduct.
Menegakis doesn't have that kind of baggage, and he's familiar to the riding. A former one-term MP and a three-time election defeatee, he won the riding in 2011. Under the Harper government, he eventually became parliamentary secretary to the immigration minister. But he lost his seat in 2015, 1,093 votes behind the Liberal candidate. He stepped aside to allow Liberal-turned-Conservative Leona Alleslev to run in Aurora in 2019. During that election, he was the Conservative candidate for the riding of Richmond Hill, where he lost by 212 votes. In 2021, he tried again in Richmond Hill and lost by 4,069.
With more than two election losses, Menegakis requires a special exemption from the Conservative Party if he wishes to run. The party granted that exemption, and he announced his run in early February, three weeks after Maddeaux.
When he was in office, Menegakis wasn't exactly a headliner. He did do good, though. He firmly said "no" to relaxing immigration rules that refuse entry to those who practice polygamy, a form of family that is often harmful to children and women. He spoke out against efforts by other parties to reduce the language requirements for those seeking citizenship. He has defended Canadian values.
He got the ball rolling on Criminal Code amendments to deal greater punishments to offenders who harm police, military and service animals (he attempted to do this in a private member's bill, but it had to be withdrawn when he was promoted to parliamentary secretary; a government bill was later passed to the same effect).
Praise aside, Menegakis has a record of losses. He might serve better in the Senate if he's good at committee work. If he's good at governance, appoint him to some department in 2025. Instead, the party appears to be running an uncompetitive race that could crown someone who might not be the right fit for the role.
The counterpoint could be that Menegakis deserves a shot at running under a popular leader, which hasn't been the case the last couple times around. Team Blue won't be so hindered anymore: poll results have been flattering for months and success is now a near-guarantee, with aggregator 338Canada certifying the riding "CPC Safe." Menegakis also has a term in Ottawa that his opponents don't have, which could make for an easier transition into government. That said, three losses in a row should mean something. There may have been other interests that could explain any potential corner-cutting, backroom dealing and looking the other way. Perhaps there are debts to be paid to the man who stepped aside as the party's candidate to make room for the Liberal floor crosser who ousted him in 2015. That's just politics - and it's possible Maddeaux and Gilliland went in deaf to any hushed horse-trading that could have been going on in the background. If this was actually the strategy, it shouldn't have been executed so clumsily that suspicion was raised.
Ideally, it would have been the membership that decided who to send up. The race was shaping up to be competitive, by accounts of the candidates. By Maddeaux's tally, she sold 200 new memberships (an anonymous source to CBC put it closer to 50) and confirmed another 1,100 supporters among the existing membership. Because voter turnout in nearby nomination contests numbered around 600 to 1,200, she figured she had a shot. Gilliland had 580 supporters, about 80 per cent of whom accounted for new memberships, she says. Plus, non-supporters can still provide a boost through the ranked ballot.
Observers with more political experience will tend to take the exits of Maddeaux and Gilliland as good news. The candidate who takes infighting public, even if based on legitimate complaints, can't be trusted to play on the team when the stakes are high. And besides, they'll say, the best revenge against the people responsible for "irregularities" is to win outright. Maddeaux didn't need to fold.
"As someone who's always used my voice to stand up for the values I believe in ... it felt to me that what was going on was so egregious that I had to go public," she explained.
But from the outside, it just looks like a mess. The Conservative party wouldn't, or couldn't, debunk any of the allegations by either of the two women who spoke up. One candidate, intentional or not, appeared to have an unfair advantage that was shrugged off. Membership data may have been handled recklessly - but it's unclear if the party even cares.
A person looking at the race from a mile away might see that two candidates were frozen out to make way for a predictable two-way race. The party has done little to dispel that.
Candidate-hopefuls sacrifice a lot to run: Maddeaux gave up a column and threeplus months of income; Gilliland has been working on her campaign for about a year, which has taken time out of her business.
Now, they look naive with little to show for their time, and the party looks ... questionable.
No one is walking away from this looking better than before.
This will be the party that almost certainly wins in 2025, which means that it will be the party that governs. Occasions like this do not inspire confidence.
National Post
